Naive polynomial multiplication can be computationally expensive.
Overlooking potential overflow with large coefficients.
Misinterpreting the final coefficient's meaning.
Forgetting to handle constant terms or zero outcomes.
invariants: The degree of the product polynomial is the sum of the degrees of the input polynomials.
The coefficient of x^k in the product is the sum of A[i]*B[j] for all i+j=k.
Input polynomial coefficients remain unchanged during multiplication.

title: Operations on polynomials and series
when_to_use: When you need to compute the product of two polynomials or formal power series, often represented by their coefficient arrays. This is useful for combining algebraic expressions or solving recurrence relations.
description: This involves computing the coefficients of the product polynomial by convolving the coefficients of the input polynomials. Algorithms like Karatsuba's use a divide-and-conquer recursive approach to achieve better than naive quadratic time complexity.
tips: Represent polynomials as arrays of coefficients
Pad shorter polynomials with zeros to match degrees
Consider recursive algorithms like Karatsuba for efficiency
Handle base cases carefully in recursive implementations
The degree of the product is the sum of input degrees

Naive O(N^2) multiplication can be too slow for large inputs
Incorrectly calculating convolution indices

title: Operations on polynomials and series
when_to_use: When problems involve manipulating algebraic polynomials or power series, especially when their properties or coefficients are defined recursively. It is also useful for problems involving generating functions to represent sequences.
description: This card describes recursive approaches to perform common operations on polynomials and power series, often represented by their coefficients. The complexity varies depending on the specific operation, but many are O(N^2) or O(N log N) with optimized techniques.
tips: Represent polynomials as arrays of coefficients.
Base cases for recursion are crucial (e.g., constant polynomial, empty series).
Consider memoization for overlapping subproblems.
Leverage properties of generating functions for sequence problems.
Use modular arithmetic for large coefficients.

Off-by-one errors in coefficient indexing.
Stack overflow with deep recursion without tail call optimization.
Ignoring potential for combinatorial explosion in recursive calls.
Floating point precision issues for non-integer coefficients.

When performing an operation recursively, the result of subproblems correctly represents the corresponding sub-polynomial or sub-series.
The degree of the resulting polynomial or series is correctly maintained or truncated.
The base cases of the recursion are correctly handled to terminate the process.

title: Operations on polynomials and series
when_to_use: When you need to model and solve combinatorial counting problems using algebraic methods, especially when dealing with recurrence relations or products of independent choices. It's also useful for analyzing properties of sequences.

These operations model combinatorial structures and solve recurrence relations, typically with complexities from O(N) to O(N log N).

Understand the combinatorial meaning of operations (e.g., product for independent choices)
Use Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) for efficient polynomial multiplication
Derivatives and integrals can manipulate coefficients
Consider exponential generating functions for labeled objects
Ordinary generating functions are for unlabeled objects

Handling large coefficients or modulo arithmetic

Computational complexity for naive multiplication

title: Operations on polynomials and series
when_to_use: Use when performing arithmetic operations like multiplication or division on polynomial expressions or formal power series. It is also applicable for modeling combinatorial problems using generating functions.
description: This involves performing fundamental arithmetic operations on polynomials or formal power series, often represented by their coefficients. Operations like multiplication and division can be implemented recursively, with complexity varying from O(N^2) to O(N log N) depending on the algorithm.
tips: Represent polynomials as arrays of coefficients, typically from lowest to highest degree.
Handle leading zero coefficients carefully, especially for division and degree calculations.
Consider Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) for asymptotically faster polynomial multiplication and division.
Use modular arithmetic for coefficients if the problem involves large numbers or specific fields.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling polynomial degrees, leading to off-by-one errors in coefficient arrays.
Attempting division by a zero polynomial, which is undefined.
Numerical stability issues when dealing with floating-point coefficients in certain algorithms.
Inefficient naive O(N^2) multiplication when N is large, where O(N log N) methods are required.
invariants: For polynomial division A(x) / B(x) = Q(x) with remainder R(x), the identity A(x) = Q(x) * B(x) + R(x) holds.
The degree of the remainder polynomial R(x) is strictly less than the degree of the divisor polynomial B(x).
The coefficients of the resulting polynomial (product or quotient) are correctly derived from the input coefficients.
The degree of the product of two polynomials with degrees m and n is m+n.

title: Operations on polynomials and series
when_to_use: When performing algebraic operations on polynomials or power series, such as finding their greatest common divisor, differentiation, integration, or computing series expansions like inverse, logarithm, and exponential, especially in modular arithmetic.
description: This card details various algebraic operations on polynomials and power series, including finding their greatest common divisor (GCD) and computing derivatives, integrals, inverses, logarithms, exponentials, and powers. These operations manipulate polynomial coefficients, often within a modular arithmetic context, with many achieving O(N log N) or O(N log NK) complexity.
tips: Represent polynomials as arrays of coefficients, typically from lowest to highest degree.
Modular arithmetic is crucial for many polynomial operations, including GCD, to prevent overflow and ensure results stay within a finite field.
The Euclidean algorithm can be adapted for polynomial GCD, using polynomial division with remainder.
Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) or Number Theoretic Transform (NTT) is often the underlying technique for O(N log N) polynomial multiplication, essential for inverse, log, exp, and power.
For derivative, multiply each coefficient by its degree and shift.
For integral, divide each coefficient by (degree+1) and shift, ensuring Q(0)=0.
pitfalls: Forgetting to handle modular inverses for division operations (e.g., integration, polynomial division in GCD, inverse, log).
Incorrectly handling the constant term or leading coefficients, especially for inverse, logarithm, or polynomial division.
Off-by-one errors when shifting coefficients for derivatives or integrals.
Not ensuring the modulus is prime for NTT-based operations or modular inverse existence.
The complexity of polynomial GCD can be high; consider optimized algorithms like half-GCD for large degrees.
Division by zero polynomial in GCD or non-invertible constant term for series inverse/log.
invariants: The degree of the resulting polynomial/series is correctly maintained after each operation.
Coefficients are always reduced modulo m if modular arithmetic is applied.
For GCD, the resulting polynomial divides both input polynomials.
For series operations like inverse, log, exp, the first n coefficients of the result are accurate.

title: Operations on polynomials and series
when_to_use: Use when performing algebraic operations or evaluations on polynomials, especially when efficiency is critical for large degrees or multiple evaluation points.
description: This card covers fundamental operations on polynomials, including degree, leading coefficient, resultant, and efficient evaluation at multiple points. It leverages techniques like divide-and-conquer for optimal time complexity, often O(N log N) or O(N^2).
tips: Represent polynomials as arrays of coefficients, usually from lowest to highest degree.
Use modular arithmetic for operations on coefficients to prevent overflow.
Leverage Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) or similar techniques for O(N log N) multiplication and evaluation.
Consider polynomial long division for resultant computation or GCD.
Precompute powers of `z` for Chirp Z-transform to optimize evaluation.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling polynomial degrees, especially for zero polynomials.
Overflow issues with large coefficients if modular arithmetic is not applied.
Inefficient naive O(N^2) evaluation when faster methods like Chirp Z-transform are applicable.
Errors in implementing polynomial long division or Euclidean algorithm for resultants.
Floating-point precision issues if coefficients are not integers or fixed-point.
invariants: For polynomial P(x) represented by `coeffs`, `coeffs[i]` stores the coefficient of `x^i`.
During binary exponentiation `x^n`, the product `current_result * base^remaining_power` always equals `x^n`.
The resultant of two polynomials `A(x)` and `B(x)` is zero if and only if they share a common root.
For Chirp Z-transform, the computed values `P(z^k)` are correct evaluations of the polynomial at the specified geometric progression points.

title: Continued fractions
when_to_use: Use when seeking the best rational approximations of real numbers or solving certain Diophantine equations. It's also applied in number theory for analyzing properties of irrational numbers.
description: Continued fractions represent a real number as a sum of an integer and the reciprocal of another number, recursively. This iterative process, based on the Euclidean algorithm, generates a sequence of best rational approximations. The computation of convergents typically has logarithmic complexity.
tips: Understand the relationship with the Euclidean algorithm for rational numbers.
Convergents provide the best rational approximations for a given denominator size.
The process is finite for rational numbers and infinite for irrational numbers.
Use recurrence relations to efficiently compute numerators and denominators of convergents.
Recognize that quadratic irrationals have periodic continued fraction expansions.
pitfalls: Floating-point precision issues can significantly affect accuracy for real numbers.
Misinterpreting 'best approximation' as globally best, rather than best for a given denominator bound.
Incorrectly handling the initial integer part or negative numbers.
Potential for integer overflow when computing large numerators and denominators of convergents.
Forgetting that the partial quotients (except the first) must be positive integers.
invariants: The current convergent p_k / q_k is the best rational approximation for its denominator size.
The sequence of partial quotients a_i are integers, with a_i >= 1 for i > 0.
The absolute value of the difference between the original number and the current convergent decreases monotonically.
The numerators and denominators of convergents p_k, q_k are non-negative and strictly increasing for k > 0.

title: Continued fractions
when_to_use: Use when representing real numbers as a sequence of integers for rational approximation, or when analyzing properties of irrational numbers. It's also useful for solving certain Diophantine equations and Pell's equation.
description: Continued fractions represent a real number as a sum of an integer and the reciprocal of another number, which is then represented in the same way. This process generates a sequence of integers, and its complexity depends on the desired precision or the number's nature.
tips: Understand the recursive definition of continued fractions.
The Euclidean algorithm forms the basis for computing finite continued fractions.
Convergents provide the best rational approximations for a given denominator size.
Periodic continued fractions correspond to quadratic irrationals.
Use arbitrary-precision arithmetic for high accuracy with irrational numbers.
pitfalls: Floating-point precision issues can severely limit accuracy for irrational numbers.
Misinterpreting convergents as exact values instead of approximations.
Incorrectly handling negative numbers or zero in the expansion process.
Forgetting that rational numbers always have finite continued fractions.
Complexity can grow significantly for very precise approximations of irrationals.
invariants: The current integer term `a_i` is `floor(x_i)`, where `x_i` is the current real value being expanded.
The next real value `x_{i+1}` is `1 / (x_i - a_i)` if `x_i - a_i` is non-zero.
The sequence of generated integer terms `[a_0, a_1, ..., a_k]` correctly represents the continued fraction up to the current step.
For rational numbers, the fractional part `x_i - a_i` will eventually become zero, terminating the expansion.

title: Continued fractions
when_to_use: Use when seeking the best rational approximations for real numbers or when analyzing properties of numbers related to their fractional parts. It is also applicable for solving certain linear Diophantine equations.
description: Continued fractions represent a real number as a sequence of integers, providing a series of increasingly accurate rational approximations called convergents. The process is analogous to the Euclidean algorithm, typically having logarithmic complexity relative to the magnitude of the number's denominator.
tips: Understand the connection between continued fractions and the Euclidean algorithm for rational numbers.
Convergents provide the 'best' rational approximations for a given denominator size.
Quadratic irrationals have eventually periodic continued fraction expansions.
The sequence of convergents alternates between being less than and greater than the actual value.
pitfalls: Numerical precision issues can arise when computing continued fractions for floating-point numbers.
Misinterpreting the 'best approximation' property; it's specific to denominator size.
For irrational numbers, the expansion is infinite, requiring a stopping condition.
Incorrectly assuming periodicity for all irrational numbers, only quadratic irrationals exhibit this.
invariants: The current remainder in the iterative process is always positive and less than 1.
Each successive convergent p_k/q_k has a denominator q_k that is strictly increasing.
The sequence of partial quotients (a_i) consists of non-negative integers (except possibly a_0).
The absolute error of approximation |x - p_k/q_k| decreases monotonically with increasing k.

title: Continued fractions
when_to_use: When representing rational or irrational numbers as a sequence of integers, especially for finding best rational approximations or analyzing number properties.
description: Continued fractions represent a real number as a sum of an integer and the reciprocal of another number, recursively. This provides a unique representation and efficient approximations for both rational (finite, O(log N)) and irrational (infinite) numbers.
tips: Understand the recursive definition of the fraction terms.
Relate the process for rational numbers to the Euclidean algorithm.
Convergents provide increasingly accurate rational approximations.
Periodic continued fractions correspond to quadratic irrationals.
Can be used to solve linear Diophantine equations.

Forgetting the distinction between finite (rational) and infinite (irrational) forms.
Numerical precision issues when working with floating-point numbers.
Complexity can increase for very large numerators/denominators.

The sequence of convergents alternates between being less than and greater than the original number.
The denominators q_k of the convergents are strictly increasing.

title: Continued fractions
when_to_use: When you need to represent real numbers as a sequence of integers, find optimal rational approximations, or solve certain number theoretic problems involving linear equations or quadratic irrationals.

tips: Understand the recursive definition and how to compute convergents.
The Euclidean algorithm is the basis for finite continued fractions.
Matrix multiplication can compute convergents efficiently for many terms.
Recognize periodic patterns for quadratic irrationals.
Use properties of convergents for error bounds.

Incorrectly handling the base case or termination condition.

Misinterpreting the 'best' approximation property.
Overlooking the connection to Pell's equation or other number theory problems.

title: Binary Exponentiation by Factoring
when_to_use: Use when computing modular exponentiation $ax^y \pmod{2^d}$ where the modulus is a power of two ($2^d$ with $d \geq 3$) and the base $x$ is odd. It leverages the specific structure of the multiplicative group modulo $2^d$ for efficient calculation.
description: This algorithm computes $ax^y \pmod{2^d}$ for odd $x$ by transforming the problem using a 'logarithm' $L(x)$ and a fixed base $b \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. It achieves $O(d)$ complexity for the exponentiation and logarithm parts, plus a single multiplication by $y$.
tips: Handle $x \equiv 3 \pmod 4$ by transforming $x \mapsto -x$ and $a \mapsto (-1)^y a$.
The modulus $2^d$ must have $d \geq 3$ for the group structure to apply.
The base $x$ must be odd for the multiplicative group properties to hold.
Precompute or efficiently calculate $L(x)$ and $b^{yL(x)}$ components.
Leverage bitwise operations for modulo $2^d$ arithmetic for efficiency.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling the $x \equiv 3 \pmod 4$ case or its sign implications.
Applying the method to even bases $x$, which breaks the underlying group structure.
Using for moduli that are not powers of two, or for $d < 3$.
Off-by-one errors in $d$ or $L(x)$ calculations, especially with modulo $2^{d-2}$ vs $2^d$.
Forgetting to include the initial $a$ factor in the final result.
invariants: The intermediate result is always congruent to the original expression modulo $2^d$.
The base used for exponentiation is always odd and congruent to $1 \pmod 4$ (after potential transformation).
The exponent $yL(x)$ is correctly computed modulo the appropriate power of two for the inner exponentiation.
The final result is always in the range $[0, 2^d - 1]$.

title: Binary Exponentiation by Factoring
when_to_use: Use when performing modular exponentiation for an odd base modulo a power of two, especially when dealing with specific logarithmic-like functions or inverses in this modulus.
description: This technique computes modular exponentiation for an odd base modulo a power of two by factoring the exponent or its inverse into a product of terms like (2^n+1). It leverages bit manipulation to efficiently find this factorization, allowing for O(d) or O(d log d) complexity where d is the exponent of the modulus.
tips: Precompute $4L(2^n+1)$ values for efficiency.
Leverage bitwise operations for multiplying by $2^n+1$ (e.g., x = x + (x << n)).
The method is particularly effective for moduli that are powers of two.
Consider the relationship $4L(x) = -4L(x^{-1})$ to simplify calculations.
The iterative approach of multiplying by $(2^n+1)$ to zero bits is key.
pitfalls: This method is specific to odd bases and moduli of the form $2^d$; it's not a general modular exponentiation.
Careful handling of negative results from $4L(x^{-1})$ when working with modular arithmetic.
Incorrectly applying bit manipulation for $x(2^n+1)$ can lead to errors if $x$ is not odd or $n$-th bit logic is flawed.
The $x \equiv 1 \pmod 4$ condition for $L(\cdot)$ might be a constraint for specific applications.

other: modulus is a power of two

invariants: During the iterative process to find $x^{-1} \pmod{2^d}$, the current value of $x$ is always odd.
After processing bit $n$, the $n$-th bit of the current $x$ (for $x^{-1}$ calculation) is zero, and lower bits are unchanged.
The accumulated $4L$ sum correctly tracks the 'logarithmic' transformation applied to $x$ to reach $1 \pmod{2^d}$.

title: Binary Exponentiation by Factoring
when_to_use: Use when computing modular exponentiation for specific bases like 2^n+1 or when the exponent can be efficiently decomposed using bitwise operations to optimize calculations.
description: This technique optimizes modular exponentiation by leveraging properties of specific bases and bitwise decomposition of the exponent, often achieving logarithmic time complexity.
tips:
Leverage the specific modular identity (2^a+1)^(2^b) 
 1 mod 2^(a+b).
Decompose the exponent or related value using bitwise operations.
Apply repeated squaring for efficient modular power calculation.
Carefully manage intermediate results to prevent overflow in modular arithmetic.
Recognize patterns in bases like 2^n+1 for specialized optimizations.
pitfalls: Misapplying the specific modular identity or its conditions.
Errors in bitwise decomposition or mapping bits to factors.
Failing to apply modulo operation at each multiplication step, causing overflow.
Incorrectly interpreting the L(x) function's role in the exponent.
Overlooking edge cases for small exponents or specific base values.
invariants: The accumulated result is the product of (2^n+1) terms for all n corresponding to the bits already processed from the original 4L(x).
The remaining current_4Lx value accurately represents the sum of 4L(2^n+1) terms not yet incorporated into the product.

The modular identity (2^a+1)^(2^b) 
 1 mod 2^(a+b) is maintained for relevant intermediate calculations.

title: Binary Exponentiation by Factoring
when_to_use: When computing a number raised to a very large power modulo another number efficiently. It is also applicable when specific properties of the base or modulus allow for specialized bitwise optimizations.
description: Binary exponentiation (also known as exponentiation by squaring) computes a^b mod m by leveraging the binary representation of the exponent b. This method significantly reduces the number of multiplications from O(b) to O(log b), achieving O(log b) time complexity.
tips: Use long long for intermediate products to prevent overflow before taking modulo.
Apply the modulo operation after each multiplication to keep numbers small.
Handle the base case where the exponent is 0, returning 1.
Consider iterative implementation to avoid recursion depth limits for very large exponents.
For moduli that are powers of two, bitwise AND operations can replace modulo for efficiency.
Euler's totient theorem can reduce the exponent if gcd(base, modulus) = 1.
pitfalls: Integer overflow if intermediate products are not reduced modulo m frequently enough.
Incorrectly handling the exponent 0 or negative exponents (if applicable).
Performance degradation if modulo operations are expensive or not optimized.
Forgetting to handle the case where the base itself is 0 modulo m.
Misapplying specific optimizations (e.g., bitwise) to general moduli where they are not valid.
invariants: (original_base^original_exponent) % m == (current_base^current_exponent * accumulated_result) % m
current_exponent is always non-negative and strictly decreases towards zero.
current_base is always original_base raised to some power of two.
accumulated_result stores the product of original_base raised to powers corresponding to processed set bits of original_exponent.

title: Binary Exponentiation by Factoring
when_to_use: Used to solve discrete logarithm problems of the form g^x = y mod m, specifically when the modulus m is a power of 2. It iteratively determines the bits of the exponent x.
description: It solves the discrete logarithm problem g^x = y mod m by iteratively determining the bits of x, specifically optimized for moduli that are powers of 2. The approach involves modular exponentiation and inverse operations, typically resulting in a complexity of O(log^2 m) or O(log m) depending on modular inverse efficiency.
tips: Carefully handle modular arithmetic with powers of 2, as properties differ from prime moduli.
The order of g modulo 2^d is crucial for determining the number of bits to recover.
Start by finding the least significant bit of x.
Use modular exponentiation efficiently for repeated squaring.
Precompute modular inverses if g is constant and its inverse is frequently needed.
pitfalls: Incorrectly calculating modular inverses for non-coprime numbers with 2^d.
Off-by-one errors when determining the number of squaring steps.
Assuming standard discrete logarithm properties apply directly to power-of-2 moduli.
Forgetting that g must be odd for g^-x0 to exist modulo 2^d.
Handling the base case or initial bit determination incorrectly.

other: base g must be odd for modular inverse to exist

invariants: After k steps, the k least significant bits of x are correctly identified.
The current effective target y' is congruent to g^(2^k * x_remaining) modulo 2^d.
The modulus 2^d remains constant and is always a power of 2.
The base g is always odd, ensuring its modular inverse exists.

title: Minimum stack / Minimum queue
when_to_use: When you need to perform standard stack operations (push, pop) and also query the minimum element currently in the stack, all in constant time.
description: Augments a standard stack by storing each element along with the minimum value encountered up to that point in the stack. This allows O(1) retrieval of the current minimum, while push and pop operations also remain O(1).
tips: Store elements as pairs: (actual_value, minimum_at_this_level).
When pushing, the new minimum_at_this_level is min(new_value, stack.top().second) if stack is not empty, else new_value.
The overall minimum of the stack is always stack.top().second.
This concept can be extended to implement a minimum queue using two such minimum stacks.
pitfalls: Forgetting to handle the empty stack case when calculating the minimum for a new element.
Incorrectly assuming the minimum can be tracked with a single auxiliary variable without considering pops.
Overcomplicating the logic for updating the minimum during push/pop operations.
Not considering potential integer overflow/underflow if values are extremely large or small.
invariants: The top element's second component (minimum_so_far) always represents the minimum value currently in the entire stack.
For any element (value, min_so_far) in the stack, min_so_far is the minimum of 'value' and all elements below it.
All push, pop, and get-minimum operations complete in O(1) time complexity.

title: Minimum stack / Minimum queue
signals: elements added to back, removed from front
when_to_use: Use this technique when you need to perform standard queue or stack operations while also querying the minimum (or maximum) element in O(1) time. It is ideal for problems involving sliding windows or dynamic collections where min/max queries are frequent.
description: This technique augments a queue or stack with an auxiliary data structure, typically a deque, to maintain elements in monotonic order. This allows for O(1) retrieval of the minimum (or maximum) element, with standard operations achieving amortized O(1) time complexity.
tips: Utilize a deque for the auxiliary structure to efficiently add and remove from both ends.
For a minimum queue, maintain the auxiliary deque in non-decreasing order from front to back.
When adding a new element, pop elements from the back of the auxiliary deque that are greater than the new element.
When removing an element from the main structure, check if it matches the front of the auxiliary deque and remove it if so.
Consider storing (value, index) pairs in the auxiliary deque for more robust removal logic, especially for method 2.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling removals when the element to be removed from the main structure is not the current minimum.
Forgetting to remove elements from the auxiliary deque when they are no longer relevant (e.g., out of window bounds).
Failing to maintain the monotonic property correctly during element insertions into the auxiliary deque.
Overcomplicating the removal logic for method 1, which requires knowing the value of the element being removed.
Off-by-one errors or incorrect index management if using method 2 to track elements.
invariants: The auxiliary deque stores elements in non-decreasing order from front to back.
The element at the front of the auxiliary deque is the minimum of all elements currently in the main queue/stack.
All elements present in the auxiliary deque are also currently present in the main queue/stack.

title: Minimum stack / Minimum queue
when_to_use: Use when needing to efficiently query the minimum element of a stack or queue in O(1) average time. Also applicable for finding minimums in all fixed-length subarrays using a sliding window.
description: This technique augments a stack or queue to support O(1) average time retrieval of its minimum element, typically by storing minimums alongside data or using auxiliary structures. It achieves amortized constant time for all operations.
tips: For a minimum stack, use an auxiliary stack to store minimums encountered so far.
Only push to the auxiliary minimum stack if the new element is less than or equal to its current top.
For a minimum queue, simulate with two minimum stacks (one for input, one for output).
When the output stack is empty, transfer all elements from the input stack to the output stack.
pitfalls: Forgetting to pop from the auxiliary minimum stack when the main stack's top is removed.
Incorrectly handling the transfer logic between the two stacks for a minimum queue.
Not accounting for empty stack/queue cases when querying the minimum.
Confusing amortized O(1) with strict worst-case O(1) for queue operations.
invariants: For a minimum stack, the auxiliary minimum stack's top element is always the minimum of all elements currently in the main stack.
For a minimum queue implemented with two stacks, the output stack is only refilled from the input stack when the output stack becomes empty.
The minimum of the queue is the minimum of the current minimums of its two internal stacks.

title: Sparse Table
when_to_use: Use when you need to answer range queries, especially Range Minimum/Maximum Queries, on an immutable array very quickly. It is ideal for scenarios where the data does not change and query performance is critical.
description: Sparse Table is a data structure that precomputes answers for all range queries of power-of-two lengths. It allows O(1) query time for idempotent operations like Range Minimum/Maximum Queries on a static array, with O(N log N) precomputation.
tips: Precompute log2 values to find the largest power of two in O(1).
Best suited for idempotent operations like minimum, maximum, or GCD.
Understand st[i][j] represents the range starting at j with length 2^i.
Leverage the overlapping nature of subproblems for efficient query combination.
Space complexity is O(N log N), which can be significant for large N.
pitfalls: Does not support array updates; any modification invalidates the structure.
Inefficient for non-idempotent operations like range sum or XOR in O(1).
High memory consumption (O(N log N)) can be a limiting factor.
Carefully handle log2 calculations and array indexing for ranges.
Ensure correct range boundaries and lengths during precomputation and query.
invariants: st[k][i] holds the result for the segment starting at index i with length 2^k.
For any k > 0, st[k][i] combines the results of two adjacent segments of length 2^(k-1).
All entries st[0][i] store the value of the single element at index i.
The table st is fully populated for all valid k and i up to log N and N - 2^k + 1 respectively.

title: Sparse Table
when_to_use: Use when you need to perform many range queries on a static array, especially when the query operation is associative and idempotent, allowing for O(1) query time after O(N log N) precomputation.
description: A Sparse Table is a data structure that precomputes answers for all possible range queries of power-of-two lengths. It allows O(1) query time for idempotent operations like min/max/gcd and O(log N) for non-idempotent associative operations, after an O(N log N) build time.
tips: Precompute log2 values for faster range length calculations.
Store dp[k][i] as the result for range [i, i + 2^k - 1].
For idempotent operations, combine two overlapping segments to cover the query range.
Order dimensions for cache efficiency, e.g., dp[k][N].
pitfalls: Does not support updates to array elements efficiently.
Requires O(N log N) space, which can be significant for large N.
O(1) query is only for idempotent operations; others may be O(log N) or require different structures.
Off-by-one errors in range and power-of-two calculations.
invariants: After precomputation, dp[k][i] stores the result for the range [i, i + 2^k - 1].
For any k > 0, dp[k][i] is the result of combining dp[k-1][i] and dp[k-1][i + 2^(k-1)].
The log_table[len] stores floor(log2(len)) for all relevant lengths.
Any query range [L, R] is covered by two precomputed segments of length 2^k where k = floor(log2(R - L + 1)).

title: Sparse Table
when_to_use: Use when performing many range queries on a static array where the query operation is idempotent (e.g., min, max, GCD) to achieve O(1) query time after O(N log N) precomputation.
description: A data structure that precomputes answers for all possible range queries of power-of-two lengths, allowing O(1) query time for idempotent operations like range minimum/maximum queries. Precomputation takes O(N log N) time and space.
tips: Precompute logarithms for O(1) lookup of `floor(log2(length))` during queries.
Sparse tables are most efficient for idempotent operations (min, max, GCD, OR, AND) due to overlapping segments.
The `st[k][i]` entry typically stores the result for the range `[i, i + 2^k - 1]`.
For non-idempotent operations like sum, queries become O(log N) and require a different strategy.
Ensure correct indexing (0-based or 1-based) when calculating range boundaries and lengths.
pitfalls: Not suitable for dynamic arrays or scenarios requiring updates, as modifications invalidate the precomputed table.
High memory usage: requires O(N log N) space, which can be significant for large N.
Inefficient for non-idempotent operations if O(1) query time is a strict requirement.
Potential for integer overflow if values or sums within ranges are very large.
Off-by-one errors are common when calculating range lengths or segment start/end points.
invariants: The `st[k][i]` entry stores the result of the operation for the range `[i, i + 2^k - 1]`.
All ranges of length `2^k` starting at `i` are correctly computed in `st[k][i]` during precomputation.
For any query `[L, R]`, the chosen `k = floor(log2(R - L + 1))` ensures `2^k <= (R - L + 1)`.
The query `op(st[k][L], st[k][R - 2^k + 1])` covers the entire range `[L, R]` using two overlapping segments.

title: Disjoint Set Union
when_to_use: Use when you need to maintain a collection of disjoint sets and efficiently perform union operations to merge sets or find the representative of an element's set.
description: Disjoint Set Union (DSU) is a data structure that stores a collection of disjoint sets and supports two primary operations: finding the representative of an element's set and merging two sets. With path compression and union by rank/size, these operations achieve nearly constant amortized time complexity, O(alpha(N)).
tips: Implement path compression in `find_set` for optimal performance.
Use union by rank or union by size to keep trees flat and balanced.
Initialize each element as its own representative in `make_set`.
Store parent pointers in an array for efficient representation.
Consider using a map for non-integer or sparse element IDs.
pitfalls: Forgetting path compression or union by rank/size leads to O(N) worst-case operations.
Incorrectly merging sets by only updating one root without considering the other's rank/size.
Not handling edge cases where elements are already in the same set during union.
Modifying the parent array directly without using `find_set` can break the structure.
Recursive `find_set` without path compression can lead to stack overflow for deep trees.
invariants: Each element `v` is either a root (parent[v] == v) or has a parent pointer to another element in its set.
The `find_set` operation, after path compression, ensures that all nodes on the path from `v` to its root directly point to the root.
The `union_sets` operation always merges two distinct sets by making the root of one set point to the root of the other, maintaining the tree structure.
The `rank` (or `size`) of a set's root accurately reflects the height (or number of elements) of its tree.

title: Disjoint Set Union
when_to_use: Use when you need to efficiently manage a collection of disjoint sets, supporting operations to merge sets and determine if two elements belong to the same set.
description: Disjoint Set Union (DSU) is a data structure that maintains a collection of disjoint sets and supports two primary operations: union (merging two sets) and find (determining the representative of an element's set). With path compression and union by rank/size, operations achieve nearly O(1) amortized time complexity.
tips: Apply path compression during `find_set` to optimize future queries.
Utilize union by rank or size to maintain balanced trees.
Initialize each element as a singleton set, pointing to itself.
The root of a set's tree is its own parent.
pitfalls: Omitting path compression or union by rank/size results in poor performance.
Failing to correctly identify the root of a set in `find_set`.
Incorrectly merging sets can lead to deep trees and slow operations.
Modifying parent pointers without proper rank/size updates.
invariants: For any element `v`, `parent[v]` is either `v` itself (if `v` is a root) or an ancestor of `v`.
All elements belonging to the same set share a common root representative.
The `parent` array always stores valid indices within the element range.
The number of disjoint sets decreases by at most one after a successful `union_sets` operation.

title: Disjoint Set Union
when_to_use: Use when you need to maintain a collection of disjoint sets and perform union operations to merge sets or find the representative of an element's set efficiently. It's suitable for problems involving dynamic connectivity or grouping elements.
description: Disjoint Set Union (DSU) is a data structure that stores a collection of disjoint sets and supports two primary operations: finding the representative of an element's set and merging two sets. It achieves nearly constant time complexity for these operations with path compression and union by rank/size optimizations.
tips: Implement path compression in `find` for optimal performance.
Use union by size or rank to maintain balanced trees.
Store aggregate information (e.g., size, min, max) at the set's representative.
For certain problems, process queries in reverse order with DSU to simplify logic.
Initialize each element as its own parent, forming singleton sets.
pitfalls: Omitting path compression or union by rank/size can degrade performance to O(N).
Failing to correctly update auxiliary data stored at representatives during a union operation.
Incorrectly initializing parent pointers or set sizes.
Modifying parent pointers without using the `find` operation, which bypasses path compression.
Not resetting the DSU structure between multiple test cases.
invariants: Every element `i` belongs to exactly one set, represented by `find(i)`.
If `parent[i] == i`, then `i` is the representative (root) of its set.
After path compression, `parent[i]` points directly to the set representative for all visited `i`.

title: Fenwick Tree
when_to_use: When you need to efficiently calculate prefix sums or range sums on an array that undergoes frequent point updates. It is ideal for scenarios requiring both fast query and update operations on an array.
description: A Fenwick tree, or Binary Indexed Tree (BIT), is an array-based data structure that efficiently calculates prefix sums and updates individual elements in an array. It supports both point updates and prefix sum queries in O(log N) time complexity.
tips: Understand the bit manipulation (`i & -i`) for navigating parent/child relationships.
Consistently choose and apply either 0-based or 1-based indexing throughout the implementation.
Initialize the Fenwick tree by performing point updates for each initial array element.
Consider extending to 2D or higher dimensions for matrix-based range queries and point updates.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling 0-based versus 1-based indexing can lead to off-by-one errors.
Forgetting to initialize the tree or performing updates incorrectly will yield wrong results.
Applying it to non-associative operations or operations without an inverse is not suitable.
Modifying the original conceptual array directly instead of using the tree's update function.
invariants: Each `T[i]` stores the sum of a specific range `[g(i), i]` from the original array.
The `sum(idx)` function correctly aggregates values from `A[0]` to `A[idx]`.
The `update(idx, delta)` function propagates `delta` to all `T[j]` where `A[idx]` contributes to `T[j]`.
The Fenwick tree `T` always reflects the current state of the underlying conceptual array `A`.

title: Fenwick Tree
when_to_use: Use when frequent point updates and range sum queries are needed on an array. It offers logarithmic time complexity for both operations, making it more efficient than a simple prefix sum array for dynamic data.
description: A Fenwick Tree, or Binary Indexed Tree (BIT), is a data structure that efficiently calculates prefix sums and updates individual elements in an array. Both operations typically run in O(log N) time.
tips: Understand the lowbit operation and its role in navigating the tree structure.
Use 1-based indexing for simpler implementation of lowbit calculations.
Initialize the tree with zeros or build it from an existing array in O(N log N) time.
Can be extended for 2D arrays or other operations like range updates with point queries.
pitfalls: Forgetting 1-based indexing can lead to off-by-one errors and incorrect results.
Incorrectly implementing the lowbit calculation or update/query logic.
Not suitable for range updates and range queries simultaneously without significant modifications.
Overlapping with segment tree use cases without considering specific performance advantages.
invariants: Each node `tree[i]` stores the sum of a specific range of elements ending at index `i`.
The length of the range summed by `tree[i]` is determined by `i & (-i)` (lowbit operation).
An `update(idx, val)` operation correctly propagates `val` to all relevant parent nodes in O(log N).
A `query(idx)` operation correctly sums up ranges from `idx` down to 1 in O(log N).

title: Fenwick Tree
when_to_use: Use when you need to efficiently calculate prefix sums or range sums on an array that undergoes frequent point updates. It provides logarithmic time complexity for both operations.
description: A Fenwick Tree, also known as a Binary Indexed Tree (BIT), is a data structure that can efficiently update individual elements and calculate prefix sums in an array. Both operations are performed in O(log N) time.
tips: Use 1-based indexing for array elements to simplify bitwise logic
The `idx & -idx` operation is key to navigating the implicit tree structure
Can be extended for range updates and point queries using a difference array
Understand how each node stores the sum of a specific range
Pre-calculate initial sums for faster construction if all elements are known
pitfalls: Confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing can lead to incorrect results
Off-by-one errors when calculating range sums (e.g., `query(R) - query(L-1)`)
Incorrectly calculating `idx & -idx` or using it in the wrong context
Not accounting for the maximum array size when initializing the BIT
Forgetting that it only supports commutative operations like sum or XOR
invariants: Each `tree[i]` element stores the sum of a contiguous range of elements from the original array, specifically from `i - (i & -i) + 1` to `i` (inclusive, 1-based).
After any `update(idx, val)` operation, `query(idx)` will reflect the new sum up to `idx`.
The `tree` array's size is always `N+1` (for 1-based indexing) to accommodate all `N` elements.

title: Fenwick Tree
when_to_use: Use when an array requires frequent point updates and efficient retrieval of prefix sums or range sums. It offers logarithmic time complexity for both operations.
description: A Fenwick Tree, or Binary Indexed Tree (BIT), is a data structure that efficiently supports point updates and prefix sum queries on an array in O(log N) time.
tips: Use 1-based indexing for simpler bitwise operations (`i & -i`).
Initialize with an existing array in O(N) time using a linear construction method.
Can be extended to 2D arrays for matrix operations.
Adapt for other associative operations like XOR sums, not just addition.
Combine with difference arrays for range updates and point queries.
pitfalls: Directly finding range minimum/maximum is not efficient or generally supported.
Operations must be associative and preferably invertible for general range queries.
Carefully manage 0-based vs. 1-based indexing to avoid off-by-one errors.
Ensure update values are handled correctly, especially for non-sum operations.
invariants: Each `bit[i]` stores the sum of a contiguous range of elements from the original array.
The range length represented by `bit[i]` is determined by `i & -i` (the least significant bit).
An update at index `i` affects `bit[j]` where `j` is an ancestor of `i` in the implicit tree.
A prefix sum query up to index `i` aggregates values from `bit[j]` where `j` is an ancestor of `i` in the implicit tree.

title: Sqrt Decomposition
when_to_use: Use when frequent range queries and point/range updates are needed on an array, offering a balanced O(sqrt(N)) time complexity for both operations. It's a good alternative when a segment tree is too complex or overkill.
description: Sqrt decomposition divides an array into blocks of size approximately sqrt(N) to optimize range queries and updates. It achieves an O(sqrt(N)) time complexity for both query and update operations.
tips: Choose block size carefully, typically sqrt(N) for optimal balance.
Precompute block aggregates (sum, min, max) for efficient queries.
Handle partial blocks at the start and end of a query range separately.
Consider lazy propagation for range updates to blocks.
Ensure all block aggregates are updated after any element modification.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling partial blocks or boundary conditions.
Off-by-one errors in block indices or element indices within blocks.
Forgetting to update block aggregates after point updates.
Overcomplicating lazy propagation for simple range updates.
Performance can be worse than O(N) for very few queries or updates.
invariants: Each block maintains an up-to-date aggregate of its elements.
For any query, fully contained blocks are processed using their precomputed aggregates.
Elements in partial blocks at the query range boundaries are processed individually.
The array is logically divided into blocks of roughly equal size.

title: Sqrt Decomposition
when_to_use: Efficiently calculate sums or properties over various ranges in a static array. Useful for many range queries or when combining range updates with point queries.
description: Prefix sums allow O(1) range sum queries after O(N) preprocessing. Difference arrays enable O(1) range updates and O(N) point queries after O(N) reconstruction.
tips: Precompute prefix sums to answer range sum queries in constant time.
Use a difference array to apply range updates efficiently.
Remember to reconstruct the original array from the difference array for final values.
Consider 1-based indexing for simpler prefix sum formulas.
pitfalls: Off-by-one errors in range boundaries for prefix sums.
Forgetting to initialize the difference array correctly (e.g., diff[0] = arr[0] for 0-indexed).
Not handling edge cases for range updates (e.g., right + 1 boundary).
Confusing prefix sum and difference array applications.
invariants: The prefix sum array P[i] stores the sum of elements from A[0] to A[i-1].
The difference array D[i] stores A[i] - A[i-1] (for i > 0).
Applying a range update [L, R] to D affects only D[L] and D[R+1].
The original array A[i] can be reconstructed by A[i] = D[0] + D[1] + ... + D[i].

title: Sqrt Decomposition
when_to_use: Use when performing frequent range queries and point updates on an array, especially when a segment tree is too complex or memory-intensive, or when query types are diverse. It offers a balanced time complexity for both operations.
description: Sqrt decomposition divides an array into blocks of size approximately sqrt(N) to optimize range queries and point updates. It achieves O(sqrt(N)) time complexity for both operations.
tips: Choose block size carefully, typically sqrt(N) for balanced performance.
Precompute block summaries (e.g., sum, min, max) for efficient queries.
Handle partial blocks at the query range ends by iterating elements individually.
Ensure point updates correctly modify both the element and its block's summary.
Consider for problems where query types are varied and not easily handled by specialized structures.
pitfalls: Off-by-one errors when calculating block indices or boundaries.
Forgetting to update the block's summary after a point update.
Incorrectly handling edge cases for very small arrays or query ranges.
Higher constant factors compared to segment trees for some specific problems.
Not suitable for extremely large N where O(sqrt(N)) is still too slow.
invariants: Each element `arr[i]` belongs to exactly one block `block[i / block_size]`.
The summary for each block `block_summary[j]` accurately reflects the state of elements within block `j`.
For any query range `[L, R]`, elements outside full blocks are processed individually.
The total number of blocks is approximately `N / sqrt(N)`.

title: Segment Tree
when_to_use: Use when you need to perform range queries (like sum, min, max) and point or range updates on an array efficiently, typically in logarithmic time.
description: A Segment Tree is a tree-based data structure that stores information about array intervals, enabling efficient O(log n) range queries and point/range updates.
tips: Build the tree recursively, dividing the array into halves.
Use lazy propagation for efficient range updates to avoid O(N) updates.
Ensure correct merging logic for parent nodes based on the query type (e.g., sum, min, max).
Consider 1-based indexing for easier parent/child calculations in the tree array.
pitfalls: Off-by-one errors when defining segment boundaries or array indices.
Forgetting to push down lazy tags before querying or updating children nodes.
Incorrectly handling partial or full overlaps during range queries/updates.
High memory consumption (typically 4N) for large arrays.
invariants: Each node in the segment tree represents a contiguous subsegment of the original array.
The value stored in a non-leaf node is an aggregation of the values from its direct children.
Leaf nodes correspond to individual elements of the original array.
If lazy propagation is used, a node's lazy tag is always applied to its children before their values are used or updated.

title: Segment Tree
when_to_use: Use for efficient range queries and updates on an array. It handles aggregated information over dynamic intervals.
description: A segment tree is a tree data structure used for storing information about intervals or segments. It allows answering range queries and range updates in logarithmic time, making it efficient for dynamic array problems.
tips: Build the tree recursively, dividing the array into halves.
Each node stores an aggregate value for its corresponding segment.
Lazy propagation optimizes range updates by deferring updates to children.
Ensure base cases for leaf nodes are handled correctly.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling array indexing (0-based vs 1-based).
Off-by-one errors in segment boundaries during queries/updates.
Forgetting to push down lazy updates before querying or updating children.
Memory overhead can be significant for large arrays (4N nodes).
invariants: Each node represents a contiguous segment of the original array.
The value stored in a parent node is an aggregation of its children's values.
For any node, its segment [L, R] is correctly split into [L, M] and [M+1, R] for children.

title: Segment Tree
when_to_use: When you need to perform range queries (like sum, min, max) and point or range updates on an array efficiently, typically in O(log N) time.
description: A tree-based data structure that stores information about array segments, allowing for efficient O(log N) range queries and point/range updates. It recursively divides the array into halves, with each node representing an interval.
tips: Use 1-based indexing for array and tree nodes to simplify parent/child calculations.
Implement lazy propagation for efficient range updates to avoid O(N) updates.
Ensure base cases for recursion are handled correctly, especially for leaf nodes and fully contained query ranges.
The tree typically requires 4N space for an array of size N.
pitfalls: Off-by-one errors in segment boundaries or query ranges are common.
Forgetting to push down lazy updates before accessing children or returning a node's value.
Incorrect merge logic for different types of queries (e.g., sum vs. min/max).
Stack overflow for very large N if recursion depth is not managed or language limits are hit.
invariants: Each node in the segment tree corresponds to a contiguous subsegment of the original array.
The value stored in a node is the aggregate result (e.g., sum, min) of its corresponding array subsegment.
For any non-leaf node, its segment is the union of its left and right children's segments.
During a query, if a node's segment is fully contained within the query range, its precomputed value is used directly.

title: Segment Tree
when_to_use: Use when processing range queries or updates on an array, or when efficient searching and sorting of data are required. It's also applicable for problems that can be broken down into smaller, independent subproblems.
description: This card describes techniques for efficiently handling range queries and updates on arrays, as well as searching and sorting elements. These methods typically achieve logarithmic time complexity for operations.
tips: Segment trees are powerful for static arrays with dynamic queries.
Merge sort's divide-and-conquer approach can be adapted for counting inversions.
Binary search requires a monotonic property in the search space.
Precompute segment tree nodes for faster queries.
Consider lazy propagation for range updates in segment trees.
pitfalls: Off-by-one errors in binary search bounds.
Incorrect segment tree node merging logic.
Forgetting to handle base cases in recursive solutions.
Integer overflow with large sums in segment trees.
Not updating parent nodes correctly after a segment tree update.
invariants: Each node in the segment tree represents a specific range of the original array.
The target element, if present, is always within the current binary search interval [low, high].
Subarrays are sorted before being merged in merge sort.
The segment tree root covers the entire input array.

title: Treap (Cartesian tree)
when_to_use: Use when average-case performance is critical and worst-case scenarios are rare or can be mitigated by randomness. It's suitable for problems where deterministic algorithms might suffer from specific input patterns.
description: Randomized algorithms incorporate random choices to achieve efficiency or correctness, often providing probabilistic guarantees on performance. They typically have an expected time complexity that is better than their worst-case deterministic counterparts.
tips: Use a good random number generator and seed it properly.
Analyze expected time complexity using probability theory (e.g., linearity of expectation).
Consider using randomized versions of standard algorithms like QuickSort or QuickSelect.
Randomized data structures like Treaps can offer strong average-case performance.
Ensure randomness is truly independent for each decision.
pitfalls: Poor random number generation can lead to predictable or biased behavior.
Worst-case scenarios, though rare, can still occur and might need handling.
Over-reliance on randomness without proper analysis can lead to incorrectness or inefficiency.
Debugging randomized algorithms can be challenging due to non-deterministic behavior.
The constant factors in expected time complexity might be higher than deterministic counterparts.
invariants: The data structure maintains its core structural properties (e.g., BST order, heap order) based on keys/priorities.
Each random decision is made independently, ensuring unbiased choices.
The expected time complexity for operations remains within a defined bound.
The probability of encountering a worst-case scenario is acceptably low.

title: Treap (Cartesian tree)
when_to_use: Use when a self-balancing binary search tree is needed, especially when randomization is acceptable for balancing. It offers expected logarithmic time complexity for dynamic sets requiring efficient insertions, deletions, and queries.
description: A Treap is a randomized binary search tree that maintains both binary search tree properties on keys and heap properties on randomly assigned priorities. It provides expected O(log N) time complexity for most operations like insertion, deletion, and search.
tips: Assign random priorities to nodes to ensure probabilistic balance.
Implement core operations like insert and erase using split and merge primitives.
Consider implicit treaps for array-like operations on dynamic sequences.
Use a robust random number generator for priorities to avoid degenerate cases.
Ensure unique priorities for simpler heap property maintenance.
pitfalls: Degenerate cases can occur with poor random number generation, leading to O(N) worst-case performance.
Higher constant factors compared to deterministic balanced trees due to randomization overhead.
Complexity of split/merge operations can be tricky to implement correctly.
Not truly worst-case O(log N), only expected time complexity.
Memory overhead for storing priorities in addition to keys.
invariants: For any node, its key is greater than all keys in its left subtree and less than all keys in its right subtree.
For any node, its priority is greater than or equal to the priorities of its children.
The set of keys in the treap always matches the set of keys inserted and not deleted.

title: Treap (Cartesian tree)
when_to_use: When you need a self-balancing binary search tree that supports efficient split and merge operations, often used for dynamic set management or range queries on mutable data.
description: A Treap is a randomized binary search tree that maintains both BST properties on keys and heap properties on priorities (randomly assigned). It supports efficient operations like split and merge, typically with an average time complexity of O(log N).
tips: Understand the recursive nature of Split and Merge operations.
Randomly assign priorities (Y values) to ensure probabilistic balancing.
Base cases for recursion (empty tree) are crucial for correctness.
Split operation separates elements based on a key into two subtrees.
Merge operation combines two treaps into one, respecting heap property.
pitfalls: Incorrect handling of null pointers or empty subtrees in recursion.
Forgetting to update parent pointers or root pointers after operations.
Non-random priority assignment can lead to degenerate trees.
Off-by-one errors in key comparisons during split/merge.
Memory leaks if nodes are not properly deallocated after deletion/split.
invariants: For any node, its key is greater than or equal to all keys in its left subtree.
For any node, its key is less than all keys in its right subtree.
For any node, its priority is greater than or equal to the priorities of its children.
After a Split(T, X) operation, the resulting left tree L contains all elements with key <= X, and the right tree R contains all elements with key > X.

title: Treap (Cartesian tree)
when_to_use: Use when dynamic ordered data requires efficient split/merge operations, order statistics, or sequence manipulation. It provides probabilistic performance guarantees for these operations.
description: A Treap is a randomized binary search tree that maintains both BST properties on keys and heap properties on randomly assigned priorities, allowing for efficient dynamic operations like split, merge, and order statistics in O(log N) average time. A Cartesian tree, which a Treap is a type of, can be constructed using a monotonic stack.
tips: Assign random priorities to nodes to ensure probabilistic balance.
Implement `split` and `merge` as fundamental operations for all other modifications.
Maintain subtree sizes (`cnt`) for efficient order statistic queries like K-th element.
Understand the inductive nature of recursive `split` and `merge` operations.
Consider building the underlying Cartesian tree structure using a monotonic stack for static data.
pitfalls: Degenerate performance can occur if priorities are not truly random or unique, leading to unbalanced trees.
Forgetting to update subtree sizes (`cnt`) after insertions, deletions, or merges.
Complexity in correctly handling edge cases for `split` and `merge` operations, especially with null subtrees.
Potential for stack overflow with deep recursion if the tree becomes highly unbalanced (though rare with random priorities).
Memory overhead for storing both key and priority, plus subtree size for each node.
invariants: For any node X, all keys in its left subtree are less than X.key, and all keys in its right subtree are greater than X.key.
For any node X, its priority X.priority is greater than or equal to the priorities of its children.
The `cnt` field of any node X correctly stores the total number of nodes in the subtree rooted at X.
The structure remains a valid Treap after any `split` or `merge` operation.

title: Treap (Cartesian tree)
when_to_use: Use when a dynamic data structure is needed to support efficient array-like operations (insert, delete, range queries/updates) on an ordered sequence, especially with frequent modifications at arbitrary positions.
description: A Treap is a randomized binary search tree that maintains both BST properties on keys and heap properties on randomly assigned priorities. It supports various dynamic array operations and range queries/updates in expected O(log N) time.
tips: Use random priorities to ensure probabilistic balance, avoiding worst-case O(N) operations.
Implement `split` and `merge` as fundamental building blocks for all other operations.
Maintain subtree sizes (`cnt`) for implicit key calculations and array indexing.
Utilize lazy propagation for efficient range updates (add, paint, reverse).
Consider offline construction for O(N) build time if keys are sorted.
pitfalls: Forgetting to push lazy tags before accessing children or performing splits/merges.
Incorrectly updating `cnt` or aggregate fields after tree modifications.
Handling null nodes (empty subtrees) carefully in recursive functions.
Random priority collisions, though rare, can lead to unbalanced trees.
Complexity of debugging recursive `split`/`merge` and lazy propagation interactions.
invariants: For any node, its key is greater than all keys in its left subtree and less than all keys in its right subtree.
For any node, its priority is greater than or equal to the priorities of its children.
The `cnt` field of a node accurately reflects the total number of nodes in its subtree.
Lazy propagation tags are pushed down before any structural modification or query on a child node.

title: Sqrt Tree
signals: segment operations (min, max, sum, gcd, xor)
when_to_use: When performing associative range queries on an array, especially when O(1) query time is critical and O(N log log N) preprocessing is acceptable.
description: The Sqrt Tree is a data structure that answers associative range queries on an array in O(1) time after O(N log log N) preprocessing. It achieves this by recursively applying sqrt decomposition, precomputing block-wise prefix/suffix operations and inter-block results.
tips: Ensure the operation is strictly associative for correctness.
Carefully handle block boundaries and partial blocks during query processing.
The recursive sqrt decomposition is key to achieving the O(log log N) factor.
Precompute prefix and suffix operations within each block for efficient access.
The 'between' array stores results for full blocks, simplifying inter-block queries.
pitfalls: Using non-associative operations will yield incorrect results.
High memory usage for the 'between' array if not optimized or if N is very large.
Off-by-one errors are common when calculating block indices or ranges.
The implementation complexity can be high due to multiple levels of recursion and arrays.
Not suitable for dynamic arrays or updates without a full or partial rebuild.
invariants: The `prefixOp` array for any block `k` stores the associative sum from the block's start to each element within it.
The `suffixOp` array for any block `k` stores the associative sum from each element within it to the block's end.
The `between[i][j]` array stores the associative sum of all elements from the start of block `i` to the end of block `j`.
Each level of the Sqrt Tree decomposition maintains the property that subproblems are of size sqrt(N) of the current level's problem.

title: Sqrt Tree
signals: O(N log log N) build time
when_to_use: Used for answering range queries on a static array by recursively decomposing it into square root sized blocks, achieving O(log log N) query time.
description: A Sqrt Tree is a recursive data structure that decomposes an array into blocks of square root size, building a similar structure on each block. It allows for efficient range queries, typically achieving O(log log N) query time after an O(N log log N) precomputation.
tips: Precompute answers for all possible block combinations within a node.
Handle queries spanning multiple blocks by combining precomputed prefix, suffix, and full block results.
Base cases for recursion are blocks of size 1 or 2, which are handled directly.
The height of the tree is very small, O(log log N), due to sqrt decomposition.
Carefully define the associative operation for combining results.
pitfalls: High constant factors due to complex precomputation per node.
Memory usage can be significant, O(N log log N), for large N.
Implementation complexity is higher than standard segment trees.
Not suitable for dynamic arrays or updates, as rebuilding is expensive.
Choosing the correct block size and recursive structure is crucial.
invariants: Each node in the Sqrt Tree represents a contiguous segment of the original array.
A node representing a segment of length k has sqrt(k) children, each covering a block of sqrt(k) elements.
Leaf nodes represent segments of length 1 or 2, with their query answers computed directly.
For any node, precomputed arrays (prefixOp, suffixOp, between) correctly store results for sub-segments within its represented range.

title: Sqrt Tree
when_to_use: Use for efficient range queries on an array, achieving very low query complexity by leveraging a hierarchical block decomposition and specific structural properties like power-of-two block sizes.
description: A Sqrt Tree is a hierarchical data structure designed for answering range queries on an array, achieving O(log log n) or better query time by recursively decomposing the array into blocks and layers.
tips: Pad the input array with dummy elements to ensure its size is a power of two for simplified block calculations.
Ensure all block sizes on each layer are powers of two to enable efficient binary representation tricks for queries.
Utilize 0-indexation consistently, especially when converting query ranges to binary form for block alignment checks.
Precompute answers for small, base-case segments (e.g., length 1 or 2) to terminate recursion efficiently.
Carefully handle queries that do not perfectly align with block boundaries by processing partial blocks separately.
pitfalls: Incorrectly calculating block boundaries or segment sizes, leading to off-by-one errors or out-of-bounds access.
Overlooking the memory overhead associated with storing precomputed values for all blocks across multiple layers.
Failing to correctly combine results from partial blocks and fully contained blocks during query processing.
Complexity in implementing the binary representation logic for fast query alignment checks.
Deep recursion depth can lead to stack overflow issues for very large inputs if not managed.
invariants: Each layer of the Sqrt Tree consists of segments of equal length.
All blocks within a given layer have an equal, power-of-two size.
The query range [l, r] always remains within the bounds of the original (possibly padded) array.
Base cases for segments of length 1 or 2 are handled directly without further recursion.

title: Sqrt Tree
when_to_use: When you need to perform O(1) range queries and support efficient updates (single element or range) on an array, leveraging a hierarchical decomposition.
description: A Sqrt Tree is a data structure that supports O(1) range queries and efficient updates on an array. It recursively decomposes the array into blocks and layers, using precomputed information and bitwise operations to quickly answer queries and propagate updates.
tips: Precompute highest bit for efficient layer selection.
Decompose queries into intra-block and inter-block segments.
Optimize root node updates by using a nested Sqrt Tree.
Utilize bitwise XOR to identify the correct processing layer.
Define `prefixOp`, `suffixOp`, and `between` arrays for each node.
pitfalls: Naive updates can result in O(N) complexity per update.
High memory consumption due to multiple layers of precomputed arrays.
Debugging recursive structures and block boundaries can be complex.
Incorrectly identifying the appropriate layer for a given query range.
Off-by-one errors in block index or element calculations.
invariants: Each element is part of exactly one block at each layer of the Sqrt Tree.
The highest differing bit between `l` and `r` identifies the specific layer for processing `q(l, r)`.
`prefixOp` and `suffixOp` arrays correctly summarize operations within their respective blocks.
An indexed Sqrt Tree's root node uses a nested Sqrt Tree (`index`) instead of a `between` array.

title: Randomized Heap
when_to_use: Use when needing a heap with efficient merge operations and guaranteed expected logarithmic time for all standard operations, including arbitrary element removal.
description: A randomized heap is a binary tree-based heap that uses randomization to maintain heap properties, ensuring all operations like insertion, deletion, and merging run in expected O(log n) time.
tips: Randomization simplifies implementation compared to other mergeable heaps.
Expected time complexity is generally sufficient for practical purposes.
Can be implemented as a min-heap or max-heap.
Useful when frequent merging of heaps is required.
pitfalls: Worst-case time complexity can be higher, though rare.
Relies on a good random number generator.
Overhead of randomization might be slightly higher than deterministic heaps for simple operations.
Debugging randomized structures can be tricky.
invariants: The root node always contains the minimum (or maximum) element.
Every node's value is less than or equal to (or greater than or equal to) its children's values.
The heap property is maintained after every insertion or deletion operation.
The tree structure remains a binary tree.

title: Randomized Heap
when_to_use: Use when standard heap operations (insert, delete-min, merge) are required with strong average-case performance and probabilistic worst-case guarantees. It is particularly efficient when frequent merging of heaps is a primary operation.
description: A randomized heap is a probabilistic data structure where all core heap operations are reduced to a single merge operation, which uses randomization to maintain balance. It achieves an average-case time complexity of O(log n) for all operations.
tips: Focus on implementing the merge operation correctly, as all other operations are built upon it.
The randomization helps maintain a balanced tree structure probabilistically, leading to good average performance.
The root node always contains the minimum element, making find-min trivial.
Consider using a robust random number generator for optimal performance distribution.
pitfalls: Worst-case performance is not strictly guaranteed, only probabilistically bounded, which might be an issue for extremely strict real-time systems.
Excessive recursion depth could be a concern for extremely large N in languages without tail call optimization, though average depth is logarithmic.
The overhead of randomization might be slightly higher than deterministic heaps in some specific scenarios.
Debugging can be more challenging due to the non-deterministic nature of the tree structure.
invariants: The root node of any (sub)heap always contains the minimum value within that (sub)heap.
The heap property (parent's value <= children's values) is maintained throughout the structure.
Each node's children are themselves valid randomized heaps (or null).
The tree structure remains probabilistically balanced, ensuring an average logarithmic height.

title: Deleting from a data structure in_log_n
when_to_use: Use when processing a sequence of additions, deletions, and queries offline, where elements have specific time intervals of existence. It efficiently handles dynamic data structure operations by leveraging a segment tree over time.
description: This technique uses a segment tree over the timeline of queries to manage elements with varying lifespans. Queries are processed offline via DFS, adding elements upon entering a node and undoing additions upon exiting, achieving O(T(n) log n) deletion complexity.
tips: Ensure the base data structure supports efficient addition and undo operations.
Carefully manage element additions and removals during DFS traversal.
Pre-process all queries and element lifespans to build the segment tree.
Use a stack or similar mechanism to track changes for easy undoing.
pitfalls: Forgetting to undo changes when backtracking from a segment tree node.
Incorrectly mapping element lifespans to segment tree nodes.
High constant factors due to repeated additions/deletions in the base DS.
Not suitable for online queries or when undoing is not feasible.
Complexity of the base data structure's add operation can dominate.
invariants: When entering a segment tree node, all elements associated with that node's time range are added to the active data structure.
When exiting a segment tree node, all elements added upon entry are removed, restoring the data structure to its state before entering.
The state of the data structure at any leaf node corresponds to the cumulative effect of all elements active at that specific query time.
The segment tree correctly partitions the overall time range into disjoint intervals, covering all query times.

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
when_to_use: Use when a problem has overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure, and a natural recursive solution is inefficient due to redundant computations. It's ideal for transforming exponential recursive solutions into polynomial time.
description: Top-down dynamic programming, or memoization, optimizes recursive solutions by storing the results of expensive function calls and returning the cached result when the same inputs occur again. This technique typically reduces time complexity from exponential to polynomial, often O(N) or O(N*M).
tips: Initialize the memoization table with a sentinel value (e.g., -1, null) to distinguish uncomputed states from valid zero/false results.
Use arrays for lookup tables when the state space is contiguous and small for optimal cache performance.
Consider hash maps (e.g., unordered_map) for sparse, non-integer, or complex state representations (e.g., vectors, strings).
Clearly define and handle all base cases of the recursive function before attempting memoization.
pitfalls: Forgetting to initialize the memoization table, leading to incorrect results or infinite loops.
Incorrectly identifying or handling base cases in the recursive function.
Using an inappropriate data structure for the lookup table, impacting performance or memory usage.
Potential for stack overflow errors with very deep recursion for large input sizes.
Failing to correctly identify overlapping subproblems or optimal substructure, making memoization ineffective.
invariants: memo[state] contains the computed result for 'state' if memo[state] is not the sentinel value.
If memo[state] is the sentinel value, the result for 'state' has not yet been computed.
The recursive function f(state) correctly computes and returns the value for 'state'.
Base cases of the recursion are correctly defined and return pre-determined values.

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
when_to_use: When a problem can be broken down into smaller, overlapping subproblems and an optimal solution to the larger problem can be constructed from optimal solutions to its subproblems. It's suitable for optimizing recursive solutions with repeated computations.
description: Dynamic Programming (DP) solves complex problems by breaking them into simpler overlapping subproblems and storing the results to avoid redundant computations. It typically achieves polynomial time complexity, often O(N) or O(N^2) for 1D DP, by trading space for time.
tips: Clearly define the DP state and its meaning.
Identify the base cases for the recurrence relation.
Determine the state transition formula.
Consider both top-down (memoization) and bottom-up (tabulation) approaches.
Optimize space complexity by observing state dependencies.
Draw dependency graphs for complex transitions.
pitfalls: Incorrectly defining the DP state or recurrence relation.
Missing or incorrect base cases leading to infinite recursion or wrong results.
Overlapping subproblems not being properly memoized/tabulated.
Off-by-one errors in array indexing or loop bounds.
Forgetting to initialize DP table values correctly.
Stack overflow with deep recursion in top-down DP.
invariants: dp[i] stores the optimal solution for the subproblem ending at index i.
All dp[j] for j < i have been correctly computed before dp[i] is calculated.
The base cases dp[0] or dp[1] are correctly initialized.
The state transition dp[i] depends only on previously computed states.

title: Knapsack Problem
signals: each item can be used once
choice between including or excluding an item
when_to_use: Use when selecting a subset of items, each with a weight and value, to maximize total value without exceeding a given capacity. It is applicable for resource allocation problems with discrete choices.
description: The Knapsack Problem aims to choose items with maximum total value such that their total weight does not exceed a given capacity. It is typically solved using dynamic programming, achieving a time complexity of O(nW) for n items and capacity W.
tips: Initialize DP table with zeros for capacity 0 and unreachable states.
For 0-1 knapsack with 1D DP, iterate capacity `j` in decreasing order.
