For unbounded knapsack with 1D DP, iterate capacity `j` in increasing order.
Carefully define base cases, especially for zero capacity or no items.
The 2D DP approach `dp[i][j]` can simplify conceptual understanding.
pitfalls: Incorrect iteration order for 1D DP in 0-1 knapsack (using increasing `j`).
Off-by-one errors in item indices or capacity calculations.
Failing to handle items whose weight exceeds the current capacity.
Potential for integer overflow if values or weights are very large.
Confusing 0-1 knapsack with unbounded knapsack logic.
invariants: `dp[j]` (in 1D DP) represents the maximum value achievable with capacity `j` using items considered so far.
When processing item `i` and capacity `j` (decreasing `j`), `dp[j-weight_i]` holds the value from previous items for that reduced capacity.
All `dp` table entries are non-negative, reflecting valid accumulated values.
The final `dp[W]` contains the maximum value for the total capacity `W` after all items are processed.

title: Knapsack Problem
when_to_use: Use when selecting items, each with a weight and value, to maximize total value within a limited capacity, where each item can be chosen multiple times. It is suitable for problems exhibiting optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems.
description: The Knapsack Problem involves selecting items to maximize total value within a fixed capacity. The complete knapsack variant, where items can be taken multiple times, is typically solved using dynamic programming in O(nW) time and O(W) space.
tips: For the complete knapsack, iterate capacity `j` forwards (from `w_i` to `W`) when using 1D DP to allow multiple selections of the current item.
The 1D DP array `dp[j]` stores the maximum value achievable for exactly capacity `j`.
Initialize `dp[0]` to 0 and all other `dp[j]` to a value indicating unreachability (e.g., negative infinity).
The 2D DP state `f[i][j]` considers the first `i` items and capacity `j`.
Space optimization from 2D to 1D is possible by observing dependencies.
pitfalls: Confusing 0-1 knapsack with complete knapsack, especially in the 1D DP iteration direction for capacity.
Incorrectly initializing the DP array, leading to incorrect base cases or propagation.
Off-by-one errors in capacity or item indices, particularly when handling item weights.
Not handling cases where an item's weight exceeds the current capacity `j`.
Potential integer overflow if values or weights are extremely large.
invariants: For 2D DP `f[i][j]`, it represents the maximum value obtainable using only the first `i` items with a total weight not exceeding `j`.
For 1D DP `dp[j]`, after processing item `i`, it stores the maximum value obtainable with a total weight not exceeding `j`, considering all items up to `i`.
`dp[0]` is always 0, representing an empty knapsack with no value.

title: Knapsack Problem
when_to_use: Use when selecting items from a set to maximize or minimize a value under a capacity constraint. It's also applicable for partitioning problems with a fixed budget.
description: This algorithm finds the maximum value that can be obtained by selecting a subset of items, each with a weight and a value, such that their total weight does not exceed a given capacity. It typically uses dynamic programming with a time complexity of O(N*W), where N is the number of items and W is the capacity.
tips: Use a 2D DP table `dp[i][w]` for item `i` and weight `w` for clarity, then optimize to 1D.
When optimizing to 1D, iterate weights downwards to avoid using an item multiple times.
Initialize `dp[0]` to 0 and all other `dp` values to a negative infinity or a sentinel value.
Consider edge cases where capacity is 0 or no items are available.
pitfalls: Incorrectly initializing the DP table can lead to wrong results or infinite loops.
Forgetting to iterate weights downwards in 1D DP can lead to items being picked multiple times (unbounded knapsack).
Off-by-one errors when indexing items or weights in the DP table.
Not handling cases where an item's weight exceeds the current capacity.
invariants: After processing item `i`, `dp[w]` stores the maximum value for capacity `w` using items up to `i`.
For each item, `dp[w]` is updated considering either including or excluding the current item.
The capacity `w` always remains non-negative and within the problem's defined bounds.

title: Knapsack Problem
when_to_use: When optimizing the knapsack problem where multiple identical items are available, reducing the DP transition from O(max_count) to O(1) using a sliding window maximum technique.
description: The Knapsack problem involves selecting items with weights and values to maximize total value within a capacity constraint. This specific variant, often for multiple identical items, uses dynamic programming optimized with a monotonic queue to achieve an O(NW) time complexity.
tips: Carefully define DP states, often `dp[c]` for the maximum value at capacity `c`.
For multiple items, iterate through items and then capacities, or vice-versa, considering `w_i` remainder groups.
The monotonic queue stores indices or values, ensuring elements are ordered for efficient max/min queries.
Divide capacities into `w_i` remainder groups (modulo `w_i`) to apply the monotonic queue optimization.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling item counts or capacity limits in DP transitions.
Off-by-one errors in monotonic queue window management, leading to incorrect maximums.
Forgetting to clear or re-initialize the monotonic queue for each distinct remainder group.
Not correctly mapping the transformed values (e.g., `G_{x-k, y}`) to the elements stored in the deque.
invariants: `dp[c]` represents the maximum value achievable for capacity `c` considering all items processed up to the current iteration.
For each remainder `y`, the deque stores indices `k` such that `G_{k, y}` values are monotonically decreasing from front to back.
All indices `k` in the deque for a given `y` are within the valid sliding window for the current capacity `x`.

title: Longest increasing subsequence
when_to_use: Use when finding the maximum length of a subsequence where elements are strictly increasing. It's suitable for problems requiring ordered selection from a sequence.
description: This algorithm finds the longest subsequence of a given sequence such that all elements of the subsequence are sorted in increasing order. The standard DP approach has a time complexity of O(N^2).
tips: Initialize DP array with 1s, as each element is an LIS of length 1.
Iterate through all previous elements to find potential predecessors.
Update DP state `dp[i]` as `max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1)` if `arr[i] > arr[j]`.
The final answer is the maximum value found in the DP array.
pitfalls: Forgetting to initialize the DP array correctly.
Incorrectly handling strict versus non-strict increasing conditions.
Off-by-one errors in loop bounds or array indexing.
Not considering all possible previous elements for each current element.
invariants: At the start of iteration `i`, `dp[j]` for `j < i` stores the length of the LIS ending at `arr[j]`.
After processing `arr[i]`, `dp[i]` holds the length of the LIS ending with `arr[i]`.
The maximum value found in the `dp` array represents the overall LIS length.

title: Longest increasing subsequence
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to find the maximum length of a subsequence where elements are strictly increasing. It is particularly efficient when optimizing the O(N^2) dynamic programming solution to O(N log N) using binary search.
description: This algorithm determines the longest subsequence of a given sequence where elements are in strictly increasing order. It can be solved with dynamic programming in O(N^2) time or optimized to O(N log N) using binary search.
tips: The O(N log N) approach maintains an array 'tails' where 'tails[i]' is the smallest ending element of an increasing subsequence of length 'i+1'.
Utilize binary search (e.g., `std::lower_bound`) to find the correct position for the current element in the 'tails' array.
If the current element is greater than all elements in 'tails', it extends the longest subsequence found so far.
The final length of the 'tails' array directly corresponds to the length of the LIS.
pitfalls: Confusing a subsequence with a contiguous subarray, which is a common mistake.
Incorrectly updating the 'tails' array, leading to non-increasing subsequences or incorrect lengths.
Off-by-one errors when mapping 'tails' array indices to subsequence lengths.
Failing to handle duplicate elements correctly if the requirement is for a strictly increasing subsequence.
invariants: The 'tails' array always stores the smallest ending element for increasing subsequences of various lengths.
The 'tails' array is always sorted in strictly increasing order.
The length of the 'tails' array represents the current maximum LIS length found so far.
For any index 'i', 'tails[i]' is the smallest tail of all increasing subsequences of length 'i+1'.

title: Longest increasing subsequence
when_to_use: Use when you need to find the longest possible subsequence of a given sequence such that elements in the subsequence are in strictly increasing order, preserving their original relative order.
description: This algorithm finds the longest subsequence where elements are in strictly increasing order. It can be solved with dynamic programming in O(N^2) time or optimized to O(N log N) using binary search or a segment tree.
tips: The O(N log N) approach maintains an array where dp[i] stores the smallest ending element of an increasing subsequence of length i+1.
Binary search is used to find the correct position for the current element in the dp array.
Coordinate compression might be needed if element values are large for the segment tree approach.
For non-decreasing subsequences, use upper_bound instead of lower_bound in the binary search optimization.
pitfalls: Confusing subsequence with subarray, which requires contiguous elements.
Incorrectly handling duplicates for strictly increasing versus non-decreasing requirements.
Off-by-one errors in binary search or DP array indexing, especially with 0-based vs 1-based length.
Forgetting to initialize the DP array correctly, often with a sentinel value like infinity.
invariants: The 'tails' array (or DP array) is always sorted in increasing order.
For any index 'i', 'tails[i]' stores the smallest possible ending element of an increasing subsequence of length 'i+1'.
The length of the 'tails' array at any point represents the current maximum LIS length found.
Elements processed so far maintain their relative order in any constructed subsequence.

title: Longest increasing subsequence
when_to_use: Use this when you need to find the longest possible subsequence of a given sequence where elements are in strictly increasing order. It is applicable for optimizing problems involving ordered selections from a sequence.
description: The Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem finds the longest subsequence of a given sequence such that all elements are sorted in increasing order. It can be solved using dynamic programming in O(N^2) time or with a greedy approach using binary search in O(N log N) time.
tips: The O(N log N) approach maintains a 'tails' array where tails[i] stores the smallest ending element of an increasing subsequence of length i+1.
Use binary search (specifically `lower_bound` for strictly increasing) to find the correct position to update the 'tails' array.
For the O(N^2) DP, initialize each element's LIS length to 1, as it forms an LIS of itself.
Consider edge cases like empty arrays or arrays with all identical elements.
pitfalls: Confusing subsequence with subarray; elements do not need to be contiguous.
Incorrectly applying binary search (e.g., using `upper_bound` instead of `lower_bound` for strict increase).
Off-by-one errors when calculating lengths or indices in the 'tails' array.
Forgetting to handle duplicate elements correctly based on problem's definition of 'increasing'.
invariants: The 'tails' array (or equivalent) is always sorted in increasing order.
For any index `i`, `tails[i]` stores the smallest ending element of an increasing subsequence of length `i+1` found so far.
The current length of the 'tails' array represents the maximum LIS length discovered.
Each element in the input array is processed exactly once.

title: Longest increasing subsequence
when_to_use: Use when seeking the longest possible sequence of elements from a given array such that each element is greater than the preceding one, preserving their original relative order.
description: The Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem identifies the longest possible subsequence where elements are in strictly increasing order, preserving their original relative positions. It can be solved efficiently in O(N log N) time using dynamic programming with binary search or a segment tree.
tips: Use a `tails` array to store the smallest ending element for LIS of each length.
Employ binary search on the `tails` array to update or extend subsequences.
For basic DP, `dp[i]` represents the LIS length ending at index `i`.
A segment tree can optimize finding the maximum LIS length ending before the current element.
pitfalls: Confusing subsequence (relative order) with contiguous subarray.
Incorrectly implementing strict vs. non-strict increasing conditions.
Off-by-one errors when updating `tails` array or DP states.
Forgetting to handle duplicate elements correctly based on problem definition.
Not initializing DP states or `tails` array properly.
invariants: The `tails` array elements are always strictly increasing.
For any index `k`, `tails[k]` holds the smallest possible ending element of an increasing subsequence of length `k+1`.
The length of the `tails` array at any point represents the length of the longest increasing subsequence found so far.

title: Longest increasing subsequence
when_to_use: Use when finding the longest sequence of elements in a given array such that each element is greater than the preceding one, without changing their relative order. It's applicable for optimizing problems involving ordered subsets.
description: The Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem finds the longest subsequence of an array where elements are in strictly increasing order. It can be solved in O(N log N) time using a greedy approach with binary search, or O(N^2) using dynamic programming.
tips: Prefer the O(N log N) greedy approach with binary search for better performance.
The 'tails' array in the greedy approach stores the smallest tail of all increasing subsequences of a certain length.
Binary search is crucial for efficiently finding the correct position to update the 'tails' array.
For O(N^2) DP, dp[i] stores the length of the LIS ending at index i.
pitfalls: Confusing LIS with Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) or contiguous subarrays.
Incorrectly handling duplicate elements when 'strictly increasing' is required.
Off-by-one errors in binary search implementation for the 'tails' array.
Misinterpreting the 'tails' array as the actual LIS itself, rather than just its ending elements.
invariants: The 'tails' array elements are always in strictly increasing order.
tails[i] stores the smallest possible ending element of an increasing subsequence of length i+1.
The current length of the LIS is equal to the number of elements in the 'tails' array.

title: Divide and Conquer DP
when_to_use: Use when a problem can be broken into smaller, independent subproblems, and their solutions combine to solve the original. Also applicable when overlapping subproblems benefit from memoization or tabulation.
description: This approach solves problems by recursively breaking them into smaller subproblems, solving each once, and storing results in a 2D table to avoid recomputation. It typically achieves a time complexity of O(N*M) or O(N^2) depending on state definition.
tips: Clearly define the state variables for your 2D DP table.
Establish the base cases before defining recursive relations.
Consider the order of computation for tabulation to ensure dependencies are met.
Visualize the DP table to understand transitions.
pitfalls: Incorrectly defining state transitions or recurrence relations.
Missing or incorrect base cases leading to infinite recursion or wrong results.
Overlapping subproblems not being properly memoized, leading to exponential time.
Off-by-one errors in array indexing or loop bounds.
invariants: The DP table entry `dp[i][j]` correctly stores the optimal solution for the subproblem defined by `i` and `j`.
All subproblems `dp[x][y]` where `x < i` or `y < j` (or based on dependency order) are already computed and correct.
The final solution is derived from the appropriate entry in the DP table after all necessary computations.

title: Knuth's Optimization
signals: dp(i, j) = min_k [dp(i, k) + dp(k+1, j) + C(i, j)]
when_to_use: When a range DP problem has a recurrence of the form dp(i, j) = min_k [dp(i, k) + dp(k+1, j) + C(i, j)] and the optimal splitting points satisfy opt(i, j-1) <= opt(i, j) <= opt(i+1, j).
description: Knuth's Optimization is a technique to speed up certain dynamic programming problems on ranges from O(N^3) to O(N^2) time complexity. It applies when the optimal splitting point for a subproblem exhibits a monotonic property across adjacent subproblems.
tips: Verify the quadrangle inequality for the cost function C(i, j).
Ensure the optimal split points exhibit monotonicity: opt(i, j-1) <= opt(i, j) <= opt(i+1, j).
Iterate DP states by increasing interval length (j-i+1).
Precompute the cost function C(i, j) if it is complex.
pitfalls: Applying the optimization without rigorously verifying the monotonicity condition.
Incorrectly calculating the cost function C(i, j) or its properties.
Off-by-one errors in interval indices or the optimal split point k.
Forgetting to correctly initialize base cases for dp(i, i) and opt(i, i).
invariants: dp[i][j] stores the minimum cost for the interval [i, j].
opt[i][j] stores the optimal split point k for interval [i, j].
For all i < j, opt[i][j-1] <= opt[i][j] <= opt[i+1][j].
The length of the current interval (j - i + 1) increases in each outer loop iteration.

title: Knuth's Optimization
signals: opt(i,j) depends on opt(i,j-1) and opt(i+1,j)
nested loops i decreasing, j increasing
when_to_use: Used to optimize certain 2D dynamic programming problems, typically interval DP, where the cost function satisfies the quadrangle inequality and the optimal split points exhibit monotonicity.
description: Knuth's Optimization reduces the time complexity of certain 2D DP problems from O(N^3) to O(N^2) by restricting the search range for the optimal split point. This is applicable when the cost function satisfies the quadrangle inequality and the optimal split points are monotonic.
tips: Verify the quadrangle inequality for the cost function C(a,c) + C(b,d) <= C(a,d) + C(b,c).
Ensure the optimal split points opt[i][j] are monotonic: opt[i][j-1] <= opt[i][j] <= opt[i+1][j].
Iterate DP states by increasing interval length, then i decreasing, j increasing.
The optimization applies to dp[i][j] = min_{k}(dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + C[i][j]) forms.
pitfalls: Incorrectly assuming quadrangle inequality holds for the cost function.
Improper iteration order for DP states, which breaks the opt dependency.
Off-by-one errors in opt bounds (opt(i, j-1) to opt(i+1, j)).
Applying to problems where the optimal split points are not monotonic.
invariants: dp[i][j] stores the minimum cost for the interval [i, j].
opt[i][j] stores the optimal split point k for dp[i][j].
For any i <= j, opt[i][j-1] <= opt[i][j] <= opt[i+1][j] holds.
The cost function C satisfies the quadrangle inequality.

title: Dynamic Programming on Broken Profile. Problem "Parquet"
when_to_use: When problems involve tiling a grid or area with specific shapes, and the state can be represented by the 'profile' or boundary between processed and unprocessed cells, often using a bitmask.
description: Dynamic Programming on Broken Profile is a technique used to solve tiling problems on grids by iterating through cells and maintaining the 'profile' of the boundary between filled and unfilled regions using a bitmask. This approach typically has a time complexity of O(N * M * 2^M) or O(N * M * 2^N), depending on the smaller dimension.
tips: Represent the profile as a bitmask where each bit indicates the state of a cell on the boundary.
Process the grid cell by cell, usually row by row, then column by column within a row.
The DP state often includes the current cell's coordinates and the bitmask representing the profile.
Consider how placing a figure affects the current cell and the next cell's profile.
Precompute valid transitions between masks to optimize state calculation.
pitfalls: Incorrectly defining the bitmask or its interpretation (e.g., which direction it represents).
Handling boundary conditions and edge cases for the grid dimensions.
Overlapping figures or leaving cells unfilled due to incorrect state transitions.
Exponential time complexity if M (or N) is too large (e.g., > 15-20).
Forgetting to account for all possible placements of figures at each cell.
Off-by-one errors in mask manipulation or cell indexing.
invariants: dp[row][mask] correctly stores the count of ways to tile the grid up to row-1, with mask representing the profile of row `row`.
All cells in rows 0 through `row-1` are completely and non-overlappingly tiled.
The `mask` accurately reflects the state of cells in the current row that are covered by tiles extending from the previous row.
During processing of a cell (r, c), all cells (x, y) where x < r or (x == r and y < c) are already considered and their tiling status is consistent with the DP state.

title: Finding the largest zero submatrix
when_to_use: When finding the maximum rectangular subregion consisting of only zeros in a binary matrix, often by transforming rows into histogram problems.
description: This algorithm finds the largest rectangular submatrix containing only zeros by precomputing heights of consecutive zeros for each column and then applying a largest rectangle in histogram approach for each row. It has a time complexity of O(rows * cols).
tips: Transform the 2D problem into a series of 1D histogram problems, one for each row.
Precompute the height of consecutive zeros ending at each cell in each column.
Use a monotonic stack to efficiently find the largest rectangle in each row's histogram in linear time.
Initialize the height array for each row based on the previous row's heights, resetting to zero if a '1' is encountered.
pitfalls: Incorrectly calculating the height array, especially at row boundaries or when encountering '1's.
Off-by-one errors when calculating rectangle areas in the histogram step.
Inefficiently solving the largest rectangle in histogram problem (e.g., O(N^2) for each row instead of O(N)).
Forgetting to handle edge cases like empty rows or columns during histogram calculation.
Not properly resetting column heights to zero when a '1' is encountered in the input matrix.
invariants: After processing row `i`, `heights[j]` accurately represents the count of consecutive zeros ending at `(i, j)` in column `j`.
The `max_area` variable correctly stores the largest rectangular area of zeros found in any submatrix from rows `0` to `i`.
During the histogram processing for a row, the monotonic stack maintains indices of columns with increasing heights.

title: Finding the largest zero submatrix
when_to_use: This algorithm is used to find the largest rectangular submatrix composed entirely of zeros within a binary matrix. It efficiently reduces the 2D problem to a series of 1D largest rectangle in histogram problems.
description: The algorithm transforms a 2D matrix problem into a series of 1D 'largest rectangle in histogram' problems, one for each row. It uses a monotonic stack to find the nearest smaller elements for each bar in O(N) time, leading to an overall O(N*M) complexity for the 2D problem.
tips: Precompute heights for each column, representing consecutive zeros upwards from the current row.
The 2D problem is effectively reduced to finding the largest rectangle in a histogram for each row.
Use a monotonic stack to efficiently find the nearest smaller element to the left and right for each bar.
Extend the histogram array with a zero at the end to ensure all stack elements are processed.
The 'height' for the histogram is often the count of consecutive zeros upwards.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling boundary conditions when using the stack (e.g., empty stack, end of array).
Off-by-one errors when calculating the width of the rectangle (k2 - k1 - 1).
Forgetting to process any remaining elements in the monotonic stack after iterating through all bars.
Incorrectly calculating or resetting the height array for each row iteration.
Not considering the case where no smaller element exists to the left or right.
invariants: The stack always stores indices of elements in strictly increasing order of their corresponding heights.
When an element is popped from the stack, the element below it is its nearest smaller element to the left.
For each column `j` in the current row `i`, `d[i][j]` correctly represents the height of consecutive zeros ending at `(i, j)`.
The maximum area found so far is always the largest valid zero submatrix encountered up to the current processing point.

title: String Hashing
when_to_use: Use when needing to compare strings or substrings quickly, reducing comparison time from linear to constant. It is ideal for problems requiring frequent string equality checks or pattern matching across large texts.
description: String hashing maps a string to an integer hash value, allowing for O(1) equality checks between strings or substrings. This technique relies on a hash function where equal strings must produce equal hashes, though the reverse is not guaranteed.
tips: Use a polynomial rolling hash function with a prime base and a large prime modulus.
Employ double hashing (two different bases/moduli) to significantly reduce collision probability.
Precompute powers of the base and prefix hashes to enable O(1) substring hash calculation.
Choose moduli that are large primes, often close to 10^9 + 7 or 10^9 + 9.
Handle character values carefully, mapping them to positive integers (e.g., 'a' to 1, 'b' to 2).
pitfalls: Hash collisions: Different strings can produce the same hash, leading to false positives if not handled.
Poor choice of base or modulus can lead to more frequent collisions.
Integer overflow if hash values exceed the capacity of the chosen integer type before applying the modulus.
Not handling empty strings or single-character strings correctly in hash calculations.
Using a weak hash function that is easily reversible or produces many collisions.
invariants: The hash of any prefix s[0...i] is correctly computed based on s[0...i-1] and s[i].
All characters processed up to the current index i contribute to the current hash value.
The computed hash value always remains within the bounds of the chosen modulus.
The base power p^k used for character s[k] is correctly maintained.

title: String Hashing
when_to_use: Use when needing to efficiently compare strings or substrings, especially in scenarios involving large texts or numerous comparisons. It's also useful for pattern matching and detecting duplicates in collections of strings.
description: String hashing converts a string into a numerical value (hash) to enable fast comparisons and lookups. This technique typically uses polynomial hashing with a base and a modulus, offering O(L) for initial hash computation and O(1) for substring hashes after O(N) precomputation.
tips: Use multiple hash functions with different bases and moduli to reduce collision probability.
Choose a large prime modulus (e.g., 10^9 + 7) and a random base for robustness.
Precompute powers of the base and their modular inverses for O(1) substring hash calculation.
Handle potential integer overflows by using `unsigned long long` or modular arithmetic carefully.
Consider randomizing the base for each test case to prevent precomputed collision attacks.
For rolling hashes, efficiently update the hash value when characters are added or removed.
pitfalls: Hash collisions are possible; always verify equality for critical applications or use multiple hashes.
Choosing a small or non-prime modulus significantly increases the risk of collisions.
Incorrectly calculating modular inverse for substring removal can lead to erroneous results.
Not handling negative results from modular arithmetic correctly (e.g., `(a - b + M) % M`).
Using a fixed, small base can make the hash function vulnerable to specific adversarial inputs.
Forgetting to precompute powers can lead to inefficient O(N*L) complexity for substring hashes.
invariants: The hash of a string S of length L is (S[0]*P^(L-1) + S[1]*P^(L-2) + ... + S[L-1]*P^0) % M.
For any substring S[i...j], its hash can be derived from precomputed prefix hashes in O(1) time.
The precomputed powers of the base P modulo M are correct for all relevant string lengths.
The modular inverse of P (or P^k) is correctly calculated and applied for rolling hash operations.

title: String Hashing
when_to_use: Use when you need to efficiently compare strings or substrings, detect duplicates, or perform pattern matching in large text datasets. It's particularly useful for speeding up operations that would otherwise be O(N*M) or O(N^2).
description: String hashing maps strings to numerical values, allowing for fast comparisons and operations like substring search. It typically uses a polynomial rolling hash function, achieving O(N) preprocessing and O(1) or O(log N) query time for substrings.
tips: Choose a large prime modulus (or two) to minimize collision probability.
Select a base (prime number) larger than the alphabet size to ensure unique contributions from characters.
Precompute powers of the base to enable O(1) substring hash calculation after O(N) preprocessing.
Employ two different hash functions (with distinct bases and moduli) to drastically reduce the chance of collisions.
Use unsigned integer types for hash values to avoid negative results and utilize the full bit range.
pitfalls: Hash collisions are possible and can lead to incorrect results if not properly handled or verified.
A poorly chosen base or modulus can significantly increase the frequency of collisions.
Vulnerable to 'hash collision attacks' if the hash function is predictable and not sufficiently robust.
Overflow issues can occur with intermediate calculations if modular arithmetic is not applied correctly at each step.
String hashing is not suitable for cryptographic security due to its predictable and non-random nature.
invariants: The hash of any prefix of the string is correctly computed based on its characters and chosen parameters.
For a rolling hash, the hash of the current window is always maintained correctly as the window slides.
All hash values remain within the fixed range defined by the chosen modulus.

title: String Hashing
when_to_use: Use string hashing to efficiently compare strings, find duplicate strings, or perform fast substring searches and pattern matching in O(1) time after initial O(N) preprocessing.
description: String hashing maps a string to a numerical value using a polynomial function, enabling O(1) comparison of strings or substrings after O(N) preprocessing.
tips: Use two different hash functions with distinct prime `p` and `m` values to minimize collision probability.
Precompute powers of `p` and prefix hashes to enable O(1) substring hash calculation.
Map characters to positive integers (e.g., 'a' to 1, 'b' to 2) to avoid issues with zero values.
Choose large prime numbers for `p` and `m` to reduce the likelihood of hash collisions.
pitfalls: Hash collisions can lead to incorrect results; always consider their possibility.
Integer overflow if modulo operations are not applied correctly at each step of calculation.
Choosing small or non-prime `p` and `m` values increases collision risk.
Incorrectly calculating substring hashes, especially when dealing with inverse modulo for division.
invariants: prefix_hash[i] stores the polynomial hash of the substring s[0...i-1].
p_power[k] stores p^k mod m for efficient calculation.
The hash of any substring s[L...R] can be derived in O(1) from prefix_hash values.

title: String Hashing
when_to_use: Use string hashing to efficiently compare substrings or patterns within a larger text, or to quickly check for duplicate strings. It's ideal for problems requiring fast equality checks on string segments.
description: String hashing converts a string or substring into a numerical hash value, allowing for O(1) comparison of substrings after O(N) precomputation. It typically uses polynomial hashing with a prime base and modulus to minimize collisions.
tips: Choose a large prime number for the base 'p' (e.g., 31, 53) to reduce collisions.
Select a large prime number for the modulus 'm' (e.g., 10^9+7, 10^9+9) to further minimize collision probability.
Precompute powers of 'p' and prefix hashes to enable O(1) substring hash calculation.
Consider using two different hash functions with distinct 'p' and 'm' values to significantly lower collision chances.
pitfalls: Avoid using 2^64 as a modulus, as specific collision-generating strings exist for this choice.
A small modulus 'm' or base 'p' drastically increases the likelihood of hash collisions.
Hash collisions are always possible, even with good parameters; handle them gracefully or use multiple hashes.
Incorrectly handling modular arithmetic (e.g., negative results from subtraction) can lead to wrong hash values.
invariants: The hash value for the prefix S[0...i] is correctly computed and stored, based on the chosen polynomial hashing scheme.
The base 'p' and modulus 'm' remain constant throughout the hash computation for consistent results.
Any substring hash S[i...j] can be derived from prefix_hash[j] and prefix_hash[i-1] in O(1) time.

title: String Hashing
when_to_use: When comparing strings or substrings efficiently, especially for equality checks or finding duplicates, to reduce comparison time from O(m) to O(1) on average.
description: String hashing converts a string into a numerical value (hash) using a polynomial rolling hash function, allowing for O(1) average-case comparison of strings or substrings. This technique typically involves modular arithmetic to keep hash values within a manageable range.
tips: Convert characters to integers starting from 1 (e.g., 'a' -> 1) to avoid zero hashes for strings like 'a', 'aa'.
Precompute powers of the base 'p' and their modular inverses for efficient rolling hash calculations.
Use two different prime moduli and bases (double hashing) to significantly minimize collision probability.
Choose a prime base 'p' larger than the alphabet size to ensure distinct character mappings.
pitfalls: Choosing 'a' -> 0 can lead to identical hashes for strings like 'a', 'aa', 'aaa', causing false positives.
Using a single hash function can lead to collisions, where different strings produce the same hash, requiring careful handling.
Forgetting to handle modulo operations correctly can result in negative numbers or incorrect hash values.
Incorrectly calculating modular inverses or handling large prime numbers can lead to errors.
invariants: The hash of any prefix s[0...i] is correctly calculated based on the chosen base and modulus.
Precomputed powers of the base 'p' are available and correct for all required string lengths.
The hash value for any string or substring remains within the bounds of the chosen modulus.

title: Rabin-Karp Algorithm for string matching
when_to_use: To efficiently find all occurrences of a given pattern string within a larger text string, especially when multiple patterns might be searched or for average-case linear time performance.
description: The Rabin-Karp algorithm finds occurrences of a pattern in a text by comparing their hash values, using a rolling hash function to efficiently update substring hashes. It achieves an average time complexity of O(|s| + |t|) and a worst-case of O(|s| * |t|).
tips: Choose a large prime modulus to minimize hash collisions.
Select a suitable base (e.g., a prime greater than alphabet size) for polynomial hashing.
Precompute powers of the base modulo the prime for O(1) hash updates.
Use two different hash functions with distinct primes/bases to further reduce collision probability.
Always perform character-by-character comparison for potential matches to handle collisions.
pitfalls: Hash collisions can lead to false positives and degrade worst-case performance to O(MN).
Poor choice of base or modulus can significantly increase collision frequency.
Integer overflow if modulo arithmetic is not applied correctly at each step of hash calculation.
Incorrectly handling negative results from modulo operations in some languages.
Forgetting to subtract the contribution of the leading character when rolling the hash.
invariants: The hash of the pattern string remains constant throughout the search.
The current window's hash value accurately represents the substring T[i...i+len(P)-1].
All prefix hashes of the text are correctly precomputed and accessible.
The rolling hash update correctly removes the oldest character's value and adds the newest character's value.

title: Prefix function. Knuth–Morris–Pratt algorithm
when_to_use: Used for efficient string searching and pattern matching in linear time. It's also fundamental for various string algorithms that leverage self-matching properties.
description: The prefix function computes for each position in a string the length of the longest proper prefix of its substring that is also a suffix of that substring. This forms the core of the Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) algorithm, enabling linear time O(N) string matching.
tips: The prefix function array (pi array) stores lengths, not indices.
It can be used to build the KMP automaton for pattern matching.
The algorithm processes the string character by character, leveraging previously computed prefix function values.
Consider using a dummy character (e.g., '#') when concatenating pattern and text for KMP search.
pitfalls: Confusing proper prefix/suffix with non-proper ones.
Incorrectly handling the base case pi[0] = 0.
Off-by-one errors when comparing characters or updating lengths.
Forgetting to reset the 'j' pointer (length of current match) when no match is found.
invariants: For each i, pi[i] is the length of the longest proper prefix of s[0...i] that is also a suffix of s[0...i].
The current j (length of the matched prefix) always represents pi[i-1] or a shorter valid prefix length.
0 <= pi[i] <= i for all i.
s[0...pi[i]-1] is equal to s[i-pi[i]+1...i].

title: Prefix function. Knuth–Morris–Pratt algorithm
when_to_use: When efficiently searching for all occurrences of a pattern string within a larger text string, or when computing the longest proper prefix of a string that is also a suffix for all its prefixes.
description: The KMP algorithm efficiently finds all occurrences of a pattern within a text by pre-processing the pattern to build a prefix function (LPS array), which avoids redundant character comparisons. It achieves a time complexity of O(N + M) for text length N and pattern length M.
tips: Precompute the prefix function (LPS array) for the pattern to guide shifts.
Use two pointers: one for the text (or current character in pattern for prefix function), one for the pattern (or length of matched prefix).
When a mismatch occurs, shift the pattern pointer using the prefix function values, avoiding re-scanning matched characters.
The prefix function value at index `i` indicates the length of the longest proper prefix of `s[0...i]` that is also a suffix of `s[0...i]`.
pitfalls: Incorrectly calculating or using the prefix function values, leading to wrong shifts.
Off-by-one errors when indexing or shifting pointers, especially at boundaries.
Forgetting to handle the base case for the prefix function (e.g., `pi[0] = 0`).
Not resetting the pattern pointer correctly after a full match is found in the text.
invariants: The prefix function array `pi` stores for each `i` the length of the longest proper prefix of `s[0...i]` that is also a suffix of `s[0...i]`.
During prefix function computation for `pi[i]`, `j` always represents the length of the longest proper prefix of `s[0...i-1]` that is also a suffix of `s[0...i-1]`.
When comparing `s[j]` and `s[i]`, the substring `s[0...j-1]` is a prefix of `s` and also a suffix of `s[0...i-1]`.
The value of `j` (length of matched prefix) never increases beyond `i` and is correctly updated based on character matches or `pi` values.

title: Prefix function. Knuth–Morris–Pratt algorithm
when_to_use: Use when efficiently finding all occurrences of a pattern in a text, analyzing string periodicity, or counting distinct substrings. It is especially useful for processing extremely long or recursively defined strings where explicit construction is impossible.
description: The Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) algorithm leverages a precomputed prefix function (LPS array) to avoid redundant comparisons during string searching, achieving linear time complexity. The prefix function itself determines the longest proper prefix of a string that is also a suffix, enabling various string property analyses in O(N) time.
tips: The prefix function `pi[i]` stores the length of the longest proper prefix of `s[0...i]` that is also a suffix.
Concatenate pattern, a unique separator, and text (`P + # + T`) to find all pattern occurrences.
The KMP automaton allows efficient processing of gigantic or recursively defined strings without explicit construction.
String periodicity can be identified using `n - pi[n-1]`, which gives the length of the smallest repeating unit if it divides `n`.

pitfalls: Off-by-one errors with array indexing or the precise definition of the prefix function.
Incorrectly handling the unique separator character in concatenated strings, leading to false matches.

invariants: For any index `i`, `pi[i]` stores the length of the longest proper prefix of `s[0...i]` that is also a suffix of `s[0...i]`.

In the KMP automaton, the current state `j` signifies that the last `j` characters of the processed text match the first `j` characters of the pattern.
`0 <= pi[i] < i+1` for all `i > 0`.

title: Z-function and its calculation
when_to_use: Use when you need to find the length of the longest common prefix between a string and all its suffixes efficiently. It is fundamental for various string matching and pattern searching problems.
description: The Z-function computes an array where each element z[i] is the length of the longest common prefix between the original string and its suffix starting at index i. This algorithm calculates the entire Z-array in O(N) time complexity.
tips: Leverage previously computed Z-values to avoid redundant comparisons.
Maintain a [l, r) window representing the rightmost segment match found so far.
When i < r, use z[i - l] to initialize z[i] and extend if possible.
If i >= r, perform a naive character-by-character comparison from scratch.
Update the [l, r) window whenever a new rightmost segment match is found.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling the base case z[0] (usually set to 0).
Off-by-one errors when calculating the length of the prefix or updating the window.
Failing to correctly update the [l, r) window, leading to incorrect optimizations.
Not considering the case where i - l exceeds the current window's bounds (r - i).
Performance degradation if not properly utilizing the already computed Z-values.
invariants: For all k < i, z[k] has been correctly computed.
The interval [l, r) always represents the rightmost segment match found so far.
s[0...z[i]-1] is equal to s[i...i+z[i]-1] for all valid i.
z[0] is always 0.

title: Z-function and its calculation
when_to_use: Use when you need to find all occurrences of a pattern within a text, or to identify repeated prefixes within a string efficiently.

invariants: For all j < i, z[j] correctly stores the length of the longest common prefix between S and S[j...].
The segment [l, r-1] represents the rightmost segment that is a prefix of S and has been fully processed.
When calculating z[i], if i < r, then S[i...i+k-1] matches S[0...k-1] for k = min(z[i-l], r-i).

title: Suffix Array

title: Suffix Array

title: Suffix Array

title: Aho-Corasick algorithm
when_to_use: Use when you need to find all occurrences of a fixed set of multiple patterns within a larger text efficiently. It's particularly effective when patterns can overlap or share prefixes.
description: The Aho-Corasick algorithm constructs a finite automaton from a set of patterns using a trie and failure links, enabling efficient simultaneous search for all patterns in a text. It achieves a time complexity of O(N + M + K).
tips: Construct the trie from all patterns first, then compute failure links.
Compute failure links using a BFS-like traversal from the root.
Implement output links to efficiently report all matching patterns, including those that are suffixes.

pitfalls: Incorrectly computing failure links, especially for nodes at depth 1 or for non-existent transitions.

Inefficient trie representation for very large alphabets, impacting memory and speed.
Not handling the case where a pattern is a prefix of another pattern correctly.
invariants: Every path from the root to a node in the trie represents a unique prefix of at least one pattern.
After processing a character, the current automaton state corresponds to the longest suffix of the processed text that is also a prefix of some pattern.
All failure links for nodes at depth `d` are correctly established before processing nodes at depth `d+1`.

title: Aho-Corasick algorithm

title: Aho-Corasick algorithm
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to efficiently find all occurrences of a fixed set of patterns within a larger text. It is ideal for scenarios requiring simultaneous searching for multiple dictionary words.

title: Lyndon factorization
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to decompose a string into a unique sequence of Lyndon words, which are lexicographically smallest among their cyclic shifts. It is also applicable for finding the smallest cyclic shift of a string.

tips: Thoroughly understand the definitions of 'simple string' and 'pre-simple string' to correctly apply the comparison logic.
The three pointers (i, j, k) are crucial; 'i' marks the start of the current pre-simple string, 'j' the start of the untouched part, and 'k' for comparison within the pre-simple string.
The algorithm's efficiency stems from the fact that the 'i' pointer always advances, ensuring linear time complexity.
The comparison logic for s[j] vs s[k] dictates pointer movements and when a Lyndon word is finalized.
pitfalls: Incorrectly resetting the 'k' pointer when s[j] > s[k], which should point back to the beginning of the simple word within s2.

invariants: The prefix s[0...i-1] always contains a valid Lyndon factorization.
The substring s[i...j-1] is always a pre-simple string.

title: Expression parsing

Push numerical operands directly onto the stack.

Ensure the final result is the only element remaining on the stack.

title: Expression parsing
