title: Manacher's Algorithm - Finding all sub-p
when_to_use: Use when you need to find all palindromic substrings within a given string, or determine the longest palindromic substring, in optimal linear time.
description: Manacher's Algorithm efficiently finds the length of the longest palindromic substring centered at each position in a given string, handling both odd and even lengths uniformly via preprocessing. It achieves this in optimal O(N) time complexity.
tips: Preprocess the string with a unique delimiter to unify odd and even length palindromes.
Leverage the mirror property around the current center to initialize palindrome radii.
Maintain `center` and `right_boundary` to skip redundant checks and optimize the scan.
The `p` array stores the radius, not the full length, of the palindrome.
pitfalls: Off-by-one errors when converting `p` array values back to original string indices or lengths.
Incorrectly handling the boundaries of the preprocessed string.
Forgetting to initialize the `p` array or edge cases for the first character.
Misunderstanding the mirror index calculation, especially near boundaries.
invariants: p[i] correctly stores the radius of the longest palindrome centered at i in the transformed string.
The right_boundary always marks the rightmost extent of the largest palindrome found so far.
For any i within right_boundary, p[i] is initialized using the mirror property, ensuring a minimum radius.
All palindromes with radius less than p[i] centered at i are implicitly covered.

title: Manacher's Algorithm - Finding all sub-p
when_to_use: When you need to find all palindromic substrings or the longest palindromic substring in a given string efficiently. It is particularly useful for achieving linear time complexity by leveraging symmetry.
description: Manacher's algorithm finds all palindromic substrings in a string by expanding around centers and utilizing symmetry to avoid redundant comparisons. It achieves a linear time complexity of O(N) by maintaining the rightmost boundary of the longest palindrome found so far.
tips: Pre-process the string with delimiters (e.g., '#') to uniformly handle both odd and even length palindromes.
Leverage symmetry: for a center 'i' within the current rightmost palindrome, its mirror 'j' provides a lower bound for the palindrome length at 'i'.
Maintain 'l' and 'r' (left and right boundaries) of the palindrome that extends furthest to the right.
When 'i' is outside the current rightmost palindrome, perform a naive expansion.
The 'd' array stores the radius (or half-length) of the palindrome centered at each position in the pre-processed string.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling boundary conditions when the mirror palindrome extends beyond the current rightmost palindrome.
Forgetting to update the 'l' and 'r' boundaries after finding a new palindrome that extends further to the right.
Off-by-one errors when calculating mirror indices or palindrome lengths/radii.
Not properly initializing the 'd' array or the 'l' and 'r' boundaries.
Failing to account for the pre-processing step when mapping results back to the original string indices.
invariants: For any index k < i, d[k] correctly stores the radius of the longest palindrome centered at k.
The pair (l, r) always represents the exclusive boundaries of the palindrome that extends furthest to the right among all processed centers.
For any center i, the expansion process correctly identifies the maximum possible palindrome length centered at i.

title: Manacher's Algorithm - Finding all sub-p
when_to_use: When you need to find all palindromic substrings or the longest palindromic substring within a given string efficiently. It is particularly useful for achieving linear time complexity for this task.
description: Manacher's algorithm finds all maximal palindromic substrings centered at each position in a string by leveraging symmetry and previously computed results. It achieves an optimal O(N) time complexity.
tips: Preprocess the string with delimiters to unify odd and even length palindromes.
Maintain the current rightmost boundary `r` of the largest palindrome found.
Use the mirror position `j` to initialize `d_odd[i]` based on symmetry.
The trivial expansion only extends beyond the current `(l, r)` boundary.
pitfalls: Forgetting to update the `(l, r)` boundaries after processing each center.
Incorrectly calculating mirror positions or handling boundary cases for `i > r`.
Off-by-one errors when converting `d_odd[i]` to actual palindrome length.
Failing to preprocess the string, leading to separate logic for odd/even palindromes.
invariants: d_odd[i] stores the radius of the longest odd-length palindrome centered at i.
The (l, r) interval always defines the rightmost palindrome found so far.
For any i <= r, d_odd[i] is initialized using symmetry from d_odd[l + r - i] or capped by r - i.
The rightmost boundary r is monotonically non-decreasing, ensuring overall linear time complexity.

title: Finding repetitions
when_to_use: This algorithm is used to find all occurrences of repeating substrings within a given string. It is suitable for problems requiring the identification of periodic patterns or structural redundancies.
description: This algorithm, often exemplified by Main-Lorentz, uses a divide-and-conquer strategy with Z-function as a subroutine to efficiently find all repetitions in a string. It typically achieves an O(N log N) time complexity for finding and describing repetitions.
tips: Understand the definition of a 'repetition' (e.g., Crochemore triples or (i, p, r) tuples).
Leverage string algorithms like Z-function for efficient substring matching in subproblems.
Divide the problem into 'left crossing' and 'right crossing' repetitions around a center point.
Use a unique separator character when concatenating strings for Z-function computations.
The algorithm often returns compact representations (tuples) of repetitions, not expanded ranges.
pitfalls: Expanding compact repetition tuples to full start/end indices can lead to O(N^2) complexity.
Incorrectly handling string reversal or concatenation for Z-function applications.
Off-by-one errors when defining the center point or calculating lengths k1 and k2.
Overlooking edge cases for very short strings or strings with no repetitions.
The definition of 'repetition' might vary; ensure alignment with problem requirements.
invariants: All repetitions fully contained within the left or right subproblems are found recursively.
Crossing repetitions are identified by combining information from both subproblems using Z-function.
The `cntr` position correctly partitions the search space for crossing repetitions.
The Z-function correctly computes the longest common prefix/suffix for the relevant concatenated strings.

title: Finding repetitions
when_to_use: This algorithm efficiently finds all repetitions (periodic substrings) within a given string by combining divide-and-conquer with the Z-function. It is particularly useful for identifying overlapping or crossing periodic patterns.
description: The Main-Lorentz algorithm finds all repetitions in a string by recursively splitting it into halves and identifying 'crossing repetitions' using the Z-function. This approach achieves an optimal O(N log N) time complexity.
tips: Employ divide-and-conquer to break the problem into subproblems for repetitions within halves and crossing repetitions.
Utilize the Z-function to compute $k_1$ and $k_2$ values in O(1) for each `cntr` position, speeding up crossing repetition detection.
Remember to apply the Z-function on the reversed string to find $k_1$ values efficiently.
Handle left and right crossing repetitions symmetrically by reversing the string or swapping roles of halves.
The master theorem can be used to analyze the overall O(N log N) time complexity.
pitfalls: Correctly identifying and defining 'crossing repetitions' is the most intricate part of the algorithm.
Careful implementation of the Z-function for both the original and reversed string segments is crucial.
Ensure all edge cases for `cntr` positions and repetition lengths ($l_1, l_2$) are handled correctly.
Avoid double-counting repetitions when combining results from recursive calls and crossing repetitions.
Misinterpreting the Z-function's application for suffix matching (requiring string reversal).
invariants: For any fixed `cntr` position, all identified crossing repetitions have a total length of $2 	imes (|u| - cntr)$.
The segment $u[cntr - k_1 	ext{ to } cntr - 1]$ is identical to $u[|u| - k_1 	ext{ to } |u| - 1]$.
The segment $u[cntr 	ext{ to } cntr + k_2 - 1]$ is identical to $v[0 	ext{ to } k_2 - 1]$.

A repetition is valid if its first half's prefix length $l_1 	ext{ is } 	ext{at most } k_1 	ext{ and } 	ext{its first half's suffix length } l_2 	ext{ is } 	ext{at most } k_2, 	ext{ given } l_1 + l_2 = |u| - cntr.

title: Gauss method for solving system of linea
when_to_use: Use when solving systems of linear equations, including those over finite fields (modulo p), to find unique, multiple, or no solutions.
description: Gauss-Jordan elimination systematically transforms a system of linear equations into an equivalent, simpler form (row echelon or reduced row echelon) using elementary row operations to find solutions. This process typically has a time complexity of O(N^2 * M) for an N x M matrix.
tips: Always perform row operations on the augmented matrix (A|b) to ensure consistency.
Choose a pivot with the largest absolute value to improve numerical stability and reduce error propagation.
Remember to handle cases where a pivot element is zero by swapping rows with a non-zero element.
If solving modulo p, ensure all arithmetic operations are performed within the finite field.
The final form of the matrix directly indicates the nature of the solution set.
pitfalls: Division by zero if a pivot element is zero and no suitable row swap is found.
Numerical instability with floating-point numbers if pivots are very small, leading to precision issues.
Incorrectly handling modulo arithmetic can lead to wrong results, especially with division.
Forgetting to apply row operations consistently to the 'b' vector (augmented column).
Misinterpreting the final matrix form for cases with multiple solutions or no solutions.
invariants: After k steps, the first k columns form an identity submatrix (or a column with 1 at pivot and 0s elsewhere) in the upper-left kxk block.
The system of equations represented by the augmented matrix remains equivalent to the original system.
All rows above the current pivot row have zeros in the current pivot column.
The pivot element for the current column is non-zero (after potential row swaps).

title: Gauss method for solving system of linea
when_to_use: Use to solve systems of linear equations, determine matrix rank, or find matrix inverses. It helps analyze if a system has unique, infinite, or no solutions.
description: Gaussian elimination transforms an augmented matrix into row echelon form using elementary row operations to solve linear systems. Gauss-Jordan extends this to reduced row echelon form for direct solutions, typically with O(N^3) complexity.
tips: Use partial pivoting to improve numerical stability and avoid division by zero.
Handle floating-point precision issues carefully, using a small epsilon for comparisons.
Check for rows of all zeros with a non-zero constant term to identify no solutions.
Normalize pivot rows by dividing by the pivot element to simplify subsequent operations.
Consider scaling rows to prevent numbers from becoming excessively large or small.
Ensure all elementary row operations are applied consistently across the entire augmented matrix.
pitfalls: Encountering division by zero if a pivot element is zero and no pivoting is applied.
Numerical instability and accumulation of floating-point errors, especially without pivoting.
Incorrectly identifying the type of solution (unique, infinite, or no solution).
Forgetting to apply row operations to the constant terms column of the augmented matrix.
Misinterpreting a row of zeros with a non-zero constant as having infinite solutions.
Inefficient pivot selection can lead to slower convergence or increased error.
invariants: The solution set of the system of equations remains unchanged after each elementary row operation.
After processing column k, all elements below the pivot in that column are zero.
The rank of the augmented matrix is preserved throughout the elimination process.
The matrix is progressively transformed towards row echelon or reduced row echelon form.

title: Gauss method for solving system of linea
when_to_use: Use when determining the number of solutions (zero, one, or infinite) for a system of linear equations, or to find the unique solution if it exists. It is suitable for solving systems represented as an augmented matrix.
description: Gaussian elimination systematically transforms an augmented matrix into row echelon form using elementary row operations to solve a system of linear equations. It determines the existence and values of solutions in O(N^3) time for an N x N system.
tips: Use a small epsilon for floating-point comparisons to handle precision issues.
Implement partial pivoting to enhance numerical stability by selecting the largest absolute value.
Carefully track variable assignments to reconstruct the solution vector correctly.
Distinguish between zero, one, and infinite solutions based on the final matrix form.
The augmented matrix includes both coefficients and constant terms.
pitfalls: Failing to account for floating-point precision errors can lead to incorrect results.
Improper handling of free variables can misrepresent infinite solution sets.
Forgetting to apply row operations consistently across the entire augmented row.
Not implementing pivoting can lead to division by zero or large errors.
Incorrectly identifying inconsistent systems or systems with infinite solutions.
invariants: The submatrix formed by rows 0 to current_row - 1 is in row echelon form.
For the current column, all elements below the pivot in previous columns are zero.
The set of solutions to the system of equations remains equivalent after each elementary row operation.

title: Calculating the determinant of a matrix by Gauss
when_to_use: When needing to compute the determinant of a square matrix, especially when Gaussian elimination is a familiar or efficient approach. It is suitable for matrices where direct cofactor expansion is computationally too expensive.
description: This algorithm computes the determinant of a square matrix by transforming it into an upper triangular form using Gaussian elimination. The determinant is the product of diagonal elements, adjusted by a sign factor from row swaps, with O(N^3) complexity.
tips: Track the number of row swaps to correctly determine the determinant's sign.
Pivot selection can improve numerical stability, especially with floating-point numbers.
Operations like adding a multiple of one row to another do not change the determinant's value.
Scaling a row by a factor 'k' multiplies the determinant by 'k'.
Aim for an upper triangular matrix; a diagonal matrix is a special case.
pitfalls: Forgetting to adjust the sign after row exchanges.
Division by zero if a pivot element is zero, requiring a row swap.
Numerical instability due to floating-point precision issues without proper pivoting.
Incorrectly applying operations that change the determinant's absolute value.
Handling singular matrices (determinant is zero) gracefully.
invariants: Before processing column 'k', all elements A[i][j] where i > j and j < k are zero.
The determinant of the current matrix, multiplied by (-1)^swaps, equals the original matrix's determinant.
For each row 'i' less than the current pivot row, the pivot element A[i][i] is non-zero.
The submatrix A[k...N-1][k...N-1] represents the remaining part to be processed.

title: Calculating the determinant using Kraut method in O(N^3)
when_to_use: Use this method to compute the determinant of a square matrix efficiently by decomposing it into lower and upper triangular matrices. It is suitable for problems requiring O(N^3) complexity for matrix operations.
description: The Kraut method computes the determinant of a square matrix by first decomposing it into a lower triangular matrix (L) and an upper triangular matrix (U), such that A = LU. The determinant is then the product of the diagonal elements of L and U, achieving O(N^3) time complexity.
tips: Ensure the input matrix is square for determinant calculation.
The determinant of a triangular matrix is the product of its diagonal elements.
For A=LU, det(A) = det(L) * det(U).
The Kraut method typically sets the diagonal elements of L to 1.
Consider using partial pivoting to improve numerical stability and avoid division by zero.
pitfalls: Division by zero can occur if a pivot element is zero, requiring pivoting strategies.
Floating-point precision issues can accumulate, especially for large matrices.
Not all matrices have a unique LU decomposition without proper pivoting.
Incorrectly handling 0-indexed vs 1-indexed matrix access during implementation.
invariants: After processing column k, the first k columns of L and first k rows of U are correctly computed.
For any i <= k and j <= k, the partial sum for A[i][j] from L and U elements up to index min(i,j) is maintained.
The diagonal elements of the lower triangular matrix L are fixed to 1.
The product of the diagonal elements of L and U (after full decomposition) equals the determinant of A.

title: Calculating the determinant using Kraut method in O(N^3)
when_to_use: Use this method to efficiently calculate the determinant of a square matrix, especially when an LU decomposition is also beneficial for other linear algebra tasks like solving systems of equations.
description: The Kraut method, a specific form of LU decomposition, factorizes a square matrix A into a lower triangular matrix L (with ones on the diagonal) and an upper triangular matrix U. The determinant of A is then computed as the product of all diagonal elements of U, achieving an O(N^3) time complexity.
tips: Ensure the matrix is invertible for a unique LU decomposition without pivoting.
The diagonal elements of the L matrix are typically set to 1 for uniqueness in Kraut's method.
The determinant is directly obtained by multiplying the diagonal elements of the U matrix.
LU decomposition can also be leveraged for solving systems of linear equations efficiently.
pitfalls: Division by zero can occur if a pivot element is zero, indicating a singular matrix or requiring pivoting strategies.
Numerical instability may arise with ill-conditioned matrices when using floating-point arithmetic.
Not all matrices have an LU decomposition without row or column permutations (pivoting).
The method assumes non-zero principal minors for a unique decomposition without pivoting.
invariants: After processing column k-1, the k-1th column of L and k-1th row of U are correctly determined.
The submatrix A[0...k-1][0...k-1] is equivalent to L[0...k-1][0...k-1] * U[0...k-1][0...k-1].
All diagonal elements of L processed so far are equal to 1.
The product of U[i][i] for i from 0 to k-1 represents the determinant of the leading k x k principal minor of A.

title: Finding the rank of a matrix
when_to_use: Use when needing to determine the maximum number of linearly independent rows or columns in a matrix. It's also applicable for understanding the dimension of the vector space spanned by the matrix's rows or columns.
description: Determines the rank of a matrix by transforming it into row echelon form using Gaussian elimination. The rank is the number of non-zero rows in the echelon form, with a time complexity of O(N^3).
tips: Always choose the largest absolute value pivot for numerical stability.
Swap rows to bring a non-zero element to the pivot position.
The rank is invariant under elementary row operations.
Keep track of selected rows to avoid re-using them as pivots.
pitfalls: Floating point precision issues can lead to incorrect zero checks.
Forgetting to increment rank only when a valid pivot is found.
Incorrectly handling all-zero columns or rows during elimination.
Not considering the case where the matrix is not square.
invariants: The number of non-zero rows processed so far equals the current rank.
All elements below the pivot in the current column are zeroed out.
The submatrix formed by processed rows and columns is in row echelon form.
The rank of the original matrix is equal to the rank of the transformed matrix.

title: Binomial Coefficients
when_to_use: Use when needing to calculate multiple binomial coefficients or when a single coefficient is required and N and K are not excessively large, allowing for table precomputation.
description: This method computes binomial coefficients C(n, k) using Pascal's recurrence relation C(n, k) = C(n-1, k-1) + C(n-1, k), typically building a table. It has a time complexity of O(N*K) or O(N^2) for building the full triangle up to N.
tips: Intermediate results for Pascal's Triangle never exceed the final answer, simplifying overflow checks if not using modular arithmetic.
Each new table element requires only one addition, making it computationally efficient per element.
Space optimization is possible by only storing the previous row to compute the current row, reducing space complexity to O(N).
Modular arithmetic should be applied at each addition step if results can exceed standard integer limits.
pitfalls: Building the entire Pascal's Triangle for a single C(N, K) can be slow for large N and K, as it computes many unnecessary values.
Directly computing C(N, K) using factorials can lead to overflow issues for large N, even if the final result fits.
Incorrectly handling base cases C(n, 0) = 1 and C(n, n) = 1 can propagate errors throughout the table.
For very large N and K where only a single value is needed, other methods like prime factorization or Lucas Theorem might be more efficient.
invariants: C[i][0] is always 1 for any row i.
C[i][i] is always 1 for any row i.
For any j such that 0 < j < i, C[i][j] stores the sum of C[i-1][j-1] and C[i-1][j].
All computed values C[r][c] for r < current_row are correct binomial coefficients.

title: Binomial Coefficients
when_to_use: Use when you need to compute many binomial coefficients, especially for values up to a certain N, or when working with modulo arithmetic where division is not straightforward.
description: This method computes binomial coefficients using Pascal's identity, building a 2D DP table where C(n, k) = C(n-1, k-1) + C(n-1, k). It has a time complexity of O(N^2) for computing all coefficients up to N.
tips: Optimize space by storing only the current and previous row of the DP table.
For O(1) lookup after precomputation, calculate all factorials and inverse factorials modulo a prime.
When modulo is not prime, use prime factorization and Lucas theorem or extended Euclidean algorithm for inverses.
pitfalls: Direct division for C(n,k) = n! / (k!(n-k)!) is problematic with modulo arithmetic unless the modulo is prime.
Integer overflow can occur for large N and K if not handled with appropriate data types or modulo operations.
Incorrectly applying Pascal's identity boundary conditions, especially for k=0 or k=n.
invariants: For each cell dp[i][j], it stores the value of C(i, j).
At the start of computing row i, all values for row i-1 are correctly calculated and available.
The base cases dp[i][0] = 1 and dp[i][i] = 1 are correctly initialized for all i.

title: Binomial Coefficients
when_to_use: When you need to calculate the number of ways to choose k items from n distinct items, often under modular constraints.
description: Binomial coefficients represent the number of ways to choose k items from a set of n distinct items. They can be computed using Pascal's identity or factorials, with complexity varying based on the method and modulo requirements.
tips: Use Pascal's triangle for small n and k values.
Precompute factorials and inverse factorials for larger n modulo a prime.
Apply Lucas Theorem for very large n and k modulo a prime p.
Utilize dynamic programming to memoize intermediate results.
pitfalls: Overflow with large factorials if not using modular arithmetic.
Division by zero when computing inverse modulo if modulus is not prime.
Incorrectly applying Lucas Theorem for non-prime moduli.
High time complexity for naive factorial calculation without precomputation.
invariants: For Pascal's triangle, dp[i][j] stores C(i, j).
For factorial method, fact[i] stores i! % M.
For factorial method, invFact[i] stores (i!)^(-1) % M.
C(n, k) = C(n-1, k-1) + C(n-1, k) (Pascal's identity).

title: Binomial Coefficients
when_to_use: Use when calculating combinations (n choose k) efficiently, especially for large n, and when results are required modulo a prime or prime power.
description: This algorithm computes binomial coefficients C(n, k) modulo a given prime m or prime power p^b. It leverages precomputed factorials and their modular inverses to achieve O(1) query time after O(MAXN) or O(MAXN log m) precomputation.
tips: Precompute factorials modulo m up to MAXN.
Precompute modular inverses of factorials for O(1) query time.
Utilize Fermat's Little Theorem for modular inverse when modulus is prime.
For prime power moduli, handle cases where p divides k or n-k separately.
pitfalls: Forgetting to take modulo at each multiplication step during factorial precomputation.
Using Fermat's Little Theorem for modular inverse when the modulus is not prime.
Incorrectly handling cases where the prime p divides k! or (n-k)! when computing modulo a prime power.
Not considering edge cases like k=0 or k=n.
invariants: Factorials `fact[i]` are correctly computed as `i! % m`.
Modular inverse factorials `invFact[i]` satisfy `(fact[i] * invFact[i]) % m == 1`.
All intermediate products are taken modulo `m` to prevent overflow.

title: Binomial Coefficients
when_to_use: Use when calculating binomial coefficients C(n, k) modulo a composite number M, especially when M is not prime and direct modular inverse computation for factorials is not possible.
description: This technique computes binomial coefficients modulo an arbitrary composite number M by factoring M into prime powers. For each prime power, it calculates prime exponents in factorials and combines coprime parts, then uses Chinese Remainder Theorem. Precomputation is O(N log N) or O(N), with queries in O(log M) or O(1).
tips: Precompute factorials and their prime exponents up to N using dynamic programming.
Apply Legendre's formula to efficiently find the exponent of a prime 'p' in 'x!'.
Handle each prime power factor of the modulus 'M' independently.
Combine the results obtained for each prime power using the Chinese Remainder Theorem.
Ensure modular inverses are computed only for numbers coprime to the current modulus.
The 'g(x)' values (factorials without prime 'p' factors) are coprime to 'p'.
pitfalls: Failing to correctly factorize the modulus 'M' into its prime power components.
Incorrectly computing modular inverses when a number is not coprime to the modulus.
Errors in applying Legendre's formula or calculating the 'g(x)' values.
Issues with combining intermediate results using the Chinese Remainder Theorem.
Forgetting to handle cases where the final exponent of a prime 'p' makes the coefficient zero modulo 'M'.
Integer overflow if intermediate values are not taken modulo the appropriate prime power.
invariants: For any `i <= N`, `c[i]` correctly stores the exponent of prime `p` in `i!`.
For any `i <= N`, `g[i]` correctly stores `(i! / p^c[i]) % (p^b)`.
The intermediate result for each prime power `p^b` is accurate before CRT combination.

title: Binomial Coefficients
when_to_use: Use when computing C(n, k) modulo m where n is large and m is small, especially when m is prime, a prime power, or a composite number with small prime factors.
description: This technique computes binomial coefficients C(n, k) modulo m, particularly when n is very large and m is small. It leverages modular arithmetic properties, such as Lucas's Theorem for prime moduli or the Chinese Remainder Theorem for composite moduli, achieving complexities like O(m + log_m n).
tips: Precompute factorials and inverse factorials modulo m for efficient base case calculations.
Apply Lucas's Theorem when the modulus m is prime to break down the problem recursively.
Utilize the Chinese Remainder Theorem for composite, square-free moduli by combining results from prime factors.
Consider generalized Lucas's theorem or specialized factorial modulo P methods for prime power moduli.
pitfalls: Failing to handle k > n correctly, which should yield 0.
Incorrectly applying Lucas's Theorem when the modulus is not prime.
Errors in computing modular inverses, especially for non-prime moduli or when a factor is not coprime to modulus.
Ignoring intermediate overflows if not taking modulo at each step for large factorials.
Assuming m is always prime; composite moduli require different strategies like CRT.
invariants: For C(N, K) mod M, the recursive calls C(N/M, K/M) and C(N%M, K%M) are valid subproblems.
All factorial and inverse factorial values used for C(x, y) where x, y < M are precomputed modulo M.
The result of each C(x, y) calculation is always within [0, M-1].
If K > N, the binomial coefficient C(N, K) is always 0.

title: Catalan Numbers
when_to_use: Use when counting specific combinatorial objects that exhibit a recursive structure, such as balanced parentheses, binary trees, or non-crossing partitions. It applies to problems where items can be split into two independent subproblems.
description: Catalan numbers are a sequence of natural numbers appearing in various combinatorial problems, often involving recursively defined objects. They are computed via a recurrence relation or a direct formula, typically achieving O(N^2) with DP or O(N) for the direct formula.
tips: Recognize the underlying recursive structure that splits a problem into two smaller, independent subproblems.
Memorize the first few Catalan numbers (1, 1, 2, 5, 14, 42, 132) to quickly identify the pattern.
The direct formula C(n) = (2n)! / ((n+1)! * n!) is often more efficient for larger N if factorials can be precomputed.
Dynamic programming is a straightforward way to compute values iteratively for smaller N.
pitfalls: Forgetting the base case C(0) = 1, which is crucial for the recurrence relation.
Integer overflow when using the direct factorial formula for large N; modular arithmetic or large number libraries may be required.
Incorrectly applying the recurrence relation, especially the summation bounds.
Confusing Catalan numbers with other combinatorial sequences like Bell numbers.
invariants: C[0] is initialized to 1, representing the base case for an empty sequence.
For any k > 0, C[k] stores the k-th Catalan number.
The sum C[j] * C[k-1-j] correctly accumulates terms for C[k] based on the recurrence.
All intermediate products and sums are handled to prevent overflow, especially for large N.

title: Catalan Numbers
signals: sum of products of previous terms
when_to_use: Use when counting arrangements of objects that follow a specific non-crossing or balancing rule, often involving recursive decomposition or paths constrained by a diagonal.
description: Catalan numbers count the number of ways to arrange certain combinatorial objects, such as balanced parentheses sequences or monotonic paths that do not cross a diagonal. They can be computed using a recurrence relation (O(N^2) with DP) or a direct binomial coefficient formula (O(N) or O(N log N)).
tips: Memoize the recursive solution to avoid redundant computations, transforming it into dynamic programming.
For large N, use modular arithmetic if results are required modulo a prime to prevent integer overflow.
Precompute factorials and their modular inverses for efficient calculation of binomial coefficients.
Recognize common combinatorial problems that are isomorphic to Catalan number definitions.
pitfalls: Forgetting to correctly handle the base cases, typically C0 = 1 and C1 = 1.
Encountering integer overflow when calculating large binomial coefficients without appropriate modular arithmetic.
Inefficiently recalculating binomial coefficients multiple times instead of precomputing or optimizing.
Misidentifying a problem as Catalan when its constraints or structure differ slightly.
invariants: dp[i] stores the i-th Catalan number, correctly computed from dp[0] through dp[i-1].
For each n, the sum sum(dp[k] * dp[n-1-k]) iterates through all valid partitions of the problem.
The base cases dp[0] and dp[1] are correctly initialized to 1.

title: The Inclusion-Exclusion Principle
signals: number of solutions with upper bounds
when_to_use: Use when calculating the size of a union of multiple sets or the probability of at least one event occurring, especially when direct counting is difficult due to overlapping conditions.
description: The Inclusion-Exclusion Principle computes the size of a union of sets by summing individual set sizes, then alternately subtracting and adding the sizes of intersections of increasing numbers of sets. It often involves iterating through subsets of properties, leading to exponential time complexity.
tips: Identify the 'properties' or 'conditions' that define the sets.
Break down the problem into calculating sizes of intersections of various subsets of properties.
Use bitmasks to iterate through all subsets of properties efficiently.

title: The Inclusion-Exclusion Principle

description: The Inclusion-Exclusion Principle is a combinatorial technique to count the size of a union of sets by summing the sizes of individual sets, then adjusting for intersections. For problems involving 'k' conditions, optimized versions can achieve O(2^N * N) complexity, where N is the total number of conditions.

title: The Inclusion-Exclusion Principle

title: The Inclusion-Exclusion Principle
when_to_use: Use when needing to count elements satisfying "at least one" of several properties, especially when direct counting is complicated by overlapping sets. It's also applicable for counting elements satisfying "none" of the properties by subtracting from the total.
description: The Inclusion-Exclusion Principle is a combinatorial method to determine the size of the union of multiple finite sets. It works by summing the sizes of individual sets, subtracting the sizes of all pairwise intersections, adding back the sizes of all three-way intersections, and so on, with alternating signs. This approach typically has a complexity ranging from O(N log N) for number theory applications to O(2^K * N) for K properties.
tips: Clearly define the universe and the properties (sets) you are interested in.
Systematically list all combinations of properties for intersection terms.
The sign for an intersection of `k` properties is `(-1)^(k-1)`.

Exponential complexity (2^K) can be prohibitive for many properties.

For the `cnt[]` array, `cnt[x]` correctly accumulates the sum/subtract terms for all relevant factors of `x`.

title: Burnside's lemma / Pólya enumeration theorem
when_to_use: Use when you need to count the number of unique arrangements or configurations of objects, considering that some arrangements are equivalent under a set of symmetry operations.
description: Burnside's Lemma and Pólya Enumeration Theorem are combinatorial techniques used to count the number of distinct configurations of objects under a given group of symmetry operations. They achieve this by averaging the number of fixed points for each permutation in the group, typically resulting in polynomial or exponential time complexity depending on the group's structure.
tips: Identify all invariant permutations relevant to the object's definition.
Manually find basic permutations from which all other permutations can be generated.
Carefully define what constitutes an 'object' and its 'representation' for the problem.
Understand the underlying group of symmetries acting on the objects.
pitfalls: Missing some invariant permutations in the symmetry group.
Incorrectly defining equivalence between different object representations.
Errors in calculating the number of fixed points for each permutation.
Overlooking the computational complexity of generating or analyzing all permutations for large groups.
invariants: The set of objects is closed under all permutations in the symmetry group.
The identity permutation is always present in the group and fixes every object.
Two objects are equivalent if and only if one can be transformed into the other by some permutation in the group.
The total count of distinct objects equals the sum of fixed points for each permutation, divided by the size of the group.

title: Burnside's lemma / Pólya enumeration theorem
when_to_use: Use when counting the number of distinct configurations or patterns that are considered equivalent under a given set of symmetries or group actions. It helps enumerate non-isomorphic structures.

Pólya Enumeration Theorem extends this to count configurations based on specific properties, often involving polynomial coefficients.

For each group element, determine its cycle decomposition to count fixed points.
Pólya Enumeration Theorem extends Burnside's for counting with specific properties (e.g., colorings).
Remember that fixed points are configurations unchanged by a specific group action.
The sum of fixed points is divided by the order of the group.
pitfalls: Misidentifying the elements of the permutation group or their actions.

Forgetting to divide the sum of fixed points by the total number of group elements.

invariants: The set of orbits forms a partition of the original set of objects.

The number of distinct orbits is equal to (1/|G|) * sum(fixed_points(g)) for all g in G.

title: Stars and bars
signals: distribute N items into K containers
when_to_use: When counting the number of ways to distribute 'n' identical items into 'k' distinct containers, or finding the number of non-negative integer solutions to an equation x1 + ... + xk = n.
description: A combinatorial technique to count arrangements of identical items into distinct bins, or non-negative integer solutions to a sum, by modeling them as permutations of 'n' stars and 'k-1' bars. It typically has a time complexity of O(1) after binomial coefficient precomputation, or O(min(n, k)) for direct calculation.
tips: Clearly identify 'n' (identical objects) and 'k' (distinct bins/variables).
Remember the formula: C(n + k - 1, k - 1) or C(n + k - 1, n).

Consider transformations if constraints are not non-negative (e.g., x_i >= 1).
pitfalls: Confusing identical objects with distinct objects.
Incorrectly identifying 'n' or 'k' in the problem statement.
Forgetting to adjust 'n' or 'k' if variables have lower bounds other than zero.
Using the wrong binomial coefficient formula (e.g., C(n, k)).
Overflow issues when calculating large binomial coefficients.
invariants: The total number of stars remains 'n' throughout the arrangement.
The total number of bars remains 'k-1' throughout the arrangement.
Each unique arrangement of stars and bars corresponds to exactly one distribution.
Each valid distribution corresponds to exactly one arrangement of stars and bars.

The function f(x) remains unimodal within the current interval [l, r].
The length of the search interval (r - l) strictly decreases in each iteration.
The lower bound l is always less than or equal to the upper bound r (l <= r).

title: Binary search
when_to_use: When searching for a specific element in a sorted collection or finding an optimal value over a monotonic function within a defined range. It efficiently reduces the search space by half in each step.
description: A search algorithm that finds the position of a target value within a sorted array or determines a value that satisfies a monotonic predicate. It operates in logarithmic time complexity, O(log N).
tips: Ensure the search space exhibits a monotonic property.
Carefully define the `low` and `high` bounds for the search interval.
Use `mid = low + (high - low) / 2` to prevent integer overflow.
For 'binary-search-answer', define a clear `check(x)` predicate function.
Handle edge cases like empty arrays or when the target is not found.
pitfalls: Incorrectly updating `low` or `high` bounds leading to infinite loops.
Off-by-one errors in array indexing or loop conditions.
Assuming the input is sorted when it is not.
Integer overflow when calculating `mid` with `(low + high) / 2` for large bounds.
Not correctly handling the final result when the loop terminates.
invariants: The potential answer always lies within the current search interval `[low, high]`.
All elements strictly less than `low` have been determined not to be the answer.
All elements strictly greater than `high` have been determined not to be the answer.

title: Binary search
when_to_use: When searching for the extremum (maximum or minimum) of a unimodal function over a continuous or discrete interval. It is efficient for functions where direct differentiation or analytical solutions are complex.
description: Ternary search is an algorithm that efficiently finds the extremum of a unimodal function within a given interval by repeatedly narrowing the search space. It typically achieves a logarithmic time complexity, O(log N), where N is the interval size.
tips: Ensure the function is strictly unimodal within the search interval.
Handle discrete intervals carefully, especially the stopping condition (r - l) < 3.
Consider Golden Section Search to reduce function evaluations per iteration.
Define the search interval [l, r] accurately.
Use appropriate precision for floating-point comparisons.
pitfalls: Applying to non-unimodal functions yields incorrect results.
Incorrect stopping conditions can lead to infinite loops or suboptimal answers.
Floating-point precision errors can cause issues with interval comparisons.
High constant factor due to two function evaluations per step (unless using Golden Section).
Off-by-one errors in discrete interval handling.
invariants: The extremum of the unimodal function always lies within the current search interval [l, r].
The function value at m1 is compared with m2 to narrow the interval while preserving unimodality.
l < m1 < m2 < r (for continuous case, or l <= m1 < m2 <= r for discrete with r-l >= 3).
The search interval [l, r] strictly decreases in size with each iteration.

title: Ternary Search
when_to_use: Use when finding the extremum (maximum or minimum) of a unimodal function over a continuous interval, where the function strictly increases then decreases, or vice versa.
description: Ternary search is an optimization algorithm that efficiently finds the extremum (maximum or minimum) of a unimodal function over a continuous interval. It operates in logarithmic time, O(log N), by repeatedly narrowing the search space.
tips: For floating-point functions, use a fixed number of iterations (e.g., 100-300) instead of an epsilon threshold for robustness.
The two comparison points m1 and m2 should divide the interval into three roughly equal parts for optimal convergence.
The case f(m1) == f(m2) can be merged with either f(m1) < f(m2) or f(m1) > f(m2) to simplify logic.
Ensure the function is truly unimodal within the given search range; otherwise, the result may be incorrect.
pitfalls: Applying ternary search to a non-unimodal function will not guarantee finding the global extremum.
Floating-point precision errors can lead to incorrect comparisons or infinite loops if epsilon is too small.
Incorrectly updating l or r based on f(m1) and f(m2) can exclude the actual extremum.
For integer domains, careful handling of m1, m2 and loop termination is crucial to avoid off-by-one errors.
The extremum might be at the boundaries l or r, which the algorithm naturally handles if the interval is correctly updated.
invariants: The global extremum of the unimodal function is always contained within the current interval [l, r].
The function f(x) remains unimodal on the interval [l, r] throughout the search process.
The length of the interval (r - l) strictly decreases with each iteration.
l <= m1 < m2 <= r holds for the chosen comparison points within the interval.

title: Ternary Search
when_to_use: Use when searching for the minimum or maximum of a unimodal function over a continuous interval. It efficiently narrows down the search space by evaluating the function at two internal points.
description: Ternary search is an optimization algorithm for finding the extremum (minimum or maximum) of a unimodal function within a given interval. It repeatedly reduces the search interval by two-thirds, achieving logarithmic time complexity with respect to the interval size.
tips: Carefully define the search interval [L, R] for the function.
Choose two internal points, typically L + (R-L)/3 and R - (R-L)/3.
Compare function values at the two internal points to narrow the interval.
Use a fixed number of iterations or a small epsilon for termination.
Ensure the function is truly unimodal within the search range.
pitfalls: Applying to non-unimodal functions yields incorrect results.
Floating-point precision issues can lead to infinite loops or incorrect bounds.
Incorrectly choosing internal points can slow convergence.
Terminating too early might miss the true extremum.
May be slower than binary search for discrete unimodal arrays if the function is simple.
invariants: The global extremum of the unimodal function within the initial range is always contained within the current interval [L, R].
The interval [L, R] is non-empty and its length strictly decreases in each iteration.
L <= R is maintained throughout the search process.

title: Ternary Search
when_to_use: Use Ternary Search to find the minimum or maximum of a unimodal or bitonic function over a continuous or discrete interval. It is effective when the function's derivative is hard to compute or unavailable.
description: Ternary search is a divide and conquer algorithm that repeatedly narrows down the search space for the extremum of a unimodal function by evaluating two points within the current interval. It reduces the search interval by one-third in each step, achieving O(log N) time complexity.
tips: Ensure the function is strictly unimodal or bitonic within the search range.
Handle floating-point precision carefully for continuous functions.
Adjust interval division for integer arguments to avoid infinite loops.
Consider the number of iterations needed for desired precision.
Compare f(m1) and f(m2) to decide which third of the interval to discard.
pitfalls: Applying to non-unimodal functions yields incorrect results.
Floating-point errors can lead to incorrect bounds or infinite loops.
Incorrectly handling integer ranges can cause off-by-one errors.
Premature termination due to insufficient precision.
Not checking boundary conditions for the extremum.
invariants: The extremum of the unimodal function lies within the current interval [l, r].
The function value at m1 is compared with m2 to narrow the search.
The interval [l, r] strictly decreases in size with each iteration.
l <= m1 < m2 <= r is maintained throughout the search.

title: Ternary Search
when_to_use: Use when searching for the extremum (maximum or minimum) of a unimodal function over a continuous or discrete interval. It efficiently narrows down the search space by evaluating the function at two internal points.
description: Ternary search is a divide and conquer algorithm that finds the extremum of a unimodal function within an interval by repeatedly reducing the search space by approximately one-third. It has a logarithmic time complexity, similar to binary search, but requires two function evaluations per step.
tips: Handle discrete intervals by checking remaining points when interval size is small.
Consider Golden Section Search to reduce function evaluations per iteration.
Ensure the function is strictly unimodal within the search interval.
Choose division points to be l + (r-l)/3 and r - (r-l)/3 for simplicity.
Be mindful of floating point precision when dealing with continuous domains.
pitfalls: Incorrectly applying to non-unimodal functions.
Infinite loops if stopping condition for discrete intervals is not handled.
Precision issues with floating-point arithmetic for continuous domains.
High constant factor due to two function evaluations per step (unless Golden Section).
Stopping too early or too late can lead to incorrect results or inefficiency.
invariants: The extremum of the unimodal function lies within the current interval [l, r].
The function value at m1 is compared with the function value at m2.
The interval [l, r] strictly shrinks in each iteration.
l <= m1 < m2 <= r is maintained throughout the search.

title: Newton's method for finding roots
when_to_use: Use when seeking to find a root of a continuous and differentiable function, especially when a good initial estimate is available and fast, quadratic convergence is desired.
description: Newton's method is an iterative root-finding algorithm that refines an initial guess by repeatedly approximating the function with its tangent line and finding its x-intercept. It typically exhibits quadratic convergence, making it very efficient for smooth functions.
tips: Provide a good initial guess x0 to ensure convergence to the desired root.
Implement a stopping criterion based on the absolute change in x or f(x) being sufficiently small.
Include a maximum iteration limit to prevent infinite loops or divergence.
Consider using a hybrid method for robustness, e.g., bisection if Newton's method fails.
pitfalls: Divergence can occur if the initial guess is poor or the derivative is near zero.
Requires the function to be both continuous and differentiable.
May converge to a different root than intended if multiple roots exist.
Division by zero if the derivative f'(x_i) is exactly zero at an iteration.
Can oscillate or cycle if the function has local extrema or inflection points.

other: Function f(x) must be continuous and differentiable

invariants: The current approximation x_i is updated based on the tangent at x_i.
The absolute value of f(x_i) tends towards zero as iterations proceed (if converging).
The derivative f'(x_i) is non-zero at each step of the iteration.
The sequence of approximations x_0, x_1, ..., x_k is generated by the Newton-Raphson formula.

title: Integration by Simpson's formula
when_to_use: Use when approximating definite integrals of functions that are sufficiently smooth over a given interval. It is effective for achieving good accuracy with a moderate number of subintervals.
description: Simpson's formula approximates a definite integral by dividing the integration interval into an even number of subintervals and approximating the function over each pair of subintervals with a parabola. This method offers a higher order of accuracy compared to simpler rules, typically achieving O(N^4) accuracy for N subintervals.
tips: Ensure the total number of subintervals (2n) is always even for the composite rule.
The weights for function evaluations follow a specific 1-4-2-4-...-2-4-1 pattern.
Choose a sufficiently large 'n' to achieve the desired accuracy, considering the function's fourth derivative.
The formula can be implemented iteratively by summing terms, avoiding explicit recursion.
pitfalls: Incorrectly applying the weighting coefficients, especially at the boundaries or interior points.
Using an odd number of subintervals, which invalidates the parabolic approximation for the composite rule.
Large approximation errors if the function is not sufficiently smooth (e.g., has sharp turns or discontinuities).
Floating-point precision issues can arise with extremely small subinterval widths or very large 'n'.
invariants: The sum accumulates weighted function values for all processed subintervals.
The subinterval width 'h' remains constant across the entire integration range.
The total number of subintervals considered for approximation is always an even number.
Each segment of length 2h is approximated by a parabola passing through its endpoints and midpoint.

title: Simulated Annealing
when_to_use: Use when seeking a near-optimal solution for complex optimization problems with many local optima, especially when exact solutions are computationally infeasible or too slow.
description: Simulated Annealing is a probabilistic metaheuristic for global optimization, inspired by the annealing process in metallurgy. It explores the search space by accepting worse solutions with a probability that decreases over time, aiming to escape local optima. Its typical time complexity is O(iterations * cost_of_neighbor_generation).
tips: Start with a sufficiently high initial temperature to allow broad exploration.
Choose a slow cooling schedule (decay rate) for better solution quality, at the cost of longer runtime.
Carefully define the neighborhood function to ensure effective state transitions.
Design the energy function accurately to reflect the problem's objective.
Run multiple trials with different random seeds to assess solution robustness.
pitfalls: Getting stuck in local minima if the cooling schedule is too fast.
Excessive runtime if the cooling schedule is too slow or initial temperature is too high.
Poor performance or convergence with an ill-defined neighborhood function.
Difficulty in tuning parameters (initial temperature, decay rate) for optimal results.
Not guaranteed to find the true global optimum, only a good approximation.
invariants: The `s_best` state always represents the lowest energy found across all iterations so far.
The temperature `T` is always non-negative and decreases monotonically with each iteration.
Any proposed `s_next` state is a valid neighbor of the current state `s` according to the defined neighborhood function.
The probability of accepting a higher-energy state decreases as `T` approaches zero.

title: Basic Geometry
when_to_use: Use when analyzing relationships between vectors, such as angles, projections, or orthogonality, especially when vector components are provided. It is fundamental for many geometric algorithms and simulations.
description: Basic geometry involves understanding fundamental vector operations like the dot product, which combines two vectors to produce a scalar. This operation is crucial for calculating angles, projections, and determining orthogonality, typically with O(D) complexity for D dimensions.
tips: Always use the algebraic definition (sum of products of components) for precise calculations.
Remember the geometric interpretation (magnitude product times cosine of angle) for conceptual understanding.
The dot product is commutative and distributive over vector addition.
Unit vectors simplify calculations involving direction and normalization.
Can be used to determine if two vectors are orthogonal (dot product is zero).
pitfalls: Confusing the scalar dot product with the vector cross product.
Incorrectly applying dot product properties to non-vector quantities.
Forgetting that the result of a dot product is always a scalar, not a vector.
Assuming vectors are always 2D or 3D; the concept extends to N dimensions.
Floating point precision issues can affect angle or orthogonality checks.
invariants: The dot product of any two vectors A and B is always a scalar value.
The commutative property A.B = B.A holds for all vector pairs.
The calculated dot product sum(A[i] * B[i]) accurately represents the geometric relationship.
All vectors involved in a dot product operation must have the same dimensionality.

title: Basic Geometry
when_to_use: When problems involve spatial relationships between points, lines, and planes, or require calculations of angles, distances, areas, and volumes using vector algebra.
description: Basic geometry applies vector algebra and coordinate systems to solve problems related to points, lines, and planes. It typically uses fundamental vector operations like dot and cross products, often with O(1) or O(N) complexity for simple calculations.
tips: Understand the geometric interpretation of dot and cross products.
Use vector subtraction to represent vectors between two points.
Normalize vectors when only direction matters for calculations.
Leverage properties like distributivity and anti-commutativity for simplification.
Consider edge cases such as collinear or parallel vectors.
Use the z-component of the 2D cross product for orientation checks.
pitfalls: Forgetting the right-hand rule for cross product direction in 3D.
Integer overflow with large coordinate values during intermediate calculations.
Floating-point precision issues when comparing distances or angles.
Incorrectly applying vector identities or properties.
Confusing 2D and 3D vector operations and their implications.
Division by zero when attempting to normalize a zero vector.
invariants: For any two vectors A and B, A ⋅ B = |A| |B| cos(theta).
For any two vectors A and B, |A x B| = |A| |B| sin(theta).
The vector (A x B) is orthogonal to both A and B.
The sum of two vectors A and B results in a vector that forms the diagonal of the parallelogram defined by A and B.

title: Basic Geometry
when_to_use: When calculating the area of a parallelogram formed by two vectors, determining the orientation of points, or finding a vector perpendicular to two given vectors in 3D space.
description: The cross product calculates a vector perpendicular to two input vectors, whose magnitude equals the area of the parallelogram they form. It also has a 2D analogue (pseudo-scalar product) for signed area calculation, typically O(1) complexity.
tips: Remember the right-hand rule for 3D cross product direction.
The cross product is anti-commutative: a x b = -(b x a).
Use the determinant form for easier calculation in 3D.
The 2D pseudo-scalar product (a.x * b.y - a.y * b.x) gives signed area.
A zero cross product magnitude implies parallel vectors.
pitfalls: Confusing cross product with dot product.
Incorrectly applying 2D pseudo-scalar product in 3D or vice-versa.
Forgetting the order of operands affects the sign of the result.
Floating point precision issues when checking for zero magnitude.
Assuming commutativity, which is false for cross product.
invariants: The resulting vector is always perpendicular to both input vectors.
The magnitude of the cross product equals the area of the parallelogram formed by the input vectors.
For 2D vectors, the pseudo-scalar product's sign indicates orientation.
If input vectors are parallel, their cross product is the zero vector.

title: Basic Geometry
when_to_use: Use when solving problems involving fundamental geometric entities like points, lines, and planes, or when performing basic vector operations to determine relationships or intersections.

It primarily involves algebraic manipulation of vector equations, typically with O(1) complexity for basic operations on fixed-dimension vectors.

Use vector equations to define lines and planes concisely.
Algebraic methods can simplify complex geometric problems.

Leverage Cramer's rule for solving systems of linear equations.

Errors in algebraic manipulation of vector equations.
Numerical precision issues with floating-point geometry calculations.

title: Finding the equation of a line for a segment
when_to_use: Use when the problem asks to count or list all possible combinations, permutations, or arrangements of elements. It's suitable when a brute-force check of all possibilities is feasible.
description: Enumeration systematically generates and examines all possible solutions to a problem. This approach guarantees finding the correct answer if one exists, typically with exponential time complexity O(k^N) or O(N!).
tips: Clearly define the search space and the properties of valid solutions.
Use recursion or iterative loops to explore all branches of the solution space.
Prune invalid branches early to optimize performance if possible.
Consider using bitmasks for enumerating subsets of small N.

Generating duplicate solutions if not handled carefully.
Exceeding time limits due to exponential complexity for large inputs.
Incorrectly defining the base case or termination condition in recursive approaches.

title: Finding the equation of a line for a segment
when_to_use: Use this method when you need to represent a line segment's equation using integer coefficients, especially to avoid floating-point inaccuracies in geometric computations.
description: This method derives the general form of a line equation (Ax + By + C = 0) from two points, ensuring integer coefficients if input coordinates are integers. It then normalizes these coefficients using GCD to achieve a unique representation, typically in O(log(max(|A|,|B|,|C|))) time due to GCD.
tips: Always compute A, B, C using the given formulas for P(Px, Py) and Q(Qx, Qy).
Use absolute values of A, B, C when calculating their greatest common divisor.
Normalize the sign of coefficients (e.g., A>0 or A=0, B>0) for a canonical form.
This approach is robust against floating-point precision errors common in geometry.
pitfalls: Forgetting to normalize coefficients leads to non-unique representations for the same line.
Incorrectly handling signs during the final normalization step can result in inconsistent forms.
Dividing by zero if all A, B, C are zero (e.g., when input points P and Q are identical).
GCD calculation can be computationally intensive for extremely large coefficient values.
invariants: The calculated coefficients A, B, C always satisfy A*Px + B*Py + C = 0 for point P(Px, Py).
After GCD division, the absolute values of A, B, C are coprime (unless all are zero).
After normalization, the first non-zero coefficient (A, or B if A=0) is positive.
The line defined by Ax + By + C = 0 passes through both given points P and Q.

title: Finding the equation of a line for a segment
when_to_use: Use this technique when representing and comparing lines in geometric algorithms, especially with real numbers, to improve numerical stability and reduce rounding errors.
description: This method normalizes line equation coefficients (Ax + By + C = 0) by dividing them by sqrt(A^2 + B^2) to ensure A^2 + B^2 = 1. This improves numerical precision and enables unique line representation in O(1) time.
tips: Always normalize line coefficients when working with real numbers.
Normalization helps in comparing lines robustly.
Consider an additional sign normalization for truly unique representations.
Use a small epsilon (ε) for floating-point comparisons.
Normalization scales coefficients to a manageable range.
pitfalls: Not normalizing can lead to significant rounding errors.
Comparing unnormalized lines directly is unreliable.
Large coordinate values amplify errors without normalization.
Division by zero if A and B are both zero (degenerate case).
Forgetting sign normalization can result in two representations for the same line.
invariants: The normalized coefficients (A', B', C') define the same line as the original (A, B, C).
The sum of squares of the first two normalized coefficients, A'^2 + B'^2, is approximately 1.
The magnitude of normalized A' and B' coefficients is always less than or equal to 1.

title: Finding the equation of a line for a segment
when_to_use: Use this method when a simple Cartesian equation is insufficient, especially in 3D or higher dimensions, to represent a line or line segment. It is ideal for defining all points along a specific direction from a starting point.
description: This method defines a line using a starting point and a direction vector, allowing all points on the line to be generated by scaling the vector and adding it to the point. It provides a robust representation for lines in any dimension, with O(1) complexity for constructing the equation.
tips: Choose one segment endpoint as the base point 'p'.
Calculate the direction vector 'v' by subtracting the coordinates of the base point from the other endpoint.
For a line segment, restrict the parameter 't' to the range [0, 1].
The direction vector can be normalized for consistent magnitude, though not strictly required for line definition.
pitfalls: Forgetting to constrain the parameter 't' to [0, 1] when defining a segment, resulting in an infinite line.
Incorrectly calculating the direction vector 'v' (e.g., subtracting endpoints in the wrong order).
Confusing the parametric form with other line representations, such as implicit equations.
Attempting to derive slope-intercept form from a vertical line in 2D, which is undefined.
invariants: The direction vector 'v' remains constant for a given line.
The base point 'p' is always on the line.
For a segment, any point generated with 't' in [0, 1] lies within the segment.
The equation P(t) = p + v*t always yields a point on the line.

title: Intersection Point of Lines
when_to_use: When given two lines in general form, use this to find their unique intersection point or determine if they are parallel. It is applicable for geometric problems involving line interactions.

Implement robust floating-point comparisons for parallelism.
Consider using a small epsilon for numerical stability.
Handle the case of coincident lines separately if needed.
pitfalls: Division by zero if the lines are parallel or coincident.
Floating-point precision errors leading to incorrect parallelism checks.
Misinterpreting coincident lines as merely parallel.
Numerical instability with extreme coefficient values.

If the lines are not parallel, a unique intersection point (x, y) exists.
The calculated (x, y) coordinates satisfy both original line equations.

title: Intersection Point of Lines
when_to_use: When you need to find the exact coordinates where two lines meet in a 2D plane, or determine if they are parallel or coincident. This is useful for geometric problems involving line segments or polygons.
description: This method calculates the coordinates of the intersection point of two lines, typically represented in general form (Ax + By + C = 0), by solving a system of linear equations. It has a constant time complexity, O(1), for each pair of lines.

Handle edge cases for parallel and coincident lines explicitly to avoid division by zero.
Use floating-point numbers for coordinates, but be mindful of precision issues.
Consider using cross products for vector-based line representations to simplify calculations.
pitfalls: Division by zero if lines are parallel or coincident without proper checks.
Floating-point precision errors can lead to incorrect intersection points or misidentification of parallel lines.
Incorrectly handling vertical lines if using slope-intercept form (m = undefined).
Assuming lines are infinite; for line segments, additional checks are needed.
invariants: The coefficients (a, b, c) for each line remain constant throughout the calculation.
If the determinant of the coefficient matrix is non-zero, a unique intersection point exists.
The calculated (x, y) coordinates satisfy both line equations.

title: Check if two segments intersect
when_to_use: Use this when determining if two line segments in a 2D plane share any common points, especially when floating-point precision issues need to be avoided.

Utilize cross products for efficient orientation checks.
Check for 1D overlap of projections for collinear segments.
Employ integer arithmetic throughout to prevent precision issues.

Introducing floating-point errors by using division for slopes.
Missing edge cases where segments touch only at an endpoint.
Incorrectly implementing orientation tests (e.g., cross product sign).

If segments are not collinear, each segment's endpoints must lie on opposite sides of the line containing the other segment.
The orientation test consistently determines the relative position of points.
All intermediate calculations involving coordinates remain within integer limits.

title: Finding intersection of two segments
when_to_use: When determining if two line segments in 2D space cross, touch, or overlap, and to find the nature of their intersection (point, segment, or empty).

Handle collinear segments as a distinct special case.
Check segment endpoints for edge cases where they touch.
Utilize cross products to efficiently determine point orientation.
Consider floating point precision issues for robust comparisons.

Floating point inaccuracies leading to false positives or negatives.
Missing edge cases where segments touch only at endpoints.
Not distinguishing between a single point and a segment as intersection.
Overlooking the performance benefits of a bounding box check.

If segments are collinear, their intersection is either empty, a point, or a sub-segment.
The orientation of endpoints relative to the other segment determines potential crossing.
Each segment's endpoints are distinct.

title: Finding intersection of two segments
when_to_use: When determining if two line segments cross each other, or finding the exact point where they meet. This is useful in computational geometry problems involving shapes or paths.

For any two segments, the algorithm correctly distinguishes between intersection, parallelism, and collinearity.

title: Finding intersection of two segments
when_to_use: Use this to determine if two line segments intersect and, if so, find the intersection point or the overlapping segment. It is useful for collision detection and computational geometry problems.
