title: Circle-Line Intersection
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to find the intersection points between a circle and a line, especially when numerical stability is a concern. It efficiently determines zero, one, or two intersection points.
description: This algorithm finds the intersection points of a circle and a line by using geometric properties like distances and vectors, avoiding direct algebraic system solving. It determines zero, one, or two intersection points in constant time, O(1).
tips: Translate the circle to the origin for simplified calculations, adjusting the line equation accordingly.
Use the distance from the origin to the line to quickly determine the number of intersection points.
Leverage perpendicular and collinear vectors to find the closest point and the intersection points.
Prioritize geometric methods for improved numerical stability compared to direct algebraic solutions.
pitfalls: Floating point precision errors can accumulate, especially with very small or large coordinates.
Incorrectly handling edge cases where the line is tangent to the circle or passes through the origin.
Division by zero if A^2 + B^2 is zero, indicating an invalid line equation (A=0 and B=0).
Forgetting to translate the circle and line back to their original positions if the problem requires it.

other: Line equation Ax+By+C=0 implies A and B are not both zero
Circle is centered at origin (or translated to origin)

invariants: The distance from the origin to the line, d0, is always correctly calculated as |C| / sqrt(A^2 + B^2).
The point (x0, y0) is always the closest point on the line Ax+By+C=0 to the origin (0,0).
The number of intersection points (0, 1, or 2) is correctly determined by comparing d0 with the circle's radius r.
If two intersection points exist, their distance d from (x0, y0) along the line satisfies d^2 = r^2 - d0^2.

title: Check if point belongs to the convex pol
when_to_use: This algorithm is used to efficiently determine if a given query point lies inside or on the boundary of a convex polygon, especially when many queries are performed on the same polygon.
description: The core idea is to decompose the convex polygon into triangles originating from a reference vertex. Binary search is then used to find the specific triangle that could contain the query point, followed by a point-in-triangle check. This approach achieves O(log N) query time after initial O(N) preprocessing.
tips: Choose a consistent reference point, typically the lexicographically smallest vertex, to simplify angular sorting.
Ensure polygon vertices are ordered (e.g., counter-clockwise) relative to the reference point for correct binary search behavior.
Utilize cross products for efficient orientation tests and area calculations to determine point-line and point-triangle relationships.
Translate all points so the reference point is at the origin (0,0) to simplify vector calculations.
Handle special cases for points lying on edges or vertices explicitly to avoid misclassification.
pitfalls: Floating point precision errors can lead to incorrect results for collinear points or boundary checks; prefer integer arithmetic with cross products.
Incorrectly handling boundary conditions (points exactly on an edge or vertex) can lead to false negatives or positives.
Assuming the polygon is convex or correctly ordered when it is not, will lead to incorrect results.
Integer overflow when computing cross products if coordinates are large; use 64-bit integers for intermediate calculations.
Failing to check if the point lies within the initial angular bounds of the polygon (between p0p1 and p0pn) before binary searching.
invariants: The reference point p_0 is the lexicographically smallest vertex of the polygon.
All polygon vertices p_1, ..., p_n are ordered counter-clockwise by polar angle around p_0.
During binary search, the query point p lies within the angular sector formed by p_0p_1 and p_0p_n.
If the point is inside, it must be contained within or on the boundary of the triangle p_0, p_i, p_{i+1} found by binary search.

title: Breadth-first search
when_to_use: Use when you need to find the shortest path (in terms of number of edges) from a source node to all other reachable nodes in an unweighted graph. It is also suitable for exploring a graph layer by layer.
description: BFS systematically explores a graph level by level, starting from a source node, ensuring all nodes at a given distance are visited before moving to the next distance level. It operates in O(N + M) time for N vertices and M edges.
tips: Always use a queue to maintain the order of nodes to visit.
Utilize a 'visited' array or set to prevent revisiting nodes and infinite loops.
Can be adapted to find all connected components by iterating over unvisited nodes.
Useful for finding all nodes reachable within a certain number of steps.
pitfalls: Does not find shortest paths in weighted graphs unless weights are 0 or 1 (use Dijkstra for general weighted graphs).
Forgetting to mark nodes as visited can lead to incorrect results or TLE.
Can consume significant memory for graphs with high branching factors due to queue size.
Incorrectly handling disconnected components if not explicitly checking for unvisited nodes.
invariants: All nodes currently in the queue are at the same or adjacent distance level from the source.
The 'used' array accurately reflects all nodes that have been added to the queue for processing.
Nodes are processed in non-decreasing order of their shortest path distance (edge count) from the source.

title: Floyd-Warshall Algorithm
signals: shortest path between all vertex pairs
when_to_use: Use when you need to find the shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in a weighted graph. It is suitable for graphs with negative edge weights but no negative cycles.
description: The Floyd-Warshall algorithm finds all-pairs shortest paths in a weighted graph using dynamic programming. It iteratively considers all possible intermediate vertices, updating path lengths in O(V^3) time.
tips: Initialize distances with edge weights or infinity, and 0 for self-loops.
The outermost loop iterates through intermediate vertices (k).
Can detect negative cycles if a vertex's distance to itself becomes negative.
Use a predecessor matrix to reconstruct the actual shortest paths.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling infinity values, especially with negative weights (e.g., infinity - 1).
Not checking for negative cycles, which can lead to incorrect shortest path values.
Using it on sparse graphs where other algorithms like repeated Dijkstra's might be faster.
Incorrect loop order (k must be the outermost loop for intermediate vertices).
invariants: After k iterations, dist[i][j] contains the shortest path from i to j using only vertices 0 to k-1 as intermediate nodes.
dist[i][j] is always non-increasing with each iteration of k.
If a negative cycle exists, dist[v][v] will eventually become negative for some vertex v.

title: Number of paths of fixed length / Shortest paths of fixed length
signals: paths don't have to be simple
when_to_use: Use when needing to count the number of paths of a specific length between all pairs of vertices in a graph, or to find reachability in a fixed number of steps.
description: This algorithm leverages matrix exponentiation to find the number of paths of a fixed length `k` between all pairs of vertices in a graph. Each matrix multiplication step extends path lengths by one, resulting in an O(N^3 log K) time complexity for an N-vertex graph.
tips: Represent the graph as an adjacency matrix where `G[i][j]` is the number of direct edges.
Matrix multiplication `C_k * G` yields `C_{k+1}` for path counts.
Use binary exponentiation (exponentiation by squaring) for `G^k` to optimize computation.
Can be adapted for shortest paths by redefining matrix multiplication using min-plus algebra.
Handles multiple edges and self-loops naturally by summing edge counts.
pitfalls: Standard matrix multiplication is O(N^3), leading to O(N^3 log K) overall time complexity.
Memory usage is O(N^2) for storing matrices, which can be prohibitive for very large graphs.
This method counts all paths, including those with repeated vertices or edges, not simple paths.
Path counts can grow very large, potentially overflowing standard integer types for large `k`.
Adapting for shortest paths requires careful handling of initial matrix values and 'infinity'.
invariants: After `m` matrix multiplications, `C_m[i][j]` stores the number of paths of length `m` from `i` to `j`.
The initial adjacency matrix `G` remains constant throughout the exponentiation process.
Each element `C_k[i][j]` is a non-negative integer representing a path count.
All intermediate matrices maintain dimensions of `N x N`.

title: Circle-Circle Intersection
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to find the common points where two circles intersect on a 2D plane, handling cases from no intersection to infinite points.
description: This algorithm determines the intersection points of two circles by algebraically transforming their equations into a system involving one circle and one line. It typically achieves O(1) time complexity for finding the intersection points.
tips: Translate one circle to the origin to simplify the equations.
Subtract the two circle equations to derive a linear equation (the radical axis).
Handle floating-point precision carefully for comparisons and calculations.
Consider edge cases such as tangent circles or coincident centers separately.
Leverage a pre-existing robust circle-line intersection function.
pitfalls: Numerical instability due to floating-point arithmetic, especially for tangent cases.
Forgetting to translate the resulting intersection points back to the original coordinate system.
Incorrectly handling degenerate cases like concentric circles with different radii.
Potential division by zero if the derived line equation coefficients are both zero.
Missing edge cases where circles are tangent, resulting in only one intersection point.
invariants: The relative positions and radii of the circles remain unchanged after coordinate translation.
The derived linear equation represents the radical axis of the two circles.
Any intersection point satisfies both original circle equations and the derived line equation.
The distance from the first circle's center to the derived line determines the number of intersections.

title: Finding common tangents to two circles
when_to_use: Use this when you need to find all lines that are simultaneously tangent to two given circles. It is applicable in computational geometry problems requiring the precise relative positioning of circles and lines.
description: This algorithm systematically determines the equations of all common tangent lines to two circles, typically identifying up to four distinct lines. It involves geometric calculations based on circle centers and radii, operating in constant time, O(1).
tips: Separate calculations for external and internal tangents.
Carefully consider the relative positions of circle centers and radii.
Utilize vector algebra or trigonometry for line equation derivation.
Normalize circle equations or coordinates to simplify calculations.
Account for cases where circles have zero radius (points).
pitfalls: Overlooking degenerate cases like coincident or concentric circles.
Numerical instability or precision errors with floating-point arithmetic.
Incorrectly handling scenarios where circles intersect or one is contained within another.
Failing to identify all possible tangents, especially in edge cases.
invariants: The distance between the two circle centers remains constant.
The radii of both circles are immutable during the tangent calculation.
Any valid common tangent line maintains a specific perpendicular distance to each circle's center, equal to its radius.
The geometric relationship between the two circles (e.g., intersecting, separate) is preserved.

title: Finding common tangents to two circles
when_to_use: When needing to find lines that are tangent to two given circles simultaneously. This method is suitable for problems requiring precise geometric line calculations.
description: This algorithm finds common tangent lines to two circles by formulating the problem as a system of algebraic equations based on distance constraints. It involves solving a quadratic equation for line coefficients, typically resulting in constant time complexity after initial setup.
tips: Normalize the line equation (a^2 + b^2 = 1) to ensure unique representations.
Shift one circle to the origin for simpler calculations, then shift the resulting lines back.
Systematically consider the four cases for opening modulus signs (±r1, ±r2).
Handle degenerate cases where circles might overlap, touch, or be identical.
pitfalls: Forgetting to normalize the line equation can lead to non-unique solutions.
Incorrectly handling the modulus signs (±r1, ±r2) can miss valid tangents.
Numerical precision issues can arise with floating-point calculations.
Not accounting for special cases like one circle completely inside another.
Selecting redundant solutions from the quadratic formula without proper filtering.
invariants: The distance from the line ax + by + c = 0 to the first circle's center is r1.
The distance from the line ax + by + c = 0 to the second circle's center is r2.
The line coefficients a and b satisfy a^2 + b^2 = 1 for normalization.
All found lines are tangent to both circles.

title: Length of the union of segments
when_to_use: Use when calculating the total length or measure of the union of multiple one-dimensional intervals or segments. It efficiently handles overlapping regions to avoid double-counting.
description: This algorithm calculates the total length of the union of given line segments by processing sorted endpoints as events. It maintains a counter for active segments, adding the length of intervals where the counter is positive, achieving O(N log N) time complexity.
tips: Represent endpoints as (coordinate, type) pairs, where type indicates start (+1) or end (-1).
Sort event points primarily by coordinate, then by type (e.g., start events before end events for same coordinate).
Maintain a counter for the number of currently active segments.
Only add the length of an interval to the total if the active segment counter is greater than zero.
Initialize the total length and active segment counter to zero before processing events.
pitfalls: Incorrect sorting order for event points, especially for coincident endpoints.
Forgetting to update the active segment counter when processing an event.
Off-by-one errors when calculating segment lengths between consecutive event points.
Not handling the case where no segments are active (counter is zero) for an interval.
Potential for integer overflow if coordinates or total length can be very large.
invariants: The `active_segments_count` accurately reflects the number of segments covering the interval `[prev_x, current_x]`.
The `total_union_length` accumulates the length of all intervals where `active_segments_count > 0`.
All event points processed up to the current iteration are correctly sorted by coordinate.
`prev_x` always stores the coordinate of the last processed event point.

title: Oriented area of a triangle
when_to_use: Use when determining the orientation of three 2D points (clockwise, counterclockwise, or collinear) or calculating the signed area of a triangle formed by them. It's crucial for geometric algorithms like convex hull construction and point-in-polygon tests.
description: Calculates the signed area of a triangle defined by three 2D points, indicating their orientation relative to each other. A positive sign typically means counterclockwise, negative means clockwise, and zero indicates collinearity. This operation has O(1) time complexity.
tips: The sign of the result directly indicates the orientation of the points.
The absolute value of the result gives the standard unsigned area of the triangle.
This concept is fundamental for many computational geometry algorithms, such as convex hull.
Can be efficiently computed using a 2x2 determinant or a 2D cross product formula.
Ensure consistent point ordering (e.g., p1, p2, p3) for predictable sign interpretation.
pitfalls: Floating-point precision issues can lead to incorrect collinearity checks or orientation.
Inconsistent definitions of 'clockwise' vs. 'counterclockwise' can flip the sign interpretation.
Misinterpreting a zero area as an error instead of collinear points.
Incorrectly assuming the order of points does not affect the sign of the result.
invariants: The sign of the result correctly indicates the orientation of p1, p2, p3 (e.g., positive for counter-clockwise).
If the points are collinear, the calculated oriented area is exactly zero.
The absolute value of the result equals half the area of the parallelogram formed by vectors (p2-p1) and (p3-p1).

title: Finding area of simple polygon in O(N)
when_to_use: Use when calculating the area of a simple polygon given its vertices in order. It is efficient for both convex and non-convex polygons.
description: This algorithm calculates the area of a simple polygon by summing signed trapezoidal areas formed by each edge and the x-axis, or equivalently, using the shoelace formula. It achieves an optimal O(N) time complexity, where N is the number of vertices.
tips: Ensure vertices are provided in a consistent order (clockwise or counter-clockwise).
Use a data type with sufficient range (e.g., long long or double) for intermediate sums to prevent overflow.
The absolute value of the final sum, after dividing by two, is the polygon's area.
The sign of the final sum indicates the polygon's winding order (positive for CCW, negative for CW).
Consider the cross product formulation for robustness and conciseness.
pitfalls: Incorrect vertex order will result in an incorrect signed area or even zero.
Integer overflow can occur if coordinates are large and sums exceed standard integer limits.
Floating point precision issues may arise with extremely large or small coordinate values.
Applying this method to self-intersecting (complex) polygons will yield an incorrect area.
Forgetting to divide the accumulated sum by two at the end.
invariants: Vertices are processed sequentially in their given order, forming edges.
The running sum `current_sum` accumulates the signed contribution from each edge processed so far.
Each edge `(V_i, V_{i+1})` contributes a signed area to the total sum.
The final accumulated sum represents twice the signed area of the polygon.

title: Finding area of simple polygon in O(N)
when_to_use: When you need to calculate the area of a simple polygon given its vertices in sequential order. It efficiently handles both convex and non-convex polygons.
description: This algorithm calculates the area of a simple polygon by summing the signed areas of trapezoids or triangles formed by its edges and a reference point/axis. It achieves O(N) time complexity.
tips: Ensure vertices are provided in sequential order (clockwise or counter-clockwise).
The absolute value of the final result is the polygon's area.
Use 64-bit integers for intermediate sums to prevent overflow with large coordinates.
The sign of the result indicates the polygon's orientation (e.g., positive for CCW, negative for CW).
pitfalls: Supplying vertices in a non-sequential or arbitrary order will yield an incorrect area.
Applying the formula to self-intersecting polygons will not give the true geometric area.
Forgetting to divide the final accumulated sum by two.
Floating-point precision errors can accumulate with many vertices or extreme coordinates.
invariants: The sum accumulates the signed area contribution of processed edges.
The current vertex and the next vertex define an edge of the polygon.
All vertices are visited exactly once in sequence.
The final sum, divided by two, represents the total signed area.

title: Minkowski sum of convex polygons
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to compute the Minkowski sum of two convex polygons efficiently. It is ideal for applications requiring the union of shapes or collision detection between convex objects.
description: This algorithm computes the Minkowski sum of two convex polygons by merging their edge vectors, sorted by polar angle, into a new sequence of edges. The resulting sum polygon is also convex and can be constructed in O(|P| + |Q|) time.
tips: Pre-sort polygon vertices to start from the lowest Y-coordinate to ensure consistent orientation.
Orient both input polygons counter-clockwise for correct edge vector generation.
Use cross products for robust angle comparisons with integer coordinates, avoiding floating-point errors.
The resulting Minkowski sum polygon will always be convex.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling polygon orientation (e.g., mixing clockwise and counter-clockwise).
Precision issues when using floating-point numbers for angle comparisons or vector additions.
Not correctly merging co-directed vectors, leading to redundant vertices or incorrect shape.
Failure to handle the cyclic nature of polygons, especially when finding the starting vertex.
invariants: The current vertex of the resulting sum polygon is the sum of the current vertices from P and Q.
All edges processed so far from both input polygons have been added to the output in polar angle order.
The partial Minkowski sum polygon constructed maintains convexity.
The total number of edges in the output polygon is equal to the sum of edges from the input polygons.

title: Minkowski sum of convex polygons
when_to_use: When needing to compute the sum of two convex polygons, or efficiently determine the minimum distance or intersection between them.
description: The Minkowski sum of two convex polygons P and Q is a new convex polygon formed by vector addition of all points from P and Q. It can be computed in O(|P| + |Q|) time by merging their edge vectors.
tips: The vertices of P+Q are sums of vertices of P and Q.
The edges of P+Q are the edges of P and Q, sorted by angle.
Reflecting Q to -Q transforms distance(P, Q) to distance(origin, P + (-Q)).
The resulting polygon is always convex if the input polygons are convex.
Use a rotating calipers-like approach to merge sorted edge vectors.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling edge cases like collinear edges or degenerate polygons.
Floating point precision issues in geometric calculations.
Forgetting to reflect one polygon when calculating distance/intersection.
Incorrectly sorting edge vectors by angle.
Not handling the origin being inside the resulting polygon for distance calculation.
invariants: The current edge added to the Minkowski sum is the one with the smallest angle among the remaining edges from both polygons.
The sequence of edges in the Minkowski sum maintains convexity.
All points in the resulting polygon P+Q can be expressed as p+q where p is in P and q is in Q.
The sum of the number of vertices of P and Q equals the number of vertices of P+Q (for non-degenerate cases).

title: Pick's Theorem
when_to_use: Use when the problem asks for the total number of arrangements, combinations, or possibilities. It applies to scenarios requiring enumeration of valid configurations.
description: Counting involves determining the total number of possible outcomes or configurations that satisfy given conditions. It often uses combinatorics, probability, or dynamic programming, typically with polynomial time complexity.
tips: Break down complex counting problems into smaller, independent subproblems.
Use the multiplication principle for sequential choices and addition principle for mutually exclusive cases.
Consider using generating functions or recurrence relations for structured counting.
Apply inclusion-exclusion for overlapping sets.
Simplify modulo operations at each step to prevent overflow.
Look for symmetry to reduce the search space.
pitfalls: Double-counting or missing cases, especially with complex conditions.
Incorrectly applying permutations vs. combinations (order matters vs. order doesn't matter).
Forgetting modulo operations, leading to integer overflow.
Misinterpreting problem constraints or definitions.
Overlooking edge cases like empty sets or single elements.
Incorrectly handling dependent events.
invariants: For a given step, all valid sub-configurations are correctly enumerated.
The count accumulated so far represents the total valid ways for processed elements.
Each distinct valid configuration is counted exactly once.
Intermediate results remain within representable integer limits (or are correctly modulo-ed).

title: Lattice points inside non-lattice polygon
when_to_use: Use this when you need to count integer coordinate points strictly inside a polygon whose vertices are not restricted to integer coordinates. It's suitable for problems requiring precise geometric counting with non-lattice boundaries.
description: This algorithm counts lattice points within a polygon with arbitrary vertices by summing signed contributions from each edge. It reduces the problem to evaluating sums of floor functions, sum(floor(kx+b)), which is solved recursively, typically achieving logarithmic complexity per edge.
tips: Decompose the polygon into signed areas under each edge, similar to the shoelace formula.
Handle edge cases where k or b are negative by appropriate transformations.
The core recursive sum sum(floor(kx+b)) can be optimized using properties of the floor function.
Ensure correct handling of points on the boundary (e.g., exclude points on x=n and y=0 initially, then add manually).
Use long double or fractions for k and b to maintain precision if coordinates are large.
pitfalls: Floating point precision issues when calculating k and b or during transformations.
Incorrectly handling boundary points (points on edges or vertices).
Off-by-one errors in summation ranges or coordinate transformations.
Not accounting for negative k or b values correctly in the sum(floor(kx+b)) function.
Division by zero if x2 - x1 is zero (vertical edge), which requires special handling.
invariants: The sum sum(floor(kx+b)) correctly accumulates points below the current edge segment.
After coordinate transformations, the problem reduces to a sum over a non-negative range with non-negative k and b.
Each recursive call to sum(floor(kx+b)) operates on a smaller range or with simplified coefficients.
The total count of lattice points is the sum of signed contributions from each polygon edge.

title: Lattice points inside non-lattice polygon
when_to_use: Use when counting pairs or elements satisfying divisibility or GCD conditions. Also applicable for summing functions over pairs using number theory properties.
description: This approach leverages number theoretic properties like GCD, LCM, and prime factorization to efficiently count elements or pairs satisfying specific criteria. It often involves iterating through divisors or multiples, achieving complexities like O(N log N) or O(sqrt N) per query.
tips: Precompute primes or divisors using a sieve for efficiency.
Utilize properties of GCD and LCM, such as gcd(a,b) * lcm(a,b) = a * b.
Consider Euler's totient function (phi) for problems involving coprime counts.
Apply inclusion-exclusion principle for complex counting conditions.
Factorization can simplify problems involving products or powers.
pitfalls: Overcounting or undercounting when using inclusion-exclusion.
Integer overflow with large products or sums.
Incorrectly handling edge cases like 0 or 1 in GCD/LCM calculations.
Inefficient factorization or divisor enumeration for large numbers.
Forgetting to handle modulo arithmetic for large results.
invariants: All numbers processed so far have their relevant number-theoretic properties (e.g., prime factors, divisors) correctly determined.
The current count or sum accurately reflects all valid combinations found up to the current iteration.
Intermediate results for GCD/LCM calculations maintain correctness based on Euclidean algorithm properties.

title: Convex Hull construction
when_to_use: Use when you need to find the smallest convex polygon that encloses a given set of points in a plane. It is fundamental for various geometric algorithms and optimization problems.
description: Convex hull construction identifies the subset of points that form the vertices of the smallest convex polygon containing all given points. Common algorithms achieve this in O(N log N) time complexity.
tips: Sort points by X-coordinate (and Y for ties) as a common preprocessing step.
Consider algorithms like Graham Scan or Monotone Chain for efficiency.
Handle collinear points carefully, especially when defining 'extreme'.
Use cross product to determine the orientation of three points.
Remove duplicate points before processing to simplify logic.
pitfalls: Floating point precision issues can lead to incorrect orientation calculations.
Incorrectly handling collinear points can result in non-minimal hulls or errors.
Edge cases with very few points (e.g., 0, 1, 2 points) require special handling.
Off-by-one errors in loop bounds or stack/deque operations are common.
Performance degradation with naive O(N^3) algorithms for large N.
invariants: The sequence of points currently forming the hull maintains a convex property.
All points considered up to the current iteration are either part of the hull or lie within the polygon defined by the hull.
The orientation of any three consecutive points on the hull (e.g., using cross product) indicates a consistent turn (e.g., counter-clockwise).

title: Convex Hull construction
when_to_use: Used to find the smallest convex polygon that encloses a given set of points. It is applied when the outer boundary of a point set is required.
description: Constructs the smallest convex polygon containing all given points, typically using algorithms like Graham's scan or Monotone Chain. These algorithms achieve an optimal time complexity of O(N log N).
tips: Handle collinear points carefully, especially during sorting and turn checks.
Choose a robust orientation test (e.g., cross product) to determine turns.
Normalize coordinates or use high-precision types for floating-point geometry.
Pre-sort points by X then Y to easily find the initial pivot (bottom-most, left-most).
Consider edge cases like all points being collinear or having only a few points.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling collinear points can lead to non-convex hulls or missing points.
Floating-point precision issues can cause incorrect turn determinations.
Off-by-one errors in stack manipulation or loop bounds.
Not correctly breaking ties during polar angle sorting (e.g., by distance).
Failure to remove points that are inside the hull but collinear with an edge.
invariants: The points on the stack, from bottom to top, form a convex chain.
For any three consecutive points A, B, C on the stack (A below B, B below C), the turn from A to B to C is always a left turn or straight.
All points processed up to the current iteration are either part of the current convex hull candidate or lie to its right (inside).
The initial pivot point (P0) is always the first element on the stack.

title: Convex Hull construction
when_to_use: When you need to find the smallest convex polygon enclosing a given set of points. It's used for problems requiring the outermost boundary of a point set.
description: Convex Hull construction algorithms, like Graham Scan or Monotone Chain, find the smallest convex polygon that encloses a given set of points. These algorithms typically have a time complexity of O(N log N) due to sorting.
tips: Use an orientation test (cross product) to determine if points make a clockwise or counter-clockwise turn.
Handle collinear points carefully, especially if they need to be included in the hull.
Sorting points by angle or coordinate is a common initial step for many algorithms.
The Monotone Chain algorithm builds upper and lower hulls separately.
A stack is crucial for maintaining the current hull candidates and removing non-convex turns.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling collinear points can lead to an incomplete or incorrect hull.
Floating-point precision issues can affect orientation tests, leading to wrong turns.
Edge cases with very few points (e.g., 0, 1, 2 points) need special consideration.
Forgetting to sort points or sorting them incorrectly can break the algorithm's logic.
Not clearing the stack or handling the wrap-around condition correctly can result in an invalid hull.
invariants: The points currently on the stack form a convex chain in the desired direction.
For any three consecutive points A, B, C on the stack, the turn from AB to BC maintains convexity.
All points processed up to the current point are either part of the current convex chain or lie to its 'inside'.

title: Convex hull trick and Li Chao tree
when_to_use: Use when the optimal solution for a subproblem depends only on solutions to smaller, contiguous subproblems. It's effective for optimizing sequential decision-making or finding optimal values in arrays.
description: Dynamic Programming 1D solves problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation. It typically achieves O(N) or O(N log N) time and O(N) or O(1) space complexity.
tips: Clearly define the state: what does dp[i] represent for the subproblem?
Establish correct base cases for the smallest subproblems.
Formulate the recurrence relation based on previous states.
Consider space optimization if dp[i] only depends on a few preceding states.
Ensure the iteration order computes subproblems before they are needed.
pitfalls: Incorrectly defining the state or recurrence relation.
Missing or incorrect base cases, leading to incorrect results.
Off-by-one errors in array indexing or loop bounds.
Forgetting to initialize DP table values, especially for min/max problems.
Not considering edge cases for small input sizes.
invariants: dp[i] correctly stores the optimal solution for the subproblem ending at index i.
All dp[j] for j < i have been computed and are correct.
The recurrence relation correctly transitions from dp[j] to dp[i].

title: Convex hull trick and Li Chao tree
when_to_use: When optimizing dynamic programming states that involve minimizing or maximizing linear functions, especially when slopes are monotonic or queries can be processed offline.
description: Convex Hull Trick optimizes dynamic programming problems by maintaining a lower (or upper) convex hull of linear functions. It allows efficient querying of the minimum (or maximum) value of these functions at a given point, typically achieving O(N log N) or O(N) complexity.
tips: Sort lines by slope for O(N) or O(N log N) insertion.
Use a deque for O(N) CHT when both slopes and query points are monotonic.
Consider point-line duality for geometric interpretation.
Pre-process all lines if queries are offline.
Ensure correct handling of parallel lines or identical slopes.
pitfalls: Incorrectly assuming monotonicity of slopes or query points.
Floating point precision issues when dealing with intersections.
Forgetting to reverse slope/query order for maximization problems or upper hulls.
Edge cases with few lines or identical lines.
Complexity increases to O(N log N) per query for non-monotonic queries without Li Chao tree.
invariants: The set of active lines always forms a lower (or upper) convex hull.
Lines within the hull are ordered by their slopes.
Intersection points of adjacent lines in the hull are monotonically increasing (or decreasing).

title: Convex hull trick and Li Chao tree
when_to_use: Used to find the minimum (or maximum) value among a set of linear functions at a given point. It is particularly efficient for dynamic sets of lines or when slopes are monotonic.
description: The Convex Hull Trick (CHT) and Li Chao Tree efficiently find the minimum (or maximum) value of a set of linear functions at a query point. CHT typically achieves O(log N) or O(1) amortized time, while Li Chao Tree offers O(log C) for updates and queries.
tips: Ensure slopes are monotonic for efficient CHT implementations.
Li Chao Tree handles non-monotonic slopes but requires a coordinate range.
Use geometric primitives like cross products for line intersection logic.
Initialize Li Chao tree nodes with a default "infinite" line.
For online CHT with non-monotonic slopes, consider square-root decomposition.
pitfalls: Floating point precision issues can lead to incorrect intersection calculations.
Incorrectly managing the convex hull structure (e.g., deque) in CHT.
Forgetting to recursively pass the "upper" function in Li Chao Tree.
Li Chao Tree's coordinate range might necessitate a dynamic segment tree.
Edge cases with parallel lines or vertical lines in CHT.

other: functions intersect at most once (for Li Chao Tree)

invariants: In CHT, the lower envelope of lines forms a convex shape, with slopes monotonically ordered.
In Li Chao Tree, each node stores a line that is optimal at the segment's midpoint for lines added to that node.
For any point, the minimum value is guaranteed to be found by evaluating lines on the path from root to the corresponding leaf in a Li Chao Tree.

title: Search for a pair of intersecting segments
when_to_use: When needing to efficiently detect if any two line segments among a given set intersect, or to find one such intersecting pair.
description: This algorithm efficiently determines if any two line segments in a given set intersect by processing events along a sweep line, typically achieving O(N log N) time complexity.
tips: Represent segments by their endpoints as event points, sorted by x-coordinate.
Use a balanced binary search tree (or similar structure) to maintain active segments ordered by their y-coordinate at the sweep line.
Handle horizontal and vertical segments as special cases or with robust comparison logic.
When an intersection is found, the algorithm can terminate early.
pitfalls: Floating point precision issues can lead to incorrect intersection detection or ordering.
Degenerate cases like collinear segments or multiple segments intersecting at a single point.
Incorrectly defining or sorting event points can lead to missed intersections.
Inefficient data structure for active segments will degrade performance from O(N log N).
invariants: All segments fully to the left of the sweep line have been processed and do not intersect each other.
The active set contains all segments currently intersecting the sweep line, ordered by their y-coordinate.
No intersection has been found among segments whose right endpoints are to the left of the sweep line.

title: Search for a pair of intersecting segments
when_to_use: To find if any two line segments in a given set intersect, or to identify all such intersecting pairs. It is particularly efficient for problems involving geometric objects that can be ordered along a sweep axis.
description: This algorithm processes geometric objects by sweeping a conceptual line across the plane, maintaining an ordered data structure of objects intersecting the line. It efficiently detects intersections by only checking adjacent objects in the sweep line's current state, achieving an O(N log N) time complexity.
tips: Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., `std::set`) to maintain segments on the sweep line, ordered by their y-coordinate.
Define event points (segment endpoints) and process them in increasing x-coordinate order.
When adding a segment, check for intersection with its immediate upper and lower neighbors in the sweep line structure.
When removing a segment, check for intersection between its former upper and lower neighbors.
Carefully handle segments that start or end at the same x-coordinate by defining a consistent tie-breaking rule for event points.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling vertical segments, which might not intersect the sweep line in a single point or have ambiguous y-order.
Floating point precision issues when comparing y-coordinates or intersection points, leading to incorrect ordering or missed intersections.
Not correctly updating the order of segments in the data structure when their relative y-order changes between event points.
Overlooking edge cases like multiple segments starting/ending at the exact same coordinates.
Using an inefficient data structure for maintaining segments on the sweep line, degrading the O(N log N) complexity.
invariants: The sweep line always moves from left to right, processing event points in strictly increasing x-coordinate order.
The data structure (e.g., balanced BST) always contains all segments currently intersecting the sweep line, ordered by their y-coordinate at the sweep line's current x-position.
Any two intersecting segments that are neighbors in the data structure at their intersection point will be detected.
All segments whose right endpoint is to the left of the current sweep line position have been fully processed for intersections.

title: Search for a pair of intersecting segments
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to efficiently determine if any pair of line segments in a given set intersect. It is particularly effective for detecting geometric overlaps in a 2D plane.
description: This algorithm processes segments by sweeping a vertical line across the plane, maintaining an ordered set of active segments to efficiently check for intersections with neighbors. It achieves an optimal time complexity of O(N log N).
tips: Process events at the same x-coordinate by adding segments before removing them to catch vertex intersections.
Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., `std::set`) to maintain active segments, ordered by their y-coordinate.
Intersection checks only need to be performed between adjacent segments in the active set.
Handle floating point comparisons with a small epsilon (EPS) to account for precision errors.
Vertical segments can be treated as starting and ending at the same x-coordinate, with any y-coordinate from their range.
pitfalls: Incorrect event ordering (e.g., removing before adding) can lead to missing intersections at shared endpoints.
Floating point precision issues can cause incorrect intersection detection or ordering of segments.
Using an inefficient data structure for active segments will degrade the algorithm's O(N log N) performance.
Failing to update neighbor relationships in the active set after adding or removing a segment.
Incorrectly handling edge cases like collinear segments or segments that perfectly overlap.
invariants: The sweep line always moves from left to right, processing events in increasing x-coordinate order.
The set of active segments contains all segments currently intersected by the sweep line, ordered by their y-coordinate at the sweep line's current x-position.
Any two segments that intersect must be adjacent in the active set at some point when the sweep line passes over their intersection.
All segments starting at the current x-coordinate are added to the active set before any segments ending at the current x-coordinate are removed.

title: Finding faces of a planar graph
when_to_use: Used to identify and enumerate all faces (regions) of a given planar graph, typically when its geometric embedding is known or can be derived.
description: This algorithm finds all faces of a planar graph by traversing edges in a specific angular order around each vertex. It achieves a time complexity of O(N log N) due to sorting, or O(N) if edges are pre-sorted.
tips: Sort adjacent edges by polar angle for each vertex.
Mark directed edges as visited to ensure each face is found once.
Precompute next edges for O(1) lookup during traversal.
The outer face is traversed in the opposite direction (e.g., counter-clockwise) compared to inner faces.
pitfalls: Errors in polar angle sorting, especially for collinear edges.
Failing to correctly mark directed edges as visited, leading to infinite loops or duplicate faces.
Floating-point precision issues when comparing angles.
Incorrectly identifying the outer face if traversal direction logic is flawed.
Assuming the graph is connected; disconnected components need separate processing.
invariants: Every directed edge is part of exactly one face boundary traversal.
At any vertex u, the next edge (u, w) is chosen such that it is immediately counter-clockwise to the incoming edge (v, u) in u's adjacency list.
The sequence of edges traversed forms a closed cycle, representing a face boundary.
Each face of the planar graph is identified exactly once.

title: Finding faces of a planar graph
when_to_use: Use when needing to identify the distinct regions (faces) bounded by edges in a planar graph, especially when the graph is constructed from intersecting line segments.
description: This algorithm identifies the distinct regions, or faces, of a planar graph. It can either process an explicitly given graph or construct one from a set of intersecting line segments, typically involving geometric sorting and traversal with a complexity often around O(V log V + E).
tips: Handle floating point comparisons with an epsilon for coordinates.
Store points in a map to ensure unique vertices for identical coordinates.
Sort intersection points lexicographically for consistent edge creation.
Distinguish between inner (CCW) and outer (CW) face traversals.
Binary search by angle can efficiently find the next edge from a vertex.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling floating point precision for intersection points.
Failing to merge coincident vertices from different segment intersections.
Errors in determining the winding order (CW/CCW) for faces.
Inefficiently finding intersections or sorting points.
Not properly handling degenerate cases like collinear segments or multiple intersections at one point.
invariants: Every edge in the graph belongs to exactly two faces (or one, if it's on the outer face boundary).
For any vertex, the edges incident to it are sorted angularly, enabling consistent face traversal.
The Euler's formula (V - E + F = 2 for connected planar graphs) holds for the constructed graph and its faces.
Each face boundary is a simple cycle of edges in the graph.

title: Point location in O(logn)
when_to_use: To efficiently determine which region (face) of a given planar subdivision a query point belongs to, especially when handling multiple queries offline.
description: This algorithm preprocesses a planar subdivision using a sweep-line approach to build a data structure that allows O(log N) time point location queries. The preprocessing typically takes O(N log N) time, where N is the number of segments in the subdivision.
tips: Represent the planar subdivision as a set of non-intersecting segments.
Sort all segment endpoints and query points by their x-coordinate to form sweep-line events.
Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., std::set or TreeMap) to maintain the order of segments intersected by the sweep-line.
Handle event types: segment start, segment end, and query point.
For a query point, find the segments immediately above and below it in the sweep-line status structure.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling horizontal segments or collinear points.
Floating point precision issues when comparing coordinates or slopes.
Inefficient updates or queries on the sweep-line status structure.
Forgetting to consider edge cases like points exactly on a segment boundary.
High constant factors due to complex geometric primitives.
invariants: The sweep-line is a vertical line moving monotonically from left to right across the plane.
The sweep-line status structure (e.g., balanced BST) accurately stores all segments currently intersecting the sweep-line, ordered by their y-coordinate at the sweep-line's current x-position.
All geometric objects (segments, query points) to the left of the sweep-line have been fully processed and their contributions accounted for.
For any query point, the face it belongs to is bounded by the segments immediately above and below it on the sweep-line.

title: Point location in O(logn)
when_to_use: Use when needing to efficiently determine which region or geometric primitive a given query point belongs to within a static set of geometric objects, such as polygons or diagrams.
description: This algorithm efficiently locates query points within a geometric structure by processing events along a sweep line. It typically achieves O(log N) query time after an initial preprocessing phase.
tips: Sort all event points by x-coordinate, then by event type for consistent processing.
Maintain an ordered data structure (e.g., balanced BST) for active edges intersected by the sweep line.
Use a custom comparator for ordering edges in the active set based on their y-intercept at the current sweep line position.
Handle different event types (add, remove, query, vertical) in a predefined, consistent order.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling edge cases like vertical edges or points exactly on edges.
Maintaining the active edge set inefficiently, leading to higher than O(log N) complexity.
Errors in event sorting order, especially for events at the same x-coordinate.
Floating-point precision issues when comparing y-coordinates or slopes of edges.
Not properly updating the active edge set when edges are added or removed.
invariants: All events with x-coordinate less than the current sweep line position have been processed.
The active set of edges contains all segments currently intersected by the sweep line, ordered by their y-intercept.
For any query point processed, its location relative to the active edges is correctly determined.
The sweep line always moves monotonically in the x-direction.

title: Point location in O(logn)
when_to_use: Use this when you need to efficiently determine which region or face a given point lies within a planar subdivision, especially when queries are frequent and an O(log N) per query time is required.
description: This algorithm uses a sweep-line approach with event points to maintain an ordered set of active non-vertical edges, augmented by a separate set for vertical edges. Point queries are resolved by binary searching these sets, achieving O(log N) query time after an initial preprocessing.
tips: Carefully define event types: add edge, remove edge, query point, vertical edge.
Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., std::set in C++) for active non-vertical edges.
The comparator for non-vertical edges must correctly order them based on their relative y-position at the current sweep-line x-coordinate.
Handle vertical edges separately, as their ordering is trivial but they require specific checks.
Consider re-evaluating queries after all events at an x-coordinate to resolve ambiguities.
pitfalls: Incorrectly implementing the geometric comparator for non-vertical edges can lead to wrong ordering.
Failing to handle degenerate cases, such as points on edges or vertices, can result in incorrect face identification.
Ignoring floating-point precision issues in geometric calculations, especially comparisons.
Not clearing the vertical edge set at each new x-coordinate can lead to stale data.
Complexity of event point sorting or set operations can degrade performance if not optimized.
invariants: The set t contains all non-vertical edges currently intersected by the sweep-line, ordered by their y-coordinate at the sweep-line's x-position.
The set vert contains all vertical edges whose minimum y-coordinate is at the current sweep-line x-position, cleared for each new x-coordinate.
All events to the left of the current sweep-line position have been fully processed.
For any point query, if it lies on an edge, that edge is present in either t or vert.

title: Point location in O(logn)
when_to_use: Use when needing to efficiently determine which region (face or edge) of a planar subdivision a given query point lies within, especially for multiple online queries.

Carefully handle points lying exactly on edges or vertices.
Preprocess the subdivision to manage non-vertical edges or specific orientations.

title: Finding the nearest pair of points
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to efficiently find the pair of points with the minimum Euclidean distance among a given set of N points in a 2D plane. It is particularly effective for large datasets where an O(N^2) approach is too slow.
description: This divide-and-conquer algorithm finds the closest pair of points in a 2D plane by recursively splitting the set of points and merging results. It achieves an optimal time complexity of O(N log N).
tips: Initial sorting by X-coordinate is crucial for efficient division of the point set.
Handle base cases (e.g., 2 or 3 points) with a simple brute-force distance calculation.
When combining results, only consider points within a narrow strip of width 2*delta around the dividing line.
Within the strip, sort points by Y-coordinate and compare only a limited number of neighbors for efficiency.
pitfalls: Forgetting to sort points by Y-coordinate within the central strip can degrade performance to O(N^2).
Incorrectly defining the strip width or comparison range in the merge step can lead to incorrect results.
Floating-point precision errors can lead to subtle bugs or incorrect minimum distances, especially with square roots.
Inefficiently handling the base cases or small subproblems can add unnecessary overhead.
invariants: At the start of any recursive call, the input point set is sorted by X-coordinate.
The returned minimum distance 'd' for a subproblem is the true minimum distance among its points.
During the merge step, all points within the 'delta'-strip are considered for Y-sorted comparison.
The final 'delta' value is the minimum of 'delta_left', 'delta_right', and 'delta_strip' minimums.

title: Finding the nearest pair of points
when_to_use: Use this when finding the pair of points with the minimum Euclidean distance in a 2D plane. It efficiently handles large datasets by recursively dividing the problem and merging results.
description: It finds the closest pair of points in a 2D plane by recursively dividing the set, solving subproblems, and merging results in O(N log N) time. The merge step efficiently handles cross-boundary pairs within a narrow strip.

Maintain a y-sorted list of points for efficient merging of the strip.
The merge step's efficiency relies on processing only points within a narrow strip.
The number of points to check in the strip for each point is constant.
Recursively solve for left and right halves, then combine results.
pitfalls: An inefficient merge step can degrade complexity from O(N log N) to O(N^2).
Failing to correctly identify and process cross-boundary pairs.
Incorrectly handling base cases for small numbers of points.
Floating point precision errors when calculating distances.
Forgetting to sort points by y-coordinate within the strip for efficient checking.
invariants: The minimum distance returned by a recursive call is the true minimum for its input subset.
All points in the left subproblem have x-coordinates less than or equal to the median x-coordinate.
All points in the right subproblem have x-coordinates greater than or equal to the median x-coordinate.
During the merge step, only points within the 'delta' strip need to be considered for cross-boundary pairs.

title: Vertical decomposition
signals: cut the plane into vertical stripes
no two segments intersect strictly inside stripe
when_to_use: Use when a geometric problem involves complex shapes or their unions, and partitioning the plane into simpler, independent vertical regions simplifies computation. It's particularly effective when intersections create many critical vertical boundaries.
description: Vertical decomposition partitions a geometric plane into vertical stripes by drawing lines through critical points like vertices and segment intersections. This transforms complex intersection problems into simpler, independent subproblems within each stripe, often solvable with a sweep-line algorithm, typically resulting in O(N^2) stripes.
tips: Identify all critical x-coordinates for vertical lines (vertices, segment intersections).
Sort critical x-coordinates to define the vertical stripe boundaries.
Within each stripe, segments maintain their relative order, simplifying interval management.
Process each stripe independently, often using a 1D sweep-line or interval data structure.
Consider using a segment tree or balanced BST for efficient interval management within a stripe.
pitfalls: The O(N^2) number of stripes can lead to high complexity for large N.
Precisely handling floating-point arithmetic for intersection points is crucial.
Carefully manage edge cases, such as perfectly vertical or horizontal segments.
Correctly identifying 'upper' and 'lower' segments within a stripe can be tricky.
Ensuring disjointness of processed regions within a stripe is vital for correct aggregation.
invariants: All critical x-coordinates (vertices, segment intersections) are identified and sorted to define stripe boundaries.
Within any given vertical stripe, no two input segments intersect.
For each stripe, the union of shapes within it can be represented as a collection of disjoint vertical trapezoids.
The sweep-line algorithm correctly maintains the active segments and their relative order across the current stripe.

title: Vertical decomposition
when_to_use: Use this technique to decompose complex geometric regions into simpler shapes like trapezoids, often for problems involving segment intersections or area calculations. It is also effective for finding the intersection of convex polygons.
description: Vertical decomposition partitions a geometric plane into elementary shapes (typically trapezoids) by drawing vertical lines through critical points, simplifying complex region analysis. Optimized implementations achieve O(N^2 log N) time and O(N) memory complexity.
tips: Fix one segment and use a horizontal scanline to process how other segments affect its 'balance of brackets' status.
Represent changes in geometric properties over an interval as events (start, value) and (end, -value) for sweep-line processing.
When segments coincide, use a consistent tie-breaking rule (e.g., lowest index) to avoid ambiguity in decomposition.
Consider the intersection of projections of segments on an axis to quickly filter out irrelevant pairs.
pitfalls: The naive O(N^3 log N) approach is often too slow; always aim for optimized O(N^2 log N) or better solutions.
Incorrectly handling edge cases like segments intersecting at a single point or coinciding over an interval can lead to errors.
Failing to properly manage the 'balance' or state across the sweep-line can result in incorrect decomposition.
Overlooking empty or single-point intersections of segment projections, which do not affect stripe interiors.
invariants: At any sweep-line position, the active data structure (e.g., balanced BST) correctly represents the segments intersecting the sweep-line.
All geometric regions to the left of the current sweep-line position have been correctly decomposed into trapezoids.
The 'balance' value at any point on the sweep-line accurately reflects the net effect of segments above/below it.
Each generated trapezoid is non-overlapping and covers a unique portion of the input geometry.

title: Vertical decomposition

The union of all generated trapezoids at any point accurately represents the processed portion of the input geometry.

title: Delaunay triangulation and Voronoi diagram
when_to_use: Use when partitioning a plane based on proximity to a set of points, or when generating a robust triangulation of points that optimizes angle properties for applications like mesh generation or nearest neighbor queries.
description: Delaunay triangulation is a triangulation of a set of points such that no point is inside the circumcircle of any triangle. The Voronoi diagram is a partition of the plane where each region contains points closest to a specific input point, and it is dual to the Delaunay triangulation. Both typically have a time complexity of O(N log N) for N points.
tips: Delaunay triangulation maximizes the minimum angle, improving triangle quality.
The Minimum Euclidean Spanning Tree is a subset of Delaunay edges.
Voronoi cells define regions of closest proximity to input points.
Consider incremental construction or divide-and-conquer algorithms.
Handle degenerate cases carefully, especially collinear points.
pitfalls: Degenerate cases like collinear points or concyclic points can complicate construction.
Numerical precision issues can arise with floating-point coordinates.
High computational cost for very large datasets without specialized algorithms.
Boundary conditions for infinite Voronoi cells need careful handling.

other: assumes general position (no three points collinear, no four points concyclic) for non-degenerate cases

invariants: For any triangle in the Delaunay triangulation, its circumcircle contains no other input points.
Each edge in the Delaunay triangulation corresponds to a shared boundary between two adjacent Voronoi cells.
Every point in a Voronoi cell is closer to its associated generator point than to any other generator point.
The union of all Voronoi cells covers the entire plane.

Memory consumption can be substantial for large datasets if not optimized.

title: Delaunay triangulation and Voronoi diagram

title: Manhattan Distance
when_to_use: When calculating distances on a grid where movement is restricted to cardinal directions, or when optimizing expressions involving sums of absolute differences of coordinates.
description: Manhattan distance, or L1 distance, measures the distance between two points as the sum of the absolute differences of their Cartesian coordinates. It is commonly used to find optimal paths on a grid or to transform coordinate systems for specific optimizations, often with O(N) or O(N log N) complexity for common problems like farthest pair.
tips: For 2D points (x, y), Manhattan distance can be transformed by rotating coordinates: (x', y') = (x+y, x-y).
After coordinate rotation, the Manhattan distance problem often simplifies to finding min/max values of the transformed coordinates.
Finding the farthest pair of points in Manhattan distance can be solved in O(N) time by finding the min/max of (x+y) and (x-y) values.
The maximum of |A-B| + |C-D| can be found by maximizing |(A+C)-(B+D)| and |(A-C)-(B-D)| independently.
pitfalls: Confusing Manhattan distance with Euclidean distance (L2 distance) or Chebyshev distance (L-infinity).
Incorrectly applying coordinate rotation for problems not involving sums of absolute differences.
Overlooking edge cases for small numbers of points (e.g., N=1 or N=2) when finding farthest pairs.
Not considering all four combinations of signs for absolute values if not using the coordinate transformation trick.
invariants: For any two points P1(x1, y1) and P2(x2, y2), the Manhattan distance is |x1-x2| + |y1-y2|.
The maximum Manhattan distance between any two points is found by considering the maximum of (max(x+y) - min(x+y)) and (max(x-y) - min(x-y)) over all points.
The transformation (x', y') = (x+y, x-y) converts Manhattan distance in (x,y) space to Chebyshev distance in (x',y') space.

title: Manhattan Distance
when_to_use: Use when calculating distance between two points on a grid where movement is restricted to horizontal and vertical directions. It is also useful for problems involving maximizing or minimizing sums of absolute coordinate differences, often transformable to Chebyshev distance.
description: Manhattan distance measures the sum of absolute differences of Cartesian coordinates between two points. It can be transformed into a Chebyshev distance problem by rotating coordinates, enabling efficient O(N) max/min distance calculations.
tips: Consider transforming to Chebyshev distance for max/min distance problems.
The transformation involves rotating coordinates: (x, y) -> (x+y, y-x).
For maximizing Manhattan distance, consider the 2^d sign combinations for each dimension.
Can be used in grid-based problems where diagonal movement is disallowed.
Often simplifies distance calculations on integer grids.
pitfalls: Confusing with Euclidean distance, which allows diagonal movement.
Incorrectly applying coordinate transformation (e.g., wrong signs).
Overlooking the 2^d sign combinations for maximizing distance in higher dimensions.
Not handling edge cases for points on axes or origin correctly.
Assuming it's always the shortest path on a grid with obstacles.
invariants: For any two points P1=(x1, y1) and P2=(x2, y2), the Manhattan distance is always non-negative.
The transformed points P1'=(x1+y1, y1-x1) and P2'=(x2+y2, y2-x2) satisfy Chebyshev(P1', P2') == Manhattan(P1, P2).
When maximizing Manhattan distance over a set of points, the maximum value is determined by the extreme values of (x+y) and (x-y) over the set.

title: Manhattan Distance
when_to_use: Use this when finding the Minimum Spanning Tree for points in a 2D plane where edge weights are Manhattan distances, or when a geometric problem can be simplified by transforming Manhattan to Chebyshev distance.
description: This algorithm finds the Minimum Spanning Tree for points in a 2D plane using Manhattan distance. It leverages a sweep-line approach to efficiently find nearest neighbors in specific octants, reducing the number of candidate edges for Kruskal's algorithm, achieving O(N log N) complexity.
tips: Consider transforming Manhattan distance problems into Chebyshev distance problems using (x+y, y-x) coordinates.
Divide the plane into 8 octants around each point to simplify nearest neighbor searches.
Utilize a sweep-line approach, sorting points by x+y, to efficiently find nearest neighbors in each octant.
Only 4 rotations of the coordinate system are needed to cover all 8 octant directions for nearest neighbor finding.
Maintain an 'active set' of points, ordered by a relevant coordinate (e.g., x or x-y), for efficient queries during the sweep.
pitfalls: Incorrectly defining or handling the 8 octants, especially near boundaries or for points with equal coordinates.
Inefficiently managing the 'active set' during the sweep-line, leading to higher than O(N log N) complexity.
Forgetting to apply a standard MST algorithm (like Kruskal's with DSU) after generating the candidate edges.
Errors in the coordinate transformation or rotation logic for different octants.
Not accounting for the amortized complexity of active set operations, which requires careful data structure choice.
invariants: For any point 's' in the active set, its nearest neighbor in the current octant has not yet been processed.
Points are processed in non-decreasing order of 'x + y' (or transformed coordinate), ensuring local optimality for distances.
The active set contains points 's' such that 'x_s <= x_p' and 'x_p - y_p < x_s - y_s' (for NNE octant), ordered for efficient search.
All edges added to the candidate list represent the shortest Manhattan distance from a point 's' to some 'p' within a specific octant.

title: Minimum Enclosing Circle
when_to_use: Use when finding the smallest circle that contains a given set of 2D points. It provides an expected linear time solution for this fundamental geometric problem.
description: Welzl's algorithm finds the unique minimum enclosing circle for a set of points using a randomized incremental approach. It achieves an expected time complexity of O(N).
tips: Randomly permute input points to ensure expected O(N) performance.
The MEC is uniquely determined by either two points forming a diameter or three points on its boundary.
Handle base cases for 0, 1, 2, or 3 points carefully in the recursive calls.
Use robust floating-point comparisons for geometric predicates like point-in-circle.
The algorithm builds the MEC incrementally, adding one point at a time.
pitfalls: Not randomizing the input can lead to a worst-case O(N^3) performance.
Floating-point precision issues can cause incorrect boundary checks or point containment tests.
Incorrectly handling degenerate cases, such as collinear points or identical points.
Assuming the MEC always passes through three points; it can be defined by two points.
Deep recursion stack for large N if not properly optimized or if worst-case input occurs.
invariants: The current candidate circle `C` encloses all points `p_1, ..., p_i` processed so far.
If `p_i` is not contained in `C_{i-1}`, then `p_i` must lie on the boundary of `C_i`.
The radius of `C` is the minimum possible for the points it currently encloses.

title: Minimum Enclosing Circle
signals: Random shuffle for expected linear time
Check if point is inside circle
when_to_use: When you need to find the smallest circle that contains all given points in a 2D plane, often with a requirement for expected linear time complexity.
description: This algorithm iteratively constructs the Minimum Enclosing Circle (MEC) by considering points one by one, potentially updating the circle if a new point lies outside. It achieves an expected linear time complexity, O(N), through randomization.
tips: Randomly shuffle input points to achieve expected linear time complexity.
The Minimum Enclosing Circle is always defined by 2 or 3 points on its boundary.
Maintain invariants at each recursive level to ensure correctness.
Efficiently check if a point lies inside a given circle in O(1) time.
pitfalls: A naive implementation without randomization can lead to O(N^3) worst-case time complexity.
Floating-point precision issues can arise in geometric calculations, requiring careful handling.
Incorrectly handling edge cases where the MEC is defined by only two points.
Forgetting to shuffle points can result in worst-case performance for specific input orders.
invariants: The current circle C is the MEC for the subset of points processed so far.
If C is defined by 1 point p_i, it is the MEC of points processed so far that passes through p_i.
If C is defined by 2 points p_i, p_j, it is the MEC of points processed so far that passes through p_i and p_j.
If C is defined by 3 points p_i, p_j, p_k, it is the MEC of points processed so far that passes through p_i, p_j, and p_k.

title: Breadth-first search
when_to_use: Use when finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph or traversing a graph level by level to explore all reachable nodes. It is also suitable for finding connected components or checking reachability.
description: Breadth-first search explores a graph layer by layer, finding the shortest path in terms of edges from a source node to all other reachable nodes. It typically runs in O(V + E) time, where V is the number of vertices and E is the number of edges.
tips: Use a queue to manage nodes to visit in a FIFO manner.
Mark nodes as visited immediately upon adding them to the queue to prevent redundant processing and cycles.
Store parent pointers to reconstruct the shortest path from the source to any reachable node.
Initialize distances to infinity for all nodes and zero for the source node.
Can be adapted for implicit graphs where nodes and edges are generated on the fly.
pitfalls: Forgetting to mark nodes as visited can lead to infinite loops in cyclic graphs or incorrect path lengths.
Not handling disconnected components if the goal is to traverse the entire graph.
Incorrectly initializing distance or parent arrays can lead to wrong results.
Using BFS for weighted graphs will not yield the shortest paths; Dijkstra's or Bellman-Ford is needed.
Potential for queue overflow if the graph is extremely large and not managed efficiently.
invariants: All nodes currently in the queue are at the same or next level of traversal from the source.
All nodes already dequeued have been fully processed, and their unvisited neighbors have been enqueued.
The distance array `d[u]` stores the shortest path length from the source to `u` found so far.
The parent array `p[u]` stores the predecessor of `u` on a shortest path from the source.

title: Breadth-first search
when_to_use: When finding the shortest path in a graph where edge weights are restricted to 0 or 1, or when minimizing operations where some operations are 'free' (cost 0) and others have a unit cost (cost 1).
description: A graph traversal algorithm that finds the shortest path from a source to all reachable nodes in a graph where edge weights are either 0 or 1. It uses a deque to prioritize 0-cost edges, achieving an optimal O(V + E) time complexity.
tips: Use a deque: push 0-cost edges to the front, 1-cost edges to the back.
Mark nodes visited to avoid cycles and redundant processing.
Initialize distances to infinity and the source distance to zero.
Can be adapted for grid problems where moves have different costs (0 or 1).
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling visited states can lead to infinite loops or incorrect paths.
Forgetting to initialize distances properly before starting the traversal.
Mismanaging deque operations, such as pushing 1-cost edges to the front.
Not suitable for general weighted graphs; use Dijkstra's algorithm instead.
invariants: Nodes are added to the front of the deque if the edge weight is 0, and to the back if 1.
The distance `dist[u]` for any node `u` in the deque is either `k` or `k+1` for some integer `k`.
All nodes removed from the deque have their final shortest path distance determined.
For any node `u` in the deque, `dist[u]` is the shortest path from the source to `u` found so far.

title: Breadth-first search
when_to_use: Use when finding the shortest path or minimum number of edges between nodes in an unweighted graph, or to explore a graph layer by layer. It is also suitable for finding shortest cycles or identifying all components of shortest paths.
description: Breadth-first search systematically explores a graph level by level, ensuring the shortest path in terms of edges is found from a source node to all reachable nodes. It operates in O(V + E) time complexity for adjacency list representation.
tips: Run multiple BFS from different sources to find global properties like shortest cycles.
Maintain a distance array to record the shortest path length from the source to each node.
The condition `dist[u] + 1 + dist_reverse[v] == dist[target]` identifies edges on a shortest path.
Use a queue to process nodes in a level-by-level order.
Mark nodes as visited immediately upon adding them to the queue.
pitfalls: Applying BFS directly to weighted graphs will not yield shortest paths.
Failing to mark visited nodes can lead to infinite loops in cyclic graphs or incorrect path lengths.
Incorrectly initializing or resetting distance arrays and visited sets for multiple BFS runs.
Not handling disconnected components if the problem requires processing the entire graph.
Confusing BFS with DFS by using a stack instead of a queue for node exploration.
invariants: All nodes currently in the queue are at depth `k` or `k+1` from the source.
The distance recorded for any visited node is its shortest path length from the source.
No node is added to the queue more than once.
All nodes at a shallower depth are processed before any node at a deeper depth.

title: Depth First Search
when_to_use: Use DFS to explore all reachable vertices from a source, find connected components, or detect cycles in a graph. It is suitable for problems requiring deep exploration before backtracking.
description: Depth First Search systematically explores a graph by going as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. It visits each vertex and edge at most once, resulting in an O(V + E) time complexity.
tips: Use a 'visited' set or array to prevent infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Recursion is common, but an explicit stack can manage deep graphs or avoid recursion limits.
Keep track of parent nodes to reconstruct paths or detect cycles.
Can be adapted for topological sorting by processing nodes in post-order.
Useful for exploring implicit graphs where nodes are generated on the fly.
pitfalls: Can lead to stack overflow errors on very deep graphs if implemented recursively.
Does not guarantee shortest paths in general unweighted graphs (BFS does).
Incorrectly handling visited states can lead to infinite loops or incorrect results.
Can be slower than BFS for finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs.
invariants: Before visiting a node 'u', it is marked as unvisited.
After visiting a node 'u', it is marked as visited, and all its reachable neighbors have been explored.
The stack (explicit or call stack) always contains nodes on the current path from the source to the current node.
Each edge (u, v) is traversed at most twice (once from u to v, once from v to u if undirected).

title: Depth First Search
when_to_use: Use DFS to explore all reachable nodes from a starting point, find paths, or traverse tree-like structures. It's suitable for problems requiring exhaustive search or connectivity checks.
description: Depth First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It typically uses a stack (explicit or implicit via recursion) and has a time complexity of O(V + E) for graphs and O(V) for trees.
tips: Use a visited set or array to prevent cycles and redundant work.
Recursion is often the most natural way to implement DFS.
Pass necessary state (e.g., current path, parent) as function arguments.
Consider iterative DFS with an explicit stack for deep graphs to avoid stack overflow.
Pre-order, in-order, and post-order traversals are specific DFS applications.
pitfalls: Stack overflow can occur with deep recursion on very large graphs.
Forgetting to mark nodes as visited can lead to infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Incorrectly handling disconnected components requires multiple DFS calls.
Modifying graph structure during traversal without careful consideration.
Not handling base cases correctly in recursive implementations.
invariants: All nodes reachable from the starting node are eventually visited.
The visited set/array accurately tracks all nodes currently in the recursion stack or already fully explored.
When exploring a node, all its unvisited neighbors are added to the conceptual stack before backtracking.
Each edge is traversed at most twice (once in each direction for undirected graphs).

title: Depth First Search
when_to_use: Use DFS to explore all reachable vertices from a starting point, find paths, detect cycles, or perform topological sorting on directed acyclic graphs.
description: Depth First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It typically runs in O(V + E) time for graphs represented with adjacency lists, where V is the number of vertices and E is the number of edges.
tips: Always use a visited set to avoid infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
For topological sort, add nodes to a list after visiting all their neighbors.
Recursive implementation is often cleaner but can hit stack limits.
Track discovery and finish times for advanced graph algorithms.
pitfalls: Risk of stack overflow for very deep graphs or trees.
Failing to mark nodes as visited before recursive calls can lead to re-processing.
Not handling disconnected components if the problem requires full graph coverage.
DFS does not guarantee the shortest path in unweighted graphs.
invariants: Every node is visited at most once.
The `visited` set accurately reflects nodes currently in the recursion stack or fully explored.
For topological sort, a node is added to the result only after all its neighbors have been processed.

title: Depth First Search
when_to_use: Use DFS to systematically explore all reachable vertices from a starting point, identify graph components, or determine properties like cycles and topological orderings. It's suitable for problems requiring full graph traversal or tree-like structure analysis.
