description: Depth First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It typically runs in O(V + E) time for graphs represented with adjacency lists.
tips: Use a 'visited' set or array to prevent infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Recursion is natural for DFS, but an iterative approach with an explicit stack can prevent stack overflow.
Track entry and exit times for each vertex to identify ancestor-descendant relationships and tree properties.
For directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), DFS can produce a topological sort by processing vertices in decreasing order of exit times.
DFS is a core component for finding strongly connected components (SCCs) using algorithms like Tarjan's or Kosaraju's.
pitfalls: Forgetting to mark nodes as visited can lead to infinite loops in cyclic graphs or incorrect results.
Deep recursion can cause stack overflow errors on large graphs, necessitating an iterative approach.
Incorrectly handling disconnected components; ensure all vertices are visited by restarting DFS from unvisited nodes.
Not distinguishing between different edge types (tree, back, forward, cross) can lead to errors in cycle detection or graph analysis.
Complexity can be higher if using an adjacency matrix (O(V^2)) instead of an adjacency list (O(V+E)).
invariants: When a vertex 'u' is entered, it is marked as 'visiting' and its 'entry_time' is recorded.
When a vertex 'u' is exited, it is marked as 'visited' and its 'exit_time' is recorded.
No vertex is processed more than once (i.e., added to the recursion stack/explicit stack) if already marked 'visiting' or 'visited'.
For any edge (u, v) in the graph, if 'u' is currently 'visiting', then 'v' is either 'unvisited', 'visiting' (back edge), or 'visited' (forward/cross edge).

title: Depth First Search
when_to_use: When you need to systematically explore all reachable vertices and edges from a starting node, or to find specific structural properties like cycles, topological order, or connected components in a graph.
description: Depth First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It visits each vertex and edge exactly once, typically running in O(V + E) time.
tips: Utilize a visited array or set to avoid reprocessing nodes and infinite loops.
Track discovery and finish times for advanced applications like topological sorting.
Implement recursively for simplicity or iteratively with a stack for large graphs.
Iterate through all vertices to ensure all connected components are visited.
Detect cycles by identifying back edges to an already active ancestor.
pitfalls: Risk of stack overflow for very deep graphs when using recursive implementation.
Failing to visit all nodes in disconnected graphs without iterating through all vertices.
Incorrectly identifying back edges, especially in undirected graphs where parent edges are not back edges.
Not marking nodes as visited before processing can lead to infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
invariants: Every vertex is visited at most once.
All edges incident to a visited vertex are eventually explored.
When a vertex `u` is on the recursion stack, all its descendants in the DFS tree are explored before `u` finishes.

title: Depth First Search
when_to_use: Use to explore all reachable nodes, find paths, detect cycles, or perform topological sorting. It's also suitable for tree traversals and connected components.
description: DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It has a time complexity of O(V + E) for graphs and O(V) for trees.
tips: Use a visited set/array to prevent infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Recursion stack can lead to stack overflow for deep graphs; consider iterative DFS with an explicit stack.
Maintain parent pointers for path reconstruction or cycle detection.
Can be used to find strongly connected components (SCC) with additional logic.
pitfalls: Stack overflow with deep recursion on large graphs.
Incorrectly handling visited states can lead to infinite loops or incorrect results.
Not suitable for finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs (BFS is better).
Order of visiting neighbors can affect results in some problems.
invariants: All nodes on the current path from source to current node are marked as visiting.
All nodes already fully explored are marked as visited.
No node is processed twice in the same DFS traversal.
The current node is always reachable from the starting node.

title: Search for connected components in a graph
when_to_use: Use when you need to partition an undirected graph into maximal connected subgraphs where every node is reachable from every other node within its subgraph.
description: This algorithm identifies all connected components in an undirected graph by iteratively performing graph traversals (DFS or BFS) from unvisited nodes. It has a time complexity of O(V + E) for adjacency list representation.
tips: Maintain a visited array or set to track processed nodes.
Iterate through all nodes, starting a new traversal if a node is unvisited.
Either DFS or BFS can be used interchangeably for component discovery.
Store nodes of each component as they are discovered.
pitfalls: Failing to iterate through all nodes to find all components in a disconnected graph.
Not correctly marking nodes as visited during traversal, leading to infinite loops or re-processing.
Using an adjacency matrix for very sparse graphs, which can be inefficient.
Incorrectly assuming the graph is always connected.
invariants: All nodes visited so far belong to an identified connected component.
Any node currently being visited is part of the current connected component being explored.
No unvisited node has a path to any node in an already identified component.
The set of all identified components forms a partition of the graph's nodes.

title: Search for connected components in a graph
when_to_use: Use when the goal is to identify distinct groups of interconnected nodes within a graph, or to find all nodes reachable from a specific starting point.
description: Depth-First Search (DFS) explores a graph by traversing as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It efficiently finds connected components by visiting all reachable nodes from an unvisited starting point, typically with O(V+E) time complexity.
tips: Maintain a 'visited' status for each node to prevent cycles and redundant work.
Iterate through all nodes and start DFS if a node is unvisited to find all components.
Use an explicit stack for iterative DFS to avoid recursion depth limits.
Adjacency list representation is efficient for sparse graphs.
pitfalls: Excessive recursion depth can lead to stack overflow errors for large graphs.
Forgetting to mark nodes as visited results in infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Missing disconnected components if DFS is only started from a single node.
High memory usage if using an adjacency matrix for sparse graphs.
invariants: All nodes in the current DFS path from the start node to the current node are on the recursion stack.
Any node marked as visited will not be re-processed by the current DFS call.
Upon completion of a DFS call from a starting node, all nodes in its connected component are marked visited.

title: Finding bridges in a graph in O(N+M)
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to identify critical edges in an undirected graph whose removal would increase the number of connected components, effectively disconnecting parts of the graph.
description: This algorithm finds all bridges in an undirected graph by performing a Depth First Search and tracking discovery times and lowest reachable ancestors. It operates with an optimal time complexity of O(N+M).
tips: Maintain discovery times (disc) and low-link values (low) for each vertex during DFS.
Initialize low[u] = disc[u] when first visiting vertex u.
Update low[u] with min(low[u], disc[v]) for back-edges to an ancestor v.
Update low[u] with min(low[u], low[v]) after visiting a child v via a tree-edge.
An edge (u, v) is a bridge if disc[u] < low[v] (where u is the parent of v in DFS tree).
pitfalls: Forgetting to mark visited nodes can lead to infinite loops or incorrect low-link values.
Incorrectly handling the parent edge during DFS, treating it as a back-edge.
Not properly initializing or updating low-link values, especially for root or back-edges.
Failing to account for disconnected graphs; run DFS from all unvisited nodes.
Potential stack overflow for very deep graphs due to recursion depth.
invariants: For any visited vertex u, disc[u] stores its discovery time in the DFS tree.
For any visited vertex u, low[u] stores the minimum discovery time reachable from u (including u) through u's subtree and at most one back-edge.
All edges (u, v) where disc[u] < low[v] (and u is parent of v) are bridges.
The DFS traversal explores each vertex and edge exactly once.

title: Finding bridges in a graph in O(N+M)
when_to_use: Use this algorithm when you need to identify edges whose removal would increase the number of connected components in an undirected graph. It's suitable for analyzing network robustness or critical connections.
description: This algorithm uses Depth First Search (DFS) to find bridges in an undirected graph by tracking discovery times and the lowest reachable discovery time from a node or its descendants. It operates in linear time, O(N+M), where N is the number of vertices and M is the number of edges.
tips: Initialize discovery times (tin) and lowest reachable times (low) arrays, along with a visited array.
Pass the parent vertex to the DFS function to avoid treating the parent edge as a back-edge.
Use a global timer to assign unique discovery times for each node.
Iterate DFS from all unvisited nodes to correctly handle disconnected graph components.
For multiple edges, ensure the parent edge is skipped only once to prevent incorrect back-edge detection.
pitfalls: Forgetting to pass the parent node or incorrectly handling the parent edge, leading to false bridge detections.
Failing to initialize `low` values correctly, especially for leaf nodes or isolated components.
Not accounting for disconnected components, which results in only partial bridge identification.
Incorrectly updating `low[u]` with `low[v]` for back-edges instead of `tin[v]`.
Misidentifying multiple edges between two vertices as distinct bridges without proper checks.
invariants: For any node `u` currently in the DFS stack, `tin[u]` is its unique discovery time.
For any node `u` and its child `v` in the DFS tree, `low[v]` represents the earliest discovery time reachable from `v`'s subtree (including `v`) using at most one back-edge.
An edge `(u, v)` is a bridge if and only if `low[v] > tin[u]`, indicating no back-edge from `v`'s subtree reaches `u` or its ancestors.

title: Finding Bridges Online
when_to_use: Use when edges are added one by one to an initially empty graph and you need to efficiently track the number of bridges or 2-edge-connected components after each addition.
description: This algorithm dynamically maintains the number of bridges in an undirected graph as edges are added incrementally. It leverages a Disjoint Set Union (DSU) data structure to achieve an amortized time complexity of O((N+M) log N).
tips: Understand the properties of 2-edge-connected components and how they relate to bridges.
Carefully manage DSU operations, including path compression and union by rank/size, for optimal performance.
Consider how each new edge addition might merge existing components or eliminate bridges.
The algorithm often involves maintaining a 'tree' structure of 2-edge-connected components.
Use a robust DSU implementation that supports efficient merging and finding operations.
pitfalls: Incorrectly updating the bridge count after an edge addition, especially when cycles are formed.
Inefficient DSU operations leading to a higher than expected time complexity.
Failing to correctly identify when an edge creates a cycle within a component, thus removing a bridge.
Handling edge cases like self-loops or multiple edges between the same pair of vertices.
Complexity of maintaining an explicit list of all bridges versus just their total count.
invariants: Each DSU set represents a 2-edge-connected component or a collection of such components connected by bridges.
The total count of bridges accurately reflects edges whose removal increases connected components.
All edges added so far are correctly incorporated into the DSU structure.
Path compression and union by rank/size maintain the DSU's amortized efficiency.

title: Finding Bridges Online
when_to_use: Use when you need to dynamically track bridges and 2-edge-connected components in a graph as edges are added. It efficiently updates the graph structure and bridge count in an online fashion.
description: This algorithm maintains 2-edge-connected components and bridges in a graph as edges are added, using a Union-Find structure to manage components and a forest to represent the relationships between them. It typically achieves near-amortized constant time per edge addition.
tips: Represent 2-edge-connected components using Union-Find for efficient merging.
Maintain a forest where nodes are 2-edge-connected components and edges are bridges.
Distinguish between adding an edge within a component versus connecting two components.
When an edge creates a cycle with existing bridges, those bridges cease to exist.
Use path compression and union by rank/size for optimal Union-Find performance.
The bridge count must be decremented when bridges are removed due to cycle formation.
pitfalls: Incorrectly identifying when an edge forms a cycle with existing bridges.
Failing to update the bridge count accurately when bridges are removed.
Not properly compressing cycles into single 2-edge-connected components.
Inefficient Union-Find implementation leading to poor performance.
Confusing connected components with 2-edge-connected components.
Handling edge cases for initial graph state or isolated vertices.
invariants: Each 2-edge-connected component is represented by a single root in the Union-Find structure.
The set of bridges forms a forest where each node is a 2-edge-connected component.
The total number of bridges is accurately maintained after each edge addition.
All edges within a 2-edge-connected component are not bridges.

title: Finding Bridges Online
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to dynamically maintain bridges and 2-edge-connected components in a graph as edges are added. It efficiently processes edge additions by updating forest structures and compressing cycles.
description: This algorithm dynamically maintains bridges and 2-edge-connected components using two DSU structures and a parent array for tree structure. Edge additions involve re-rooting smaller trees and compressing cycles via LCA, achieving amortized O(log N) per edge operation.
tips: Employ two DSU structures: one for connected components and another for 2-edge-connected components.
Optimize re-rooting operations by always re-rooting the smaller of the two trees being joined.
Compress detected cycles by merging all cycle vertices into their Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA).
Maintain component sizes within DSU to facilitate efficient re-rooting decisions.
LCA can be found by simple path traversal since cycle length is the dominant factor for compression.
pitfalls: Failing to consistently update both DSU structures and the parent array after operations.
Incorrectly handling parent pointers during re-rooting, leading to broken tree structures.
Not accounting for the decrease in the number of bridges when a cycle is formed and compressed.
Inefficient re-rooting without size optimization can degrade performance to O(N) per operation.
Overlooking the need to update DSU representatives for all vertices within a compressed cycle.
invariants: The par[] array always defines a forest structure where each tree corresponds to a connected component of 2-edge-connected components.
Each 2-edge-connected component is represented by a single canonical element in its dedicated DSU structure.
For any two vertices u and v, find(dsu_cc, u) == find(dsu_cc, v) if and only if u and v are in the same connected component.

title: Finding articulation points in a graph in O(N+M)
when_to_use: Use when identifying critical nodes whose removal would increase the number of connected components in an undirected graph. It's essential for analyzing network robustness and vulnerability.
description: This algorithm finds all articulation points (cut vertices) in an undirected graph using a single Depth First Search traversal. It operates in linear time, O(N+M), where N is vertices and M is edges.
tips: Initialize `tin` (discovery time) and `low` (lowest reachable ancestor) arrays for all vertices.
The root of the DFS tree is an articulation point if it has more than one child in the DFS tree.
For a non-root vertex `u`, it is an articulation point if there exists a child `v` such that `tin[u] <= low[v]`.
Pass the parent node in DFS to avoid treating the edge back to the immediate parent as a back-edge.
Update `low[u]` with `min(low[u], low[v])` for tree edges and `min(low[u], tin[v])` for back-edges.
pitfalls: Forgetting to initialize `low[u]` with `tin[u]` before processing children.
Incorrectly applying the `tin[u] <= low[v]` condition to the root node of the DFS tree.
Failing to pass the parent in DFS, leading to incorrect back-edge detection and `low` values.
Not considering disconnected components; ensure DFS is run from all unvisited nodes.
Applying the algorithm to directed graphs, as articulation points are typically defined for undirected graphs.
invariants: For any node `u`, `tin[u]` is its discovery time, and `low[u]` is initialized to `tin[u]`.
After visiting all children `v` of `u` (and their subtrees), `low[u]` will hold the minimum `tin` value reachable from `u` or its descendants via tree edges and at most one back-edge.
During DFS, `visited[u]` is true for all nodes `u` currently in the recursion stack or already fully processed.
The parent `p` of the current node `u` is explicitly excluded from back-edge checks to avoid trivial cycles.

title: Strongly connected components and the condensation graph
when_to_use: When you need to identify maximal subgraphs where all vertices are mutually reachable, or to simplify a directed graph into a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of components for further processing.
description: This concept defines SCCs as maximal vertex subsets in a directed graph where all pairs are mutually reachable. It also introduces the condensation graph, which represents SCCs as nodes and forms a DAG, typically found in O(V+E) time.
tips: Understand that SCCs partition the graph's vertices.
The condensation graph is always a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG).
Algorithms like Tarjan's or Kosaraju's efficiently find SCCs.
SCCs are useful for simplifying complex directed graphs.
Consider the implications of edges between different SCCs.
pitfalls: Confusing SCCs with simple cycles or connected components in undirected graphs.
Incorrectly assuming the condensation graph has cycles.
Failing to handle disconnected components correctly.
Not distinguishing between paths within an SCC and paths between SCCs.
invariants: Every vertex belongs to exactly one strongly connected component.
Within any SCC, a path exists from any vertex u to any other vertex v, and vice-versa.
The condensation graph contains no directed cycles.
An edge exists from SCC C_i to C_j in the condensation graph if and only if there's an edge from some vertex in C_i to some vertex in C_j in the original graph.

title: Strongly connected components and the condensation graph
when_to_use: When you need to find all maximal strongly connected subgraphs in a directed graph. It is also used to simplify a directed graph into its condensation graph, where each node is an SCC.
description: This algorithm finds strongly connected components (SCCs) in a directed graph using two depth-first searches. The first DFS calculates exit times, and the second DFS on the transpose graph, processed in decreasing order of exit times, identifies each SCC. It runs in O(N + M) time.
tips: Ensure the first DFS correctly records exit times for all vertices without resetting the counter.
Construct the transpose graph accurately by reversing all edge directions.
Process vertices for the second DFS in strictly decreasing order of their first DFS exit times.
Each DFS call in the second pass, starting from an unvisited vertex, identifies exactly one SCC.
Use a `visited` array for both DFS passes, ensuring it's reset or managed appropriately between passes.
pitfalls: Forgetting to build the transpose graph correctly or using the original graph for the second pass.
Incorrectly sorting or processing vertices in the second DFS (e.g., not strictly decreasing exit times).
Not visiting all vertices in the first DFS, leading to missed components.
Resetting the exit time counter between separate DFS calls within the first pass.
Confusing entry times with exit times, or using entry times for sorting.
invariants: After the first DFS pass, every vertex has a unique, monotonically increasing exit time.
The list of vertices sorted by decreasing exit times provides a topological order of the condensation graph.
During the second DFS on the transpose graph, all reachable unvisited vertices from the current source belong to the same SCC.
Each vertex is assigned to exactly one strongly connected component.

title: Strongly connected components and the condensation graph
when_to_use: Use this when systematically exploring all possible configurations, paths, or subsets within a graph or array. It's ideal for finding every solution or verifying properties across all potential states.
description: Depth-First Search (DFS) is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. When combined with enumeration, it systematically generates all possible combinations or paths, typically with exponential time complexity O(V+E) for traversal or O(N!) / O(2^N) for enumeration.
tips: Define clear base cases for recursion termination.
Manage state changes carefully when entering and exiting recursive calls.
Use pruning (memoization or early exit) to optimize when possible.
Consider iterative DFS with an explicit stack for deep recursion.
pitfalls: Infinite recursion due to missing or incorrect base cases.
Redundant computations if states are not properly managed or memoized.
Stack overflow for very deep recursion without iterative conversion.
Incorrectly handling visited states in graph traversals.
invariants: The current path/subset being built is valid up to the current depth.
All previously explored branches from the current node have been fully processed.
Each recursive call explores a distinct extension of the current state.

title: Strong Orientation
when_to_use: Use this when you need to convert an undirected, bridgeless, and connected graph into a strongly connected directed graph, ensuring reachability between any two vertices.
description: This algorithm transforms a bridgeless, connected undirected graph into a strongly connected directed graph by performing a Depth-First Search. It orients DFS tree edges away from the root and back edges towards ancestors, achieving strong connectivity in O(V+E) time.
tips: Verify the input graph is connected and bridgeless before applying the algorithm.
During DFS, mark visited vertices and track parent-child relationships to distinguish edge types.
Orient DFS tree edges from the parent vertex to its child in the traversal order.
Orient back edges from the child (current vertex) to its ancestor.
The resulting directed graph will guarantee a path from any vertex to any other vertex.
pitfalls: Applying to a graph with bridges will result in a non-strongly connected graph.
Incorrectly identifying or orienting back edges can break the strong connectivity property.
Not handling disconnected components; the algorithm assumes a single connected graph.
Overlooking the need for a separate bridge-finding step if the graph is not guaranteed bridgeless.
invariants: All DFS tree edges discovered so far are oriented from parent to child.
All back edges discovered so far are oriented from child to ancestor.
Any vertex for which DFS has completed can reach its DFS tree parent (if any) and be reached by its DFS tree children.

title: Strong Orientation
when_to_use: Use when you need to identify maximal subgraphs where removing any single vertex does not disconnect the subgraph. This is crucial for analyzing network robustness and fault tolerance.
description: This algorithm decomposes a graph into maximal 2-vertex-connected subgraphs, meaning removing any single vertex does not disconnect them. It uses DFS to identify articulation points and bridges, achieving O(V+E) time complexity.
tips: Maintain discovery times and low-link values for each vertex.
Use a stack to store edges or vertices of the current DFS path.
When an articulation point is found, pop edges from the stack until the articulation point's edge is reached, forming a BCC.
Handle disconnected graphs by running DFS from all unvisited vertices.
pitfalls: Incorrectly calculating low-link values, especially for back-edges.
Missing the root node articulation point condition (at least two children in DFS tree).
Not properly managing the edge stack for component extraction.
Confusing biconnected components with strongly connected components.
invariants: All edges on the DFS stack form part of a potential BCC.
A vertex's low-link value is the minimum discovery time reachable from it (including itself) through its DFS subtree and at most one back-edge.
Every vertex is visited exactly once during the DFS traversal.

title: Dijkstra Algorithm
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to find the shortest paths from a single source to all other vertices in a graph. It is applicable when all edge weights are non-negative.
description: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest paths from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. It iteratively selects the unvisited vertex with the smallest known distance, relaxing its outgoing edges, achieving O(V^2 + E) or O(E log V) complexity.
tips: Use a priority queue for sparse graphs to optimize performance to O(E log V).
Initialize distances to infinity for all vertices except the source, which is zero.
Maintain a predecessor array to reconstruct the actual shortest paths.
Stop the algorithm early if the selected vertex has an infinite distance, indicating unreachable nodes.
Adjacency lists are generally efficient for representing graphs in Dijkstra's algorithm.
pitfalls: Dijkstra's algorithm does not work correctly with negative edge weights; use Bellman-Ford or SPFA instead.
Failing to use an efficient data structure (like a priority queue) for vertex selection can lead to O(V^2) performance on sparse graphs.
Forgetting to mark vertices as visited can lead to infinite loops or incorrect path calculations.
Potential for integer overflow if path lengths can exceed standard integer limits.
Incorrectly handling unreachable vertices, which will retain their initial 'infinity' distance.
invariants: For any marked vertex v, d[v] holds the true shortest path distance from s to v.
For any unmarked vertex v, d[v] holds the shortest path distance from s to v using only marked intermediate vertices.
All edge weights in the graph are non-negative.
The chosen vertex v in each iteration has the minimum d[v] among all unmarked vertices.

title: Dijkstra on sparse graphs
when_to_use: When finding the shortest paths from a single source to all other nodes in a graph with non-negative edge weights, especially when the graph is sparse. It offers optimal time complexity for such scenarios.
description: This algorithm finds the shortest paths from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a graph with non-negative edge weights. By using a priority queue (like a Fibonacci heap), it achieves an optimal time complexity of O(E + V log V) for sparse graphs.
tips: Use a priority queue (min-heap) to efficiently extract the minimum distance vertex.
Initialize distances to infinity and source distance to zero.
Relax edges only if a shorter path is found.
Consider adjacency list representation for sparse graphs.
Fibonacci heaps offer theoretical optimal performance for this variant.
pitfalls: Does not work correctly with negative edge weights.
Incorrectly updating distances can lead to suboptimal paths.
Using an inefficient priority queue can degrade performance significantly (e.g., array scan).
For dense graphs, a simple O(V^2) approach might be faster due to constant factors.
invariants: All vertices in the priority queue have a tentative distance from the source.
For any vertex already extracted from the priority queue, its distance is finalized and represents the shortest path.
All edges from settled vertices to unsettled vertices have been relaxed.
The priority queue always contains vertices whose current shortest path estimate is the minimum among all unsettled vertices.

title: Dijkstra on sparse graphs
when_to_use: Use when finding the shortest paths from a single source to all other vertices in a graph with non-negative edge weights, especially efficient for sparse graphs.
description: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest paths from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a graph with non-negative edge weights. For sparse graphs, it achieves an optimal time complexity of O(M log N) using a priority queue or set.
tips: Use a min-priority queue or `std::set` for efficient vertex selection.
For `std::set`, remove and re-insert vertices to update distances.
Overload comparison operators when storing only vertex indices for performance.
Consider the variant without explicit decrease-key operations for practical speedups.
pitfalls: Do not use Dijkstra's algorithm on graphs with negative edge weights.
Failing to correctly update distances in the priority queue can lead to incorrect paths.
`std::priority_queue` sorts descending by default; ensure min-heap behavior.
Naively updating elements in a `std::priority_queue` is not supported; re-insert.
invariants: dist[u] for any extracted vertex u is its final shortest path distance.
All vertices in the priority queue have a tentative distance from the source.
For any edge (u, v), dist[v] is always less than or equal to dist[u] + weight(u, v) after processing u.

title: Dijkstra on sparse graphs
when_to_use: When you need to find the shortest paths from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a graph with non-negative edge weights, especially efficient for sparse graphs.
description: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest paths from a single source to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. It uses a priority queue to greedily explore vertices, achieving O(E log V) complexity with a binary heap.
tips: Use a priority queue (min-heap) to efficiently retrieve the unvisited vertex with the smallest distance.
Initialize all distances to infinity and the source distance to zero.
When using a standard library priority queue that doesn't support decrease-key, push new (distance, vertex) pairs and ignore stale entries.
For sparse graphs, a priority queue (heap-based) implementation is generally more efficient than an adjacency matrix or set-based approach.
Store pairs of (distance, vertex_id) in the priority queue.
pitfalls: Does not work correctly with negative edge weights; Bellman-Ford or SPFA is needed.
Forgetting to mark vertices as visited or handle duplicate entries in the priority queue can lead to incorrectness or TLE.
Incorrectly updating distances without pushing new entries or removing old ones (if using a set).
Overlooking the 'stale entry' check when using a priority queue without decrease-key functionality.
Using an adjacency matrix for sparse graphs can lead to O(V^2) complexity, which is inefficient.
invariants: At the start of each iteration, dist[u] holds the shortest distance found so far from the source to u.
All vertices already extracted from the priority queue have their final shortest distance determined.
For any vertex v in the priority queue, pq.top().distance is the shortest distance to v found through already processed vertices.
The priority queue contains (distance, vertex) pairs for all reachable, unvisited vertices, ordered by distance.

title: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
when_to_use: Finds single-source shortest paths in a weighted directed graph, especially when negative edge weights are present. It can also detect negative cycles reachable from the source.
description: Bellman-Ford iteratively relaxes all edges n-1 times to find single-source shortest paths in a weighted directed graph. It runs in O(VE) time and can detect negative cycles.
tips: Initialize source distance to 0 and all other distances to infinity.
Perform exactly n-1 full passes, relaxing all edges in each pass.
An optional nth pass can detect negative cycles if any distance improves.
Store predecessor pointers to reconstruct the shortest path.
Early termination optimization: stop if a pass makes no distance changes.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling unreachable vertices (distances remaining infinity).
Not detecting negative cycles when they exist, leading to incorrect path lengths.
Slower than Dijkstra's algorithm for graphs without negative edge weights.
Forgetting to initialize distances properly before the first pass.
Not considering the directed nature of edges during relaxation.
invariants: After k phases, dist[u] holds the shortest path length from source to u using at most k edges.
For any edge (u, v) with weight w, dist[v] <= dist[u] + w if dist[u] is finite and v is reachable.
dist[source] always remains 0 throughout the algorithm.
If no negative cycle exists, dist[u] will converge to the true shortest path length after n-1 phases.

title: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
when_to_use: Use when finding single-source shortest paths in a weighted graph that may contain negative edge weights. It is also essential for detecting and identifying negative cycles reachable from the source.
description: The Bellman-Ford algorithm iteratively relaxes all edges in a graph V-1 times to find single-source shortest paths, correctly handling negative edge weights. It runs in O(V*E) time, where V is the number of vertices and E is the number of edges.
tips: Initialize source distance to 0 and all other distances to infinity.
Perform V-1 iterations, relaxing all edges in each iteration.
A V-th iteration where any edge can still be relaxed indicates a negative cycle.
To reconstruct a negative cycle, trace predecessors from a vertex relaxed in the V-th iteration.
Use a predecessor array to reconstruct the shortest path to any vertex.
pitfalls: Does not work if shortest paths are required in the presence of negative cycles (distances become -infinity).
Forgetting to properly initialize distances can lead to incorrect results.
Not handling unreachable vertices, which should retain their initial infinity distance.
Potential for integer overflow if distances or edge weights are very large.
Incorrectly assuming V iterations are sufficient for shortest paths if negative cycles are not handled.
invariants: After k iterations, dist[u] holds the shortest path distance from source to u using at most k edges.
dist[u] always represents an upper bound on the true shortest path distance from the source to u.
If no negative cycle exists, after V-1 iterations, dist[u] is the actual shortest path distance for all u.
The predecessor array parent[u] correctly points to the previous vertex on the current shortest path to u.

title: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
when_to_use: Use when finding shortest paths in a graph that may contain negative edge weights. It is also suitable for detecting the presence of negative cycles reachable from a source.
description: The Bellman-Ford algorithm computes shortest paths from a single source to all other vertices in a weighted graph, even with negative edge weights. It iteratively relaxes all edges V-1 times, achieving O(VE) complexity.
tips: Initialize source distance to 0 and all other distances to infinity.
Perform exactly V-1 relaxation passes over all edges to guarantee shortest paths if no negative cycles exist.
A V-th relaxation pass can detect negative cycles if any distance value decreases.
The SPFA optimization uses a queue to process only vertices whose distances have changed, potentially improving average-case performance.
Be mindful of potential integer overflow when summing many negative edge weights, especially for large graphs.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling negative cycles can lead to infinite loops if not explicitly checked.
SPFA's worst-case time complexity remains O(VE), making it inefficient for certain graph structures.
Bellman-Ford cannot find shortest paths if a negative cycle is reachable from the source.
For graphs with only non-negative weights, Dijkstra's algorithm is significantly faster.
The algorithm's O(VE) complexity can be too slow for very dense graphs.
invariants: After k iterations, dist[v] holds the shortest path from source to v using at most k edges.
If no negative cycle exists, dist[v] eventually converges to the true shortest path distance.
For any edge (u, v) with weight w, dist[v] <= dist[u] + w holds after convergence (if no negative cycle).
Vertices in the SPFA queue are those whose dist value has been updated and whose neighbors might need relaxation.

title: 0-1 BFS
when_to_use: Use when finding shortest paths in a graph where edge weights are restricted to only 0 or 1. It efficiently determines minimum distances from a source to all reachable nodes, outperforming Dijkstra's for this specific weight constraint.
description: 0-1 BFS is a graph traversal algorithm that finds shortest paths from a single source in a graph where all edge weights are either 0 or 1. It achieves this in O(V + E) time complexity by using a deque to prioritize 0-weight edges.
tips: Use a deque to maintain order: 0-weight edges go to front, 1-weight edges to back.
Initialize distances to infinity and source distance to 0.
Mark nodes as visited when first extracted from the deque to prevent redundant processing.
Can be adapted for grid problems where movement costs vary (e.g., 0 for straight, 1 for diagonal).
pitfalls: Forgetting to update distances for 0-weight edges before adding to deque front.
Incorrectly handling visited states, leading to TLE or incorrect paths.
Using a standard queue instead of a deque, which degrades performance.
Not resetting distances or visited arrays for multiple queries in competitive programming.
invariants: All nodes in the deque have a distance value less than or equal to any node not yet processed.
Nodes added to the front of the deque (0-weight edges) always have a distance equal to the node they came from.
Nodes added to the back of the deque (1-weight edges) always have a distance one greater than the node they came from.
The distance to any node 'v' stored in 'dist[v]' is the shortest path found so far from the source to 'v'.

title: 0-1 BFS
when_to_use: Use when finding the shortest path in a graph where all edge weights are either 0 or 1. It efficiently computes single-source shortest paths, outperforming Dijkstra's for this specific weight constraint.
description: 0-1 BFS is a graph traversal algorithm that finds the single-source shortest paths in a graph where all edge weights are restricted to 0 or 1. It uses a deque to process nodes, achieving an optimal time complexity of O(V + E).
tips: Always push 0-weight edges to the front of the deque.
Push 1-weight edges to the back of the deque.
Initialize all distances to infinity, except the source to zero.
Use a visited array or check if current path is shorter to avoid cycles and redundant work.
It's an efficient alternative to Dijkstra's for binary-weighted graphs.
pitfalls: Pushing 0-weight edges to the back or 1-weight edges to the front of the deque.
Not correctly initializing distances or the source node.
Failing to update a node's distance if a shorter path is discovered.
Applying this algorithm to graphs with negative or general positive edge weights.
Forgetting to handle disconnected components if all nodes need to be reached.
invariants: When a node 'u' is extracted from the front of the deque, 'dist[u]' is its shortest path from the source.
The deque always contains nodes whose distances are either 'D' or 'D+1', where 'D' is the distance of the last node extracted.
All nodes with distance 'D' are at the front of the deque, followed by nodes with distance 'D+1'.
No node is added to the deque if a shorter path to it has already been finalized.

title: 0-1 BFS
when_to_use: Use when finding the shortest path in a graph where all edge weights are either 0 or 1. It efficiently determines minimum costs without a full priority queue.
description: A specialized Breadth-First Search variant that finds shortest paths in graphs with only 0 or 1 edge weights. It uses a deque to process 0-weight edges before 1-weight edges, achieving O(V+E) time complexity.
tips: Utilize a deque to maintain the order of processing nodes.
Add nodes reached via 0-weight edges to the front of the deque.
Add nodes reached via 1-weight edges to the back of the deque.
Initialize all distances to infinity, source to zero.
pitfalls: Incorrectly using a standard queue instead of a deque.
Failing to initialize distances to infinity for all nodes except the source.
Not updating a node's distance if a shorter path is discovered.
Applying to graphs with negative or arbitrary positive edge weights.
invariants: All nodes currently in the deque have a distance value of either `k` or `k+1` for some integer `k`.
Nodes pushed to the front of the deque always have a distance equal to the current node's distance.
Nodes pushed to the back of the deque always have a distance one greater than the current node's distance.
The distance `dist[u]` for any node `u` popped from the deque is its shortest path distance.

title: D´Esopo-Pape algorithm
when_to_use: Use when finding single-source shortest paths in graphs with potentially negative edge weights, especially when the graph is sparse or has few negative cycles. It offers a practical speedup over standard Bellman-Ford.
description: The D'Esopo-Pape algorithm is a queue-based variant of Bellman-Ford for single-source shortest paths, optimizing relaxation order using a deque. It typically performs better than standard Bellman-Ford, with an average time complexity often closer to O(E) or O(E log V) in practice, but worst-case O(VE).
tips: Use a deque to prioritize nodes: newly discovered nodes go to the front, re-visited nodes go to the back.
Maintain a `visited` array or counter for each node to track its state (not in queue, in queue, being processed).
Initialize distances to infinity for all nodes except the source, which is zero.
Keep track of visit counts for each vertex to detect negative cycles.
pitfalls: Worst-case time complexity remains O(VE), similar to standard Bellman-Ford.
Fails or loops indefinitely if negative cycles are reachable from the source, unless explicitly handled.
Performance can degrade significantly on dense graphs or graphs with many negative edges.
Requires careful implementation of deque logic for optimal performance.
invariants: For any vertex `u`, `dist[u]` holds the shortest path distance found so far from the source to `u`.
All vertices in the deque are candidates for relaxation and have had their distance improved at least once.
If a vertex `u` is not in the deque, its `dist[u]` value is either finalized or has not yet been improved.
The `dist[u]` values are always non-increasing during the algorithm's execution.

title: D´Esopo-Pape algorithm
when_to_use: Use this algorithm for finding single-source shortest paths in graphs with non-negative or negative edge weights, but without negative cycles. It is often faster than Bellman-Ford in practice for many graph types.
description: The D'Esopo-Pape algorithm finds single-source shortest paths in graphs with negative edge weights (no negative cycles) by using a deque to manage vertex relaxation across three distinct sets. It offers an average time complexity often better than O(VE), though worst-case remains O(VE).
tips: Utilize a deque to efficiently manage vertices in the M1 set.
Prioritize re-inserting M0 vertices to the front of the deque for faster re-evaluation.
Maintain distinct sets (M0, M1, M2) to track the processing status of each vertex.
Initialize source distance to zero and all other distances to infinity.
Always update the predecessor array `p` whenever a distance `d` is improved.
pitfalls: The algorithm does not correctly handle graphs containing negative cycles.
Can be slower than Dijkstra's algorithm for graphs with only non-negative edge weights.
Incorrectly placing re-evaluated vertices (front vs. back of deque) can lead to performance degradation or incorrect results.
Forgetting to update predecessor pointers can lead to incorrect path reconstruction.
invariants: For any vertex v, d[v] is always an upper bound on the true shortest path distance from the source.
All vertices currently in M1 are present in the deque.
If a vertex u is in M0, it has been processed from the deque at least once.
The predecessor p[v] correctly points to the vertex that led to the current d[v] value.

title: Floyd-Warshall Algorithm
signals: shortest path between any two nodes
when_to_use: Use when needing to find the shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in a weighted graph, especially if negative edge weights are present but no negative cycles.
description: The Floyd-Warshall algorithm is a dynamic programming approach that finds the shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in a weighted graph. It iteratively considers all possible intermediate vertices to update path lengths, achieving an O(N^3) time complexity.
tips: Initialize direct edge weights and infinity for non-existent edges.
Set distance from a vertex to itself to zero initially.
Ensure the intermediate vertex loop (k) is the outermost loop.
Can detect negative cycles if d[i][i] becomes negative after processing.
Path reconstruction is possible by storing predecessor vertices.
pitfalls: Incorrect loop order (k must be outermost) will yield incorrect results.
Improper handling of 'infinity' values can lead to overflow or incorrect comparisons.
Forgetting to initialize d[i][i] = 0 for all i.
The algorithm does not work correctly in the presence of negative weight cycles.
High space complexity (O(N^2)) can be an issue for very large graphs.
invariants: After k iterations, d[i][j] stores the shortest path from i to j using only vertices {1, ..., k} as intermediate nodes.
d[i][j] always represents a valid path length or infinity, never an invalid negative value unless a negative cycle is detected.
For any i, d[i][i] is 0 if no negative cycle involving i exists, otherwise it can become negative.

title: Floyd-Warshall Algorithm
signals: shortest path between any two vertices
when_to_use: When you need to find the shortest paths between all possible pairs of vertices in a graph. It is particularly suitable for dense graphs or when negative edge weights are present, as long as no negative cycles are reachable.
description: Floyd-Warshall is a dynamic programming algorithm that finds the shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in a weighted graph. It iteratively considers all possible intermediate vertices to update path distances, achieving an O(V^3) time complexity.
tips: Initialize distances: direct edge weights, infinity for no direct edge, zero for self-loops.
The outermost loop must iterate through intermediate vertices (k).
Can detect negative cycles if d[i][i] becomes negative after processing all k.
Path reconstruction requires an additional predecessor matrix to store intermediate vertices.
Consider using for transitive closure by setting edge weights to 1 and infinity.
pitfalls: Incorrect loop order (k not outermost) leads to incorrect results.
Integer overflow with large path sums if not using appropriate data types.
Not suitable for sparse graphs due to its O(V^3) complexity.
Forgetting to initialize self-loop distances to zero.
Path reconstruction can be tricky to implement correctly, especially recursively.
invariants: After the k-th iteration, dist[i][j] contains the shortest path from i to j considering only vertices 0 to k-1 as intermediate nodes.
For any pair (i, j), dist[i][j] is always greater than or equal to the true shortest path distance from i to j (or infinity if unreachable).
dist[i][i] remains 0 unless a negative cycle is detected, in which case it becomes negative.

title: Floyd-Warshall Algorithm
when_to_use: Use when you need to find the shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in a weighted graph, especially if it contains negative edge weights but no negative cycles. It is well-suited for dense graphs.
description: The Floyd-Warshall algorithm computes all-pairs shortest paths in a weighted graph by iteratively considering all vertices as intermediate nodes. It is a dynamic programming approach with a time complexity of O(V^3).
tips: Initialize dist[i][j] with edge weights, 0 for i=j, and infinity otherwise.
After running, check dist[t][t] < 0 for any t to detect negative cycles.
Use a sufficiently large negative value for -INF to prevent integer overflow when dealing with negative distances.
Consider long long for distance values to accommodate large or highly negative path sums.
pitfalls: Floating-point errors accumulate exponentially, making it unsuitable for high-precision float weights.
Standard algorithm does not correctly handle paths involving negative cycles; special detection is required.
Integer overflow can occur if distances become extremely negative without proper capping.
High cubic time complexity (O(V^3)) makes it inefficient for sparse or very large graphs.
invariants: After the k-th outer loop iteration, dist[i][j] holds the shortest path from i to j using only vertices 0 to k-1 as intermediate nodes.
For any i, dist[i][i] is non-positive; if dist[i][i] < 0, a negative cycle exists through i.
All dist[i][j] values are monotonically non-increasing throughout the algorithm's execution.
The dist matrix always contains valid path lengths or an indicator for unreachable/infinite paths.

title: Number of paths of fixed length / Shortest paths of fixed length
when_to_use: Use when finding the number or shortest length of paths between all pairs of vertices that consist of exactly a given number of edges. It's suitable for problems requiring path properties over a fixed number of steps.
description: This technique extends matrix multiplication to find path properties (count or shortest length) of a fixed number of edges in a graph. It leverages binary exponentiation on an adjacency matrix with a modified 'min-plus' or 'sum-product' operation, achieving O(N^3 log K) complexity.
tips: For shortest paths, initialize non-existent edges with infinity in the adjacency matrix.
For counting paths, initialize non-existent edges with zero in the adjacency matrix.
The base matrix for exponentiation is the adjacency matrix representing paths of length 1.
Utilize binary exponentiation (exponentiation by squaring) for efficient computation of matrix powers.
Understand the analogy between standard matrix multiplication and the modified 'min-plus' or 'sum-product' operations.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling the identity matrix for base cases, especially for k=0 paths.
Off-by-one errors when defining the base matrix or the number of matrix multiplications.
Forgetting to initialize non-existent edges to appropriate values (infinity for shortest, zero for counts).
Integer overflow for path counts or sum of weights if not using sufficiently large data types.
Misunderstanding the specific 'min-plus' or 'sum-product' operations required for the problem.
invariants: After k matrix multiplications, L_k[i][j] stores the shortest path length (or count) from i to j using exactly k edges.
The original adjacency matrix G remains unchanged throughout the exponentiation process.
Each element L_k[i][j] represents a valid path length or count, respecting the problem's constraints (e.g., non-negative lengths).
The modified matrix multiplication operation correctly combines paths of length A and B to form paths of length A+B.

title: Number of paths of fixed length / Shortest paths of fixed length
when_to_use: Use this approach when you need to count the number of paths or find the shortest path between two nodes in a graph, specifically constrained to an exact number of edges or at most a certain number of edges.
description: This technique leverages matrix exponentiation on an adjacency matrix to find paths of a fixed length k. For problems involving paths of length up to k, the graph is modified by duplicating vertices and adding specific edges. The typical complexity is O(N^3 log K) due to matrix exponentiation.
tips: For exactly K edges, compute the K-th power of the adjacency matrix.
For at most K edges, duplicate each vertex v to v' and add edges (v, v') and (v', v') with appropriate weights (1 for counting, 0 for shortest path).
Matrix multiplication for shortest paths uses min(+) instead of (+*) operations.
Binary exponentiation (exponentiation by squaring) efficiently computes matrix powers in O(log K) matrix multiplications.
pitfalls: Naive matrix multiplication is O(N^3), leading to high complexity for large N.
Incorrectly handling edge weights (e.g., for shortest paths) can lead to wrong results.
For 'at most K' paths, ensure the graph modification correctly establishes the bijection to 'exactly K+1' paths.
Overflow issues can arise when counting paths if intermediate sums exceed standard integer limits.
invariants: After p matrix multiplications, M^p[i][j] represents the count/shortest path length between i and j using exactly p edges.
The modified graph for 'at most k' paths ensures a path of length m <= k in the original graph corresponds to a path of length k+1 in the new graph.
The adjacency matrix M accurately reflects direct connections (or weights) between vertices.
Matrix exponentiation maintains the property that M^(a+b) = M^a * M^b for the defined matrix multiplication.

title: Minimum spanning tree - Prim's algorithm
signals: growing a tree from a single source
when_to_use: Use Prim's algorithm to find a minimum spanning tree in a connected, undirected, weighted graph. It is often preferred for dense graphs due to its O(V^2) or O(E log V) complexity.
description: Prim's algorithm is a greedy algorithm that builds a minimum spanning tree by iteratively adding the cheapest edge connecting a vertex in the growing tree to one outside it. It typically runs in O(E log V) time with a binary heap or O(V^2) with an adjacency matrix.
tips: Use a priority queue to efficiently retrieve the minimum weight edge connecting to the MST.
Initialize distances for all vertices to infinity, except the starting vertex (0).
Mark vertices as visited once added to the MST to prevent cycles and redundant processing.
Consider the O(V^2) array-based implementation for dense graphs where E is close to V^2.
Update edge weights in the priority queue when a shorter connection to the MST is found.
pitfalls: Not suitable for disconnected graphs; it will only find an MST for the component of the starting vertex.
Forgetting to handle edge cases like graphs with no edges or a single vertex.
Incorrectly updating priority queue entries, leading to suboptimal edges or incorrect results.
Assuming non-negative edge weights; Prim's works with negative weights as long as there are no negative cycles.
High memory usage for dense graphs if using adjacency matrix for very large V.
invariants: The set of vertices already included in the MST forms a connected component.
For any vertex not yet in the MST, its associated key/distance stores the minimum weight of an edge connecting it to a vertex already in the MST.
All edges chosen so far are part of the minimum spanning tree of the subgraph induced by the visited vertices.

title: Minimum spanning tree - Prim's algorithm
when_to_use: When you need to find a minimum spanning tree in a connected, weighted graph, especially efficient for dense graphs or when starting from an arbitrary vertex.
description: Prim's algorithm is a greedy algorithm that constructs a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) by iteratively adding the cheapest edge connecting a vertex in the growing tree to a vertex outside it. It typically runs in O(E log V) with a priority queue or O(V^2) with an adjacency matrix.
tips: Initialize distances (or minimum edge weights) for all vertices not in the MST to infinity.
Use a priority queue to efficiently retrieve the minimum weight edge connecting to the current MST.
Mark vertices as visited or included in the MST to prevent cycles and redundant processing.
Consider using an adjacency matrix for dense graphs (O(V^2)) and an adjacency list with a priority queue for sparse graphs (O(E log V)).
pitfalls: Failing to correctly update edge weights to neighbors when a new vertex is added to the MST.
Not handling disconnected graphs, which will result in only an MST for the starting component.
Incorrectly implementing the priority queue's decrease-key operation or its equivalent.
Overlooking edge cases like graphs with a single vertex or no edges.
invariants: The set of edges chosen so far forms a tree (no cycles) and connects a subset of vertices.
At each step, the chosen edge is the minimum weight edge connecting a vertex in the current MST to a vertex outside it.
All vertices included in the current MST are connected by the chosen edges.
The current set of chosen edges is a minimum spanning tree for the vertices it connects.

title: Minimum spanning tree - Prim's algorithm
when_to_use: Use Prim's algorithm to find a minimum spanning tree in a connected, weighted graph. It is efficient for both dense graphs (O(V^2)) and sparse graphs (O(E log V)) depending on the implementation.
description: Prim's algorithm is a greedy algorithm that builds an MST by iteratively adding the cheapest edge connecting a vertex in the growing tree to a vertex outside the tree. It has a time complexity of O(V^2) for dense graphs using an adjacency matrix or O(E log V) for sparse graphs using a priority queue.
tips: For dense graphs, an O(V^2) implementation using an an adjacency matrix and simple array scans is efficient.
For sparse graphs, use a priority queue (min-heap) with an adjacency list to achieve O(E log V) complexity.
Initialize the minimum edge weights for all unselected vertices to infinity, except for the starting vertex which is 0.
When a new vertex is added to the MST, iterate through its neighbors to update their minimum connection weights if a shorter path is found.
Keep track of the parent vertex for each edge to reconstruct the MST.
pitfalls: Incorrectly updating the `min_e` array or priority queue entries can lead to an incorrect MST or performance issues.
Forgetting to mark vertices as 'selected' can lead to cycles or infinite loops.
Not handling disconnected graphs: Prim's will only find an MST for the connected component of the starting vertex.
Using the O(V^2) approach for very sparse graphs will be slower than the O(E log V) approach.
Off-by-one errors when dealing with 0-indexed vs. 1-indexed graphs.
invariants: All selected vertices and edges form a connected component of the final MST.
For any unselected vertex `v`, `min_e[v]` stores the minimum weight of an edge connecting `v` to the currently selected component.
The set of edges chosen so far always forms a forest where each tree is a component of the MST.

title: Minimum spanning tree - Kruskal's algorithm
when_to_use: Use when you need to find a minimum spanning tree in a graph, especially when the graph is sparse or when edge weights are the primary concern for selection.
description: Kruskal's algorithm constructs a Minimum Spanning Tree by iteratively adding the lowest-weight edge that connects two previously disconnected components, avoiding cycles. It has a time complexity of O(E log E) or O(E log V) due to sorting edges and Union-Find operations.
tips: Always sort all edges by weight in non-decreasing order initially.
Use a Disjoint Set Union (Union-Find) data structure to efficiently track connected components and detect cycles.
When adding an edge, ensure its endpoints belong to different components to avoid forming a cycle.
The algorithm naturally handles disconnected graphs by finding an MST for each component (a minimum spanning forest).
pitfalls: Forgetting to sort edges or sorting them incorrectly (e.g., decreasing order for MST).
Not correctly implementing the Union-Find operations, leading to incorrect cycle detection or component merging.
Adding an edge that connects two vertices already in the same component, which creates a cycle.
Incorrectly handling graphs with duplicate edge weights; the order of equal-weight edges can matter for specific MSTs but not for the total weight.
invariants: The set of selected edges always forms a forest (no cycles).
Each component in the Union-Find structure represents a connected subtree of the potential MST.
All edges considered so far, if they connect different components, are part of the minimum spanning forest for those components.
Edges are processed in non-decreasing order of their weights.

title: Minimum spanning tree - Kruskal's algorithm
when_to_use: Use when you need to find a minimum spanning tree in a connected, undirected, weighted graph, especially when the graph is sparse. It's suitable for graphs with many vertices and relatively few edges.
description: Kruskal's algorithm is a greedy algorithm that finds a minimum spanning tree for a connected, undirected, weighted graph. It works by sorting all edges by weight and iteratively adding the smallest weight edge that does not form a cycle, achieving an O(E log E) or O(E log V) time complexity using a Disjoint Set Union data structure.
tips: Sort all edges by weight in non-decreasing order as the first step.
Utilize a Disjoint Set Union (DSU) data structure to efficiently check for cycles and merge components.
The algorithm terminates when V-1 edges have been added to the MST, where V is the number of vertices.
Handle disconnected graphs by ensuring the final forest has V-1 edges; otherwise, no spanning tree exists.
pitfalls: Forgetting to sort edges by weight will lead to an incorrect MST.
Incorrectly implementing the DSU's find or union operations can lead to wrong cycle detection or performance issues.
Not handling edge cases like graphs with a single vertex or disconnected components.
Using an inefficient cycle detection method instead of DSU, which can degrade performance significantly.
invariants: The set of edges chosen so far forms a forest, where each tree is a component of a minimum spanning tree.
For any two vertices in the same component of the current forest, the path connecting them consists only of edges from a minimum spanning tree.
All edges processed up to the current point, which do not form a cycle with already chosen edges, are included in the current forest.
The Disjoint Set Union structure correctly maintains the connected components of the graph formed by the edges selected for the MST.

title: Minimum spanning tree - Kruskal with Disjoint Set Union
when_to_use: Use when you need to find a minimum spanning tree in a graph, especially when the graph is sparse or the number of edges is not excessively large compared to vertices.
description: Sorts all graph edges by weight and iteratively adds the cheapest edge that connects two previously disconnected components using a Disjoint Set Union (DSU) data structure. This process builds an MST with O(M log M) or O(M log N) time complexity.
tips: Sort edges by weight in non-decreasing order.
Initialize DSU such that each vertex is in its own set.
Use path compression and union by rank/size for optimal DSU performance.
Stop adding edges once N-1 edges have been included in the MST.
Consider edge cases like disconnected graphs or graphs with parallel edges.
pitfalls: Forgetting to sort edges, leading to an incorrect MST.
Inefficient DSU implementation without path compression or union by rank/size.
Not handling graphs where not all vertices are reachable (results in a minimum spanning forest).
Incorrectly counting edges, potentially adding too many or too few.
Integer overflow if edge weights or total weight are very large.
invariants: After processing k edges, the chosen edges form a forest where each tree is a component of the MST.
Any edge not yet processed has a weight greater than or equal to all processed edges.
The DSU structure accurately represents the connected components formed by the chosen edges.
The number of edges in the MST being built is always less than or equal to N-1.

title: Second Best Minimum Spanning Tree
when_to_use: Use this when you need to identify an alternative network configuration that is marginally more expensive than the optimal one, or for robustness analysis in network design.
description: This algorithm identifies the spanning tree with the second minimum total edge weight by finding the primary MST and then systematically replacing one MST edge with a non-MST edge. The overall time complexity is typically O(E log E + E log V).
tips: First compute the primary MST using Kruskal's or Prim's algorithm.
For each non-MST edge (u, v), adding it to the MST creates a unique cycle.
To find the heaviest edge on the path between u and v in the MST, use techniques like Binary Lifting or a specialized LCA structure.
The second best MST is formed by replacing one MST edge with one non-MST edge.
Consider using Kruskal's for MST as it naturally sorts edges.
pitfalls: Incorrectly identifying the heaviest edge on the cycle formed by adding a non-MST edge.
Forgetting to handle disconnected graphs (MST implies connected).
Not considering all non-MST edges for replacement.
Edge cases with multiple edges of the same weight.
Overlooking the need for efficient path maximum query in the MST.
invariants: The primary MST T always connects all vertices with minimum total weight.
Any candidate second-best MST T' is a spanning tree with exactly V-1 edges.
Each candidate T' is formed by adding one non-MST edge and removing one MST edge from T.
The removed MST edge is the heaviest on the unique cycle formed by adding the non-MST edge.

title: Second Best Minimum Spanning Tree
when_to_use: Use when you need to find a spanning tree with the second smallest total edge weight, often for redundancy or alternative network path planning. It's applicable in scenarios requiring fault tolerance beyond a single optimal solution.
description: This algorithm finds a spanning tree with the second minimum total edge weight in a connected, undirected graph. It involves finding the primary MST, then modifying it by replacing one MST edge with a non-MST edge. Complexity is typically O(E log E + V log V).
tips: First, compute the primary Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) using an algorithm like Kruskal's or Prim's.
For each edge not in the MST, adding it to the MST creates a unique cycle. Find the maximum weight edge on this cycle.
The second best MST candidate is formed by replacing the heaviest edge on the cycle with the non-MST edge.
The minimum of all such candidate MSTs is the second best MST.
Efficiently find the maximum weight edge on a path in the MST using techniques like Binary Lifting for LCA.
pitfalls: Naively recomputing an entire MST for each removed edge is inefficient (O(V * E log E)).
Incorrectly identifying the maximum weight edge on the cycle formed by adding a non-MST edge.
Forgetting to consider all non-MST edges as potential replacements.
Handling graphs with duplicate edge weights requires careful tie-breaking or consideration of all valid second MSTs.
Assuming the graph is connected; the algorithm requires a connected graph to find a spanning tree.
invariants: The primary MST is always a valid spanning tree with minimum total weight.
Adding any non-MST edge (u,v) to the MST creates exactly one cycle.
Removing the heaviest edge from the cycle formed by (u,v) results in a new spanning tree.
The second best MST's weight is greater than or equal to the primary MST's weight.

title: Second Best Minimum Spanning Tree
when_to_use: When you need to find a spanning tree with the second smallest total edge weight, typically after finding the primary Minimum Spanning Tree.
description: This algorithm finds the second best Minimum Spanning Tree by iterating through non-MST edges, adding each to the MST to form a cycle, and then removing the maximum weight edge on that cycle (excluding the added edge). It leverages Kruskal's for the initial MST and LCA with binary lifting for efficient path maximum queries, achieving an overall time complexity of O(E log E + E log V).
tips: Precompute LCA and maximum edge weights on paths using binary lifting for efficiency.
The initial MST can be found using Kruskal's or Prim's algorithm.
When adding a non-MST edge, the cycle formed will always involve the two endpoints of the added edge and their LCA in the MST.
Carefully handle the case where the added edge has a weight less than or equal to the maximum edge on the cycle; this is a candidate for the second MST.
Store the maximum edge weight on the path from a node to its ancestors during binary lifting.
pitfalls: Incorrectly identifying the maximum weight edge on the cycle when adding a non-MST edge.
Forgetting to exclude the newly added edge itself when finding the maximum edge on the cycle.
Inefficiently finding the maximum edge on the cycle without LCA/binary lifting, leading to O(VE) complexity.
Errors in LCA or binary lifting implementation, especially path maximum queries.
Not considering all non-MST edges, or not correctly updating the minimum second MST weight.
invariants: The initial MST is always a valid spanning tree with minimum total weight.
For any non-MST edge (u, v) added, a unique cycle is formed in the current graph.
The cycle formed by adding (u, v) always includes the path between u and v in the original MST, and the edge (u, v) itself.
After removing the maximum weight edge (excluding the added edge) from the cycle, the graph remains a spanning tree.

title: Kirchhoff's theorem. Finding the number of spanning trees
when_to_use: Used to determine the exact count of distinct spanning trees in a connected undirected graph, even when multiple edges are present. It is particularly useful for problems requiring precise enumeration of graph structures.
description: This theorem calculates the number of spanning trees in a graph by finding the determinant of any cofactor of its Laplacian matrix. The typical complexity for an N-vertex graph is O(N^3) due to matrix determinant calculation.
tips: Construct the Laplacian matrix (L = D - A) carefully, ensuring correct degrees and adjacency entries.
Account for multiple edges by summing their counts in the adjacency matrix and degree matrix.
Any cofactor (minor) of the Laplacian matrix will yield the correct number of spanning trees.
Ensure the graph is connected; otherwise, the number of spanning trees is zero.
pitfalls: Incorrectly calculating vertex degrees, especially with multiple edges or self-loops.
Using the determinant of the full Laplacian matrix, which is always zero.
Errors in matrix subtraction (D - A) when forming the Laplacian.
Numerical precision issues when calculating determinants for very large matrices.
invariants: The sum of degrees in the graph is always twice the number of edges.
The sum of any row or column in the Laplacian matrix is zero.
The Laplacian matrix is symmetric for an undirected graph.
The determinant of the full Laplacian matrix is always zero.

title: Prüfer code
when_to_use: Use when counting labeled trees or converting between a labeled tree and a unique sequence representation for combinatorial analysis. It provides a bijection for problems involving N labeled nodes.
description: Prüfer code is a unique sequence of length N-2 that represents a labeled tree with N nodes. It is constructed by iteratively removing the smallest-labeled leaf and recording its neighbor, and can be reconstructed back into the tree. The encoding and decoding processes typically run in O(N log N) time using a min-priority queue or O(N) with careful degree tracking.
tips: Maintain an array of node degrees for efficient updates.
Use a min-priority queue to quickly find the smallest-labeled leaf.
For decoding, keep track of available nodes (not yet connected or part of the sequence).
The Prüfer sequence has N-2 elements for a tree with N nodes.
pitfalls: Incorrectly updating node degrees after removal.
Off-by-one errors when dealing with 0-indexed vs. 1-indexed labels.
Forgetting to handle the last two nodes during reconstruction.
Misinterpreting the sequence elements as the leaves themselves rather than their neighbors.
Not considering the edge case of a tree with only two nodes (empty Prüfer code).
invariants: The Prüfer sequence always contains N-2 elements for a tree with N nodes.
At each step of encoding, the removed node is a leaf with the smallest label among all current leaves.
During decoding, the smallest available node is always connected to the current sequence element.
The sum of degrees of all nodes in the original tree is 2*(N-1).

title: Prüfer code
when_to_use: When needing to encode a labeled tree into a unique sequence of N-2 numbers, particularly when an efficient linear-time construction is required.
description: The Prüfer code algorithm converts a labeled tree with N vertices into a unique sequence of N-2 numbers by iteratively removing the smallest-numbered leaf and recording its neighbor. This process can be optimized to run in O(N) time.
tips: Use a degree array to efficiently track vertex degrees.
Maintain a pointer to the current smallest leaf candidate for linear time.
When a leaf is removed, decrement its neighbor's degree.
If a neighbor becomes a leaf, consider it for future removal.
The linear time approach avoids explicit priority queue operations.
pitfalls: Incorrectly identifying the smallest-numbered leaf after removals.
Not updating degrees correctly, leading to wrong leaf identification.
Off-by-one errors in the number of iterations (N-2).
Failing to handle the initial setup for the moving pointer correctly.
Incorrectly managing the state of vertices that become leaves.
invariants: After 'k' iterations, 'k' leaves have been removed and 'k' numbers have been added to the Prüfer code.
The current tree always contains 'N - k' vertices and 'N - k - 1' edges.
All vertices with degree 1 are considered leaves and are candidates for removal.
The Prüfer code generated so far represents a partial encoding of the original tree.

title: Prüfer code
when_to_use: Use to uniquely encode a labeled tree into a sequence of numbers or reconstruct a labeled tree from such a sequence. It's valuable for counting labeled trees and problems involving tree generation.
description: Prüfer code uniquely encodes a labeled tree into a sequence by iteratively removing the smallest-labeled leaf and recording its neighbor. This process, which can be implemented in O(N log N) or O(N) time, continues until only two nodes remain.
tips: Maintain an array of node degrees for O(1) degree updates.
Use a min-priority queue or a `next_leaf` pointer for efficient smallest leaf retrieval.
A node becomes a leaf candidate when its degree reduces to 1.
The Prüfer sequence always has length N-2 for N nodes.
The degree of a node in the tree is one more than its occurrences in the code.
pitfalls: Forgetting to update neighbor degrees after removing a leaf.
Incorrectly identifying the next smallest leaf, especially when new leaves are formed.
Off-by-one errors in sequence length or node indexing.
Not handling the base case of a tree with only two nodes (empty code).
Applying to unlabeled trees, where Prüfer codes are not unique.
invariants: The generated sequence contains `k` elements after `k` leaf removals.
All nodes with degree 1 are correctly identified as leaves.
The sum of degrees of all nodes in the current tree equals `2 * (number_of_nodes_remaining - 1)`.
The `ptr` variable correctly tracks the smallest possible leaf index to check next.

title: Prüfer code
when_to_use: Use when needing to uniquely encode or decode a labeled tree into a sequence of numbers, or to count the number of spanning trees for a complete graph. It's also useful for problems involving tree properties like degree sequences.
description: Prüfer code is a unique sequence representing a labeled tree, allowing conversion between a tree and a sequence of numbers. Its generation and decoding typically involve an iterative process of finding and removing leaves, achieving O(N log N) or O(N) complexity with optimized leaf finding.
tips: Maintain a degree array to efficiently find leaves.
Use a min-priority queue or a two-pointer approach for finding the smallest leaf.
The Prüfer sequence length is N-2 for a tree with N vertices.
Understand Cayley's formula (N^(N-2)) in relation to Prüfer codes.
For decoding, reconstruct the edges by pairing elements from the sequence and the available vertices.
pitfalls: Off-by-one errors in sequence length or vertex indexing.
Incorrectly handling the last two vertices during decoding.
Inefficiently finding the smallest leaf can degrade performance.
Forgetting to update degrees or available vertices correctly.
Confusing 0-indexed vs. 1-indexed vertex labels.
invariants: The Prüfer sequence always has N-2 elements for an N-vertex tree.
Each vertex's degree is correctly maintained throughout the process.
The `ptr` always points to a vertex that has not yet been processed as the smallest available leaf.
The set of remaining vertices always forms a valid forest or tree.

title: Checking a graph for acyclicity and finding a cycle in O(M)
when_to_use: Use when needing to determine if a graph contains any cycles or to identify one such cycle. It is applicable to both directed and undirected graphs.
description: This algorithm uses Depth First Search to detect cycles in a graph by tracking visited states of nodes during traversal. It runs in O(V + E) time, where V is the number of vertices and E is the number of edges.
tips: For directed graphs, use a three-color scheme (white, gray, black) to distinguish states.
For undirected graphs, a simple visited array and checking against the immediate parent is sufficient.
Reconstruct the cycle by backtracking from the detected back edge using a parent array.
Ensure all disconnected components are visited by iterating through all nodes and starting DFS from unvisited ones.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling the parent edge in undirected graphs can lead to false cycle detections.
Failing to distinguish between nodes currently in the recursion stack (gray) and fully processed nodes (black) in directed graphs.
Not resetting visited states or processing all components can miss cycles in disconnected graphs.
Deep recursion on large graphs can lead to stack overflow errors; consider iterative DFS for very large inputs.
invariants: All nodes currently on the DFS recursion stack are marked as 'gray' (or equivalent).
All nodes fully explored and not part of the current path are marked as 'black' (or equivalent).
If DFS encounters a 'gray' node, a cycle is present and the path to it can be reconstructed.
