Every node is visited at most once as 'gray' and at most once as 'black' during the entire process.

title: Finding a negative cycle in the graph
when_to_use: Use when a graph may contain negative edge weights and you need to find the shortest paths from a single source, or specifically to detect and identify negative cycles.
description: The Bellman-Ford algorithm computes single-source shortest paths in a weighted graph, even with negative edge weights, and can detect negative cycles. It has a time complexity of O(VE).
tips: Perform V-1 iterations for shortest path calculation, then one more for cycle detection.
A negative cycle exists if any distance can still be reduced in the V-th iteration.
To find the cycle, trace back from a node whose distance was updated in the V-th iteration.
Initialize all distances to infinity, except the source to zero.
pitfalls: Inefficient for graphs with only non-negative weights compared to Dijkstra's.
Does not directly work for undirected graphs with negative edges.
Reconstructing the actual cycle path can be tricky.
Can be slow on dense graphs due to O(VE) complexity.
invariants: After k iterations, dist[v] is the shortest path from the source to v using at most k edges.
If no negative cycle is reachable, dist[v] will be the true shortest path distance after V-1 iterations.
For every edge (u, v) with weight w, dist[v] <= dist[u] + w holds if no negative cycle exists.

title: Finding a negative cycle in the graph
when_to_use: Use when you need to find if a negative cycle exists in a graph, especially when dealing with all-pairs shortest paths or when edge weights can be negative. It is suitable for dense graphs.
description: The Floyd-Warshall algorithm can detect negative cycles by checking if any vertex has a negative distance to itself after all iterations. It computes all-pairs shortest paths in O(V^3) time.
tips: Initialize distance matrix with edge weights or infinity for non-existent edges.
Set dist[i][i] to 0 for all i initially.
After the algorithm, check dist[i][i] < 0 for any i to detect a negative cycle.
The algorithm works for both directed and undirected graphs (if undirected, represent each edge as two directed edges).
pitfalls: Incorrect initialization of the distance matrix, especially dist[i][i].
Forgetting to check the main diagonal for negative values after computation.
Not handling disconnected components correctly (infinity values).
High time complexity (O(V^3)) makes it unsuitable for very large graphs.
invariants: After k iterations, dist[i][j] stores the shortest path from i to j using only intermediate vertices from 0 to k-1.
For any i, dist[i][i] remains 0 unless a negative cycle involving i is found.
The dist matrix always reflects the shortest path found so far between any two pairs of vertices.
If dist[i][i] < 0 for any i, a negative cycle exists.

title: Finding the Eulerian path in O(M)
when_to_use: Use when the problem requires visiting every edge in a graph exactly once, either starting and ending at the same vertex (cycle) or at two different vertices (path).
description: An Eulerian path is a trail in a graph which visits every edge exactly once. An Eulerian cycle is an Eulerian path that starts and ends on the same vertex. It can be found in O(E) or O(V+E) time, where E is the number of edges and V is the number of vertices.
tips: Check vertex degrees to determine path/cycle existence before attempting to find it.
For an Eulerian path, start traversal from one of the two odd-degree vertices.
For an Eulerian cycle, any vertex can be a starting point.
Use a stack or recursion to build the path, removing edges as they are visited.
Ensure the graph is connected (ignoring isolated vertices) for a path/cycle to exist.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling multi-edges or self-loops can lead to errors in degree calculation or traversal.
Failing to check graph connectivity can result in incomplete paths or infinite loops.
Not distinguishing between directed and undirected graphs for degree calculations (in-degree/out-degree vs total degree).
Modifying the graph (removing edges) during traversal without proper care can lead to issues.
Starting traversal from an even-degree vertex when only an Eulerian path exists.
invariants: Every edge in the graph is considered exactly once for inclusion in the path.
The current path segment always forms a valid trail in the graph.
The degree parity of unvisited vertices remains consistent with the remaining graph structure.
At any point, the number of odd-degree vertices in the remaining graph is either 0 or 2.

title: Finding the Eulerian path in O(M)
when_to_use: Use when you need to find a path or cycle that traverses every edge exactly once in a given graph. It's applicable when specific vertex degree conditions are met for path or cycle existence.
description: This algorithm constructs an Eulerian path or cycle by first verifying vertex degree conditions and graph connectivity, then performing a recursive DFS-like traversal to build the path. It achieves a time complexity of O(M), where M is the number of edges.
tips: Verify graph connectivity, ignoring isolated vertices.
For an Eulerian path, ensure exactly two vertices have odd degrees.
For an Eulerian cycle, all vertices must have even degrees.
Use a stack or recursion for the DFS-like traversal.
Add a temporary "fictitious" edge to convert a path problem into a cycle problem.
Mark edges as visited or remove them to prevent re-traversal.
pitfalls: Failing to check graph connectivity before starting.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to efficiently find the lowest common ancestor of two nodes in a tree. It is particularly effective for answering multiple LCA queries on a static tree after initial preprocessing.
description: This algorithm preprocesses a tree using a DFS to build an Euler tour and record node depths. It transforms Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA) queries into Range Minimum Queries (RMQ) on the Euler tour, enabling O(1) query time after O(N log N) preprocessing.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor

title: Lowest Common Ancestor

For any two nodes u and v, their LCA is the node with minimum depth in the Euler tour segment between their first occurrences.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor - Binary Lifting
signals: Jump up by powers of two
when_to_use: When you need to find the lowest common ancestor of multiple pairs of nodes in a static tree efficiently. It's suitable for scenarios requiring fast query times after initial preprocessing.
description: Binary lifting precomputes ancestors at powers of two distances for each node, enabling O(log N) LCA queries after O(N log N) preprocessing. It finds the LCA by lifting nodes to the same depth and then simultaneously upwards.
tips: Precompute depths of all nodes using DFS from the root.
Initialize `up[u][0]` as the direct parent of `u` for all nodes.
Iterate `j` from 1 to `logN` to compute `up[u][j]` from `up[up[u][j-1]][j-1]`.
Always lift both nodes to the same depth before finding the LCA.
Handle the root node carefully, as its parent might be null or a sentinel.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling the root's parent or depth calculation.
Off-by-one errors in `logN` calculation for array dimensions.
Forgetting to lift nodes to the same depth before the main LCA search.
Not considering cases where one node is an ancestor of the other.

For any node `u`, `depth[u]` is its distance from the root.
During LCA query, `u` and `v` are always at the same depth after initial lifting.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor - Binary Lifting
signals: Constant time ancestor check (using visit times)

During the climb for LCA, `u` always remains a descendant of the original query node `u_orig` and is not an ancestor of `v`.
The `up` array is fully precomputed such that `up[u][k] = up[up[u][k-1]][k-1]`.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor - Farach-Colton and Bender Algorithm

For any two nodes u and v, their LCA is the node with the minimum depth in the Euler tour segment between their first appearances.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor - Farach-Colton and Bender Algorithm
when_to_use: This algorithm is used to answer Lowest Common Ancestor queries on a tree in O(1) time after O(N) preprocessing. It's particularly efficient for many queries on a static tree.

All possible block configurations (where adjacent elements differ by one) have their internal RMQ precomputed.

title: Solve RMQ (Range Minimum Query) by finding LCA (Lowest Common Ancestor)
when_to_use: Use this technique for efficiently answering multiple Range Minimum Queries on a static array, especially when an asymptotically optimal O(1) query time solution is required.

The value of the LCA of two nodes in the Cartesian tree corresponds to the minimum in the original array range between their indices.

The in-order traversal of the Cartesian tree nodes' original array indices yields the original array `A`'s indices in increasing order.
For any two nodes `u` and `v` in the Cartesian tree, their LCA node `w` has `value(w)` equal to the minimum in `A[min(index(u), index(v)) .. max(index(u), index(v))]`.

title: Solve RMQ (Range Minimum Query) by finding LCA (Lowest Common Ancestor)
when_to_use: This technique is used to answer Range Minimum Queries efficiently on a static array by transforming them into Lowest Common Ancestor queries on a Cartesian Tree.
description: This approach solves Range Minimum Query problems by constructing a Cartesian Tree from the input array, where the root of any subtree is its minimum element. RMQ for a given range then reduces to finding the Lowest Common Ancestor of the range's endpoints in this tree, allowing O(N) construction and O(1) query time after O(N) preprocessing.
tips: A Cartesian Tree is a binary tree where each node's value is less than its children's values, and an in-order traversal yields the original array.
Utilize a monotonic stack to construct the Cartesian Tree in linear O(N) time.
After tree construction, apply any efficient LCA algorithm (e.g., binary lifting, sparse table) for query processing.
The root of the Cartesian Tree for a subarray is the minimum element within that subarray.
pitfalls: Correctly implementing the monotonic stack for Cartesian Tree construction can be challenging.

The chosen LCA algorithm's preprocessing and query complexities must be understood and correctly implemented.
For smaller input sizes, simpler O(logN) LCA solutions might outperform O(1) solutions due to higher constant factors.
invariants: For any node `u` in the Cartesian Tree, `value(u)` is the minimum among all values in the subtree rooted at `u`.
For any node `u`, all nodes in its left subtree have indices smaller than `index(u)`, and all nodes in its right subtree have indices larger than `index(u)`.
An in-order traversal of the Cartesian Tree visits nodes in the same order as their indices in the original array.
The Lowest Common Ancestor of nodes `i` and `j` in the Cartesian Tree represents the minimum element in the original array's range `[min(i, j), max(i, j)]`.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor - Tarjan's off-line algorithm
when_to_use: Use this algorithm when you need to find the lowest common ancestor for multiple pairs of nodes in a tree, and all queries are available before processing begins.
description: Tarjan's off-line algorithm efficiently computes the Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA) for all given query pairs in a tree. It leverages a Depth First Search (DFS) traversal combined with a Union-Find data structure to achieve an optimal O(N + M) time complexity.
tips: Process queries associated with a node during its DFS post-order traversal.
Use Union-Find to keep track of visited nodes and their ancestors.

The parent pointer in Union-Find should point to the representative of the component.
Ensure correct initialization of the Union-Find structure.

Incorrectly handling the Union-Find union or find operations.
Not associating queries with both nodes in a pair.

Issues with tree representation (e.g., adjacency list vs. matrix).
invariants: When DFS returns from a node u, all nodes in the subtree rooted at u have been visited and their components merged into u's representative.
For any node v, find(v) in the Union-Find structure returns the representative of the component containing v and its fully processed descendants.
When processing a query (u, v) during DFS of u, if v has already been visited and its subtree fully processed, LCA(u,v) is the representative of v's component.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor - Tarjan's off-line algorithm
when_to_use: Use when you need to answer multiple Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA) queries on a static tree, and all queries are available before processing.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor - Tarjan's off-line algorithm

For any node 'v' whose subtree has been fully processed, 'find_set(v)' returns the representative of 'v''s component.

All nodes in a processed subtree are part of the same Union-Find set, whose representative's 'ancestor' points to the LCA candidate.

title: Maximum flow - Ford-Fulkerson and Edmonds-Karp
when_to_use: When needing to find the maximum possible flow from a source to a sink in a flow network. It is particularly useful for problems involving resource allocation, transportation, or matching.
description: Ford-Fulkerson finds maximum flow by repeatedly augmenting paths in a residual graph. Edmonds-Karp uses BFS for shortest augmenting paths, ensuring O(VE^2) time complexity and termination.
tips: Always construct a residual graph to represent remaining capacities and backward flow.
BFS ensures finding the shortest augmenting path, leading to the O(VE^2) complexity.
Remember to update capacities for both forward and backward edges in the residual graph.
For integer capacities, the flow increases by at least 1 per path.
pitfalls: Incorrectly updating residual capacities for backward edges can lead to wrong results.
Using DFS for Ford-Fulkerson can lead to poor performance or non-termination with irrational capacities.
Forgetting to initialize flow to zero or capacities correctly.
Not handling disconnected components or unreachable sink.
invariants: The flow through any edge never exceeds its capacity.
For every vertex (except source and sink), total incoming flow equals total outgoing flow (flow conservation).
The residual graph always correctly reflects remaining capacities and allows for flow reversal.
Each BFS iteration finds an augmenting path from source to sink in the residual graph.

title: Maximum flow - Push-relabel algorithm
when_to_use: Use when computing the maximum flow in a network, especially for dense graphs where its O(V^2 E) complexity can be competitive. It's an alternative to augmenting path algorithms.
description: The Push-relabel algorithm computes maximum flow by maintaining a preflow and a height function, iteratively pushing excess flow from vertices and relabeling them until a valid flow is achieved. It typically runs in O(V^2 E) time.
tips: Initialize the graph with a valid preflow and height function.
Prioritize pushing excess from vertices with the highest labels.
Carefully manage residual capacities when performing push operations.
Ensure the height function properties are maintained after each relabel operation.
pitfalls: Incorrectly updating residual capacities after a push operation.
Violating the height function validity condition (h(u) <= h(v) + 1).
Inefficiently selecting vertices for push/relabel operations, leading to worse performance.
Not properly handling excess flow at the source or sink nodes.
invariants: For any edge (u, v) in the residual graph with positive capacity, h(u) <= h(v) + 1.
Excess flow at the source (s) is always non-negative, and at the sink (t) is always non-positive.
The total excess flow in the network (excluding source and sink) is non-negative.
No augmenting path exists in the residual graph with respect to the current height function.

title: Maximum flow - Push-relabel algorithm
when_to_use: Use when finding the maximum flow in a network, particularly for dense graphs or when the Ford-Fulkerson method is too slow due to many augmenting paths.
description: The Push-relabel algorithm computes maximum flow by maintaining a preflow and a height function, iteratively pushing excess flow from higher to lower nodes and relabeling nodes until all excess is pushed to the sink. It is a highly efficient polynomial-time algorithm for various graph structures.
tips: Understand the concepts of preflow and excess flow.
Correctly implement push and relabel operations.
Maintain a valid height function throughout the algorithm.
Use a global relabeling heuristic for improved performance.
Consider different discharge strategies for active vertices.
pitfalls: Incorrectly updating residual capacities after a push.
Failing to maintain the height function property (h(u) <= h(v) + 1).
Infinite loops if relabeling logic is flawed or inconsistent.
Handling multiple parallel edges or self-loops in the graph.
High constant factors can make it slow for smaller graphs despite good asymptotic complexity.
invariants: For any vertex v (not source or sink), the total incoming flow minus total outgoing flow is non-negative (excess flow).
For any edge (u, v) in the residual graph with positive capacity, the height of u is at most h(v) + 1.
The flow pushed from source s equals the flow received at sink t once the algorithm terminates.
The total excess flow in the network (excluding source and sink) is always non-negative.

title: Maximum flow - Push-relabel algorithm
when_to_use: Used for finding the maximum flow in a flow network. It is particularly efficient for dense graphs or when the Ford-Fulkerson method is too slow due to many augmenting paths.
description: The Push-relabel algorithm computes maximum flow by maintaining a preflow and a height function, iteratively pushing excess flow from higher to lower nodes and relabeling nodes when flow cannot be pushed. It typically achieves a time complexity of O(V^2 * E) or O(V^3) with specific implementations.
tips: Initialize preflow by saturating edges from the source.
Initialize source height to |V| and others to 0.
Use a queue or stack to manage active vertices with excess flow.
Consider different discharge strategies (e.g., FIFO, LIFO, highest label).
Implement global relabeling periodically for better performance.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling residual capacities.
Not maintaining the height invariant during pushes.
Inefficiently selecting active vertices can degrade performance.
Forgetting to update reverse edges when pushing flow.

invariants: For any edge (u, v) with positive residual capacity, h(u) <= h(v) + 1.
The source s always has h(s) = |V| and the sink t always has h(t) = 0.
Any vertex u (not s or t) with excess flow x(u) > 0 is either able to push flow to a neighbor v where h(u) = h(v) + 1, or u needs to be relabeled.
The total flow entering any intermediate vertex v equals the total flow leaving v plus its current excess x(v).

title: Maximum flow - Push-relabel method improved

This specific vertex selection strategy leads to a better theoretical time complexity, typically O(V^2 * sqrt(E)) or O(V^3) depending on implementation details.

Maintain a data structure (e.g., buckets or lists) to quickly find highest-height vertices.

Ensure efficient updates of vertex heights after relabeling.
pitfalls: Incorrectly managing the list of highest-height vertices.
Failing to update residual capacities after a push operation.
Infinite loops if relabeling logic is flawed.
Overlooking edge cases for source and sink vertices.
Performance degradation if vertex selection is not truly O(1) or amortized O(1).
invariants: For any edge (u, v) in the residual graph, h(u) <= h(v) + 1.
The total excess flow in the network is always non-negative.
Flow conservation holds for all intermediate vertices.
All flow values are within their capacity bounds.

title: Maximum flow - Dinic's algorithm
when_to_use: When you need to find the maximum possible flow from a source to a sink in a flow network, especially for larger graphs where Edmonds-Karp might be too slow.
description: Dinic's algorithm finds the maximum flow in a flow network by repeatedly constructing a layered network using BFS, then finding a blocking flow in this layered network using DFS. It runs in O(V^2E) time in general graphs, or O(min(V^(2/3), E^(1/2)) * E) for unit capacities.
tips: Implement BFS to build the layered graph (level graph) correctly, ensuring only shortest paths are considered.
Use DFS to find a blocking flow in the layered graph, pushing flow along multiple paths simultaneously.
Maintain a 'ptr' (or 'current_edge') array for each vertex in DFS to avoid re-exploring edges that have already been saturated or cannot lead to the sink in the current blocking flow phase.
Ensure residual capacities are updated correctly for both forward and backward edges.
pitfalls: Incorrectly building the layered network, e.g., not using only edges that increase the level by exactly one.
Failing to implement the 'ptr' optimization in DFS, leading to a much slower algorithm.
Errors in updating residual capacities, especially for back edges, which can lead to incorrect flow values.
Integer overflow if capacities are very large.
invariants: All flow paths from source to sink in the residual graph are non-decreasing in length across phases.
The flow pushed through the network never exceeds the capacity of any edge.
The total flow into any intermediate vertex equals the total flow out of it (flow conservation).
Each BFS phase correctly identifies the shortest path distance from the source to all reachable vertices in the residual graph.

title: Maximum flow - Dinic's algorithm
when_to_use: Use Dinic's algorithm to find the maximum flow in a flow network. It is particularly efficient for dense graphs or when its polynomial time complexity is advantageous.
description: Dinic's algorithm is a strongly polynomial time algorithm for computing the maximum flow in a flow network. It operates in phases, each involving constructing a layered network using BFS and then finding a blocking flow using DFS, achieving a time complexity of O(V^2E).
tips: Use BFS to construct the layered network from source to sink, ensuring shortest paths.
Implement edge pointers (current edge optimization) to efficiently skip saturated edges during DFS.
Ensure the residual graph is correctly updated with both forward and backward edges after each flow push.
Reset edge pointers for each vertex at the start of every new phase (after BFS).
The layered network should only include edges that increase the distance from the source.
pitfalls: Incorrectly building the layered network, leading to non-shortest augmenting paths.
Failing to update residual capacities for both forward and reverse edges after pushing flow.
Not resetting edge pointers for DFS in each new phase, leading to incorrect blocking flow calculations.
Potential for infinite loops or incorrect results if flow conservation is violated.
Off-by-one errors when handling capacities or flow values, especially with integer types.
invariants: The flow pushed through any edge never exceeds its original capacity.
For any vertex other than the source or sink, the total incoming flow equals the total outgoing flow.
The residual graph accurately reflects the remaining capacities and reverse edges for all paths.
Each phase's BFS correctly identifies the shortest path distances from the source to all reachable nodes in the current residual graph.

title: Maximum flow - Dinic's algorithm
when_to_use: Use Dinic's algorithm to find the maximum flow from a source to a sink in a flow network. It is efficient for dense graphs and particularly optimized for unit capacity networks.
description: Dinic's algorithm computes the maximum flow by repeatedly constructing a layered network using BFS and then finding a blocking flow in this layered network using DFS. It offers a general time complexity of O(V^2 * E) and O(E * sqrt(V)) for unit capacity networks.
tips: Use BFS to construct the layered graph, assigning levels to nodes from the source.
Employ DFS to find augmenting paths within the layered graph, only traversing edges to higher levels.
Optimize DFS by using a `pointer` array for each node to track the next available edge, avoiding redundant traversals.
Ensure residual capacities are correctly updated for both forward and backward edges after each augmentation.
pitfalls: Forgetting to update residual capacities for reverse edges, which can prevent finding valid augmenting paths.
Incorrectly constructing the layered graph or level assignments during the BFS phase.
Not using the `pointer` optimization in DFS, leading to significantly worse performance per phase.
Potential stack overflow issues with deep DFS recursion on very large graphs without iterative DFS or proper stack management.
invariants: The flow on any edge (u, v) never exceeds its original capacity.
For any vertex v (excluding source and sink), the sum of flow into v equals the sum of flow out of v.
All edges traversed in a DFS phase within a layered graph connect a node at level L to a node at level L+1.
The residual capacity of any edge in the residual graph remains non-negative throughout the algorithm.

title: Maximum flow - MPM algorithm
when_to_use: When solving maximum flow problems, especially in scenarios where Dinic's algorithm is applicable but a different approach to finding blocking flow is desired.
description: The MPM algorithm solves the maximum flow problem by iteratively finding blocking flows in a layered residual network. It achieves an overall time complexity of O(V^3).
tips: Construct the layered network using BFS from the source.
Calculate inner and outer potentials for each node in the layered network.
Identify a reference node with the minimum potential to guide flow pushes.
Push flow from the source to the reference node and from the reference node to the sink.
Remove saturated edges and isolated nodes from the layered network within each phase.
pitfalls: Incorrectly computing inner or outer potentials can lead to suboptimal flow or incorrect reference nodes.
Failure to properly update residual capacities after pushing flow can break correctness.
Not efficiently removing saturated edges or isolated nodes can degrade performance.
Errors in constructing the layered network can lead to incorrect paths or cycles.
Managing the state of potentials and graph modifications across iterations can be complex.
invariants: The layered network L remains acyclic throughout a phase.
For any node v, p(v) = min(p_in(v), p_out(v)) accurately reflects its current flow potential.
After processing a reference node r, its potential p(r) becomes zero, and it is effectively removed from consideration for the current phase.
All flow pushed or pulled within a phase respects residual capacities and path directions in L.

title: Flows with demands
when_to_use: Use when a problem involves finding a flow in a network where edges have both lower and upper capacity bounds, or nodes have supply/demand requirements, and an optimal value needs to be found.
description: This technique transforms a network with lower and upper capacity bounds on edges (or node demands) into a standard max-flow problem. It often involves adding a new source and sink to handle demands, and then binary searching on the answer if an optimal value is sought, typically with O(MaxFlow * log(Range)) complexity.
tips: Convert lower bounds to demands by adjusting capacities and adding a super source/sink.
A feasible flow exists if the max flow in the transformed network equals the total demand.
Binary search is applied on the value of the flow or a related parameter, not on the graph structure itself.
Ensure all demands are met by checking the flow from the auxiliary source.
Carefully define new capacities and demands in the transformed network.
pitfalls: Incorrectly calculating residual capacities after demand satisfaction.
Forgetting to add a super source/sink for handling demands.
Not properly handling negative capacities or demands if they arise.
Off-by-one errors in capacity adjustments.
Max flow algorithm might be too slow if not chosen carefully for the graph size.
invariants: The total flow out of the super source equals the total demand that needs to be satisfied.
For any node, the sum of incoming flow minus outgoing flow equals its net demand (0 for internal nodes in a circulation).
All edge capacities (lower and upper bounds) are respected by the flow.
The residual graph correctly reflects available capacity for augmenting paths.

title: Minimum-cost flow - Successive shortest path algorithm
when_to_use: When you need to find a flow of a certain amount (or maximum possible flow) through a network while minimizing the total cost associated with sending flow along edges. It is suitable for problems involving resource allocation with varying costs.
description: The successive shortest path algorithm finds a minimum-cost flow by repeatedly finding a shortest augmenting path in the residual graph and pushing flow along it. Its complexity depends on the shortest path algorithm used (Bellman-Ford for general costs, Dijkstra with potentials for non-negative residual costs).
tips: Use potentials (reduced costs) with Dijkstra to efficiently handle non-negative edge costs in the residual graph.
Initialize potentials to zero and update them after each shortest path computation.
Ensure the residual graph correctly reflects remaining capacities and reverse edges for flow return.
For problems with negative cost edges, Bellman-Ford is initially necessary, or ensure no negative cycles are reachable.
pitfalls: Incorrectly updating residual capacities or reverse edge costs can lead to wrong results.
Failing to use potentials with Dijkstra can lead to incorrect shortest paths in graphs with negative edges.
Infinite loops if negative cost cycles are present and not properly handled or detected.
High complexity for large graphs or high flow demands if not optimized.
Off-by-one errors in capacity management or flow calculations.
invariants: At the start of each iteration, the current flow is a valid flow from source to sink.
The residual graph correctly represents remaining capacities and reverse edges for the current flow.
The potentials (if used) ensure that all edges in the residual graph have non-negative reduced costs.
The total cost of the flow found so far is minimized for the amount of flow pushed.

title: Minimum-cost flow - Successive shortest path algorithm
when_to_use: This algorithm finds a minimum-cost flow of a given amount from a source to a sink in a flow network. It is suitable when both flow capacity and edge costs are present.
description: The successive shortest path algorithm iteratively finds augmenting paths with minimum cost in a residual graph, using shortest path algorithms like Bellman-Ford or Dijkstra with potentials, until the desired flow is achieved. Its complexity depends on the shortest path algorithm used and the number of augmentations.
tips: Use potentials with Dijkstra for non-negative edge costs in the residual graph.
Bellman-Ford is necessary if initial edge costs can be negative or if potentials are not used.
Ensure the residual graph correctly reflects capacities and reverse edges.
Initialize potentials to zero or run Bellman-Ford once to find initial potentials.
The algorithm terminates when the desired flow is pushed or no more augmenting paths exist.
pitfalls: Incorrectly updating potentials can lead to wrong shortest paths.
Forgetting to add reverse edges with negative costs in the residual graph.
Using Dijkstra without potentials when negative cost edges appear in the residual graph.
Integer overflow with large costs or capacities.
Infinite loops if negative cycles are not handled (though rare in typical problems).
invariants: The total flow pushed from source to sink never exceeds the sum of capacities of edges leaving the source.
For any edge (u, v) with flow f_uv, 0 <= f_uv <= capacity_uv.
The flow conservation property holds for all intermediate nodes (flow in equals flow out).
The cost of the current flow is always minimized for the amount of flow pushed so far.

title: Solving assignment problem using min-cost-flow
when_to_use: Use when finding a one-to-one assignment between two sets of entities, such as workers to tasks, to minimize the total cost. It's suitable for problems modelable as min-cost max-flow in a bipartite graph.
description: This technique models the assignment problem as a minimum cost maximum flow problem on a specially constructed bipartite graph. It finds a perfect matching with the lowest total cost, typically with a polynomial time complexity depending on the shortest path algorithm used.
tips: Construct a bipartite graph with a source and sink nodes.
Set capacities to 1 for all assignment-related edges to ensure one-to-one mapping.
Costs are applied only to edges representing actual assignments between entities.
Use Dijkstra with potentials for non-negative costs, or Bellman-Ford if negative costs are present.
pitfalls: Incorrectly modeling capacities or costs on graph edges can lead to wrong solutions.
Forgetting to ensure a perfect matching (flow value equals N) is found for a complete assignment.
Using Dijkstra without potentials when negative costs might arise during successive shortest path computations.
High computational complexity for large graphs can be a significant concern.
invariants: Flow conservation holds for all intermediate nodes in the network.
The flow on any edge never exceeds its defined capacity.
The total cost of the current flow is minimal for its current magnitude.
The residual graph accurately represents available capacity and cost for augmenting paths.

title: Check whether a graph is bipartite
signals: every edge connects two vertices from different sets
when_to_use: Use this when you need to determine if an undirected graph can be divided into two independent sets such that all edges connect vertices from different sets, or if it is two-colorable.
description: This algorithm determines if an undirected graph is bipartite by attempting to 2-color it using BFS. It assigns alternating colors (or sets) to adjacent vertices, detecting non-bipartite conditions if an edge connects two vertices of the same color. The time complexity is O(V + E).
tips: Use two distinct colors (e.g., 0 and 1) to represent the two sets.
Iterate through all vertices and start a new BFS from any unvisited vertex to handle disconnected components.
Assign the starting vertex of each BFS to one color, then assign the opposite color to its uncolored neighbors.
If an edge connects two vertices that already have the same color, the graph is not bipartite.
pitfalls: Forgetting to handle disconnected graphs by ensuring all vertices are visited.
Incorrectly assigning colors, leading to false positives or negatives.
Not marking visited nodes, which can lead to infinite loops or redundant processing in cyclic graphs.
Misinterpreting the condition for non-bipartite graphs (presence of an odd-length cycle).
invariants: All visited vertices within the current BFS component are correctly 2-colored.
All edges processed so far connect vertices of different colors.
The queue contains only vertices that have been colored but whose neighbors have not yet been processed.

title: Kuhn's Algorithm for Maximum Bipartite Matching
when_to_use: Use DFS to traverse or search a tree or graph, exploring each branch as far as possible before backtracking. It's ideal for connectivity, pathfinding, topological sorting, and cycle detection.
description: DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It's a graph traversal algorithm with O(V+E) time complexity for adjacency list representation.
tips: Use a visited set/array to prevent cycles and redundant work.
Recursion stack can lead to stack overflow for deep graphs.
Iterative DFS uses an explicit stack.
Can be used to find connected components.
pitfalls: Stack overflow with deep recursion on large graphs.
Incorrectly handling visited states can lead to infinite loops.
Not suitable for shortest path on unweighted graphs (BFS is better).
Order of visiting neighbors can affect results for some problems.
invariants: All nodes reachable from the starting node within the current path have been visited.
The current path from the start node to the current node is maintained.
No node is processed twice in the current path before backtracking.
Upon backtracking, all descendants of the current node have been fully explored.

title: Kuhn's Algorithm for Maximum Bipartite Matching
when_to_use: Use when finding a maximum matching in a bipartite graph, particularly when performance on random graphs can be improved by heuristic pre-matching.
description: This algorithm finds a maximum matching in a bipartite graph by iteratively searching for augmenting paths using Depth-First Search. An improved version incorporates greedy heuristics to find an initial matching, potentially speeding up practical runtime while maintaining the O(V*E) worst-case complexity.
tips: Pre-calculate an initial matching using a simple heuristic to reduce DFS calls.
Consider heuristics like iterating over vertices or selecting from smallest degree vertices first.
Ensure the main loop correctly handles vertices already included by the heuristic.
Heuristics can significantly improve performance on average and random graphs.
pitfalls: Forgetting to reset the 'visited' array for each new DFS search in the main loop.
Incorrectly modifying the main loop logic when integrating heuristic pre-matching.
Assuming heuristics guarantee optimality; they only provide a starting point for Kuhn's algorithm.
Not all heuristics are equally effective across different graph structures.
invariants: After each successful DFS call, the size of the current matching increases by one.
The 'mt' array correctly stores the current matching pairs for all vertices.
The 'used' array accurately tracks visited vertices within a single DFS path attempt.
All vertices in the first part are considered for matching, either initially or through augmentation.

title: Hungarian algorithm for solving the assignment problem
when_to_use: Finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs or uniform-cost state-space searches. Explores states layer by layer.
description: Explores all reachable nodes in a graph layer by layer, finding the shortest path in unweighted graphs. It operates in O(V + E) time complexity.
tips: Use a queue to manage nodes to visit.
Mark nodes as visited to prevent cycles and redundant processing.
Store parent pointers to reconstruct paths if needed.
Can be adapted for grid problems by treating cells as nodes.
Useful for finding all nodes within a certain distance.
pitfalls: Forgetting to mark nodes as visited can lead to infinite loops.
Incorrectly handling disconnected components.
Not clearing visited states between multiple BFS runs.
Using it for weighted graphs without modification yields incorrect shortest paths.
invariants: All nodes currently in the queue are at the same distance from the source.
All nodes visited so far have their shortest distance from the source determined.
No unvisited node has a shorter path than the current layer's distance.
The queue only contains nodes that are reachable and not yet fully processed.

title: Hungarian algorithm for solving the assignment problem
when_to_use: Use when finding a minimum cost perfect matching in a bipartite graph, typically represented as an N x N cost matrix, where each row must be assigned to exactly one column.
description: The Hungarian algorithm finds a minimum weight perfect matching in a bipartite graph, solving the assignment problem for an N x N cost matrix. It iteratively finds augmenting paths in an equality graph and adjusts potentials, achieving O(N^3) time complexity.
tips: Understand the concept of potentials and their role in maintaining the equality graph.
The algorithm can be adapted for maximum profit problems by negating costs.
It relies on finding augmenting paths using BFS or DFS in the equality graph.
Ensure the cost matrix is square; pad with dummy rows/columns and appropriate costs if not.
pitfalls: Incorrectly updating potentials can lead to non-optimal solutions or infinite loops.
Handling non-square matrices requires careful padding with dummy values.
Misinterpreting the dual problem (maximum profit vs. minimum cost) can lead to errors.
Complexity can be an issue for very large N; consider approximations if N is huge.
invariants: For all i, j, u[i] + v[j] <= A[i][j] (potential condition).
The current matching is always valid (each row/column used at most once).
The equality graph contains all edges (i, j) where u[i] + v[j] == A[i][j].
The sum of potentials f = sum(u) + sum(v) is a lower bound for the optimal assignment cost.

title: Topological Sorting
when_to_use: Use for graph or tree traversal, pathfinding, cycle detection, or topological sorting in DAGs. It's also suitable for exploring all states in a search space.
description: DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, typically using recursion or an explicit stack. Topological sort orders nodes in a DAG such that for every directed edge u -> v, u comes before v, achievable with DFS in O(V+E) time.
tips: Use a visited set/array to prevent infinite loops in graphs and redundant computations.
Recursion is natural for DFS, but an explicit stack can avoid recursion depth limits.
Topological sort requires a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) and can be implemented by processing nodes in reverse order of their DFS finish times.
For backtracking, remember to undo changes after a recursive call returns.
pitfalls: Forgetting to mark nodes as visited can lead to infinite loops or incorrect results.
Exceeding recursion depth limits for very deep graphs or trees.
Incorrectly handling disconnected components; ensure all nodes are eventually visited.
Applying topological sort to graphs with cycles will produce incorrect results.
invariants: All nodes reachable from the current path have been explored or are in the recursion stack.
A node is marked 'visited' only after all its descendants have been fully explored.
For topological sort, all predecessors of a node are processed before the node itself is added to the sorted list (when using Kahn's algorithm or reverse finish times).

title: Edge connectivity / Vertex connectivity
when_to_use: Use when quantifying a graph's resilience to component separation by removing edges or vertices. It helps understand the structural integrity and robustness of networks.
description: Edge connectivity is the minimum edges to disconnect a graph; vertex connectivity is the minimum vertices. These measures quantify graph robustness, typically found using max-flow min-cut algorithms, which can be computationally intensive.
tips: Understand the Whitney inequalities: kappa <= lambda <= delta.
Edge connectivity can be computed via multiple max-flow min-cut calculations.
Vertex connectivity can be transformed into an edge connectivity problem using vertex splitting.
Consider special cases like complete graphs or trees for simpler analysis.
pitfalls: Confusing edge connectivity with vertex connectivity, as they are distinct measures.
Incorrectly applying Menger's theorem or max-flow algorithms.
Overlooking the impact of graph density on connectivity values.
Not accounting for disconnected components in the initial graph.
invariants: The graph remains connected if fewer than lambda edges are removed.
The graph remains connected if fewer than kappa vertices are removed.
The vertex connectivity (kappa) is always less than or equal to the edge connectivity (lambda).
The edge connectivity (lambda) is always less than or equal to the minimum degree (delta).

title: Edge connectivity / Vertex connectivity
when_to_use: Used to quantify a graph's resilience by finding the minimum number of edges or vertices whose removal disconnects it, or separates a specific pair of nodes.
description: Edge connectivity is the minimum edges to disconnect a graph, while vertex connectivity is for vertices. Both are often computed using max-flow min-cut theorems, typically in polynomial time.
tips: Edge connectivity between two specific vertices can be found using a max-flow min-cut algorithm.
To find global edge connectivity, iterate over all pairs of vertices or use specialized algorithms.
Vertex connectivity can be transformed into an edge connectivity problem by splitting each vertex into two, connected by an edge.
A connected graph with no bridges has an edge connectivity of at least 2.
Menger's theorem provides a fundamental link between connectivity and disjoint paths.
pitfalls: Confusing edge connectivity (edges) with vertex connectivity (vertices) requirements.
Incorrectly applying max-flow for global connectivity without considering all relevant source-sink pairs.
When transforming for vertex connectivity, ensure the source and sink vertices are handled carefully to avoid splitting them.
Overlooking the specific graph properties (e.g., directed/undirected, weighted/unweighted) when applying algorithms.
The computational complexity can be high for dense graphs or when checking all pairs of vertices.
invariants: The flow through any edge in the network does not exceed its capacity.
For any intermediate vertex (not source or sink), the total incoming flow equals the total outgoing flow.
The maximum flow value from source to sink is equal to the minimum capacity of an s-t cut.
The edge connectivity between two vertices equals the maximum number of edge-disjoint paths between them.

title: Paint the edges of the tree
when_to_use: Used for efficiently handling queries on a tree that involve updating edge properties (like color) and querying aggregated properties (like count of colored edges) along paths between two nodes.
description: This technique uses an Euler tour and two segment trees to maintain edge properties and query path sums. It allows for O(log N) updates and queries after an O(N) preprocessing step.
tips: Reduce general path queries (i, j) to ancestor-descendant queries (LCA(i,j), i) and (LCA(i,j), j) using Lowest Common Ancestor.
The Euler tour transforms tree paths into contiguous segments in an array, enabling segment tree operations.
Use two segment trees: one for forward edge traversals and one for backward, to distinguish path edges from non-path edges.
Precompute the first occurrence position of each vertex in the Euler tour for O(1) segment boundary identification.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling the LCA step can lead to wrong path decomposition for general queries.
Off-by-one errors when defining the segment tree query range [p, q-1] in the Euler tour.
Forgetting to update both segment trees when an edge's color changes, as each edge appears twice.
Misunderstanding why the difference of sums (T1 - T2) correctly isolates path edges.
invariants: The Euler tour correctly records each edge traversal exactly twice (once forward, once backward).
Each segment tree accurately maintains the sum of colored edges for its respective traversal direction.
For any ancestor-descendant path (i, j), the segment [p, q-1] in the Euler tour contains all path edges once in the forward direction and other non-path edges twice (forward and backward).

title: 2-SAT
when_to_use: When a problem can be modeled as finding a satisfying assignment for a Boolean formula where each clause contains exactly two literals. It is used to determine if such an assignment exists and to construct one if it does.
description: 2-SAT solves the Boolean satisfiability problem for formulas in conjunctive normal form where each clause has exactly two literals. It converts the formula into an implication graph and uses Strongly Connected Components (SCCs) to find a satisfying assignment or determine unsatisfiability in O(N+M) time.
tips: Convert each clause (A OR B) into two implications: (NOT A => B) and (NOT B => A).
Represent each variable X and its negation NOT X as distinct nodes in the implication graph.
If a variable and its negation are in the same SCC, the formula is unsatisfiable.
Use topological sort of the SCCs to determine a valid assignment.
Assign values based on the topological order of SCCs, typically assigning false to variables in SCCs that appear earlier.
pitfalls: Incorrectly converting clauses to implications, missing one of the two directions.
Failing to handle the critical case where a variable and its negation are in the same SCC.
Errors in constructing the implication graph, such as incorrect edges or node representation.
Misinterpreting the topological order of SCCs when deriving the final truth assignment.
Not considering both the positive and negative literals for each variable in the graph construction.
invariants: Each clause (A OR B) is correctly represented by two implications: (NOT A => B) and (NOT B => A).
The implication graph correctly reflects all logical implications derived from the 2-CNF formula.
For any variable X, nodes representing X and NOT X are distinct unless they are part of the same SCC.
All nodes within a single SCC must have the same truth value in any satisfying assignment.

title: 2-SAT
when_to_use: Use when solving problems involving boolean variables constrained by implications of the form (A OR B). It determines if a satisfying assignment exists and can construct one if it does.
description: 2-SAT solves boolean satisfiability problems where each clause has exactly two literals, by transforming them into an implication graph. It checks for contradictions using Strongly Connected Components (SCCs) and finds a valid assignment in O(N+M) time.
tips: Construct an implication graph where A => B means NOT A OR B.
For each clause (A OR B), add edges (NOT A => B) and (NOT B => A).
Ensure the contrapositive (NOT B => NOT A) is added for every (A => B) edge.
Use Tarjan's or Kosaraju's algorithm to find SCCs efficiently.
A solution exists if no variable X and its negation NOT X are in the same SCC.
A valid assignment can be derived from the topological order of SCCs.
pitfalls: Forgetting to add both implication edges for each clause.
Incorrectly handling the negation of variables in graph construction.
Misinterpreting the condition for no solution (X and NOT X in same SCC).
Errors in constructing the implication graph from the given clauses.
Not considering the topological order of SCCs when constructing a satisfying assignment.
invariants: For any variable X, X and its negation NOT X must belong to different SCCs for a solution to exist.
If an implication A => B is present, then the contrapositive NOT B => NOT A must also be present in the graph.
Within any strongly connected component, all literals must be assigned the same truth value.
The implication graph correctly represents all given 2-CNF clauses.

title: Heavy-light decomposition
when_to_use: When you need to efficiently perform path queries or updates on a tree, transforming tree paths into linear segments for data structures like segment trees.
description: Heavy-light decomposition partitions a tree into disjoint heavy paths, ensuring any root-to-node path crosses at most O(log N) heavy paths. This reduces tree path queries to segment operations, typically achieving O(log^2 N) or O(log N) complexity.
tips: Precompute subtree sizes for all nodes to identify heavy edges.
Map heavy paths to contiguous array segments for efficient range queries.
Utilize a segment tree or similar structure on each heavy path for queries/updates.
Split path queries (a, b) into two (a, LCA(a,b)) and (b, LCA(a,b)) segments.
pitfalls: Misidentifying heavy edges or having more than one heavy child per node.
Off-by-one errors when converting tree path segments to array indices.
Incorrectly handling the LCA node, leading to missed or double-counted values.
Forgetting that path queries might require O(log^2 N) without prefix sum optimization.
invariants: Every node is part of exactly one heavy path.
Traversing from any node to the root involves at most O(log N) heavy path switches.
For any node, its heavy child (if exists) has a subtree size at least half of the node's own subtree size.

title: Heavy-light decomposition
when_to_use: When you need to perform path queries or updates on a tree efficiently, often involving operations like sum, min, max, or XOR on edges/nodes along a path. It transforms tree paths into linear segments for data structure application.
description: Heavy-light decomposition partitions a tree into disjoint heavy paths and light edges, allowing tree path operations to be performed efficiently using data structures like segment trees. It typically achieves O(log^2 N) or O(log N) complexity per query/update after O(N log N) or O(N) preprocessing.
tips: Perform two DFS traversals: one for subtree sizes and parent pointers, another for heavy/light edges and path IDs.
Map tree nodes to a 1D array for segment tree operations based on their position in heavy paths.
LCA is crucial for path queries between arbitrary nodes, often precomputed with binary lifting.
Handle queries on paths that span multiple heavy paths by traversing up to the LCA.
Remember to process both segments from the query nodes up to their common ancestor.
pitfalls: Incorrectly identifying heavy edges (largest subtree size) during the first DFS.
Off-by-one errors when mapping nodes to segment tree indices or handling path boundaries.
Forgetting to handle edge cases like the root node or paths consisting of a single node.
Complexity of LCA implementation can be a bottleneck if not chosen carefully.
Careful distinction between node-based and edge-based queries/updates.
Managing multiple segment trees or a single large one requires careful indexing.
invariants: Each node belongs to exactly one heavy path.
Moving from a node to its parent via a light edge reduces the subtree size by at least half.
Any path from the root to an arbitrary node traverses at most O(log N) light edges.
Heavy paths are disjoint and cover all edges of the tree.

title: Range Minimum Query
when_to_use: Use when you need to find the minimum element within specified ranges of an array efficiently, especially for many queries after initial setup.
description: A Range Minimum Query (RMQ) problem asks to find the minimum value in a specified range [L, R] of an array. It can be solved with various data structures, often achieving O(1) or O(log N) query time after O(N log N) or O(N) precomputation.
tips: For static arrays, Sparse Table offers O(1) query time after O(N log N) precomputation.
For dynamic arrays (with updates), Segment Tree provides O(log N) query and update times.
Consider using a Cartesian Tree and LCA for O(N) precomputation and O(1) query time for static arrays.
Precompute powers of 2 for Sparse Table log_2 calculations.
Ensure query ranges are valid and within array bounds.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling edge cases for range boundaries (L, R).
High memory usage for Sparse Table if N is very large (N log N space).
Overhead of Segment Tree for very few queries or small N.
Forgetting to initialize data structures correctly (e.g., identity element for Segment Tree).
Off-by-one errors in index calculations.
invariants: For any query range [L, R], the returned value is the minimum of elements A[i] where L <= i <= R.
The precomputed data structure correctly stores minimums for all relevant sub-intervals.
Query operations do not modify the underlying data or the precomputed structure.

title: Range Minimum Query
when_to_use: Use when needing to efficiently query minimums over arbitrary ranges and perform point or range updates on an array.
description: A segment tree is a tree data structure used for storing information about intervals or segments. It allows answering range queries and performing updates in logarithmic time, typically O(log N) for both.
tips: Build the tree recursively, dividing the array into halves until leaf nodes are reached.
Query operations involve traversing relevant segments, combining results from child nodes.
Update operations propagate changes from the affected leaf node up to the root.
Consider lazy propagation for efficient handling of range updates to avoid O(N) updates.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling array indexing (0-based vs. 1-based) can lead to off-by-one errors.
Forgetting to update parent nodes after a child's value changes during an update operation.
Complexity of implementing lazy propagation correctly, especially with multiple types of updates.
Memory overhead can be significant, typically 2N to 4N nodes for an array of size N.
invariants: Each node in the segment tree represents a contiguous subsegment of the original array.
A parent node's value is derived from its children's values (e.g., minimum of children).
Leaf nodes correspond to individual elements of the original array.
The segment represented by a node is always fully contained within the segment of its parent.

title: Range Minimum Query
when_to_use: When you need to efficiently query the minimum value within a range and perform point updates on an array. It's suitable for scenarios requiring logarithmic time complexity for both operations.
description: A Fenwick tree (BIT) can be adapted to answer Range Minimum Queries and perform point updates in O(log N) time. It achieves this by storing prefix minimums in a tree-like structure, allowing efficient aggregation.
tips: Adapt standard BIT operations for minimums instead of sums.
Use `min(current_value, new_value)` for updates and queries.
Precompute initial prefix minimums if the array is static initially.
Remember that BIT for RMQ is less common than for RSQ.
pitfalls: Standard BIT operations are for sums; direct application for minimums is incorrect.
Querying a range `[L, R]` for minimum is not a simple `query(R) - query(L-1)`.
Updates must propagate minimums correctly, not just replace values.
Deleting elements or range updates are complex/inefficient.
invariants: Each node `tree[i]` stores the minimum of a specific range `[i - (i & -i) + 1, i]`.
An update to `arr[idx]` correctly propagates the new minimum to all relevant `tree` nodes.
A query for `min(arr[1...idx])` correctly aggregates minimums from relevant `tree` nodes.

title: Range Minimum Query
when_to_use: When performing many range minimum queries on a static array, especially when query time needs to be logarithmic after initial preprocessing.
description: A data structure that precomputes minimums for all possible ranges of power-of-two lengths, allowing O(1) query time after O(N log N) preprocessing. It efficiently answers range minimum queries on an immutable array.
tips: Precompute log values or use `std::log2` for efficient length calculations.
The `dp[i][j]` entry stores the minimum in the range `[i, i + 2^j - 1]`.
Handle overlapping ranges by taking the minimum of two power-of-two segments.
Memory complexity is O(N log N).
pitfalls: Does not support updates to the array elements; it's for static data only.
Higher memory consumption compared to some other range query structures.
Can be complex to implement correctly, especially indexing and power-of-two calculations.
Not suitable for dynamic arrays or when updates are frequent.
invariants: For any `j`, `dp[i][j]` stores the minimum value in the segment starting at `i` with length `2^j`.
The `log_table[k]` stores `floor(log2(k))` for efficient length calculation.
During query, the two chosen segments `[L, L + 2^k - 1]` and `[R - 2^k + 1, R]` completely cover the query range `[L, R]`.

title: Range Minimum Query
when_to_use: When needing to find the minimum (or maximum) value within various specified ranges of an array efficiently. Suitable for scenarios with many queries on a fixed or frequently updated dataset.
description: This technique precomputes answers for subproblems, typically by building a tree-like structure or a table, to efficiently answer queries for the minimum (or maximum) value within a given range. It achieves O(log N) or O(1) query time after O(N log N) or O(N) preprocessing, depending on the specific data structure used.
tips: For static arrays, Sparse Table offers O(1) query time after O(N log N) preprocessing.
For dynamic arrays with updates, Segment Tree offers O(log N) query and update time after O(N) preprocessing.
Use 0-based indexing consistently to avoid off-by-one errors.
Consider the trade-off between preprocessing time, query time, and memory usage.
pitfalls: Off-by-one errors when defining range boundaries or calculating indices.
Incorrect merge logic for segment tree nodes (e.g., using sum instead of min).
High memory usage for large N, especially with Sparse Table (O(N log N)).
Forgetting to handle edge cases like single-element ranges or out-of-bounds queries.
invariants: For a Segment Tree node covering range [L, R], its value stores the minimum of all elements in the original array from index L to R.
For any query range [Q_L, Q_R], the combined result from relevant tree nodes correctly represents the minimum in [Q_L, Q_R].
In a Sparse Table, st[i][j] stores the minimum of the subarray starting at i with length 2^j.
The decomposition of a query range into powers of two for Sparse Table always covers the entire range without gaps.

title: Range Minimum Query
when_to_use: Use when range minimum queries are needed on an array where elements can be dynamically removed or merged, and Union-Find is employed to manage the active state or connectivity of indices.
description: Union-Find can be adapted to assist in range minimum queries, particularly when elements are dynamically removed or merged. It helps maintain connectivity of active elements or segments, allowing efficient traversal to find the minimum among available items, often with near-constant amortized time per Union-Find operation.
tips: Utilize path compression and union by rank/size for optimal Union-Find performance.
Store relevant properties (e.g., minimum value, next available index) within the representative of each set.
For 'next available element' pattern, `parent[i]` points to the smallest index `j >= i` that is currently active.
Consider offline processing of queries and updates to leverage Union-Find's strengths.
Combine with other data structures (e.g., segment tree) for full RMQ functionality if Union-Find only manages availability.
pitfalls: Union-Find alone does not directly provide range minimums; it typically manages connectivity or availability.
Naive iteration through active elements found by Union-Find can be too slow for large ranges.
Incorrectly updating minimum values or properties in merged sets can lead to wrong results.
The approach is highly specific and not suitable for general static RMQ problems.
Complexity can be high if not carefully combined with other techniques or if not applied to specific problem types.
invariants: The `find` operation correctly returns the canonical representative for any element `i`.
Each set in the Union-Find structure maintains a property (e.g., minimum value, next available index) relevant to its merged elements.
For 'next available element' pattern, `parent[i]` always points to the smallest index `j >= i` that is currently active/unprocessed.

title: Range Minimum Query
when_to_use: Use when needing to find the minimum element within various specified ranges of a static array efficiently, especially when many queries are expected after initial preprocessing.
description: Range Minimum Query (RMQ) finds the minimum value in a given contiguous subsegment of an array. It typically involves preprocessing the array to achieve O(1) or O(log N) query time, with preprocessing in O(N log N) or O(N).
tips: For static arrays, Sparse Table offers O(1) query time after O(N log N) preprocessing.
Segment Trees provide O(log N) query and update times, suitable for dynamic arrays.
RMQ can be reduced to LCA on a Cartesian tree, and vice-versa, highlighting their close relationship.
Precompute powers of 2 or their logarithms for efficient range length calculations in Sparse Table.
Consider block decomposition for an O(N) preprocessing and O(1) query time solution.
pitfalls: Sparse Tables do not support updates efficiently; they are primarily for static data.
Off-by-one errors are common when calculating range indices or log values for segment lengths.
High memory usage for Sparse Table (O(N log N)) can be an issue for very large N.
Incorrectly handling edge cases such as single-element ranges or empty query ranges.
Overlooking the constant factors for O(1) solutions, which might be slower than O(log N) for small N.
invariants: For a Sparse Table `st[i][j]`, `st[i][j]` stores the minimum in the range `[i, i + 2^j - 1]`.
During a query `[L, R]`, the chosen blocks `[L, L + 2^k - 1]` and `[R - 2^k + 1, R]` completely cover the target range.
The preprocessing step correctly computes minimums for all powers-of-two length segments.
The query function always returns the minimum value within the exact specified range `[L, R]`.

title: MEX (minimal excluded) of a sequence
signals: find smallest integer not in set
when_to_use: Use when you need to find the smallest non-negative integer that is not present in a given collection of numbers. It's often applied in problems involving game theory or combinatorial analysis.
description: This algorithm finds the smallest non-negative integer not present in a given sequence or set. It typically involves marking present numbers and then iterating from zero to find the first unmarked number, achieving O(N) or O(N log N) complexity.
tips: Use a boolean array or hash set for efficient presence checking.
The MEX value is at most N (for N elements) if all numbers are distinct and within [0, N-1].
Filter out negative numbers and numbers greater than N early to optimize space/time.
Consider sorting the array first for a simpler linear scan approach.
pitfalls: Forgetting to handle the case where all numbers from 0 to N-1 are present, meaning MEX is N.
Using an inefficient method for presence checking, leading to higher time complexity.
Not considering the maximum possible value of MEX, which can be N.
Potential memory limits if N is very large and a boolean array is used.
invariants: All non-negative integers less than the current candidate MEX value have been found in the input sequence.
The data structure used for presence tracking accurately reflects which non-negative integers up to a certain bound are present.
The search for MEX progresses incrementally from 0, ensuring the first missing number found is indeed the minimal one.

title: MEX (minimal excluded) of a sequence
when_to_use: Use when you need to efficiently find the MEX of a sequence after multiple point updates, where the sequence elements are non-negative integers.
description: This approach uses a segment tree or similar data structure to maintain counts of elements, allowing efficient O(log N) updates and O(log N) queries for the minimal excluded number (MEX). The core idea is to find the smallest non-negative integer not present in the sequence.
tips: Build the tree over the range of possible non-negative integer values, typically up to N or a maximum constraint.
Each tree node should store the count of distinct numbers present within its represented range.
When querying for MEX, traverse the tree by prioritizing the left child if its range is not 'full' (i.e., missing elements).
Initialize the tree with the frequencies of elements from the initial array state.
pitfalls: Forgetting to handle numbers larger than the maximum range covered by the segment tree.
Incorrectly updating counts for elements, leading to stale or erroneous MEX values.
Off-by-one errors in defining segment tree ranges or during traversal logic.
Performance degradation if the range of possible numbers is excessively large, requiring coordinate compression.
invariants: Each node in the segment tree correctly stores the count of distinct numbers within its represented range.
The MEX query always explores the leftmost child first if its range is not 'full' (i.e., missing elements).
The segment tree covers the entire range of possible non-negative integer values relevant to the problem.
After any update, the counts in all affected ancestor nodes are correctly propagated.

title: MEX (minimal excluded) of a sequence
when_to_use: Use when needing to efficiently find the smallest non-negative integer not present in a dynamic sequence or range, supporting both updates and queries in logarithmic time.
description: This technique finds the Minimal Excluded (MEX) value in a sequence by maintaining counts of distinct elements in ranges using a specialized data structure, typically a segment tree. It supports both updates and queries in O(log N) time.
tips: Implement a segment tree where each node stores the count of distinct numbers in its range.
The binary search for MEX checks if a range [0, k) contains k distinct numbers to decide which child to traverse.
If a left child's range [L, M) is full (contains M-L distinct numbers), the MEX must be in the right child's range.
Ensure the data structure can handle updates to individual elements, affecting distinct counts.
pitfalls: Incorrectly calculating distinct counts in segment tree nodes, especially after updates.
Off-by-one errors when comparing the number of distinct elements with the expected count for a full range.
Failing to initialize the data structure correctly for the initial sequence or empty ranges.
Not considering the maximum possible value for MEX, which might exceed the sequence length.
invariants: Each segment tree node accurately reflects the count of distinct numbers within its corresponding range.
If a range [L, R) contains R-L distinct numbers, then all integers from L to R-1 are present.
During MEX search, if the left child's range [L, M) is 'full', the MEX must reside in the right child's range [M, R).
The search path always leads to the smallest non-negative integer that is not present in the current sequence.

title: Kth order statistic in O(N)
when_to_use: Use when you need to find the Kth smallest or largest element in an unsorted array efficiently, without fully sorting the entire data structure. It's particularly useful for large datasets where a full sort is too slow.
description: Quickselect is a selection algorithm that finds the Kth smallest element in an unsorted list. It uses a divide-and-conquer approach similar to Quicksort, but only recurses on one side, achieving an average time complexity of O(N).
tips: Choose a good pivot strategy, such as a random pivot, to minimize the chance of worst-case performance.
Handle duplicate elements carefully during the partitioning step to ensure correctness.
Consider tail recursion optimization to prevent stack overflow issues on very large inputs.
Adjust the target K value based on whether the array is 0-indexed or 1-indexed.
For guaranteed worst-case O(N), consider the median-of-medians pivot selection, though it's often slower in practice.
pitfalls: Poor pivot selection can lead to a worst-case time complexity of O(N^2).
Deep recursion without tail call optimization can cause stack overflow errors.
Off-by-one errors are common when calculating indices or adjusting the K value.
Incorrectly handling edge cases like empty arrays or K being out of bounds for the array size.
Not accounting for the possibility of duplicate elements affecting partition logic.
invariants: All elements to the left of the pivot are less than or equal to the pivot's value.
All elements to the right of the pivot are greater than or equal to the pivot's value.
The pivot element is at its final sorted position after partitioning.
The search range for the Kth element is strictly reduced in each recursive call.

title: Kth order statistic in O(N)
when_to_use: When you need to efficiently find the Kth smallest or largest element in an unsorted array. It is ideal when a full sort is unnecessary and average linear time performance is acceptable.
description: Quickselect is a selection algorithm that finds the Kth smallest element in an unsorted list by repeatedly partitioning the array around a pivot. It achieves an average time complexity of O(N) and a worst-case complexity of O(N^2).
tips: Shuffle the input array or choose a random pivot to achieve average O(N) performance.
The algorithm modifies the input array in place, so a copy might be needed if the original must be preserved.
Can be implemented iteratively to avoid potential recursion depth limits on large inputs.
The core partitioning logic is similar to Quicksort's partition step.
pitfalls: A consistently bad pivot choice (e.g., always smallest/largest) leads to O(N^2) worst-case time.
Not suitable if the relative order of other elements needs to be preserved (it's not a stable algorithm).
Recursive implementations can lead to stack overflow for very deep recursion on large inputs.
Off-by-one errors are common when handling array indices and the target K value.
invariants: All elements in the left partition are less than or equal to the pivot element.
All elements in the right partition are greater than or equal to the pivot element.
The pivot element is at its final sorted position after each partitioning step.
The search range for the Kth element is strictly reduced in each iteration.

title: Kth order statistic in O(N)
when_to_use: Use when you need to find the Kth smallest or largest element in an unsorted array efficiently, without sorting the entire dataset. It's ideal for problems requiring a specific order statistic in linear average time.
description: This algorithm finds the Kth smallest (or largest) element in an unsorted array in expected linear time, O(N), by repeatedly partitioning the array around a pivot. It is a selection algorithm, conceptually similar to quicksort but only recursing on one side.
tips: Choose a good pivot (e.g., random element) to minimize the chance of worst-case performance.
The algorithm can be implemented iteratively to avoid potential recursion depth limits for very large inputs.
For guaranteed worst-case O(N) performance, use the Median-of-Medians algorithm for pivot selection.
Handle edge cases for K, such as K=1 (minimum) or K=N (maximum), and array boundaries carefully.

Off-by-one errors are common when translating K (1-based) to array indices (0-based).
Not handling duplicate elements correctly during partitioning can lead to incorrect results or infinite loops.
Standard recursive implementations can hit stack overflow limits on extremely large inputs.
invariants: All elements to the left of the chosen pivot are less than or equal to the pivot.
All elements to the right of the chosen pivot are greater than or equal to the pivot.
The Kth element, once found, is in its correct sorted position relative to the entire array.
The search space for the Kth element is reduced in each partitioning step.

title: Games on arbitrary graphs
signals: determine outcome for all starting positions
count outgoing edges to specific states
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to determine the outcome (win, lose, or draw) for every possible starting position in a two-player game played on a directed graph, assuming both players play optimally.
description: This algorithm classifies each vertex in a directed graph as a winning, losing, or draw state for a two-player game. It iteratively applies rules based on neighbor states, optimized by a Depth-First Search on the reversed graph, achieving an O(M) time complexity.
tips: Construct the reversed graph to efficiently propagate state classifications from successors to predecessors.
Initialize states for terminal vertices (those with no outgoing edges) as losing states.
Maintain a counter for each undefined vertex, tracking how many of its outgoing edges lead to winning states.
The DFS should only explore undefined vertices, marking them as winning or losing when conditions are met.
pitfalls: Forgetting to account for draw states, which occur when neither winning nor losing conditions are met.
Incorrectly classifying terminal nodes; they are typically losing states in normal play games.
Failing to use the reversed graph, leading to inefficient state propagation or higher complexity.
Errors in updating or checking the count of outgoing edges to winning states, which is crucial for identifying losing states.
invariants: A vertex `u` is a winning state if and only if it has at least one successor `v` that is a losing state.
A vertex `u` is a losing state if and only if all its successors `v` are winning states.
Vertices with no outgoing edges are always classified as losing states.
Any vertex that cannot be classified as winning or losing after all propagations is a draw state.

title: Sprague-Grundy theorem. Nim
when_to_use: Use when analyzing impartial games with perfect information and no draws, especially to determine winning/losing states or to find a winning strategy. It's particularly useful for games that can be reduced to Nim.
description: The Sprague-Grundy theorem states that every impartial game under the normal play convention is equivalent to a Nim pile of a certain size (its nim-value or grundy number). For Nim, a position is a winning position if and only if the XOR sum of all pile sizes is non-zero, allowing for O(N) classification.
tips: Understand the definition of impartial games and their properties.
Calculate the XOR sum of game components to determine the nim-sum.
A game state with a nim-sum of zero is a losing position for the current player.
A game state with a non-zero nim-sum is a winning position for the current player.
The theorem applies to any impartial game, not just the classic Nim game.
pitfalls: Confusing impartial games with partisan games where players have different moves.
Incorrectly calculating the XOR sum, leading to wrong state classifications.
Applying the theorem to games with draws, imperfect information, or non-normal play conventions.
Forgetting the base case: an empty Nim game (all piles empty) has an XOR sum of zero.
Misinterpreting the 'winning strategy' as always having a forced win, rather than just the existence of one.
invariants: The XOR sum of the pile sizes (nim-sum) represents the game's current nim-value.
If the nim-sum is zero, any valid move will result in a non-zero nim-sum for the opponent.
If the nim-sum is non-zero, there always exists a move to make the nim-sum zero for the opponent.
The game terminates when all piles are empty, and the nim-sum is zero.

title: Sprague-Grundy theorem. Nim
when_to_use: Use to analyze impartial games under normal play convention, determining winning/losing positions and optimal strategies. It applies when a game can be decomposed into independent subgames.
description: The Sprague-Grundy theorem states that every impartial game is equivalent to a Nim pile of a certain size (its nim-value or Grundy number). This allows complex games to be analyzed by XORing their individual game states, typically with polynomial time complexity for Grundy number calculation.
tips: Understand the concept of Nim-sum (XOR sum) for game states.
Calculate Grundy numbers (nim-values) for subgames using the mex (minimum excluded value) rule.
A game state is a winning position if its Nim-sum is non-zero, and a losing position if it's zero.
Break down complex impartial games into simpler independent subgames.
Use memoization for Grundy number calculation to avoid redundant computations.
pitfalls: Applying to non-impartial games or games not under normal play convention.
Incorrectly calculating mex values or Nim-sums.
Forgetting that terminal states have a Grundy value of 0.
Overlooking game states that are not truly independent.
