Complexity of Grundy number calculation can be high for large state spaces.
invariants: The Nim-sum of a losing position is always zero.
From any winning position, there exists a move to a losing position.
The Grundy value of a game state is the smallest non-negative integer not present in the set of Grundy values of states reachable in one move.
The Nim-sum of independent games is the XOR sum of their individual Grundy values.

title: Sprague-Grundy theorem. Nim
when_to_use: Use to determine winning and losing positions in any finite impartial game under normal play. It allows reducing complex games to an equivalent Nim pile, simplifying analysis.
description: The Sprague-Grundy theorem states that every impartial game under normal play is equivalent to a Nim pile of a certain size, called its Grundy value or nim-value. This value is computed recursively as the minimum excludant (mex) of the Grundy values of all states reachable in one move, typically with memoization for efficiency.
tips: Memoize Grundy values for each game state to avoid redundant computations.
Ensure all possible moves from a given state are correctly identified.
The mex function finds the smallest non-negative integer not present in a set.
A game state is a losing position (P-position) if its Grundy value is 0.
A game state is a winning position (N-position) if its Grundy value is non-zero.
The sum of Grundy values (XOR sum) determines the overall game state for sums of impartial games.
pitfalls: Applying the theorem to non-impartial games or games not under normal play.
Incorrectly calculating the mex function or missing reachable states.
Forgetting to memoize, leading to exponential time complexity for large state spaces.
Misrepresenting game states, especially for complex game rules.
Failing to handle base cases where no moves are possible (Grundy value is 0).
Infinite loops or cycles if the game graph is not correctly handled as acyclic or finite.
invariants: For any state, all its successor states' Grundy values are computed or will be computed before its own.
The mex function always returns the smallest non-negative integer not found in its input set.
A game state's Grundy value is 0 if and only if all moves from it lead to states with non-zero Grundy values.

title: Optimal schedule of jobs given their deadlines and durations
when_to_use: Use when given a set of tasks with deadlines and durations, and the objective is to maximize the number of completed tasks without interruption.
description: This greedy algorithm aims to maximize the number of jobs completed by prioritizing tasks that finish earliest. It typically involves sorting and using a min-heap, achieving an O(N log N) time complexity.
tips: Sort jobs by their deadlines in ascending order.
Maintain a min-heap of durations for jobs currently scheduled.
Iterate through sorted jobs, scheduling if possible or replacing a longer job in the heap.
The total count of jobs in the heap at the end is the maximum.
pitfalls: Confusing job count maximization with total duration or profit.
Failing to update the current time correctly after scheduling a job.
Incorrectly managing the heap when a new job conflicts with existing ones.
Not handling cases where no jobs can be scheduled.
invariants: All jobs processed up to the current point have been considered for scheduling.
The set of jobs in the heap represents the jobs currently scheduled to meet their deadlines.
The current time tracks the completion time of the last scheduled job.
The number of completed jobs is maximized up to the current iteration.

title: Optimal schedule of jobs given their deadlines and durations
when_to_use: When needing to find an optimal schedule for a set of jobs, each with a deadline and duration, typically to maximize completed jobs or minimize total lateness. This approach often involves processing jobs based on deadlines and dynamically selecting tasks.
description: This algorithm finds an optimal job schedule by processing jobs sorted by their deadlines, using a priority queue to manage available tasks. It greedily selects jobs with the shortest run-time that can be completed within the current time window, achieving an optimal solution in O(N log N) time complexity.
tips: Sort jobs by deadline in descending order to process later deadlines first.
Use a min-priority queue to efficiently retrieve the job with the least run-time.
Consider the time intervals between consecutive deadlines for scheduling.
The greedy choice is to always pick the shortest job that fits.
pitfalls: Incorrectly sorting jobs (e.g., ascending deadline or by duration).
Not handling overlapping time intervals or deadlines properly.
Using a data structure that doesn't efficiently support 'extract min' (e.g., a regular queue).
Forgetting to account for the total time elapsed when scheduling.
invariants: All jobs with deadlines greater than the current job's deadline have been considered.
The priority queue contains jobs that can potentially be scheduled within the current time window.
The total time spent on scheduled jobs does not exceed the current job's deadline.
The set of scheduled jobs is always optimal for the jobs considered so far.

title: Scheduling jobs on one machine
when_to_use: Use when scheduling a set of jobs on a single machine to minimize the total weighted completion time, where each job has a processing time and a linear penalty coefficient.
description: This algorithm finds an optimal schedule for jobs on a single machine with linear penalty functions by sorting jobs based on a specific ratio. It achieves an optimal solution in O(N log N) time complexity.
tips: Derive the sorting criterion using an exchange argument for adjacent jobs.
The optimal order is determined by the ratio of cost coefficient to processing time.
Handle cases where processing time is zero or cost coefficient is zero appropriately.
Ensure all penalty coefficients are non-negative as per problem statement.
pitfalls: Incorrectly applying the sorting criterion, especially the ratio order.
Overlooking edge cases like zero processing times or zero penalty coefficients.
Assuming non-linear penalty functions can be solved with the same method.
Not considering the impact of constant terms in penalty functions.
Integer overflow if processing times or coefficients are very large.
invariants: The partial schedule constructed so far is optimal for the jobs included.
For any two adjacent jobs 'j' and 'k' in the final schedule, if 'j' precedes 'k', then 'c_j/t_j >= c_k/t_k'.
The completion time of job 'i' is the sum of processing times of all jobs scheduled before or at 'i'.

title: Scheduling jobs on one machine
when_to_use: When tasks need to be processed sequentially on a single resource to optimize a cumulative metric like total completion time or weighted sum of completion times.
description: This algorithm finds an optimal sequence for processing jobs on a single machine by applying a greedy strategy, often involving sorting based on specific criteria. It typically achieves an optimal schedule in O(N log N) time complexity.
tips: Identify the correct sorting key (e.g., processing time, weight/time ratio) for the objective function.
Prove optimality using an exchange argument to justify the greedy choice.
Consider different objective functions, such as minimizing total completion time or maximum lateness.
Pre-sort jobs based on the determined greedy criterion.
Handle edge cases like zero processing times or weights carefully.
pitfalls: Applying an incorrect sorting criterion for the specific optimization objective.
Assuming a greedy approach is always optimal without a formal proof.
Incorrectly calculating cumulative metrics, leading to suboptimal results.
Overlooking dependencies between jobs if they are not explicitly handled.
Potential floating-point precision issues when sorting by ratios.
invariants: The list of jobs is sorted according to the chosen greedy criterion.
For any prefix of the sorted jobs, the current schedule minimizes the objective function for those jobs.
The completion time of the i-th job in the schedule is the sum of processing times of the first i jobs.

title: Scheduling jobs on one machine
when_to_use: Use when tasks with associated processing times and costs need to be sequenced on a single machine to minimize a total objective function, typically involving weighted completion times or penalties. The optimal sequence is found by a greedy sort.
description: This method finds an optimal schedule for jobs on a single machine by sorting them according to a greedy criterion derived from an exchange argument. It achieves an optimal solution with O(N log N) time complexity, dominated by the sorting step.
tips: Clearly define the objective function to be minimized.
Formulate the greedy sorting rule using an exchange argument for any two adjacent jobs.
Ensure the derived comparison function is transitive and consistent.
Consider using a stable sort if jobs with identical priorities need to maintain their original relative order.
pitfalls: Incorrectly deriving the greedy comparison criterion.
Applying this method to problems with multiple machines or complex precedence constraints without adaptation.
Failing to account for edge cases like zero processing times or costs.
Overlooking potential integer overflow issues when calculating comparison values.
invariants: The relative order of any two jobs i and j in the sorted sequence is consistent with the greedy comparison rule.
Any prefix of the sorted schedule represents an optimal ordering for the jobs within that prefix.
The greedy comparison (c_i / t_i) vs (c_j / t_j) (or c_i * t_j vs c_j * t_i) correctly determines the optimal relative order of jobs i and j.

title: Scheduling jobs on two machines
when_to_use: Use when determining the optimal sequence of jobs to minimize the total completion time (makespan) on two machines, with a fixed processing order (machine 1 then machine 2) for all jobs.
description: This problem involves ordering N jobs, each with two processing times (one for each machine), to minimize the overall makespan. Johnson's rule provides an optimal greedy solution for this specific two-machine flow shop problem in O(N log N) or O(N) time.
tips: Sort jobs based on Johnson's rule criteria: jobs with min(a_i, b_i).
Prioritize jobs with shorter machine 1 times or longer machine 2 times for early scheduling.
Prioritize jobs with shorter machine 2 times or longer machine 1 times for later scheduling.
Divide jobs into two sets based on whether a_i < b_i or a_i >= b_i.
The optimal sequence is formed by concatenating the sorted first set and the reverse-sorted second set.
pitfalls: Applying Johnson's rule to problems with more than two machines, where it is not optimal.
Incorrectly sorting jobs within the two partitioned sets.
Not strictly adhering to the fixed processing order (machine 1 then machine 2).
Confusing the objective of minimizing makespan with minimizing total flow time.
Overlooking edge cases such as jobs with zero processing time on one machine.
invariants: All jobs processed so far maintain the optimal relative order determined by Johnson's rule.
The current makespan is the minimum possible for the jobs already scheduled.
No machine processes more than one job at any given time.
Every job completes processing on machine 1 before starting on machine 2.

title: Scheduling jobs on two machines
when_to_use: Use when optimizing the order of a sequence of tasks on two machines to minimize total idle time or makespan. It is typically applied when a greedy exchange argument can prove optimality.
description: This algorithm determines an optimal sequence for jobs on two machines by iteratively applying a greedy choice, often proven optimal via an exchange argument. It typically achieves an optimal solution in polynomial time, commonly O(N log N) or O(N^2).
tips: Focus on the local effect of swapping adjacent elements to derive the greedy criterion.
Identify a metric (e.g., total idle time or makespan) that changes predictably with swaps.
The greedy choice often involves sorting jobs based on a derived property.
Verify the greedy choice property with a formal exchange argument.
Consider Johnson's Rule as a specific application for two-machine flow shop scheduling.
pitfalls: The greedy choice might not be globally optimal for problems with more than two machines.
Incorrectly defining the cost function for idle time or makespan can lead to suboptimal solutions.
Overlooking edge cases, such as an empty sequence of jobs or a single job.
Assuming a greedy approach without a formal proof (e.g., exchange argument) can be risky.
Not accounting for dependencies between jobs or varying setup times.
invariants: The relative order of jobs already placed in the schedule is optimal.
The total idle time for the current prefix of scheduled jobs is correctly calculated.
No swap of adjacent jobs in the current partial schedule would decrease the total idle time.

title: 15 Puzzle Game: Existence Of The Solution
when_to_use: Use this to quickly determine if a given 15-puzzle board configuration can be solved, avoiding exhaustive search. It's applicable when the goal is to check solvability rather than finding the solution path.
description: This algorithm determines the solvability of a 15-puzzle configuration by analyzing the parity of inversions in the tile permutation and the blank tile's row. It involves counting inversions, which typically takes O(N^2) time for N tiles, or O(N log N) with a more advanced counting method.
tips: Remember to exclude the blank tile (0) when forming the permutation for inversion counting.
For a 4x4 grid, the blank tile's row (from bottom, 1-indexed) is crucial for the parity check.
Inversion counting can be done efficiently using merge sort or a Fenwick tree.
The parity rule is a necessary and sufficient condition for solvability.
Ensure consistent indexing for rows (e.g., 1-indexed from bottom).
pitfalls: Incorrectly including the blank tile (0) in the inversion count.
Miscalculating the blank tile's row index (e.g., 0-indexed from top vs. 1-indexed from bottom).
Applying the wrong parity rule for different grid sizes (e.g., 3x3 vs. 4x4).
Off-by-one errors when counting inversions or determining row parity.
Forgetting that the rule applies to the *number* of inversions, not the inversions themselves.
invariants: The permutation used for inversion counting always excludes the blank tile.
Each pair of tiles (i, j) with i < j is considered exactly once for inversion counting.
The blank tile's row index is consistently determined relative to the bottom of the grid.

title: Josephus Problem
when_to_use: Used to determine the position of the last remaining person in a circle where every k-th person is eliminated until only one remains. It models sequential removal processes in a circular arrangement.
description: The Josephus Problem determines the position of the survivor in a circle of N people, where every K-th person is eliminated. The standard solution uses a recursive relation or dynamic programming, typically achieving O(N) time complexity.
tips: Use 0-indexing for a simpler and more elegant recursive formula.
Establish the base case for N=1 (the survivor is always 0 or 1).
The modulo operation correctly handles wrapping around the circle.
Consider the effect of removing a person on the indexing of the remaining circle.
pitfalls: Off-by-one errors when switching between 0-indexing and 1-indexing.
Incorrectly applying the modulo operator, especially with negative results in some languages.
Forgetting the base case or defining it incorrectly.
Misunderstanding how the circle shrinks and re-indexes after an elimination.
invariants: The result J(n, k) represents the survivor's original 0-indexed position among n people.
Each recursive step J(n, k) correctly transforms the result from J(n-1, k) based on the k-th elimination.
The modulo n ensures the result stays within the valid range [0, n-1] for the current circle size.

title: Josephus Problem
when_to_use: Use when determining the survivor's position in a circular game where every k-th person is eliminated, especially for large N where direct simulation is too slow.
description: This algorithm finds the position of the last remaining person in the Josephus problem by iteratively reducing the problem size using a recursive formula and modulo arithmetic. It achieves an optimized time complexity of O(k log n).
tips: Handle the base case where n < k separately to avoid infinite loops or incorrect calculations.
Use 0-indexing for simpler modulo arithmetic in the recursive formulas.
The recursive relation involves carefully shifting indices based on the number of elements removed.
Consider the effective number of elements remaining after each pass to adjust indices correctly.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling index shifts after elements are removed can lead to wrong results.
Forgetting the base case n < k can lead to infinite recursion or incorrect behavior.
Off-by-one errors are common when converting between 0-indexing and 1-indexing.
Naive simulation for large n will be too slow, resulting in a Time Limit Exceeded error.
invariants: The number of remaining elements decreases in each recursive step.
The relative order of the remaining elements is preserved throughout the process.
The current starting position is correctly adjusted after each elimination round.
The result of each recursive call is a valid index within the current set of remaining elements.

title: Josephus Problem
when_to_use: Use when simulating a circular elimination process with a fixed step size, especially when seeking the survivor's position. It's particularly efficient for specific step sizes like two.
description: The Josephus Problem determines the last remaining position in a circular arrangement after elements are eliminated at fixed intervals. The recursive solution for a step size of two has a logarithmic time complexity, O(log N), due to its divide-and-conquer nature.
tips: Base cases are crucial for recursive solutions, typically for N=1.
Observe patterns for specific step sizes, especially k=2, which has a simpler formula.
Carefully handle 0-indexed versus 1-indexed positions to avoid off-by-one errors.
For k=2, finding the largest power of two less than N can simplify the direct formula.
The problem can be generalized using modular arithmetic for arbitrary step sizes.
pitfalls: Off-by-one errors when converting between 0-indexed and 1-indexed results.
Incorrectly applying the position 'shift' after elements are eliminated in subproblems.
Stack overflow for very large N if a naive recursive solution is used without optimization.
Misinterpreting the circular nature of the arrangement, especially when re-indexing.
Applying the specific k=2 formulas to problems with a general step size k.
invariants: The number of participants N is always positive in each recursive subproblem.
The returned position is always within the valid range [0, current N-1] (or [1, current N]).
Each recursive call correctly maps the survivor's position from the reduced problem to the original problem's context.

title: Search the subarray with the maximum/minimum sum
when_to_use: When needing to find the maximum or minimum sum of any contiguous subarray within a given array efficiently. It is particularly useful for problems requiring an O(N) solution.
description: This algorithm finds the maximum (or minimum) sum of a contiguous subarray by iterating through the array, maintaining a running prefix sum, and tracking the minimum (or maximum) prefix sum encountered so far. It achieves an optimal O(N) time complexity with O(1) auxiliary space.
tips: Use a running sum variable instead of explicitly storing the entire prefix sum array.
Keep track of the minimum prefix sum encountered up to the current point.
For minimum subarray sum, negate all elements and find the maximum subarray sum.
Initialize running sum and minimum prefix sum carefully (e.g., 0 for sum, 0 for min_prefix_sum before any elements).
pitfalls: Incorrectly initializing the minimum prefix sum (e.g., to INT_MAX instead of 0 or s[0]).
Off-by-one errors when calculating subarray sums using prefix sums (s[r] - s[l-1]).
Forgetting to handle cases where all numbers are negative (max sum would be the largest negative number).
Not considering an empty subarray if allowed by problem constraints.
invariants: max_global_sum stores the maximum subarray sum found among all subarrays ending at or before the current element.
min_prefix_sum_value correctly tracks the minimum prefix sum encountered up to the previous element's prefix sum.
current_running_sum is the sum of elements from the beginning of the array up to the current element.

title: Search the subarray with the maximum/minimum sum
when_to_use: When you need to find the contiguous subarray within a one-dimensional array of numbers that has the largest or smallest sum, typically in linear time.
description: This algorithm efficiently finds the maximum (or minimum) sum of a contiguous subarray by iterating through the array and maintaining a current sum, resetting it if it becomes detrimental. It operates in O(N) time complexity with O(1) space complexity.
tips: Initialize current and global maximums appropriately (e.g., to negative infinity or first element).
Adapt for minimum sum by resetting current sum if it becomes positive.
Track start and end indices to reconstruct the actual subarray.
Consider edge cases like all negative or all positive numbers.
pitfalls: Failing to correctly initialize maximum/minimum sum variables.
Not handling arrays where all elements are negative (for max sum) or positive (for min sum).
Incorrectly updating the current sum, leading to non-contiguous segments.
Errors in tracking the start and end indices of the optimal subarray.
invariants: `global_max` holds the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray encountered up to the current element.
`current_max` holds the maximum sum of a contiguous subarray ending at the current element.
If `current_max` becomes negative, it is reset to zero (or the current element for all-negative cases), ensuring only positive contributions are extended.

title: Search the subarray with the maximum/minimum sum
signals: sum of elements in a region
when_to_use: Use this when you need to find a submatrix within a 2D array that yields the maximum or minimum possible sum of its elements. It's applicable when the problem can be reduced to multiple 1D maximum/minimum subarray sum problems.
description: This technique extends the 1D maximum/minimum subarray sum problem to two dimensions. It typically involves iterating over all possible pairs of row (or column) boundaries, reducing the problem to a 1D array, and then applying a 1D dynamic programming approach like Kadane's algorithm, resulting in an O(N^3) time complexity for an N x N matrix.
tips: Precompute prefix sums for rows to efficiently calculate column sums between fixed row boundaries.
The 2D problem can be reduced to multiple 1D problems by fixing two row indices and creating a temporary 1D array of column sums.
Kadane's algorithm is the standard O(N) solution for the 1D maximum subarray sum, which is crucial here.
Consider both maximum and minimum sum variants; Kadane's can be adapted or run on negated values for minimum.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling negative numbers when searching for maximum sum; Kadane's algorithm correctly handles this, but custom logic might fail.
Off-by-one errors when defining submatrix boundaries or calculating sums for the 1D reduction.
Forgetting to reset the 1D temporary array or its DP state for each new pair of row/column boundaries.
Assuming the optimal submatrix must contain positive numbers; it might contain negatives if the overall sum is maximized.
invariants: For any fixed pair of top and bottom row indices (r1, r2), the temporary 1D array correctly represents the sum of elements in columns between r1 and r2.
Kadane's algorithm, when applied to the temporary 1D array, correctly finds the maximum/minimum subarray sum for that specific row range.
The overall maximum/minimum sum found so far is always the true maximum/minimum among all submatrices processed up to the current iteration.
The submatrix boundaries (r1, r2, c1, c2) are always valid indices within the original matrix.

title: Search the subarray with the maximum/minimum sum
when_to_use: Use when the problem asks for the minimum or maximum value that satisfies a monotonic condition.
description: This technique finds a target value within a range by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half, based on a monotonic condition. It typically achieves O(log N) time complexity for a search space of size N.
tips: Clearly define the search space [low, high] for the answer.
Implement a `check` function that determines if a given `mid` value is a feasible solution.
Ensure the `check` function's property is monotonic (e.g., if `X` is feasible, all `Y > X` are also feasible).
Adjust `low` or `high` pointers carefully based on whether you're searching for minimum or maximum.
pitfalls: Incorrectly defining the search range [low, high] for the answer.
Errors in the `check` function leading to incorrect monotonicity or logic.
Off-by-one errors when updating `low` or `high` pointers, leading to infinite loops or incorrect results.
Forgetting to handle edge cases or when no solution exists within the defined range.
invariants: The optimal answer always lies within the current search range [low, high].
All values less than 'low' (for min search) or greater than 'high' (for max search) are known to be non-optimal.
The `check(mid)` function consistently returns true for values on one side of the optimal answer and false on the other.

title: The Stern-Brocot tree and Farey sequences
when_to_use: To systematically enumerate all positive irreducible rational numbers or to find a specific fraction efficiently using a binary search-like approach.
description: The Stern-Brocot tree is a binary search tree-like structure that generates all positive irreducible fractions by recursively inserting mediants between adjacent fractions. It provides an ordered enumeration of rational numbers with logarithmic time complexity for finding a specific fraction.
tips: Start with 0/1 and 1/0 as initial bounds for generation.
The mediant (a+c)/(b+d) is always irreducible if a/b and c/d are irreducible.
Can be used to find the best rational approximation for a real number.
The tree structure naturally maintains fractions in sorted order.
pitfalls: The tree is infinite; direct full construction is impossible.
Careful handling of the initial 'infinity' fraction (1/0) is crucial.
Floating-point arithmetic is unsuitable for precise fraction representation.
Potential for deep recursion if not managed carefully for specific searches.
invariants: All generated fractions are irreducible.
Each new mediant lies strictly between its two parent fractions.
The current fraction (a+c)/(b+d) is always between a/b (left ancestor) and c/d (right ancestor).
Fractions are generated in increasing order within any given level of the tree.

title: The Stern-Brocot tree and Farey sequences
when_to_use: Use when you need to represent all positive irreducible fractions in a structured tree or efficiently search for a specific fraction. It's also useful for generating Farey sequences or understanding rational number properties.
description: The Stern-Brocot tree is an infinite binary tree containing every positive irreducible fraction exactly once, ordered by value. Searching for a fraction or generating parts of the tree typically involves a logarithmic number of steps relative to the sum of the numerator and denominator, making it efficient for O(log(x+y)) operations.
tips: The mediant of a/b and c/d is (a+c)/(b+d), which is always irreducible if a/b and c/d are.
All fractions in the Stern-Brocot tree are inherently irreducible.
The tree is implicitly defined; explicit construction is often unnecessary for search or generation.
Use 0/1 and 1/0 as initial 'ancestors' to define the root's children and start the generation process.
The search path length for a fraction x/y is bounded by x+y.
pitfalls: Integer overflow when calculating mediants if numerators or denominators are very large.
Incorrectly handling the initial 'infinite' fractions (0/1 and 1/0) as boundary conditions.
Forgetting that the tree contains *all* positive irreducible fractions, not just a subset.
Off-by-one errors or incorrect comparison logic when generating the 'L'/'R' path.
Confusing the Stern-Brocot tree with other fraction-generating trees like the Calkin-Wilf tree.
invariants: For any node m/n, all fractions in its left subtree are strictly less than m/n.
For any node m/n, all fractions in its right subtree are strictly greater than m/n.
During the search for target_p/target_q between left_p/left_q and right_p/right_q, the invariant left_p/left_q < target_p/target_q < right_p/right_q holds.

title: The Stern-Brocot tree and Farey sequences
when_to_use: Use this when representing positive rational numbers as unique paths in a binary tree or when efficiently approximating irrational numbers. It's ideal for finding a fraction's path or its position within ordered sequences.
description: The Stern-Brocot tree uniquely maps every positive rational number to a binary path, constructed by repeatedly taking mediants. This allows for efficient search and representation of fractions with O(log(p+q)) time complexity.
tips: Initialize search boundaries with 0/1 and 1/0 (or 1/1 for positive fractions).
Calculate the mediant (pL+pR)/(qL+qR) to determine the next candidate fraction.
Group consecutive identical moves (L or R) using run-length encoding for logarithmic efficiency.
The path to a fraction p/q is a sequence of L/R choices, effectively a binary search.
pitfalls: Naive O(p+q) search without run-length encoding can be too slow for large fractions.
Incorrectly handling initial boundary conditions (e.g., 0/1, 1/0, 1/1).
Using floating-point arithmetic instead of rational arithmetic can lead to precision errors.
Miscalculating mediants or misinterpreting path directions (L vs. R).
invariants: The target fraction p/q always lies strictly between the current left boundary pL/qL and right boundary pR/qR.
The current left and right boundary fractions (pL/qL, pR/qR) are always adjacent terms in some Farey sequence.
The search interval (pL/qL, pR/qR) is progressively narrowed with each step towards the target fraction.

title: The Stern-Brocot tree and Farey sequences
when_to_use: When enumerating or searching for rational numbers in lowest terms within a specific range, or when constructing continued fraction representations.
description: The Stern-Brocot tree is a binary search tree for all positive rational numbers in lowest terms, generated by mediant operations. It allows for efficient enumeration and search of fractions, often with logarithmic complexity for finding a specific fraction.
tips: Understand the mediant operation: (p/q) + (r/s) = (p+r)/(q+s).
The tree is implicitly defined; explicit construction is rarely needed.
Use two 'current' fractions (left and right bounds) to navigate the tree.
The path to any fraction corresponds to its continued fraction expansion.
pitfalls: Integer overflow when dealing with large numerators/denominators.
Incorrectly handling edge cases like 0/1 and 1/0 initial bounds.
Forgetting that fractions must be in lowest terms (Stern-Brocot naturally produces them).
Confusing Stern-Brocot with Farey sequences (related but distinct generation).
invariants: The current search interval [left_num/left_den, right_num/right_den] always contains the target fraction.
All fractions p/q generated by mediant operations are irreducible (in lowest terms).
The mediant (left_num + right_num) / (left_den + right_den) is the simplest fraction between the current left and right bounds.
For adjacent fractions p/q and r/s in the traversal, p*s - q*r = 1.

title: Floyd's Linked List Cycle Finding Algorithm
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to efficiently detect if a linked list contains a cycle and, optionally, find the entry point of that cycle, using only constant extra space.
description: This algorithm uses two pointers, one moving faster than the other, to detect if a cycle exists in a linked list. If a cycle is found, it can then determine the cycle's starting node in O(N) time complexity and O(1) space complexity.
tips: Initialize both slow and fast pointers at the head of the list.
Move the slow pointer by one step and the fast pointer by two steps in each iteration.
If the pointers meet, a cycle exists; otherwise, if fast reaches null, no cycle.
To find the cycle's starting node, reset one pointer to the head and move both pointers one step at a time until they meet again.
Handle edge cases like empty lists or lists with a single node carefully.
pitfalls: Forgetting to check for null on 'fast' and 'fast.next' before dereferencing them.
Incorrectly assuming the list is non-empty or has at least two nodes.
Miscalculating the cycle length or the starting point after detection.
Entering an infinite loop if the fast pointer logic is flawed and no cycle exists.
invariants: The slow and fast pointers are always valid nodes or null.
In the detection phase, the fast pointer always covers twice the distance of the slow pointer.
If a cycle exists, the fast pointer will eventually meet the slow pointer.
If the fast pointer or its next node becomes null, no cycle is present in the list.

title: Linked-list implementations of collections
when_to_use: Use when a Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) data structure is required, and dynamic size with efficient O(1) push and pop operations is crucial, especially without a fixed capacity.
description: This describes implementing a stack using a singly linked list, where elements are added and removed from the head of the list. Both push and pop operations achieve O(1) time complexity.
tips: Maintain a 'first' pointer to the head of the linked list, representing the stack's top.
Push operation involves creating a new node, linking it to the current 'first', then updating 'first'.
Pop operation involves storing the 'first' item, updating 'first' to 'first.next', and returning the stored item.
Always check if the stack is empty before attempting a pop operation to prevent errors.
Keep a size counter ('n') to provide O(1) retrieval of the stack's current number of elements.
pitfalls: Forgetting to handle the empty stack case for pop, leading to `NoSuchElementException`.
Incorrectly updating the 'first' pointer during push or pop, which can break the list structure.
Not updating the size counter ('n') correctly after push or pop operations.
Accidental modification of 'next' pointers, causing elements to be lost or the list to become corrupted.
Ignoring memory management in languages without automatic garbage collection, leading to leaks.
invariants: The 'first' pointer always points to the top element of the stack, or is null if the stack is empty.
Each node's 'next' pointer correctly links to the subsequent element in the stack, maintaining the LIFO order.
The 'n' variable (size) accurately reflects the total number of elements currently in the stack.
All elements reachable from 'first' are part of the stack and are in the correct LIFO sequence.

title: Linked-list implementations of collections
when_to_use: Use when frequent insertions or deletions at the beginning or end of a collection are required, or when the collection size is highly dynamic and unpredictable.
description: Linked lists implement dynamic collections like queues and stacks by chaining nodes, each holding an item and a reference to the next node. Operations like adding or removing elements at the ends typically achieve O(1) time complexity, while random access is O(N).
tips: Maintain separate `first` and `last` pointers for O(1) enqueue/dequeue operations.
Keep a `size` counter to provide O(1) size retrieval.
Implement an `Iterator` for convenient traversal using for-each loops.
Consider a dummy head node for simpler code, especially for singly linked lists.
Always handle edge cases such as an empty list or a list with a single element.
pitfalls: Forgetting to update `first` or `last` pointers correctly can lead to broken lists or memory leaks.
Null pointer exceptions are common when traversing or modifying an empty or partially traversed list.
Incorrectly managing `next` pointers can create cycles or disconnect parts of the list.
Lack of random access means indexed operations require O(N) traversal.
Memory overhead per element due to storing pointers alongside data.
invariants: The `first` pointer always references the head node, or is null if the list is empty.
The `last` pointer always references the tail node, or is null if the list is empty.
The `n` variable accurately stores the current number of elements in the collection.
Each node's `next` field points to the subsequent node, or is null if it is the last node.

title: Linked-list implementations of collections
when_to_use: When you need dynamic collections that efficiently support insertions and deletions at specific points, or when memory usage needs to be precisely managed without contiguous memory allocation.
description: This describes how fundamental data structures like stacks, queues, and bags can be implemented using linked lists, providing dynamic resizing and efficient element manipulation. Operations typically achieve O(1) time complexity for additions and removals at the ends.
tips: Use a Node class to encapsulate data and a reference to the next element.
Maintain head and tail pointers for efficient O(1) operations in queues.
For stacks, only a head pointer is sufficient for O(1) push/pop.
Implement isEmpty() and size() methods for utility.
Consider using a static nested Node class for better encapsulation.
pitfalls: Forgetting to handle edge cases like empty lists or single-element lists.
Introducing 'loitering' by not nullifying references to removed objects, preventing garbage collection.
Off-by-one errors when traversing or manipulating pointers.
Memory leaks if references are not properly managed, especially in languages without automatic garbage collection.
Increased memory overhead per element due to pointer storage compared to arrays.
invariants: The head pointer always points to the first element, or is null if the list is empty.
The tail pointer always points to the last element, or is null if the list is empty.
Each Node's next reference points to the subsequent node, or is null if it's the last node.
The size variable accurately reflects the number of elements in the collection.

title: Elementary Sorts
when_to_use: Selection sort is simple but inefficient. It's suitable for very small datasets or educational purposes where simplicity of implementation is prioritized over performance.
description: Selection sort repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted portion of the array and places it at the beginning of the sorted portion. It has a time complexity of O(N^2) and a space complexity of O(1).
tips: Easy to understand and implement.
Performs a minimal number of swaps (exactly N-1 swaps).
Useful for very small arrays or when memory writes are costly.
pitfalls: Inefficient for large datasets due to O(N^2) time complexity.
Performance is not affected by the initial order of elements.
Not a stable sort, meaning relative order of equal elements may change.
Many comparisons are performed, even if swaps are few.
invariants: The elements in the prefix of the array are sorted.
All elements in the sorted prefix are less than or equal to all elements in the unsorted suffix.
The current minimum element found in the unsorted portion is correctly identified.
The size of the sorted prefix increases by one in each iteration.

title: Elementary Sorts
when_to_use: Use when a simple, in-place sorting algorithm is needed for medium-sized arrays, offering better performance than insertion sort but less complexity than advanced sorts.
description: Shell sort is an in-place comparison sort that improves upon insertion sort by sorting elements that are far apart, then progressively reducing the gap between elements to be sorted. Its average-case time complexity is typically O(N log N) or O(N^(3/2)) depending on the gap sequence, while its worst-case is O(N^2).
tips: Choose an effective gap sequence (e.g., Sedgewick, Knuth) for better performance.
Start with a large gap and reduce it to 1 for the final pass.
The final pass with a gap of 1 guarantees a fully sorted array.
Can be implemented in-place with minimal auxiliary space.
pitfalls: Performance heavily depends on the chosen gap sequence.
Not a stable sort; the relative order of equal elements may change.
Worst-case time complexity can be quadratic (O(N^2)) with poor gap sequences.
More complex to implement correctly than simple insertion sort.
invariants: For any gap 'h', all 'h'-subarrays are sorted.
After each pass with a gap 'h', elements are 'h'-sorted.
The array always contains the same elements as the original input, just potentially reordered.
The final pass with gap 1 ensures the entire array is sorted.

title: Mergesort
when_to_use: Use when a stable sort with guaranteed O(N log N) time complexity is required, even if O(N) auxiliary space is acceptable. It is suitable for sorting linked lists efficiently.
description: Mergesort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that recursively divides an array into two halves, sorts them, and then merges the sorted halves. It guarantees O(N log N) time complexity and uses O(N) auxiliary space.
tips: Utilize an auxiliary array for the merging step to simplify implementation and ensure stability.
Mergesort is a stable sorting algorithm, preserving the relative order of equal elements.
Its O(N log N) time complexity is guaranteed in all cases (best, average, worst).
pitfalls: The primary disadvantage is the requirement for O(N) extra space for the auxiliary array.
Implementing an in-place merge without significant extra space is complex and often less efficient.
Recursive implementations can lead to stack overflow for very large inputs if not optimized or converted to an iterative approach.
invariants: Before merging, the left subarray `a[lo..mid]` and right subarray `a[mid+1..hi]` are individually sorted.
After each recursive call `sort(a, aux, sub_lo, sub_hi)`, the subarray `a[sub_lo..sub_hi]` is sorted.
During the merge step, `a[lo..k-1]` contains the smallest `k-lo` elements from `aux[lo..hi]` in sorted order.

title: Mergesort
when_to_use: Use when a stable sort with guaranteed O(N log N) time complexity is required, especially for large datasets or external sorting where data might not fit in memory.
description: Mergesort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that recursively divides an array into halves, sorts them, and then merges the sorted halves. It has a time complexity of O(N log N) and requires O(N) auxiliary space.
tips: Implement the merge step carefully to ensure stability.
Consider an iterative bottom-up approach to avoid recursion overhead.
Optimize small subarrays with insertion sort for better performance.
Use an auxiliary array for merging to simplify logic and ensure stability.
pitfalls: High auxiliary space complexity (O(N)) can be a concern for memory-constrained systems.
Recursive calls can lead to stack overflow for very large inputs without tail call optimization.
The constant factor overhead can make it slower than quicksort for average cases on arrays.
Incorrect indexing in the merge step can lead to unsorted results or out-of-bounds errors.
invariants: The elements `index[lo...k-1]` are sorted and contain the smallest `k-lo` elements from the original `aux[lo...mid]` and `aux[mid+1...hi]` segments.
Pointer `i` always indicates the next element to consider from the left sorted sub-array (`aux[lo...mid]`).
Pointer `j` always indicates the next element to consider from the right sorted sub-array (`aux[mid+1...hi]`).

title: Mergesort
when_to_use: Use when a stable, guaranteed O(N log N) time complexity sort is required, especially for large datasets or when external sorting is beneficial. It is also suitable for linked lists where random access is inefficient.
description: Mergesort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that recursively divides an array into two halves, sorts them, and then merges the sorted halves. It guarantees O(N log N) time complexity and O(N) space complexity.
tips: Utilize an auxiliary array for merging to maintain stability and simplify the merge logic.
Consider the bottom-up iterative implementation to avoid recursion depth limits and potentially improve cache performance.
Mergesort is inherently stable, preserving the relative order of equal elements.
It is efficient for sorting linked lists as it primarily relies on sequential access.
pitfalls: Requires O(N) auxiliary space, which can be a significant concern for memory-constrained environments.
Recursive implementations can lead to stack overflow errors for extremely large input arrays.
The overhead of copying elements to and from the auxiliary array can be noticeable.
Not an in-place sorting algorithm, unlike some other O(N log N) sorts such as heapsort.
invariants: Before merging, the left subarray `a[lo...mid]` and the right subarray `a[mid+1...hi]` are sorted.
During the merge, `a[lo...k-1]` contains the `k-lo` smallest elements from `aux[lo...hi]` in sorted order.
After a merge operation, the segment `a[lo...hi]` is sorted.

title: Quicksort
when_to_use: Use when an efficient in-place sorting algorithm is needed, especially for large datasets where average-case performance is critical. It's a good general-purpose choice.
description: Quicksort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that partitions an array around a pivot element, then recursively sorts the two resulting subarrays. It has an average time complexity of O(N log N) and a worst-case complexity of O(N^2).
tips: Choose a good pivot strategy (e.g., random pivot, median-of-three) to avoid worst-case scenarios.
Handle small subarrays with insertion sort to reduce recursion overhead.
Implement partitioning carefully to ensure correctness and efficiency.
Consider tail recursion optimization for the larger subarray to save stack space.
pitfalls: Poor pivot selection can lead to O(N^2) worst-case time complexity.
Excessive recursion depth can cause stack overflow for very large inputs.
Not a stable sort; the relative order of equal elements is not preserved.
Can be slower than other O(N log N) sorts for nearly sorted or reverse-sorted data without randomization.
invariants: After partitioning, the pivot element is at its final sorted position.
All elements to the left of the pivot are less than or equal to the pivot.
All elements to the right of the pivot are greater than or equal to the pivot.
The relative order of elements within the left and right subarrays is not guaranteed until they are recursively sorted.

title: Quicksort
when_to_use: Use when an in-place, average-case efficient sorting algorithm is needed, especially for large datasets where O(N log N) performance is critical. It is also suitable for finding the k-th smallest element.
description: Quicksort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that works by partitioning an array around a pivot element and recursively sorting the sub-arrays. It has an average time complexity of O(N log N) and a worst-case complexity of O(N^2).
tips: Choose a good pivot strategy (e.g., random pivot, median-of-three) to avoid worst-case scenarios.
Handle small sub-arrays with insertion sort for better performance due to lower overhead.
Implement the partitioning scheme carefully to ensure correctness and efficiency.
Consider tail recursion optimization to reduce stack depth for very large inputs.
pitfalls: Poor pivot selection can lead to O(N^2) worst-case time complexity.
Quicksort is not a stable sort, meaning the relative order of equal elements might change.
Deep recursion can lead to stack overflow for very large inputs without proper optimization.
Performance can degrade significantly with highly structured or already sorted data if a naive pivot is used.
invariants: Elements in a[lo...i-1] are less than or equal to the chosen pivot v.
Elements in a[j+1...hi] are greater than or equal to the chosen pivot v.
After partitioning, the element at index j is in its final sorted position relative to a[lo...hi].
The pointers i and j move towards each other, ensuring i <= j until the partition is complete.

title: Quicksort
when_to_use: Use Quicksort for efficient in-place sorting of arrays, especially when average-case performance and cache efficiency are prioritized. It is suitable for large datasets where memory usage is a concern.
description: Quicksort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that partitions an array around a pivot element, then recursively sorts the two resulting subarrays. Its average time complexity is O(N log N), but can degrade to O(N^2) in the worst case.
tips: Randomly shuffle the input or choose a random pivot to ensure average-case performance.
Employ 3-way partitioning to efficiently handle duplicate keys and avoid quadratic worst-case scenarios.
Implement a cutoff to insertion sort for small subarrays to reduce overhead.
Stop left scan for items greater than or equal to pivot and right scan for items less than or equal to pivot to handle duplicates correctly.
Ensure pointers do not run off the array ends during partitioning.
pitfalls: Failing to handle duplicate keys can lead to quadratic time complexity.
Incorrect pointer crossing logic can cause infinite loops or array out-of-bounds errors.
Not ensuring at least one item is placed correctly can lead to infinite recursion.
Poor pivot selection without randomization can result in O(N^2) performance.
Forgetting to terminate recursion for base cases (e.g., single-element arrays).
invariants: All elements to the left of the `lt` pointer are less than the pivot value.
All elements to the right of the `gt` pointer are greater than the pivot value.
Elements between `lt` and `i` (in 3-way partitioning) are equal to the pivot value.
The pivot element is eventually placed in its final sorted position within its partition.

title: Priority Queues
when_to_use: Use when you need to efficiently retrieve the element with the highest (or lowest) priority from a collection, especially when elements are added or removed frequently.
description: A priority queue is a data structure that allows efficient retrieval of the element with the highest or lowest priority, and efficient insertion of new elements. Operations typically run in logarithmic time, O(log N).
tips: Use a min-priority queue to find the K largest elements by keeping at most K elements.
Use a max-priority queue to find the K smallest elements by keeping at most K elements.
Ensure elements implement a comparable interface or provide a custom comparator.
For sorted output, transfer elements from the priority queue to a temporary structure like a stack.
pitfalls: Confusing min-priority queue with max-priority queue for the desired extremum.
Inefficient underlying implementation leading to O(N) operations instead of O(log N).
Not handling duplicate keys or elements with equal priorities correctly.
Memory overhead for very large K or numerous elements if not managed carefully.
invariants: The priority queue maintains the heap property: parent nodes are always greater than/equal to (or less than/equal to) their children.
For a K-selection problem, the priority queue contains at most K elements, representing the K largest/smallest seen so far.
The root of the heap always holds the extremum (minimum or maximum) element among all elements currently in the queue.

title: Priority Queues
when_to_use: Use when you need to efficiently retrieve the highest or lowest priority item from a dynamic collection. It's ideal for scenarios requiring frequent insertions and extractions of extreme values without full sorting.
description: A Priority Queue is an abstract data type that provides efficient retrieval of the maximum or minimum element. It is typically implemented using a binary heap, which stores elements in an array representing a complete binary tree, maintaining the heap-ordered property for O(log N) insertion and deletion.
tips: Represent the complete binary tree using an array, often 1-indexed for simpler parent/child calculations.
Use 'swim' (bottom-up reheapify) after insertion to restore heap order.
Use 'sink' (top-down reheapify) after removing the root to restore heap order.
For min-priority queues, reverse comparison logic (parent smaller than children).
Consider an index priority queue for updating priorities of existing items.
pitfalls: Forgetting to handle 0-indexed vs 1-indexed array representations correctly (parent/child formulas differ).
Violating the heap-ordered property during updates or custom operations.
Incorrectly implementing 'swim' or 'sink' logic, leading to an invalid heap.
Modifying keys of items already in the queue without reheapifying, invalidating invariants.
Performance degradation if not using a heap-based implementation for both operations.
invariants: For a max-heap, every parent node's key is greater than or equal to its children's keys.
The heap structure is always a complete binary tree, represented contiguously in an array.
The largest (or smallest) element is always accessible at the root of the heap.

title: Priority Queues
when_to_use: Use when an in-place sorting algorithm with O(N log N) worst-case time complexity is required, especially when memory constraints are a concern. It's also applicable for efficiently finding the K largest/smallest elements.
description: Heapsort is an in-place comparison-based sorting algorithm that uses a binary heap data structure. It first builds a max-heap from the input array, then repeatedly extracts the maximum element and places it at the end of the array, achieving O(N log N) time complexity.
tips: Heap construction can be done in linear time using a bottom-up sink-based approach.
Heapsort is an in-place sorting algorithm, requiring O(1) auxiliary space.
The sortdown phase involves repeatedly extracting the maximum and re-heapifying the remaining elements.
Optimizations exist to reduce comparisons during sortdown by promoting children until the bottom is reached.
pitfalls: Heapsort is not a stable sorting algorithm; the relative order of equal elements is not preserved.
Cache performance can be poor due to non-sequential memory access patterns during heap operations.
Correctly implementing `swim` and `sink` operations, especially with array indexing, is crucial.
Understanding the difference between 0-based and 1-based indexing for parent/child relationships in an array-based heap.
invariants: During heap construction, the processed subarray forms a valid heap-ordered complete binary tree.
During sortdown, the elements in `arr[0...i]` form a valid heap, and `arr[i+1...N-1]` contains sorted elements.
For any node `i` in the heap, its value is greater than or equal to the values of its children (for a max-heap).

title: Binary Search Trees
when_to_use: Use when you need to store ordered data in a hierarchical structure that allows for efficient searching, insertion, and deletion operations. It is suitable for dynamic datasets where elements frequently change while maintaining sorted order.
description: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a node-based binary tree data structure where each node has a key, and all keys in the left subtree are less than the node's key, while all keys in the right subtree are greater. Basic operations like search, insertion, and deletion typically have an average time complexity of O(log N) but can degrade to O(N) in the worst case.
tips: Understand the recursive definition for traversal and operations.
Handle edge cases like empty tree, single node, or leaf nodes carefully.
Deletion of a node with two children requires finding an in-order successor or predecessor.
Consider using iterative approaches for some operations to avoid recursion depth limits.
For balanced performance, consider self-balancing variants like AVL or Red-Black trees.
pitfalls: Unbalanced trees can lead to O(N) worst-case performance for all operations.
Incorrect handling of duplicate keys can lead to data loss or incorrect structure.
Memory overhead for pointers can be significant for very large trees.
Deletion logic, especially for nodes with two children, is prone to errors.
Forgetting to update parent pointers during insertion or deletion.
invariants: For every node N, if L is in N's left subtree, then L.key < N.key.
For every node N, if R is in N's right subtree, then R.key > N.key.
Each subtree rooted at a child of N is also a valid Binary Search Tree.
All keys stored in the tree are unique (assuming no duplicates are allowed).

title: Binary Search Trees
when_to_use: When you need to store ordered data that allows efficient searching, insertion, and deletion while maintaining sorted order. It's suitable for dynamic datasets where elements frequently change.
description: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a node-based binary tree where keys in the left subtree are smaller than the node's key, and keys in the right subtree are larger. Operations like search, insert, and delete typically run in O(log N) average time and O(N) worst-case.
tips: Maintain the BST property: left child < parent < right child.
Recursion simplifies many operations like search, insert, and traversal.
Keep track of subtree sizes for efficient order statistics operations.
Handle null nodes (empty subtrees) carefully in recursive base cases.
pitfalls: Unbalanced trees can degrade performance to O(N) for all operations.
Incorrect handling of duplicate keys can break BST properties or lead to data loss.
Deletion of nodes with two children requires careful successor/predecessor finding.
High memory overhead due to storing multiple pointers per node.
invariants: For any node, all keys in its left subtree are strictly less than its key.
For any node, all keys in its right subtree are strictly greater than its key.
The left and right children of any node are themselves roots of BSTs.
The count of nodes in a subtree rooted at 'node' is '1 + count(node.left) + count(node.right)'.

title: Binary Search Trees
when_to_use: Use when maintaining an ordered collection of elements that requires efficient search, insertion, and deletion operations. It is also suitable for finding minimum, maximum, floor, and ceiling values.
description: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a node-based binary tree where keys in the left subtree are smaller than the node's key, and keys in the right subtree are larger. Core operations like search, insert, and delete typically run in O(h) time, where h is the tree's height.
tips: Maintain subtree sizes for order-statistic operations like finding the k-th smallest element.
Consider self-balancing variants (AVL, Red-Black) to guarantee O(log N) worst-case performance.
Recursive implementations are often cleaner for tree traversals and modifications.
Handle null nodes carefully in recursive calls to prevent errors.
The successor for deletion (when two children exist) is typically the minimum in the right subtree.
pitfalls: Unbalanced trees can degrade to O(N) performance for all operations.
Forgetting to update parent pointers or subtree sizes during modifications.
Incorrectly handling edge cases like deleting root, leaf nodes, or nodes with one child.
Recursive depth can lead to stack overflow for very deep, unbalanced trees.
Not handling duplicate keys correctly based on problem requirements.
invariants: For any node, all keys in its left subtree are less than its own key.
For any node, all keys in its right subtree are greater than its own key.
The size field of any node correctly represents 1 + size(left_subtree) + size(right_subtree).
The tree structure remains valid after insertion and deletion operations.

title: Balanced Search Trees
when_to_use: When you need to store a dynamic set of ordered data and require guaranteed logarithmic time complexity for search, insertion, and deletion operations, even in worst-case scenarios.
description: Balanced search trees are a class of self-balancing binary search trees that maintain a logarithmic height to ensure O(log N) time complexity for core operations like search, insertion, and deletion. They achieve this balance through structural modifications such as rotations or node splitting/merging.
tips: Understand the specific balancing rules (e.g., AVL rotations, Red-Black tree color properties, 2-3 tree node splits).
Consider the trade-offs between different balanced tree types (e.g., simpler implementation vs. stricter balance).
Utilize them when worst-case performance guarantees are critical.
Remember that insertion and deletion involve rebalancing steps.
pitfalls: Implementation complexity can be high due to intricate rebalancing logic.
Overhead for rebalancing operations might be noticeable for very small N.
Debugging can be challenging due to many edge cases in rotations/splits.
Not always necessary if average-case performance of unbalanced BSTs is sufficient.
invariants: For any node, all keys in its left subtree are less than its key(s), and all keys in its right subtree are greater than its key(s).
The height of the tree is always proportional to log N, ensuring O(log N) operations.
After any modification, the specific balance property (e.g., height difference, color rules, node key/link count) is maintained.

title: Balanced Search Trees
when_to_use: When needing a self-balancing search tree that guarantees logarithmic time complexity for search and insertion operations. Useful for maintaining data in sorted order with consistent performance, especially when frequent updates are expected.
description: A 2-3 tree is a self-balancing search tree where every node has either two children (a 2-node) or three children (a 3-node), ensuring all leaves are at the same depth. It guarantees O(log N) time complexity for search, insertion, and deletion operations.
tips: Understand the two node types: 2-nodes (one key, two children) and 3-nodes (two keys, three children).
Insertion always starts by finding the leaf position, then propagates splits upwards if a 4-node is formed.
Splitting a 4-node involves promoting the middle key to the parent and creating two new 2-nodes.
The tree remains perfectly balanced by ensuring all null links are equidistant from the root.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling the promotion of keys during node splits can break the tree's structure.
Forgetting to update parent links after a node split or merge operation.
Edge cases like inserting into an empty tree or a tree with only a root 3-node require careful handling.
Complexity of implementation can be higher than simpler BSTs due to multiple node types and balancing logic.
invariants: All leaves are at the same depth from the root.
Every node is either a 2-node (one key, two children) or a 3-node (two keys, three children).
Keys in a node are sorted, and keys in subtrees follow the search tree property relative to the node's keys.
The tree remains perfectly balanced after every insertion.

title: Balanced Search Trees
when_to_use: Use when needing a dynamic ordered symbol table with guaranteed logarithmic time complexity for all major operations, ensuring efficient performance even in worst-case scenarios.
description: A balanced search tree, like a Red-Black BST, maintains logarithmic height by encoding 2-3 trees using red and black links, ensuring efficient search, insertion, and deletion. All major operations are guaranteed O(log N) time complexity.
tips: Understand the 2-3 tree analogy for conceptual clarity.
Master the three core transformations: left rotation, right rotation, and color flip.
Always ensure the root node remains black after any modification.
For Left-Leaning Red-Black BSTs, red links must always lean left.
Recursive implementations simplify balancing logic during traversal.
pitfalls: Forgetting to update node sizes after structural changes.
Violating red-black tree properties, especially during deletion.
Handling null children correctly in rotation and color flip operations.
Performance degradation from excessive recursion depth on very large trees without tail call optimization.
Complexities of deletion, particularly the moveRedLeft and moveRedRight cases.
invariants: Every node is either red or black.
The root node is always black.
No two red links are adjacent (a red node cannot have a red child).
Every path from the root to any null link contains the same number of black links.

title: Hash Tables
when_to_use: Use when you need to store and retrieve key-value pairs efficiently by transforming keys into array indices, managing potential collisions.
description: Hash tables map keys to array indices using a hash function, enabling average O(1) time complexity for insertions, deletions, and lookups by efficiently managing potential collisions.
tips: Design hash functions for uniform key distribution across the array.
Ensure hash functions are deterministic and efficient to compute.
Implement hashCode() consistent with equals() for custom objects.
Use modular hashing, especially for strings, often with a prime multiplier.
pitfalls: Using a hash function that does not uniformly distribute keys, leading to many collisions.
Failing to implement a proper collision resolution strategy.
Violating the hashCode() and equals() contract for custom types.
Choosing a hash function that is computationally expensive.
invariants: The hash function always produces an index within the valid array bounds [0, M-1].
For any two equal keys, their hash values remain identical.
After collision resolution, each key is stored and retrievable at its designated location.
The set of keys currently in the hash table is accurately represented by the stored key-value pairs.

title: Hash Tables
when_to_use: Use when efficient average-case O(1) insertion, deletion, and lookup of data based on keys is required, especially for non-integer or complex key types.
description: Hash tables store key-value pairs by mapping keys to array indices using a hash function, aiming for O(1) average-case time complexity for basic operations. Collisions are handled to ensure correctness and data integrity.
tips: Choose a good hash function to minimize collisions and distribute keys uniformly across buckets.
Use prime numbers for modular arithmetic in polynomial hashing to reduce collision frequency.
Implement dynamic resizing (rehashing) when the load factor exceeds a threshold to maintain performance.
For compound keys, combine fields carefully to create a unique and well-distributed hash value.
pitfalls: A poorly designed hash function can lead to frequent collisions and degrade performance to O(N) in the worst case.
Forgetting to handle integer overflow during hash computation, especially with large multipliers or long keys.
Not resizing the hash table when the load factor becomes too high, which significantly slows down operations.
Inconsistent hash function implementation between key insertion and subsequent lookup operations.
invariants: Each key maps to a unique value, or collisions are correctly resolved and handled.
The hash function consistently produces the same hash value for identical keys.
The load factor remains below a predefined threshold to ensure average O(1) performance.
All elements are stored in their respective buckets or collision chains within the table.

title: Hash Tables
when_to_use: Use hash tables when efficient storage and retrieval of data by a key are required. They are ideal for implementing dictionaries, sets, or caches where average O(1) time complexity for operations is crucial.
description: Hash tables store key-value pairs by mapping keys to array indices using a hash function and a collision resolution strategy. They provide average O(1) time complexity for insertion, deletion, and lookup operations.
tips: Design hash functions to distribute keys uniformly across the table.
Ensure hashCode() and equals() methods are consistent for custom objects.
Manage the load factor and resize the table to maintain performance.
Use prime numbers for table size to improve modular hashing distribution.
Understand and choose an appropriate collision resolution technique.
pitfalls: Poor hash functions lead to excessive collisions and O(N) worst-case performance.
Inconsistent hashCode() and equals() can cause data retrieval failures.
Neglecting to handle negative hash codes can result in invalid array indices.
High load factors without resizing degrade performance significantly.
Not considering the distribution of input data can lead to performance bottlenecks.
invariants: Each key, if present, is stored at an index derived from its hash code.
If key1.equals(key2), their hash codes produce the same array index (before collision resolution).
The table's load factor remains below a predefined threshold to ensure efficient operations.
All elements mapped to the same initial index are accessible via the collision resolution mechanism.

title: Undirected Graphs
when_to_use: When modeling symmetric relationships between entities and needing to explore, analyze connectivity, or find paths within the network. Useful for problems involving reachability or structural properties.
description: Undirected graphs represent symmetric relationships between pairs of vertices. Simulation on them involves systematically exploring or processing these connections, often with O(V+E) complexity for basic traversals.
tips: Use an adjacency list for sparse graphs to optimize space and traversal time.
Use an adjacency matrix for dense graphs or when O(1) edge existence checks are critical.
Implement Breadth-First Search (BFS) for finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs.
Implement Depth-First Search (DFS) for cycle detection and finding connected components.
Always mark visited nodes to prevent infinite loops and redundant computations during traversal.
Consider Disjoint Set Union (DSU) for efficient tracking of connected components.
pitfalls: Forgetting to handle disconnected components, leading to incomplete graph processing.
Incorrectly representing undirected edges (e.g., only adding one direction to an adjacency list).
Stack overflow errors when using recursive DFS on very deep graphs without iterative conversion.
Not marking nodes as visited, which can cause infinite loops or re-processing of nodes.
Choosing an inefficient graph representation (e.g., adjacency matrix for a very sparse graph).
Off-by-one errors when converting between 0-indexed and 1-indexed vertex representations.
invariants: All vertices reachable from the source within 'k' edges have been processed by the 'k'-th step of BFS.
Each edge in the graph is traversed at most twice (once for each direction) during a full graph traversal.
The 'visited' status of a vertex correctly indicates whether it has been added to the traversal queue/stack.

title: Undirected Graphs
when_to_use: Use when modeling symmetric relationships between entities, analyzing graph properties like connectivity or degrees, or simulating processes on a network where connections are bidirectional.
description: An undirected graph consists of a set of vertices and a set of edges, where each edge connects two vertices symmetrically. Basic operations often have a time complexity of O(V+E) or O(V^2) depending on representation.
tips: Choose adjacency list for sparse graphs, adjacency matrix for dense graphs.
Degrees of vertices are easily calculated from adjacency representations.
Ensure consistent representation for edges (e.g., (u,v) and (v,u) in adjacency list).
Consider using a visited array for traversals to prevent infinite loops.
pitfalls: Forgetting to add both (u,v) and (v,u) when using an adjacency matrix or list.
Not handling disconnected components during full graph traversals.
Incorrectly calculating total edges (each edge counted once, not twice).
Infinite loops in traversals if visited nodes are not tracked.
invariants: For every edge (u, v) in the graph, the edge (v, u) also exists.
The sum of degrees of all vertices equals twice the number of edges.
The graph structure (vertices and edges) remains unchanged during observation or non-modifying operations.

title: Depth-first search
when_to_use: Use when you need to explore as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, such as for finding paths, connected components, or cycles in a graph.
description: DFS systematically explores a graph by going as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. It runs in O(V + E) time and uses O(V) extra space.
tips: Use a 'visited' array or set to prevent infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Recursion stack can be managed explicitly with an iterative approach using a stack.
Keep track of a 'parent' or 'edgeTo' array to reconstruct paths.
Can be easily adapted for various graph problems like cycle detection or topological sort.
Consider the base case for recursive calls carefully to ensure termination.
pitfalls: Stack overflow errors for deep graphs if implemented purely recursively.
Incorrectly handling visited states can lead to infinite loops or incorrect results.
Not suitable for finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs (BFS is generally better).
For directed graphs, distinguish between visited and currently-in-recursion-stack states for cycle detection.
The order of visiting neighbors can affect the specific path found, but not overall reachability.
invariants: All vertices reachable from the starting vertex through already explored paths are marked as visited.
When exploring a vertex 'u', all its unvisited neighbors 'v' are either currently being visited or will be visited recursively.
The recursion stack (or explicit stack) always contains the current path from the start node to the node being processed.
No vertex is added to the recursion stack (or explicit stack) if it has already been fully processed.

title: Depth-first search
when_to_use: Use when you need to explore all reachable vertices from a starting point or systematically visit all nodes in a graph or tree. It's suitable for problems requiring full exploration of a branch before backtracking.
description: Depth-first search is an algorithm for traversing or searching tree or graph data structures. It explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, typically using a stack (explicit or implicit via recursion). Its time complexity is O(V + E) for graphs and O(V) for trees.
tips: Use a `visited` array or set to prevent cycles and redundant work.
Recursion is often cleaner for implementation, but iterative DFS with an explicit stack avoids recursion depth limits.
Keep track of parent nodes for path reconstruction or cycle detection.
Can be used to find connected components by repeatedly starting DFS from unvisited nodes.
Consider post-order traversal for certain tree/graph problems.
pitfalls: Stack overflow can occur with deep recursion on large graphs.
Forgetting to mark nodes as visited leads to infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Incorrectly handling disconnected components if not starting DFS from all unvisited nodes.
Not optimal for finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs (BFS is better).
The order of visiting neighbors can affect results in some applications.
invariants: Every node `v` in the graph is either visited or not yet visited.
The recursion stack (or explicit stack) always contains a path from the source node to the current node being explored.
After `dfs(u)` completes, all nodes reachable from `u` (that were not previously visited) have been explored and marked.

title: Depth-first search
when_to_use: Use DFS when you need to explore all reachable nodes from a starting point, find paths, detect cycles, or perform topological sorting in a graph. It is suitable for problems requiring deep exploration before backtracking.
description: Depth-first search is an algorithm for traversing or searching tree or graph data structures. It explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, typically running in O(V + E) time for graphs.
tips: Use a `visited` array or set to track explored nodes.
Pass the parent node in recursive calls to correctly detect cycles.
Iterative DFS uses an explicit stack to manage exploration.
Mark nodes as visited immediately upon adding to stack/calling recursively.
Handle disconnected graphs by iterating DFS from all unvisited nodes.
pitfalls: Risk of stack overflow for very deep graphs with recursive implementations.
Failure to use a `visited` array leads to infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Incorrectly detecting cycles by not distinguishing parent from other neighbors.
Missing parts of a disconnected graph if not initiated from all unvisited nodes.
Not optimal for shortest path in unweighted graphs; BFS is preferred.
invariants: All nodes currently on the recursion stack (or explicit stack) are part of the current path from the source.
Once a node `v` is marked `visited`, it will not be processed again by the main traversal logic.
For any edge `(u, v)` explored, if `v` is already visited and not the parent `u`, a cycle is detected.
The `edgeTo` array correctly stores the predecessor for each node in the DFS tree.

title: Depth-first search
when_to_use: Use when exploring all reachable nodes from a starting point, detecting cycles, or performing a topological sort on a graph. It's suitable for problems requiring deep exploration before backtracking.
description: Depth-first search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It typically runs in O(V + E) time for graphs and O(V) for trees.
tips: Use a 'visited' array or set to prevent infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Maintain a 'parent' or 'edgeTo' array for path reconstruction or cycle detection.
Recursion is common, but an explicit stack can manage deep graphs or avoid stack overflow.
For directed graphs, distinguish between visited and currently-in-recursion states for cycle detection.
pitfalls: Stack overflow with deep recursion on large graphs.
Incorrectly handling cycles in undirected graphs without parent tracking.
Not resetting visited states for multiple DFS calls on the same graph.
Missing disconnected components if not called from all unvisited nodes.
Infinite loops if 'visited' check is missing or flawed.
invariants: For any visited node 'u', 'marked[u]' is true.
If a path exists from source 's' to 'v', then DFS from 's' will eventually mark 'v'.
For any edge (u, v) explored, if 'v' is not 'u''s parent and 'v' is marked, then a cycle is detected.
All nodes reachable from the starting node 's' will eventually be visited.

title: Depth-first search
when_to_use: Use when exploring all reachable nodes from a starting point, finding paths, or detecting cycles in a graph. It's suitable for problems requiring a deep, exhaustive search.
description: Depth-first search is an algorithm for traversing or searching tree or graph data structures. It explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, typically running in O(V + E) time for graphs represented with adjacency lists.
tips: Maintain a visited set/array to avoid revisiting nodes and infinite loops.
Pass the parent node in recursive calls to correctly identify back-edges for cycle detection.
Consider iterative DFS with an explicit stack for large graphs to avoid recursion depth limits.
