Utilize pre-order and post-order processing for different problem types like topological sort or component analysis.
pitfalls: Failing to mark nodes as visited can lead to infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Deep recursion can cause stack overflow errors on large graphs; use an iterative approach if needed.
Misinterpreting back-edges to parent as cycles, requiring careful parent tracking.
Not iterating through all nodes to find all connected components in a disconnected graph.
invariants: Every node `v` is visited exactly once.
When `dfs(G, u, v)` is called, `v` is unvisited and `u` is its immediate predecessor in the current path.
All nodes reachable from the starting node will eventually be added to the recursion stack or visited.

title: Breadth-first search
signals: find all nodes at a certain distance
when_to_use: Use when finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph or when exploring all reachable nodes level by level. It is also suitable for problems requiring traversal of all nodes at a specific distance from a source.
description: Breadth-first search systematically explores a graph level by level, ensuring that all nodes at distance k are visited before any nodes at distance k+1. It finds the shortest path in terms of number of edges for unweighted graphs in O(V + E) time.
tips: Use a queue to manage nodes to visit.
Mark nodes as visited to prevent cycles and redundant processing.
Store parent pointers (edgeTo) to reconstruct paths.
Initialize distances (distTo) to infinity and source to zero.
Can be used to find connected components.
pitfalls: Forgetting to mark nodes as visited can lead to infinite loops or incorrect paths.
Not handling disconnected graphs correctly.
Incorrectly initializing distances or visited array.
Using it for weighted graphs will not yield shortest paths.
Queue overflow for very large graphs if not managed carefully.
invariants: All nodes currently in the queue have been discovered and marked, but their adjacent nodes have not yet been fully explored.
For any node v marked as visited, distTo[v] stores the shortest path length from the source s to v.
All nodes at distance k from the source are processed before any nodes at distance k+1.
The edgeTo[v] array correctly points to the predecessor on a shortest path from s to v for all visited v.

title: Breadth-first search
when_to_use: Use when finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph or when exploring all reachable nodes layer by layer from one or more starting points. It's ideal for problems requiring minimum steps or level-order processing.
description: Multi-source BFS explores a graph layer by layer, starting from multiple initial nodes simultaneously, to find the shortest path (in terms of edges) to all reachable nodes. It operates in O(V + E) time and O(V) space for adjacency list representation.
tips: Initialize distances for all source nodes to zero and add them to the queue.
Mark nodes as visited immediately upon adding them to the queue to prevent redundant processing.
Use a queue to strictly maintain the order of exploration by layers.
Can be adapted for 0-1 BFS using a deque for specific edge weights.
pitfalls: Forgetting to mark nodes as visited can lead to infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Incorrectly initializing distances or the queue for multiple sources.
Applying BFS directly to weighted graphs where edge weights are not uniform.
Not handling disconnected components if the problem requires exploring the entire graph.
invariants: All nodes currently in the queue are at the same distance from one of the source nodes.
Every node added to the queue has been marked as visited.
The distance to any visited node v is the minimum number of edges from any source.
No node is processed more than once.

title: Breadth-first search
when_to_use: Use when finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph or traversing a graph level by level. It is suitable for exploring all reachable nodes from a source in increasing order of distance.
description: Breadth-First Search (BFS) is a graph traversal algorithm that explores all the neighbor nodes at the current depth level before moving on to nodes at the next depth level. It finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph in O(V + E) time.
tips: Use a queue to manage nodes to visit, ensuring level-by-level exploration.
Maintain a 'visited' array or set to prevent cycles and redundant processing.
Initialize distances to infinity and source distance to zero for shortest path calculations.
Can be adapted for multi-source BFS by enqueuing all sources initially.
pitfalls: Forgetting to mark nodes as visited can lead to infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Incorrectly using a stack instead of a queue will result in Depth-First Search behavior.
Not handling disconnected components if the goal is to visit all nodes in the graph.
Overlooking the need to reset 'marked' and 'distTo' arrays for multiple BFS runs on the same graph object.
invariants: All nodes currently in the queue are at the same or adjacent depth level.
All 'marked' nodes have been visited, and their 'distTo' values are the shortest path from the source.
For any node 'v' in the queue, 'distTo[v]' represents the shortest distance from the source to 'v'.
'edgeTo[w] = v' implies 'v' was the first node to discover 'w' and 'distTo[w] = distTo[v] + 1'.

title: Directed Graphs
when_to_use: Use when modeling relationships with a specific flow direction between entities. It is ideal for determining reachability or finding paths from one or more sources.
description: Directed graphs represent connections where edges have a specific direction, often implemented using adjacency lists. Depth-first search (DFS) and breadth-first search (BFS) are fundamental algorithms for traversing these graphs to find paths and determine reachability in O(V+E) time.
tips: Always use a 'visited' set or array to prevent infinite loops in cycles and redundant processing.
Adjacency list representation is generally preferred for sparse graphs due to better space and time complexity for traversal.
DFS is suitable for finding any path or checking connectivity, while BFS finds shortest paths in unweighted directed graphs.
Consider topological sort for directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) to process nodes in a valid order.
pitfalls: Forgetting to handle cycles can lead to infinite loops or stack overflows in DFS.
Incorrectly applying algorithms designed for undirected graphs to directed graphs without modification.
Not accounting for disconnected components, which may require iterating through all vertices to ensure full traversal.
Using adjacency matrix for very sparse graphs can be inefficient in terms of space and time.
invariants: The 'visited' set accurately reflects all nodes for which traversal has been initiated.
For DFS, all nodes reachable from the current node via unvisited paths will eventually be explored.
For BFS, all nodes at depth 'k' are processed before any node at depth 'k+1'.
Any path discovered from a source 's' to a target 'v' consists solely of valid directed edges.

title: Directed Graphs
when_to_use: Use when you need to traverse all reachable vertices in a directed graph, find specific vertex orderings, or determine a valid linear ordering of vertices based on dependencies. It's also used for cycle detection in directed graphs.
description: Depth-First Search (DFS) explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. When applied to directed graphs, it can determine reachability, detect cycles, and compute topological orderings in O(V + E) time.
tips: Use a visited array/set to prevent infinite loops and redundant work.
Maintain a recursion stack or visiting array to detect cycles during DFS.
For topological sort, process vertices in reverse postorder.
Adjacency list representation is generally preferred for sparse graphs.
Preorder and postorder can be useful for various graph properties.
pitfalls: Forgetting to handle disconnected components in the graph.
Incorrectly implementing cycle detection, especially in directed graphs.
Stack overflow with deep recursion on large graphs; consider iterative DFS.
Misinterpreting preorder/postorder vs. reverse postorder for specific applications.
Not correctly handling self-loops or parallel edges if the problem context requires.
invariants: A vertex is marked visited only after all its descendants in the DFS tree have been fully explored.
If a back-edge is encountered (to a vertex currently in the recursion stack), a cycle is detected.
All vertices reachable from the starting vertex are eventually visited.
For topological sort, vertices are added to the result list (or stack) in reverse postorder.

title: Directed Graphs
when_to_use: When you need to identify maximal sets of vertices in a directed graph where every vertex is reachable from every other vertex within the set.
description: This algorithm identifies strongly connected components (SCCs) in a directed graph using two passes of Depth-First Search. It runs in O(V + E) time, where V is the number of vertices and E is the number of edges.
tips: The first DFS pass is performed on the original graph to determine vertex finish times.
Store vertices in a stack based on their finish times (reverse postorder).
The second DFS pass is performed on the transpose (reverse) graph.
Process vertices in the second DFS pass according to the order obtained from the first DFS (from the stack).
Each DFS tree in the second pass corresponds to a single SCC.
pitfalls: Incorrectly constructing the reverse graph.
Forgetting to use the reverse postorder from the first DFS for the second DFS.
Not marking visited vertices correctly, leading to re-processing or incorrect component identification.
Confusing the original graph with its transpose during the two DFS passes.
invariants: After the first DFS, the stack contains vertices in decreasing order of their finish times in the original graph.
During the second DFS, all vertices explored from a starting vertex form a single strongly connected component.
Each vertex is assigned to exactly one strongly connected component.
The reverse graph G_R has the same SCCs as the original graph G.

title: Directed Graphs
when_to_use: Use when needing to determine reachability between all pairs of vertices in a directed graph, especially for small or dense graphs where O(V*(V+E)) time is acceptable.
description: This approach determines all-pairs reachability in a directed graph by performing a Depth-First Search starting from each vertex. It has a time complexity of O(V*(V+E)) and a space complexity of O(V^2).
tips: Initialize a boolean matrix to store reachability results.
For each vertex, perform a full DFS to mark all nodes reachable from it.
The reachability matrix can be an adjacency matrix where entries are boolean.
Consider using an iterative DFS to avoid recursion depth limits.
pitfalls: High time complexity O(V*(V+E)) makes it unsuitable for large sparse graphs.
High space complexity O(V^2) can be prohibitive for graphs with many vertices.
Does not provide shortest paths, only the existence of paths.
Can be slow if the graph has many edges (E is large).
invariants: For any vertex u, after its dedicated DFS completes, TC[u][v] is true for all v reachable from u.
The transitive closure matrix TC accurately reflects all-pairs reachability in the original graph.
During any DFS traversal, all nodes currently on the recursion stack are reachable from the starting node of that specific DFS.

title: Minimum Spanning Trees
when_to_use: When you need to find a subset of edges in a connected, edge-weighted graph that connects all vertices with the minimum possible total edge weight. Useful for network design problems where minimizing connection costs is crucial.
description: A Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) algorithm finds a subset of edges in an edge-weighted graph that connects all vertices with the minimum possible total weight, forming a tree. This is typically solved using greedy approaches with a time complexity often involving sorting edges or using a priority queue.
tips: Greedy approaches often work by iteratively adding the cheapest valid edge or expanding from a starting vertex.
The cut property and cycle property are fundamental for proving correctness of MST algorithms.
Handle disconnected graphs by computing a Minimum Spanning Forest (MSF) for each component.
Edge weights can be zero or negative, but the graph must be connected for a single MST to exist.
If edge weights are equal, multiple MSTs may exist, but their total weight will be identical.
pitfalls: Assuming the graph is always connected; disconnected graphs require finding a minimum spanning forest.
Incorrectly applying the greedy choice without ensuring it maintains the MST property (e.g., creating a cycle).
Confusing MST with shortest path algorithms; MST connects all vertices, shortest path finds a path between two.
Overlooking the performance impact of underlying data structures like Union-Find or priority queues.
Not considering the implications of non-unique edge weights on the specific MST structure.
invariants: The set of edges selected so far always forms a forest.
Each connected component formed by the chosen edges is a valid subtree of some Minimum Spanning Tree.
Any edge added to the MST connects two previously disconnected components or a new vertex to an existing component.

title: Minimum Spanning Trees
when_to_use: Use Prim's algorithm to find a Minimum Spanning Tree in a connected, edge-weighted graph, especially when the graph is dense or when you need to grow the tree from a specific starting vertex.
description: Prim's algorithm constructs a Minimum Spanning Tree by iteratively adding the minimum-weight edge connecting a vertex in the growing tree to a vertex outside the tree. The eager implementation achieves O(E log V) time complexity and O(V) space complexity using an indexed priority queue.
tips: Utilize an indexed priority queue for the eager implementation to efficiently perform decrease-key operations and achieve optimal O(E log V) time complexity.
Initialize the priority queue with all vertices, setting the starting vertex's distance to 0 and others to infinity, then update as edges are processed.
Maintain a boolean array to track which vertices are already included in the MST to avoid redundant processing and cycles.
Consider the lazy implementation if an indexed priority queue is not readily available or if E is very small, though it has a higher worst-case time complexity.
pitfalls: Forgetting to handle disconnected graphs; Prim's algorithm will only find an MST for the component containing the starting vertex.
Incorrectly updating edge weights in the priority queue in the eager version, leading to suboptimal edges or incorrect complexity.
Using a standard priority queue for the eager version without efficient decrease-key, which degrades performance to O(E log E) or worse.
Not marking vertices as 'in_mst' correctly, potentially adding redundant edges or forming cycles.
invariants: The set of edges added to the MST always forms a single connected component.
For every vertex not yet in the MST, the priority queue stores the minimum-weight edge connecting it to the current MST (eager version).
The total number of edges in the MST never exceeds V-1.

title: Minimum Spanning Trees
when_to_use: Use Kruskal's algorithm to find a subset of edges that connects all vertices in an edge-weighted graph with the minimum possible total edge weight. It is suitable for sparse graphs or when edges are easily sorted.
description: Kruskal's algorithm is a greedy algorithm that builds a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) by iteratively adding the cheapest available edge that does not form a cycle. It typically runs in O(E log E) time, dominated by sorting edges or Union-Find operations.
tips: Sort all edges by their weights in non-decreasing order before processing.
Utilize a Union-Find data structure to efficiently track connected components and detect cycles.
Iterate through sorted edges, adding an edge to the MST if its endpoints are in different components.
Stop when V-1 edges have been added to the MST, where V is the number of vertices.
pitfalls: Incorrectly implementing Union-Find can lead to performance bottlenecks or incorrect cycle detection.
Failing to sort edges correctly will result in a non-minimal spanning tree.
Adding an edge that connects two vertices already in the same component will create a cycle.
Not handling disconnected graphs correctly; Kruskal's finds a Minimum Spanning Forest in such cases.
invariants: The set of edges chosen so far forms a forest, where each tree is a component of the MST.
All edges considered so far that do not form a cycle are part of the current forest.
For any two vertices in the same component of the current forest, the path between them consists only of MST edges.

title: Shortest Paths
when_to_use: To find the path with the minimum total weight or cost between two vertices, or from a single source to all other reachable vertices, in an edge-weighted graph.
description: Shortest path algorithms determine the path with the least cumulative weight between nodes in a graph. This often involves a dynamic programming approach, iteratively updating minimum distances to achieve an optimal O(V*E) or O(E log V) complexity.
tips: Initialize the source distance to 0 and all other distances to infinity.
Use a `distTo` array to store the current shortest distances from the source.
Utilize an `edgeTo` array to reconstruct the actual shortest path.
The core operation is relaxation: `distTo[w] = min(distTo[w], distTo[v] + weight(v,w))`.
pitfalls: Failing to detect or handle negative cycles, which can lead to infinite loops or incorrect results.
Incorrectly initializing distances or edge relaxation logic.
Forgetting to update the `edgeTo` array when a shorter path is found.
Not accounting for disconnected components in the graph.
invariants: distTo[v] always stores the minimum path length from the source to v discovered so far.
For any edge u -> v, if distTo[u] is final, then distTo[v] will eventually be min(distTo[v], distTo[u] + weight(u,v)).
The path formed by tracing edgeTo[v] backwards from v to the source s is a valid path in the graph.

title: Shortest Paths
when_to_use: When finding the shortest path from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted graph. It is specifically applicable when all edge weights are non-negative.
description: Dijkstra's algorithm computes the shortest paths from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. It uses a greedy approach with a priority queue, achieving a typical time complexity of O(E log V) or O(E + V log V).
tips: Use a min-priority queue to efficiently extract the vertex with the smallest current distance.
Initialize source distance to 0 and all other distances to infinity.
Relax edges by updating distances if a shorter path is found through the current vertex.
Store edgeTo predecessors to reconstruct the actual shortest paths.
Can be used on undirected graphs by modeling each edge as two directed edges.
pitfalls: Fails if the graph contains any negative edge weights; use Bellman-Ford or SPFA instead.
Inefficient priority queue implementation can degrade performance significantly.
Not marking vertices as visited can lead to redundant processing or infinite loops.
Incorrectly handling unreachable vertices or disconnected components.
invariants: For any vertex v extracted from the priority queue, distTo[v] is the shortest path distance from the source.
The distTo array always stores the shortest known distance from the source to each vertex found so far.
All vertices not yet extracted from the priority queue have distTo values representing an upper bound on their true shortest path distance.

title: Shortest Paths
when_to_use: This algorithm finds the shortest paths from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a directed acyclic graph (DAG) with arbitrary edge weights.
description: This algorithm computes single-source shortest paths in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) by processing vertices in topological order, relaxing outgoing edges. It achieves a time complexity of O(V + E).
tips: First, perform a topological sort on the DAG to determine the processing order.
Initialize all distances to infinity, except the source vertex which is zero.
Process vertices strictly in topological order, relaxing all outgoing edges for each visited vertex.
This method correctly handles negative edge weights, as long as no cycles exist.
pitfalls: Applying this algorithm to a graph containing cycles will yield incorrect results.
An incorrect topological sort order will lead to incorrect shortest path calculations.
Failing to initialize distances properly (e.g., source not zero, others not infinity).
Not relaxing all outgoing edges for a vertex before moving to the next in topological order.
invariants: The distance to the source vertex, distTo[s], remains 0 throughout the algorithm.
For any vertex v, distTo[v] always stores the shortest path found so far from s to v.
Once a vertex u is processed in topological order, distTo[u] holds its final shortest path value.
For any edge u -> v, distTo[v] <= distTo[u] + weight(u,v) after u has been processed.

title: Shortest Paths
when_to_use: Use when finding single-source shortest paths in edge-weighted digraphs that may contain negative edge weights, or to detect negative cycles. It can also be adapted for longest paths in DAGs.
description: The Bellman-Ford algorithm solves the single-source shortest paths problem in edge-weighted digraphs, even with negative edge weights, or detects negative cycles. Its worst-case time complexity is O(V*E).
tips: Use a queue-based optimization (SPFA) for better average-case performance.
It can detect the presence of negative cycles reachable from the source.
Adapt for longest paths by negating edge weights or reversing relaxation logic.
Essential for graphs with negative edge weights where Dijkstra fails.
Initialize source distance to zero, others to infinity.
pitfalls: Shortest paths are undefined if a negative cycle is reachable from the source.
O(V*E) worst-case time complexity can be prohibitive for large, dense graphs.
Susceptible to floating-point precision issues with real-valued edge weights.
Slower than Dijkstra's algorithm for graphs with only non-negative edge weights.
Does not guarantee finding *all* negative cycles, only one reachable from source.
invariants: For any vertex v, distTo[v] is always an upper bound on the shortest path distance from the source s to v.
If distTo[v] is finite, there exists a path from s to v of length distTo[v].
After k iterations, distTo[v] contains the shortest path length from s to v using at most k edges.
The edgeTo[v] array, if maintained, always points to the predecessor on the current shortest path found to v.

title: String Sorts
signals: small range of keys (R, e.g., 256 for ASCII)
when_to_use: Use when sorting a large collection of fixed-length strings or integers, especially when the key range (alphabet size) is small. It is efficient for data with uniform key lengths.
description: LSD Radix Sort sorts elements by processing digits or characters from the least significant to the most significant, using a stable counting sort for each position. It achieves a time complexity of O(W * (N + R)), where W is the key length, N is the number of items, and R is the radix.
tips: Utilize counting sort as the stable inner sorting algorithm for each digit pass.
Process characters or digits from right to left (least significant to most significant).
Can sort integers by treating them as fixed-length byte strings.
Ensure all keys have the same length; pad shorter keys if necessary for variable-length data.
The auxiliary array is essential for maintaining stability during each pass.
pitfalls: Inefficient for variable-length strings without proper padding or specialized handling.
Can be slower than comparison sorts for small input sizes or very large radix (R).
Requires additional space for the auxiliary array and the counting array.
Not an in-place sorting algorithm due to the use of an auxiliary array.
Fails if the inner sorting algorithm used for each digit is not stable.
invariants: After each pass 'd' (from 'w-1' down to '0'), the array 'a' is stably sorted based on characters from position 'd' to 'w-1'.
The 'count' array accurately reflects the frequency of each character at the current digit 'd' before cumulation.
The 'count' array, after cumulation, correctly provides the starting index for each character in the 'aux' array.
The 'aux' array, after data movement, contains the elements sorted by the current digit 'd' while preserving relative order from previous passes.

title: String Sorts
when_to_use: Use when sorting a large collection of strings or integers with a limited range of character/digit values. It is particularly efficient for fixed-length keys but can be adapted for variable lengths.
description: Radix sort is a non-comparative integer sorting algorithm that sorts data with integer keys by grouping keys by individual digits or characters which share the same significant position and value. It typically achieves O(N*W) time complexity, where N is the number of items and W is the key length.
tips: LSD (Least Significant Digit) is simpler for fixed-length keys.
MSD (Most Significant Digit) is better for variable-length keys or when keys are very long.
Use counting sort as a stable subroutine for each pass.
Pad shorter strings with a special character for LSD if lengths vary.
Consider alphabet size (R) for memory and performance.
Optimize by swapping array references instead of copying.
pitfalls: Not efficient for small N or very large W due to constant factors.
Requires extra space for auxiliary array and counting array.
MSD can have high overhead for small subproblems; use a cutoff to insertion sort.
Handling negative numbers or floating-point numbers requires special mapping.
Alphabet size (R) significantly impacts memory and performance.
Stability of the inner sorting routine (e.g., counting sort) is crucial for correctness.
invariants: After each pass `d`, the elements are stably sorted according to their digits up to position `d`.
The counting sort subroutine correctly places elements into their sorted positions for the current digit, preserving relative order.
For MSD, within any recursive call, all elements in the current sub-array share a common prefix up to the digit being processed.
The auxiliary array `aux` temporarily stores elements in their sorted order for the current digit before being transferred back.

title: String Sorts
when_to_use: Use this algorithm to sort a collection of strings lexicographically, especially when string lengths vary or are large, and the character set is limited.
description: This algorithm sorts an array of strings lexicographically by recursively applying a counting sort-like distribution based on characters from most significant to least significant digit. It typically achieves O(N*L + R*L) time complexity, where N is the number of strings, L is the average string length, and R is the radix size.
tips: Use an auxiliary array for stable distribution during each pass to maintain relative order.
Implement a cutoff to insertion sort for small sub-arrays to improve performance due to lower overhead.
Handle strings of different lengths by returning a sentinel value (e.g., -1) for `charAt` beyond string length.
Choose an appropriate radix (R) based on the character set, such as 256 for extended ASCII.
pitfalls: Can have high overhead for small N or short strings compared to comparison sorts.
Potentially memory intensive due to the auxiliary array and recursion stack depth proportional to max string length.
Performance degrades if string lengths vary widely and many strings are exceptionally long.
Incorrect handling of `charAt` for strings shorter than the current depth `d` can lead to errors.
invariants: All strings in the current `a[lo...hi]` sub-array share a common prefix of length `d`.
After the distribution pass for character `d`, `aux` contains strings from `a[lo...hi]` stably sorted by their `d`-th character.
The `count` array accurately tracks the cumulative frequencies of characters at index `d` within `a[lo...hi]`.

title: Tries
when_to_use: Use when managing string keys in a symbol table and needing efficient prefix, wildcard, or longest prefix matching operations, offering flexibility beyond binary search trees and speed comparable to hashing.
description: A trie (prefix tree) is a tree-like data structure used to store a dynamic set or associative array where keys are typically strings. It allows for efficient retrieval of keys based on their prefixes, achieving O(L) time complexity for operations, where L is the key length.
tips: Choose an appropriate branching factor (R) based on the alphabet size.
Represent children using arrays for fixed alphabets or hash maps for sparse ones.
Mark nodes explicitly to indicate the end of a complete key.
Consider using a compressed trie (radix tree) to save space for long common prefixes.
pitfalls: High memory consumption for large alphabets or sparse data.
Deletion of keys can be complex, requiring careful handling of empty branches.
Not directly suitable for numerical keys without conversion to string representations.
Can be slower than hashing for exact key lookups if L is very large.
invariants: Every path from the root to a node represents a unique prefix.
A node exists only if it is part of at least one stored key.
If a node represents a complete key, it is marked as such.

title: Tries
when_to_use: Use when managing a collection of strings and requiring efficient prefix-based operations like search, insertion, deletion, or finding the longest common prefix.
description: A trie (prefix tree) is a tree-like data structure used to store a dynamic set of strings, where nodes represent characters and paths from the root represent strings. Operations like insertion, search, and deletion typically take O(L) time, where L is the length of the key.
tips: Mark nodes as 'end of word' to distinguish prefixes from complete words.
Use an array for children pointers if the alphabet size is small and fixed (e.g., ASCII).
Consider using a map for children pointers if the alphabet is large or sparse.
Implement recursive helper functions for cleaner insertion and search logic.
pitfalls: High memory consumption for large alphabets or very deep tries due to many empty child pointers.
Deletion can be complex, requiring careful handling of nodes that are part of other words.
Performance degrades if keys do not share common prefixes, making it similar to a hash map.
Overhead of node creation and pointer management can be significant for small datasets.
invariants: Every path from the root to a node represents a unique prefix.
A node's children pointers correspond to the next character in a string.
If a node marks the end of a word, its associated value is non-null.
All keys stored in the trie are reachable by traversing paths from the root.

title: Substring Search
when_to_use: Use when efficiently finding occurrences of a pattern string within a larger text string, especially for average-case fast string matching. It is suitable for scenarios involving large texts or multiple patterns.
description: The Rabin-Karp algorithm finds pattern occurrences in a text by comparing hash values of the pattern and text substrings. It uses a rolling hash function for O(1) substring hash updates, achieving O(N+M) average time complexity and O(NM) worst-case.
tips: Choose a large prime modulus to minimize hash collisions.
Use a random base for polynomial hashing to enhance collision resistance.
Always perform a character-by-character check for true matches after a hash match.
Precompute R^(m-1) % q for efficient rolling hash updates.
pitfalls: Hash collisions can lead to false positives, increasing worst-case time.
Improper choice of prime modulus or base can degrade performance.
Arithmetic overflow if modulo operations are not applied correctly.
Worst-case O(NM) complexity can occur with pathological inputs.
invariants: The pattern's hash value patHash remains constant throughout the search.
At each step i, txtHash accurately represents the hash of txt[i...i+m-1].
RM stores R^(m-1) % q, correctly used for removing the leading character's contribution.

title: Substring Search
when_to_use: Use when efficiently finding all occurrences or the first occurrence of a pattern within a larger text, especially when the pattern might have repeating or overlapping prefixes.
description: The Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) algorithm is an efficient string-searching algorithm that avoids re-scanning characters in the text by pre-processing the pattern to build a 'longest proper prefix suffix' (LPS) array. It achieves optimal O(N + M) time complexity, where N is the text length and M is the pattern length.
tips: Pre-compute the LPS array (also known as failure function or border array) for the pattern.
The LPS array helps determine the next best match position after a mismatch, avoiding redundant comparisons.
Visualize the state transitions or the LPS array construction process for better understanding.
Consider the alphabet size if implementing a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) based KMP.
pitfalls: Incorrectly building the LPS array can lead to wrong matches or infinite loops.
Off-by-one errors when indexing the pattern or text are common.
Forgetting to reset the pattern pointer correctly after a full match or a mismatch.
Failing to handle edge cases like empty patterns or texts, or patterns longer than the text.
invariants: The `j` pointer always indicates the length of the longest proper prefix of `pattern[0...j-1]` that is also a suffix of `text[0...i-1]`.
The `lps[j]` value stores the length of the longest proper prefix of `pattern[0...j-1]` that is also a suffix of `pattern[0...j-1]`.
At any point, `text[i-j ... i-1]` matches `pattern[0 ... j-1]`.
The `i` pointer always points to the current character being examined in the text.

title: Substring Search
when_to_use: Use when needing to find all occurrences or the first occurrence of a pattern string within a larger text string efficiently. It is particularly useful when the pattern might have significant self-overlapping prefixes and suffixes, allowing for linear time complexity.
description: The Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) algorithm efficiently searches for occurrences of a pattern within a text by avoiding redundant comparisons. It preprocesses the pattern to build a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) or Longest Proper Prefix Suffix (LPS) array, achieving O(N + M) time complexity.
tips: Precompute the LPS array or DFA for the pattern to guide the search.
The LPS array indicates the longest proper prefix that is also a suffix, enabling efficient shifts.
KMP avoids re-checking characters in the text that have already been matched.
It's optimal for single pattern search in terms of worst-case time complexity.
Can be extended to find all occurrences of the pattern.
pitfalls: Incorrect construction of the LPS array or DFA is a common source of errors.
High constant factors for small alphabets or short patterns might make simpler algorithms faster.
Space complexity for the DFA can be large if the alphabet size is very big.
Misunderstanding the state transitions can lead to incorrect logic.
Edge cases like empty pattern or text, or pattern longer than text, need careful handling.
invariants: During DFA construction, dfa[c][j] correctly stores the next state if character c is encountered in state j.
During DFA construction, x maintains the length of the longest proper prefix of pattern[0...j-1] that is also a suffix of pattern[0...j-1].
During search, j represents the length of the longest prefix of the pattern that matches a suffix of txt[0...i-1].
During search, txt[i-j ... i-1] is always equal to pattern[0 ... j-1].

title: Substring Search
when_to_use: Use KMP to efficiently find all occurrences of a pattern string within a larger text. It is particularly useful when the pattern might appear multiple times or when avoiding redundant text scans is crucial for performance.
description: The Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) algorithm efficiently finds all occurrences of a pattern string within a text string by pre-processing the pattern to avoid re-scanning characters in the text. It achieves a time complexity of O(N + M), where N is text length and M is pattern length.
tips: Pre-compute the 'next' array (also known as LPS or failure array) for the pattern.
The 'next' array stores the length of the longest proper prefix of the pattern that is also a suffix of the pattern up to that index.
Use two pointers: one for iterating through the text and one for the current match position in the pattern.
When a mismatch occurs, shift the pattern using the pre-computed 'next' array to avoid re-checking already matched characters.
The algorithm guarantees that the text pointer never moves backward.
pitfalls: Incorrectly building the 'next' (LPS) array, especially handling the base cases or transitions.
Off-by-one errors in array indexing for either the text or pattern pointers.
Not correctly resetting the pattern pointer 'j' after a full match is found if continuing to search for more occurrences.
Misunderstanding the role of the 'next' array values, leading to incorrect shifts.
Performance issues if character comparison is very expensive, though rare for standard types.
invariants: The `next` array is correctly pre-computed such that `next[k]` indicates the longest proper prefix of `pattern[0...k-1]` that is also a suffix of `pattern[0...k-1]`.
During the search phase, `text[i-j ... i-1]` always matches `pattern[0 ... j-1]` if `j > 0`.
The text pointer `i` always moves forward or stays in place, never backtracking.
The pattern pointer `j` always points to the current character in the pattern being compared, or indicates the length of the matched prefix.

title: Data Compression
when_to_use: Use when you need to losslessly compress data by assigning shorter bit sequences to more frequent symbols, aiming for the shortest possible average code length. It is effective for data with non-uniform character distributions.
description: Huffman coding is a greedy algorithm that constructs an optimal prefix code for a given set of symbols and their frequencies. It builds a binary tree (Huffman trie) by repeatedly merging the two nodes with the smallest frequencies, resulting in an encoding with O(N + K log K) time complexity, where N is input length and K is alphabet size.
tips: Use a min-priority queue to efficiently retrieve and combine the two lowest-frequency nodes.
Represent the Huffman tree explicitly to generate codes and for decoding.
Store the frequency table or the Huffman tree structure with the compressed data for proper decompression.
Handle edge cases like an empty input or an input with only one distinct character.
pitfalls: The overhead of storing the Huffman tree or frequency table can negate compression benefits for small inputs.
Not truly optimal for all data types; it's a statistical compression method.
Requires two passes over the data: one to compute frequencies, one to encode.
Can be less effective than other algorithms (e.g., LZ77/LZ78 based) for highly repetitive patterns.
Incorrectly reconstructing the tree during decoding will lead to corrupted data.
invariants: The priority queue always contains valid Huffman nodes, each representing a subtree or a leaf character.
Every internal node in the Huffman tree has exactly two children, and its frequency is the sum of its children's frequencies.
All original input characters are represented as leaf nodes in the final Huffman tree.
All generated codes are prefix codes, meaning no code is a prefix of another code.

title: Data Compression
when_to_use: When needing to reduce the size of data by encoding frequently occurring patterns or symbols with shorter representations, especially for lossless compression of text or binary streams.
description: Data compression algorithms like Huffman coding and LZW reduce data size by assigning variable-length codes based on frequency or by replacing repeated patterns with shorter references. Huffman coding typically involves O(N log N) for building the tree and O(N) for encoding/decoding, while LZW can be O(N) for both, where N is the input size.
tips: Use a min-priority queue to efficiently build the Huffman tree from character frequencies.
Represent Huffman codes as bit strings for efficient storage and transmission.
For LZW, dynamically build the dictionary of encountered sequences.
Consider the trade-off between compression ratio and computational overhead.
Pre-calculate character frequencies for Huffman coding.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling edge cases like empty input or single-character input.
Off-by-one errors when reading/writing bit streams.
Dictionary overflow or inefficient dictionary management in LZW.
Not properly serializing and deserializing the Huffman trie for decompression.
Performance degradation for highly random data where compression is ineffective.
invariants: The encoding process consistently maps input symbols or sequences to their assigned codes.
The decoding process reconstructs the original data by traversing the encoding structure using the compressed bitstream.
For prefix codes, no code is a prefix of another code, ensuring unambiguous decoding.
For dictionary-based methods, the dictionary accurately stores mappings from sequences to codes.

title: Aho-Corasick Algorithm for Pattern Searching
when_to_use: Use when you need to find all occurrences of a fixed set of patterns (a dictionary) within a larger text efficiently. It is particularly effective for scenarios with many patterns or long texts where patterns might overlap.
description: The Aho-Corasick algorithm constructs a finite automaton from a dictionary of patterns, then uses this automaton to search for all pattern occurrences in a text in a single pass. It achieves a time complexity of O(N + M + K) where N is text length, M is total pattern length, and K is total matches.
tips: Build the trie first from all patterns, marking terminal nodes.
Compute failure links using a BFS-like approach, similar to KMP's LPS array.
Failure links point to the longest proper suffix of the current prefix that is also a prefix of some pattern.
Use output links (dictionary links) to quickly find all patterns ending at a state via failure links.
Process the text character by character, transitioning through the automaton.
pitfalls: Incorrectly building failure links can lead to missed matches or infinite loops.
Forgetting to handle output links (dictionary links) will miss patterns found via failure transitions.
High memory usage for very large dictionaries or long patterns due to trie structure.
Not resetting the automaton state correctly when processing a new text or query.
Edge cases with empty patterns or single-character patterns.
invariants: The trie structure correctly represents all prefixes of all patterns in the dictionary.
Each node's failure link points to the longest proper suffix of the string ending at that node which is also a prefix in the trie.
When traversing the text, the current state in the automaton always represents the longest suffix of the processed text that is a prefix of some pattern.
All patterns ending at the current trie node, including those found via failure links, are correctly identified.

title: Aho-Corasick Algorithm for Pattern Searching
when_to_use: Use when searching for occurrences of multiple patterns simultaneously within a larger text. It is efficient for finding all matches, including overlapping ones, in linear time.
description: The Aho-Corasick algorithm is a string-searching algorithm that finds all occurrences of a finite set of patterns within a text. It constructs a finite automaton (trie with failure links) from the patterns, allowing for O(N + M + K) matching.
tips: Build the trie from all patterns first to establish common prefixes.
Compute failure links using a breadth-first search (BFS) traversal.
Ensure output links correctly aggregate all patterns ending at or through a state.
Optimize space by sharing common prefixes in the trie structure.
Consider using a queue for efficient BFS during automaton construction.
pitfalls: Incorrectly computing failure links can lead to missed or false matches.
Forgetting to handle output links for patterns that are suffixes of other patterns.
High memory usage for a very large number of patterns or extremely long patterns.
Complexity in debugging the trie and link construction process.
Not handling edge cases like empty patterns or duplicate patterns gracefully.
invariants: Every node in the trie represents a prefix of at least one pattern.
During failure link computation, all nodes at depth d-1 have their failure links correctly set before processing nodes at depth d.
When traversing the text, the current state in the automaton always represents the longest suffix of the processed text that is also a prefix of some pattern.

title: Aho-Corasick Algorithm for Pattern Searching
when_to_use: Use when searching for multiple patterns simultaneously within a larger text. It is highly efficient for finding all occurrences of a dictionary of keywords.
description: The Aho-Corasick algorithm constructs a finite automaton from a set of patterns, allowing efficient simultaneous searching for all patterns in a text. It typically achieves O(N + M + K) time complexity, where N is text length, M is total pattern length, and K is total matches.
tips: Build the trie first from all patterns, representing each pattern as a path.
Use BFS to construct failure links, pointing to the longest proper prefix of the current state's string that is also a suffix.
Optimize output links to quickly find all patterns ending at a state, including those found via failure links.
Consider using a bitset for output to save space if the number of patterns is large.
pitfalls: Incorrectly building failure links can lead to wrong matches or infinite loops.
High memory usage if the pattern set is very large or patterns are excessively long.
Forgetting to handle the root node's failure link, which typically points to itself or null.
Not optimizing output links can degrade performance for many overlapping patterns, especially if traversing failure links repeatedly.
invariants: All patterns in the dictionary are correctly represented as paths in the trie structure.
For any state `s`, its failure link `fail[s]` points to the longest proper prefix of the string represented by `s` that is also a suffix of `s` and exists in the trie.
During BFS for failure links, all nodes at depth `d` have their failure links correctly computed before processing nodes at depth `d+1`.
The automaton correctly transitions to the next state based on the current character, following failure links when no direct transition exists.

title: Aho-Corasick Algorithm for Pattern Searching
when_to_use: Use when you need to find all occurrences of a fixed set of patterns within a larger text efficiently. It's particularly effective for scenarios involving many patterns and potential overlaps.
description: The Aho-Corasick algorithm constructs a finite automaton from a dictionary of patterns, then uses it to find all occurrences of these patterns in a text in a single pass. It operates in O(N + M + K) time, where N is text length, M is total pattern length, and K is total matches.
tips: Build the trie (goto function) first from all patterns.
Construct failure links using a BFS-like approach to ensure correct shortest suffix matching.
Implement the output function to collect matches at each state, propagating through failure links.
Map characters to small integer indices for efficient trie array access.
pitfalls: Incorrectly building failure links can lead to missed matches or infinite loops.
Forgetting to propagate output matches through failure links can miss valid pattern occurrences.
Large alphabet sizes can make the trie (goto function) memory intensive.
Off-by-one errors in character to index mapping can cause incorrect state transitions.
invariants: All patterns in the dictionary are correctly represented in the trie (goto function).
The failure link `f[u]` for any state `u` points to the state representing the longest proper suffix of the string corresponding to `u` that is also a prefix of some pattern.
For any state `u` and character `c`, `g[u][c]` correctly points to the next state representing the longest suffix of the string ending at `u` followed by `c` that is also a prefix of some pattern.
The `out[u]` mask (or list) correctly identifies all patterns ending at state `u` or any state reachable via failure links from `u`.

title: Backtracking Algorithm
when_to_use: Used when exploring multiple possibilities to find a solution incrementally, often involving undoing choices if they lead to a dead end.
description: Backtracking is a problem-solving technique that incrementally builds a solution by trying options and undoing them upon reaching a dead end. It typically explores a search space in a depth-first manner, often resulting in exponential time complexity.
tips: Define clear base cases for recursive termination.
Identify decision points and available choices at each step.
Prune the search space early by checking constraints.
Maintain the current partial solution state efficiently.
Use a helper function to manage recursive calls and state.
pitfalls: Forgetting to undo changes (backtrack) after exploring a path.
Incorrectly defining base cases, leading to infinite recursion or wrong results.
Redundant computations if states are not properly managed or memoized.
Exploring invalid states without proper pruning, wasting computation.
Hitting recursion depth limits for very deep search trees.
invariants: The current partial solution is valid up to the current decision point.
All choices made to reach the current state can be systematically undone.
Each recursive call explores a distinct extension of the current partial solution.
The algorithm systematically explores the search space without missing valid paths.

title: bucket-sort
when_to_use: When the input elements are uniformly distributed across a specific range, allowing for efficient partitioning and sorting.
description: Bucket sort distributes elements into a fixed number of buckets, sorts each bucket, then concatenates them. It achieves O(N+K) average time complexity, where N is elements and K is buckets, assuming uniform distribution.
tips: Choose the number of buckets carefully based on data range and distribution.
Use an efficient sorting algorithm for individual buckets (e.g., insertion sort for small buckets).
Can be stable if the internal sorting algorithm is stable.
Pre-calculate min/max values to define the range for bucket mapping.
Consider using linked lists for buckets to handle variable sizes efficiently.
pitfalls: Poor performance with non-uniform data distribution, leading to elements clustering in few buckets.
Requires knowing the range of input values beforehand to define bucket boundaries.
Can use significant extra space for storing the buckets.
Worst-case time complexity degrades to O(N^2) if all elements fall into a single bucket.
Not suitable for data with a very wide range and few elements due to sparse buckets.
invariants: All elements from the input are initially present in the unsorted collection.
After distribution, each element resides in exactly one bucket, corresponding to its value range.
Elements within each bucket are sorted in non-decreasing order before concatenation.
The final concatenated array contains all original elements in non-decreasing order.

title: bucket-sort
when_to_use: Use when sorting elements, especially floating-point numbers, that are uniformly distributed over a range. It offers linear time complexity on average for such distributions.
description: Bucket sort distributes elements into a fixed number of buckets, sorts each bucket individually using another sorting algorithm, and then concatenates the sorted buckets. It achieves an average time complexity of O(N+K), where N is the number of elements and K is the number of buckets.
tips: Use a stable sorting algorithm like Insertion Sort for individual buckets to maintain overall stability.
The number of buckets should be chosen carefully, often proportional to N, to optimize performance.
For non-uniform distributions, consider using a different sorting algorithm or a more sophisticated bucketing strategy.
Can be efficient for data that is uniformly distributed across a range.
pitfalls: Performance degrades significantly with non-uniform data distributions, potentially becoming O(N^2).
Requires extra space for the buckets, which can be substantial for many buckets or large elements.
Not suitable for data with a very wide range or highly clustered values without careful bucket mapping.
The choice of sub-sorting algorithm impacts overall performance and stability.
invariants: Before sorting any bucket, all elements are correctly placed in their respective buckets based on their range.
After sorting each individual bucket, all elements within that bucket are in sorted order.
After concatenating all sorted buckets, the final output array contains all original elements in sorted order.

title: bucket-sort
when_to_use: Use when sorting elements with a known, uniform distribution over a specific range. It is efficient for numbers that are uniformly distributed across a range, often achieving linear time complexity.
description: Bucket sort distributes elements into a fixed number of buckets, sorts each bucket individually, and then concatenates them. It can achieve O(N+K) average time complexity for uniformly distributed data.
tips: Choose the number of buckets appropriately based on data distribution and range.
Normalize input data to a [0, 1) range for simpler bucket indexing.
Use an efficient sorting algorithm for small buckets, such as insertion sort.
Consider using a O(N log N) algorithm like Merge Sort for buckets to improve worst-case performance.
Ensure the bucket mapping function distributes elements as evenly as possible.
pitfalls: Poor performance with non-uniform data distribution, potentially degrading to O(N^2).
Requires extra space for the buckets, proportional to N+K.
Not suitable for data with a very wide or unknown range without prior scaling.
The choice of sub-sort algorithm significantly impacts worst-case complexity.
Can be less efficient than comparison sorts if data is not uniformly distributed.
invariants: After distributing elements, each element `x` is in bucket `floor(N * x)`.
After sorting buckets, every bucket `buckets[i]` contains its elements in sorted order.
During concatenation, the `arr` prefix `arr[0...index-1]` contains all elements from previously processed buckets in sorted order.
The total number of elements across all buckets remains equal to the original array's length.

title: Digit DP
signals: number representation as array of digits
when_to_use: Use Digit DP to count numbers within a given range [a, b] that satisfy a property based on their digits. It's effective when the property can be checked digit by digit.
description: Digit DP is a dynamic programming technique to count numbers within a given range [A, B] that satisfy specific properties related to their digits. It typically involves a recursive function with memoization, building numbers digit by digit, and has a complexity roughly proportional to (number of digits * state space).
tips: Define the DP state carefully, including index, tight constraint, and leading zero flag.
Handle leading zeros correctly, as they can affect properties like sum or count of digits.
Break down range [A, B] problems into G(B) - G(A-1) where G(X) counts numbers from 1 to X.
Use memoization (often a multi-dimensional array) to store results of subproblems.
Represent the upper bound number as an array of its digits for easier iteration.
The 'tight' constraint is crucial for ensuring numbers do not exceed the original upper bound.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling the 'tight' constraint, leading to overcounting or undercounting.
Forgetting to account for leading zeros or mismanaging their impact on the property.
Incorrect base cases for the recursive function, especially for empty prefixes.
Defining an insufficient DP state that misses crucial information for future decisions.
Off-by-one errors when calculating the range (e.g., G(B) - G(A-1) vs. G(B) - G(A)).
Stack overflow for extremely large numbers if recursion depth is not managed or iterative DP is not used.
invariants: The `tight` parameter accurately reflects if the current prefix matches the upper bound's prefix.
The `is_leading_zero` parameter correctly indicates if all preceding digits chosen were zero.
The DP state `dp[index][tight][leading_zeros][...other_properties]` stores the count of valid suffixes.
Each recursive call explores valid digits from 0 (or 1 if not leading zero) up to the current `limit`.

title: Digit DP
signals: sum of digits in range [A, B]
when_to_use: Used for counting numbers or summing properties of numbers within a given range [A, B] that satisfy certain digit-based conditions.
description: Digit DP is a dynamic programming technique to count numbers or calculate sums of properties for numbers in a given range [A, B] by building them digit by digit. It typically operates in O(D * S) time, where D is the number of digits and S is the maximum sum/property value.
tips: Convert the upper bound number into a digit array for easier processing.
Define the DP state to include current index, tight constraint, and any relevant accumulated property (e.g., sum, count).
Memoize results of subproblems to avoid redundant calculations.
Solve for `count(X)` and then compute the answer for range `[A, B]` as `count(B) - count(A-1)`.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling the `tight` constraint, leading to overcounting or undercounting.
Forgetting to memoize or using an insufficient DP table size for state parameters.
Off-by-one errors in digit ranges or base cases for recursion.
Not correctly handling leading zeros if the problem distinguishes between numbers like '05' and '5'.
Integer overflow when calculating large sums or counts for the final answer.
invariants: The `tight` parameter correctly reflects if the current digit choice is restricted by the original number's digit at the current position.
The `sum` parameter accurately accumulates the sum of digits chosen for the prefix constructed so far.
The `idx` parameter always points to the current digit position being considered, decreasing towards the base case.
All subproblems defined by `(idx, tight, sum)` are computed exactly once and stored in the DP table.

title: Digit DP
when_to_use: When counting numbers within a given range [A, B] that satisfy certain digit-based properties, or calculating a sum of such properties.
description: Digit DP is a dynamic programming technique used to count numbers or calculate sums of properties for numbers within a given range, based on their digits. It typically involves a recursive function with memoization, processing digits from most significant to least significant, achieving a time complexity of O(log(N) * S * T) where S is sum/state range and T is tight constraint.
tips: Handle leading zeros carefully, often with a dedicated boolean state in the DP function.
Use a 'tight' or 'limit' boolean parameter to manage the upper bound constraint imposed by the original number.
Memoize states based on current digit index, current accumulated property (e.g., sum), and tight/leading zero constraints.
Break down range [A, B] problems into `solve(B) - solve(A-1)` for easier implementation.
Precompute the digits of the upper bound number into an array for easier access during recursion.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling the 'tight' constraint can lead to overcounting or undercounting.
Forgetting to reset the memoization table for each new query or test case, especially in competitive programming.
Off-by-one errors when calculating `solve(A-1)` for the lower bound.
Incorrectly propagating the `is_leading_zero` state, especially when the first non-zero digit is chosen.
State definition might become too complex if too many independent properties need to be tracked simultaneously.
invariants: The `idx` parameter always represents the current digit position being processed, from most significant to least significant.
The `tight` boolean parameter accurately indicates if the current digit choice is restricted by the original number's corresponding digit.
The `is_leading_zero` boolean parameter correctly tracks if all preceding digits chosen were zeros.
The memoization table `dp[idx][sum][tight][is_leading_zero]` stores the computed result for the given state, avoiding redundant calculations.

title: Disjoint Sparse Table tutorial
when_to_use: When you need O(1) query time for any associative operation (like sum, min, max, GCD) on a static array, after an O(N log N) preprocessing step.
description: A Disjoint Sparse Table is a data structure that supports O(1) range queries for any associative operation on a static array. It achieves this by precomputing results for specific disjoint and overlapping ranges, with an O(N log N) construction time and space complexity.
tips: Understand the block decomposition based on powers of two for construction.
Queries combine results from two precomputed blocks that cover the query range.
The middle point for queries is crucial for leveraging disjoint precomputed segments.
Precompute logarithms for efficient block size determination during queries.
It is particularly useful when the input array is static and updates are not required.
Can handle any associative operation, unlike standard sparse tables which are limited for sum.
pitfalls: Does not support point updates efficiently; any modification requires a full rebuild.
Higher space complexity of O(N log N) compared to some other range query structures.
Can be complex to implement correctly, especially the block logic and query combination.
Requires the underlying operation to be strictly associative.
Not suitable for dynamic arrays or scenarios with frequent data changes.
invariants: table[k][i] stores the result of the operation for a range of length 2^k starting at index i.
For any query [L, R], the result is obtained by combining two precomputed values from blocks that cover [L, mid] and [mid+1, R].
The chosen mid for a query [L, R] ensures that [L, mid] and [mid+1, R] are covered by precomputed blocks of the same power-of-two length.
The underlying array remains unchanged after the initial construction of the table.

title: Disjoint Sparse Table tutorial
when_to_use: Use when you need to perform O(1) range queries on a static array for an associative operation, after an O(N log N) preprocessing step.
description: A data structure that precomputes answers for all possible range queries using an associative operation on a static array. It achieves O(1) query time and O(N log N) build time by storing prefix-like sums within logarithmically sized blocks.
tips: Ensure the operation is associative (e.g., min, max, sum, gcd, bitwise operations).
Preprocessing takes O(N log N) time and space.
Useful for immutable arrays where queries are frequent.
Can be adapted for operations without an identity element.
pitfalls: Not suitable for mutable arrays; updates require rebuilding the table.
Requires O(N log N) memory, which can be substantial for very large N.
The logic for splitting blocks and calculating midpoints can be tricky to implement correctly.
Does not support non-associative operations.
invariants: For each level k, the array is partitioned into 2^k segments.
Within each segment, table[k][j] stores the result of the associative operation from j to the segment's midpoint (or midpoint to j).
Any range query [L, R] can be answered by combining precomputed results from at most two sub-ranges within a single segment at a specific level.

title: Disjoint Sparse Table tutorial
when_to_use: Use when you need to answer range queries on a static array in O(1) time, after an initial O(N log N) preprocessing phase. It is particularly efficient for associative operations like sum, min, or max.
description: A Disjoint Sparse Table is a data structure that precomputes results for all possible range queries on a static array using a hierarchical block decomposition. It achieves O(N log N) construction time and space complexity, enabling O(1) query time for associative operations.
tips: Precompute log2 values or highest set bit for efficient level determination.
Ensure the operation (e.g., sum, min, max) is associative for correct combination.
The table is built by recursively dividing the array into blocks and computing results within them.
Querying involves finding the highest differing bit between l and r to identify the split level.
pitfalls: Not suitable for arrays that require updates, as it's a static data structure.
Only works for associative operations; non-associative operations cannot be combined this way.
Higher memory consumption (O(N log N)) compared to some other range query structures.
Off-by-one errors are common when calculating block boundaries or indices for levels.
invariants: ST[k][i] stores the result for a range of length 2^k starting at i within its block.
For any level k, blocks are disjoint, but ranges within ST[k] can overlap with ranges in ST[k-1].
A query [l, r] is answered by combining two precomputed values from the same level k, where k is determined by log2(l ^ r).
The total number of levels is log2(N).

title: interval-dp
when_to_use: When a problem involves finding an optimal value for a contiguous range of elements, and solutions for larger ranges can be derived by merging optimal solutions of smaller sub-ranges.
description: An extension of dynamic programming where subproblems are defined over contiguous intervals, solving for dp[i][j] by iterating through all possible split points k within [i, j]. This often results in O(N^3) time complexity.
tips: Define dp[i][j] as the optimal solution for the interval [i, j].
Iterate by interval length, then by start index.
Enumerate all possible split points k within the current interval [i, j].
Base cases are typically single-element or two-element intervals.
Ensure the merging operation correctly combines optimal subproblems.
pitfalls: Off-by-one errors in interval boundaries or split points.
Incorrect iteration order, leading to uncomputed subproblems.
Failing to define proper base cases for minimal intervals.
Assuming dp[i][j] directly combines dp[i][k] and dp[k+1][j] without additional cost.
invariants: dp[i][j] represents the optimal solution for the interval [i, j].
When computing dp[i][j], all subproblems dp[x][y] where y - x + 1 < j - i + 1 are already solved.
The split point k is always chosen such that i <= k < j.

title: interval-dp
signals: minimum or maximum value for a range
prefix sums for efficient range queries
when_to_use: Use when finding an optimal value (min/max) for operations on contiguous subarrays or segments, especially when subproblems are defined by intervals and can be combined from smaller adjacent intervals. Useful for problems involving circular arrangements by duplicating the array.
description: Interval DP is a dynamic programming technique that solves problems by breaking them into subproblems defined over contiguous intervals. It typically computes solutions for smaller intervals first, then uses these to build solutions for larger intervals, often with an O(N^3) time complexity.
tips: Define dp[i][j] as the optimal solution for the interval [i, j].
Iterate by interval length first, then by start index.
Use prefix sums to efficiently calculate sums or costs of intervals.
For circular arrays, duplicate the array to handle wrap-around cases.
The split point k divides [i, j] into two sub-intervals, e.g., [i, k-1] and [k, j].
pitfalls: Incorrectly defining base cases for intervals of length 1.
Off-by-one errors in interval boundaries or split points.
Forgetting to handle circular array cases by duplicating the input.
Not initializing DP table values correctly (e.g., INF for min, -INF for max).
Miscalculating the cost of merging the two subproblems.
invariants: For any `len`, `dp[l][r]` for `r - l + 1 < len` are already computed.
`dp[l][r]` represents the optimal cost for the operation on the range `[l, r]`.
`a[i]` stores the cumulative sum of elements from the original `a[1]` to `a[i]`.
The split point `k` always divides the current interval `[l, r]` into two valid sub-intervals.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
when_to_use: When you need to find the maximum length of a subsequence where elements are strictly increasing. It is applicable for problems requiring optimal ordering of non-contiguous elements.
description: This DP approach calculates the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at each position in an array. It builds upon previously computed subsequence lengths, resulting in an O(N^2) time complexity.
tips: Initialize DP array with 1s, as each element itself forms an LIS of length 1.
Iterate through all previous elements to find potential predecessors.
Update the current element's LIS length if a longer one is found.
The final answer is the maximum value found in the entire DP array.
pitfalls: Forgetting to initialize the DP array correctly.
Incorrectly handling the strict increasing condition (e.g., allowing equality).
Not considering all previous elements for potential extensions.
Confusing subsequence with subarray, as subsequence elements don't need to be contiguous.
invariants: dp[i] stores the length of the LIS ending at index i.
For any j < i, if A[j] < A[i], then dp[j] + 1 is a candidate length for dp[i].
After iterating through all j < i, dp[i] holds the maximum possible LIS length ending at A[i].

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
when_to_use: Use when you need to find the longest possible subsequence of a given sequence such that elements in the subsequence are in strictly increasing order. It is often applied to optimization problems involving ordered subsets.
description: The Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem finds the longest subsequence of a given sequence where all elements are sorted in increasing order. It can be solved in O(N log N) time using dynamic programming with binary search optimization, or O(N^2) using basic DP.
tips: The O(N log N) approach uses a 'tails' array to store the smallest tail of all increasing subsequences of length 'i+1'.
Binary search is crucial for efficiently finding the correct position for the current element in the 'tails' array.
For the O(N^2) DP, initialize each dp[i] to 1, representing the element itself as an LIS of length 1.
Carefully distinguish between strictly increasing and non-decreasing requirements.
The 'tails' array elements do not necessarily form an actual LIS, but its length is the LIS length.
pitfalls: Confusing subsequence with contiguous subarray or substring.
Incorrectly updating or searching in the 'tails' array, leading to wrong lengths or values.
Off-by-one errors in binary search indices or array access.
Failing to handle duplicate elements correctly if strict increasing is required.
Misinterpreting the 'tails' array as the LIS itself, rather than a helper structure.
invariants: The 'tails' array always stores the smallest ending element for all increasing subsequences of a given length.
The 'tails' array remains sorted in strictly increasing order throughout the algorithm's execution.
The length of the LIS found so far is equal to the current effective size of the 'tails' array.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
when_to_use: Use when finding the maximum length of a subsequence where elements must be strictly increasing. It is applicable for problems requiring optimal substructure on sequences with an increasing property.
description: The Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem finds the longest subsequence of a given sequence such that all elements of the subsequence are in increasing order. It can be solved in O(N log N) time using dynamic programming with binary search or a segment tree with coordinate compression.
tips: The O(N log N) solution uses a `dp` array storing the smallest ending element for all increasing subsequences of a given length.
Binary search (`lower_bound`) efficiently finds the correct position to update in the `dp` array.
Coordinate compression is necessary when using segment trees if element values are large.
A segment tree can optimize the DP transition for range maximum queries over compressed values.
pitfalls: Confusing subsequence with subarray, which requires contiguous elements.
Incorrectly handling duplicates; LIS typically requires strictly increasing elements.
Off-by-one errors in binary search or segment tree range queries.
Forgetting coordinate compression when element values exceed segment tree index limits.
invariants: The `dp` array (or segment tree) stores information about the smallest ending element for increasing subsequences of various lengths.
The `dp` array, if used, remains sorted in strictly increasing order.
The length of the `dp` array (or maximum value in segment tree) represents the current longest increasing subsequence found.

title: Multi source BFS
signals: all reachable nodes from a set
when_to_use: Use when you need to find the shortest distance from any of a given set of source nodes to all other reachable nodes in an unweighted graph or grid.
description: Multi-source BFS explores a graph or grid by starting from multiple initial nodes simultaneously, finding the shortest path from any of these sources to all other reachable nodes. It operates in O(V + E) time for graphs or O(rows * cols) for grids.
tips: Initialize distances for all sources to 0 and add them to the queue.
Use a `visited` array or set to prevent redundant processing of nodes.
Ensure all initial sources are added to the queue *before* starting the main loop.
Can model 'wave propagation' or 'fire spreading' from multiple origins.
For grid problems, consider using a 2D array for distances and visited states.
pitfalls: Forgetting to mark initial sources as visited before adding to the queue.
Incorrectly initializing distances for non-source nodes (should be infinity or a large value).
Processing a node multiple times if the `visited` check is missing or misplaced.

Not handling edge cases like an empty set of sources or a disconnected graph.
invariants: All nodes currently in the queue are at the same shortest distance level from any source.
When a node `u` is dequeued, `dist[u]` represents the finalized shortest distance from any source to `u`.
The `visited` set contains all nodes for which the shortest distance has been determined.
For any node `v` not yet visited, its `dist[v]` is either infinity or a distance greater than or equal to the current processing level.

title: Ordered Set and GNU C++ PBDS

description: An ordered set is a policy-based data structure (PBDS) in GNU C++ that stores unique elements in sorted order. It offers O(log N) operations for standard set functionalities and order statistics, such as finding the k-th element or counting elements smaller than a value.

For any element `k`, `order_of_key(k)` correctly reflects the count of elements strictly less than `k`.

title: Rejection Sampling in Artificial Intelligence
when_to_use: Use when direct sampling from a complex target probability distribution is difficult or computationally expensive. It allows generating samples using a simpler, more tractable proposal distribution.
description: Rejection sampling generates random samples from a complex target distribution by drawing from a simpler proposal distribution and probabilistically accepting or rejecting candidates. The process has a variable time complexity per sample due to potential rejections.
tips: Ensure the proposal distribution q(x) 'covers' the target distribution p(x) when scaled by M.
Choose a proposal distribution q(x) that is easy to sample from and closely approximates p(x).
Minimize the constant M to reduce the rejection rate and improve efficiency.
Repeat the sampling process until the desired number of accepted samples is obtained.
pitfalls: High rejection rates can occur if M is large or q(x) poorly approximates p(x), leading to inefficiency.
Finding an appropriate bounding constant M and a suitable proposal distribution q(x) can be challenging.
The method can become inefficient or impractical for very high-dimensional target distributions.
The accepted samples are only guaranteed to follow p(x) if the M*q(x) >= p(x) condition holds strictly.
invariants: The scaled proposal distribution M * q(x) is always greater than or equal to the target distribution p(x).
The acceptance probability alpha(x) is always within the range [0, 1].
The uniform random number u is always within the range [0, 1].
Accepted samples are statistically equivalent to samples drawn directly from p(x).

title: Rejection Sampling in Artificial Intelligence
when_to_use: When you need to sample from a complex target probability distribution that is difficult to sample directly, especially when its probability density function is known up to a normalizing constant.

Its average time complexity is O(M * sample_cost_q) per accepted sample, where M bounds the ratio of target to proposal PDFs.
tips: Choose a proposal distribution that is easy to sample from.
Ensure the proposal distribution closely resembles the target distribution.
Minimize the constant M to improve acceptance rate.
Verify M is an upper bound for p(x)/q(x) over the entire domain.
Normalize the proposal distribution if necessary for proper PDF interpretation.
pitfalls: High rejection rate if proposal distribution is poorly chosen.
Difficulty in finding an optimal M for complex or high-dimensional distributions.
Inefficient for target distributions with narrow peaks or wide tails.
Can be computationally expensive if M is large.
Requires the target PDF to be bounded by M * proposal PDF everywhere.

The constant M always satisfies p(x) <= M * q(x) for all x.
The accepted samples are drawn from the target distribution p(x).

title: Rejection Sampling in Artificial Intelligence
when_to_use: Use when direct sampling from a target probability distribution is difficult, but its probability density function can be evaluated. It is suitable for generating independent samples from complex or unnormalized distributions.

title: Rejection sampling
when_to_use: Use when you need to generate random samples from a complex or non-standard probability distribution for which direct sampling methods are difficult or impossible. It's effective when a suitable proposal distribution can be found.

tips: Choose a proposal distribution g(x) that is easy to sample from and closely resembles the target f(x).
