title: Reservoir Sampling
when_to_use: When you need to randomly select a fixed number of items (k) from a stream or a very large dataset of unknown size, ensuring each item has an equal probability of selection in a single pass.
description: Reservoir sampling is a randomized algorithm that selects k items from a list containing n items, where n is either a very large or unknown number, ensuring each item has an equal probability of being selected. It processes the input in a single pass with O(N) time complexity and O(k) space complexity.
tips: Initialize the reservoir with the first k elements from the stream.
For each subsequent element, generate a random index from 0 to current_stream_index.
If the random index is less than k, replace the element at that index in the reservoir.
The probability of any item being in the final reservoir is k/n.
pitfalls: Failing to correctly initialize the reservoir with the first k elements.
Generating random indices outside the valid range [0, current_stream_index] or [0, k-1].
Using a biased random number generator, leading to non-uniform selection.

This allows elements to move large distances, improving efficiency over basic insertion sort, with typical average-case complexity better than O(N^2) but worse than O(N log N).

pitfalls: Naive O(N^2 log N) construction is too slow for large input strings.
Implementing linear-time algorithms like SA-IS or DC3 can be complex and error-prone.
Memory usage can be significant for very long strings, as it stores N integers.
Off-by-one errors with suffix indices and string boundaries are common.
Different alphabet types (constant, integer, general) may require specific algorithm variants.
invariants: The suffix array SA[i] stores the starting index of the i-th lexicographically smallest suffix.
All suffixes of the input string are present exactly once in the suffix array.
For any two adjacent suffixes SA[i] and SA[i+1], suffix S[SA[i]...] is lexicographically less than or equal to S[SA[i+1]...].

title: Suffix array
when_to_use: Use when needing to perform fast substring searches, pattern matching, or other string operations on a fixed text, especially when space efficiency is critical compared to suffix trees.
description: A suffix array is a sorted array of all suffixes of a given string, storing only their starting indices. It is often paired with an LCP (Longest Common Prefix) array to enable efficient string algorithms, typically achieving O(N log N) or O(N) construction and O(M + log N) or O(M) search complexity.
tips: Pair with an LCP array to significantly speed up pattern searching and other string queries.
Consider Enhanced Suffix Arrays (ESA) for improved space and time efficiency over suffix trees, especially for large datasets.
Specialized linear-time algorithms (e.g., SA-IS) exist for efficient suffix array construction.
Can be used as a building block for other string algorithms like the Burrows-Wheeler Transform.
pitfalls: Naive pattern searching without LCP information can be O(M log N), which might be too slow for long patterns.
Construction can be complex to implement efficiently in linear time.
Requires careful handling of special end-of-string characters for correct lexicographical sorting.
While space-efficient compared to suffix trees, it still requires O(N) space for the array itself and potentially the LCP array.
invariants: The suffix array `A` stores indices such that `S[A[i]:]` is lexicographically smaller than `S[A[i+1]:]` for all valid `i`.
The `lcp[i]` value represents the length of the longest common prefix between `S[A[i-1]:]` and `S[A[i]:]`.
For any pattern `P`, the range `[s, r)` in the suffix array `A` contains all suffixes that start with `P`.

title: Non-negative Integers without Consecutive Ones
when_to_use: Use when counting numbers up to a given limit that satisfy a specific bit pattern constraint, particularly when the limit is small enough for direct iteration and checking.
description: This approach iterates through every non-negative integer from 0 up to the given limit num. For each integer, it simulates checking its binary representation to determine if it contains any two consecutive '1's. The time complexity is O(num * log(num)).
tips: Use bitwise operations like (i & (i >> 1)) to efficiently detect consecutive ones.
Remember to include 0 in the count as it satisfies the condition (no consecutive ones).
Consider the maximum possible value of num to estimate performance implications.
Convert to binary string for easier debugging if bitwise operations are unfamiliar.
pitfalls: Performance degradation for large num due to linear scan and per-number bit check.
Incorrectly handling the upper bound of the iteration (e.g., i < num instead of i <= num).
Errors in bitwise logic for detecting consecutive ones.
Overlooking the base case of 0 or small numbers.
invariants: count stores the number of integers k (where 0 <= k < i) that do not have consecutive ones.
All integers j such that 0 <= j < i have been processed.
The current integer i is being evaluated against the consecutive ones condition.
i is always within the range [0, num].

title: Non-negative Integers without Consecutive Ones
when_to_use: Use when counting numbers within a range that satisfy a specific digit-based or bit-based pattern constraint, especially when the constraint involves adjacent digits or bits.
description: This algorithm recursively generates binary numbers bit by bit, ensuring no two consecutive ones are formed. It prunes branches that exceed a given upper limit or violate the consecutive one constraint, effectively counting valid numbers up to N in O(log N) time.
tips: Generate numbers bit by bit, typically from LSB or MSB, to build up valid sequences.
Maintain state about the previously placed bit to enforce the 'no consecutive ones' rule.
Prune the search space early by stopping generation if the current number exceeds the given limit.
Consider using memoization (dynamic programming) to optimize repeated subproblems for a true O(log N) solution.
pitfalls: Failing to correctly handle the base cases for the recursive function.
Incorrectly managing the 'previous bit' state, leading to invalid sequences.
Not pruning branches that exceed the upper bound, resulting in unnecessary computation.
Off-by-one errors when dealing with bit positions or the upper limit.
invariants: The `sum` generated so far never exceeds the target `num`.
The `prev` boolean accurately reflects whether the last bit added was a '1'.
No two consecutive '1's exist in the binary representation of the `sum` generated at any step.
The `i` parameter correctly tracks the current bit position being considered for placement.

title: palindrome-partitioning
when_to_use: Use this when you need to find all possible ways to break down a string into parts, where each part must satisfy a specific property. It is suitable for generating all combinations or permutations with constraints.
description: This algorithm recursively explores all possible ways to partition a string into substrings, ensuring each substring is a palindrome. It employs a backtracking approach to build valid partitions incrementally, typically resulting in exponential time complexity.
tips: Utilize a helper function to manage additional state, such as the current partition path and the overall result list.
Pass the remaining string or a start index to recursive calls to define the subproblem for subsequent partitioning.
Implement a separate utility function to efficiently check if a given substring is a palindrome.
Ensure to 'unchoose' or backtrack by removing the last added element from the current path before exploring other options.
Consider memoizing palindrome checks for substrings to optimize performance, especially for overlapping subproblems.
pitfalls: Forgetting to create a new copy of the current partition path before adding it to the final result list, leading to incorrect results.
Incorrectly defining the base case for recursion, which can cause infinite loops or missed valid partitions.
Failing to handle edge cases such as null or empty input strings gracefully.
Inefficiently re-checking the palindrome status for the same substrings multiple times without memoization.
Incorrectly managing the start and end indices for substrings in recursive calls, leading to out-of-bounds errors or incorrect partitions.
invariants: At the start of each recursive call, the 'step' list contains a valid palindrome partition for the string segment processed so far.
The current processing index (or remaining string) always points to the unpartitioned suffix of the original input string.
Any substring considered for addition to the current partition path is verified to be a palindrome before being chosen.
The 'result' list correctly accumulates all complete and valid palindrome partitions found upon reaching a base case.

title: palindrome-partitioning
when_to_use: When a string needs to be decomposed into all possible sets of palindromic substrings. It's suitable for problems requiring exhaustive enumeration of such partitions.
description: This algorithm finds all possible ways to partition a given string such that every substring in the partition is a palindrome. It typically uses a recursive exploration approach combined with a two-pointer check for palindrome validation, resulting in an exponential time complexity.
tips: Implement the `isPalindrome` check efficiently using two pointers for O(length) time.
Use a recursive helper function to explore all possible partition points.
Pass the remaining suffix of the string to the recursive call.
Maintain a temporary list to store the current partition being built.
The base case for recursion is when the remaining string is empty, indicating a complete partition.
pitfalls: Incorrectly handling substring boundaries, leading to off-by-one errors or incorrect partitions.
Forgetting to backtrack (remove the last added substring) after a recursive call returns.
Inefficiently checking for palindromes by repeatedly creating new string objects.
Missing the base case or having an incorrect termination condition for the recursion.
Performance issues on very long strings due to the exponential nature of the solution.
invariants: At any point, the `step` list contains a valid palindromic partition of the prefix processed so far.
The `s` parameter in the recursive function always represents the unpartitioned suffix of the original string.
Each candidate substring `temp` is verified to be a palindrome before being added to the current partition.
The `result` list accumulates complete palindromic partitions only when the remaining string `s` is empty.

title: kth-largest-element-in-an-array
when_to_use: Use Merge Sort when a stable, guaranteed O(N log N) time complexity is required, especially for large datasets or linked lists where random access is costly.
description: Merge Sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that recursively divides an array into halves, sorts them, and then merges the sorted halves. It has a time complexity of O(N log N) and a space complexity of O(N).
tips: Implement the merge step carefully to maintain stability.
Consider iterative implementation to avoid recursion depth limits.
Optimize space by reusing auxiliary arrays where possible.
pitfalls: High space complexity due to auxiliary arrays.
Overhead of recursion can be significant for small inputs.
Incorrect merge logic can lead to unstable or incorrect sorts.
invariants: Each sub-array passed to merge is sorted.
The merge operation combines two sorted sub-arrays into a single sorted array.
The final array is a permutation of the original elements and is sorted.

title: kth-largest-element-in-an-array
when_to_use: Use this when you need to find the k-th largest (or smallest) element in an unsorted array efficiently without fully sorting it. It is particularly effective when k is much smaller than N.
description: This algorithm uses a min-heap of size k to store the k largest elements encountered so far. It iterates through the array, comparing each element with the heap's minimum; if larger, it replaces the minimum, resulting in O(N log K) time complexity and O(K) space complexity.
tips: Use a min-heap to find the k-th largest element, and a max-heap for the k-th smallest.
Initialize the heap with the first k elements from the input array.
Always compare the current array element with the heap's root (smallest in a min-heap).
Ensure the heap size is strictly maintained at k by removing the smallest element before inserting a larger one.
pitfalls: Incorrectly using a max-heap when trying to find the k-th largest element.
Failing to handle edge cases such as k=1 or k equal to the array's length.
Not properly managing heap size, leading to a heap larger than k or incorrect elements.
Off-by-one errors when dealing with k or array indices.
invariants: The min-heap always contains at most k elements.
Every element in the min-heap is greater than or equal to any element that has been processed and discarded.
The root of the min-heap holds the smallest value among the elements currently stored in the heap.

title: kth-largest-element-in-an-array
when_to_use: Use when you need to efficiently find the k-th smallest or largest element in an unsorted array without fully sorting it, especially when average-case linear time complexity is acceptable and in-place modification is desired.
description: QuickSelect is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that partitions an array around a pivot and recursively searches for the k-th element in the relevant partition. It offers an average-case O(N) time complexity and O(1) space.
tips: Randomly select pivots to ensure average-case O(N) performance and avoid worst-case scenarios.
Adjust the target index k based on whether you're seeking the k-th smallest or k-th largest element.
The algorithm modifies the input array in place, which is memory efficient but alters the original data.
Only recurse into the partition that contains the target k-th element, unlike QuickSort which recurses on both.
pitfalls: Poor pivot selection can lead to a worst-case O(N^2) time complexity, though rare with randomization.
Off-by-one errors are common when mapping k-th largest/smallest to 0-indexed array positions.
Incorrect partitioning logic can result in infinite loops or incorrect element placement.
Deep recursion in worst-case scenarios might lead to stack overflow issues for very large inputs.
invariants: The pivot element is always placed at its correct sorted position within the current sub-array.
All elements to the left of the pivot are less than or equal to it, and all elements to its right are greater than or equal to it.
The target k-th element is always contained within the current [left, right] search boundaries.
The size of the search space (right - left + 1) strictly decreases in each recursive step.
